Iteration 2 Retrospective  
Team 036 - Dream Studio

What went well?
1. Improved communication: We started having regular stand-ups every second day, which helped us keep track of progress and identify blockers early.
2. Better use of Git: All team members began using Git branches for their features, and we merged changes more systematically, reducing code conflicts.
3. Database improvements: After reviewing the previous issues, we updated our ERD and rebaked CakePHP models, which made development smoother.
4. More detailed user stories: We spent more time refining user stories and acceptance criteria, which made task allocation and development clearer.
5. Team roles: Assigning programming and documentation leads helped clarify responsibilities and improved coordination.

What didn’t go well?
1. Time management: Despite planning, some tasks were still left until close to the deadline, causing last-minute rushes.
2. Incomplete research: Some team members still struggled with CakePHP concepts, especially with associations and form handling.
3. Testing: We did not allocate enough time for thorough testing, leading to some bugs being found late in the iteration.
4. Client feedback delays: We did not always get timely feedback from the client, which delayed some changes and acceptance testing.
5. Documentation gaps: Some technical documentation was not updated as features were developed, leading to confusion during handover.

How can we improve?
1. Stick to the schedule: We will set internal deadlines a few days before the official ones to allow buffer time for testing and documentation.
2. More focused research: Team members will be assigned specific CakePHP topics to research and present to the group, so everyone is on the same page.
3. Prioritise testing: Allocate dedicated time for testing before the iteration ends, and assign a testing lead to coordinate this.
4. Proactive client communication: Schedule regular check-ins with the client to ensure feedback is received promptly.
5. Update documentation continuously: Make it a habit to update technical and user documentation as soon as features are completed, not just at the end.

==================== Page 2 ====================

6. Encourage knowledge sharing:
- Hold short knowledge-sharing sessions during stand-ups for any new CakePHP or Git tips.
- Create a shared document for common issues and solutions encountered during development.

7. Strengthen team accountability:
- Each member will report on their progress and blockers during stand-ups.
- Leads will follow up with members who are falling behind and offer support.

8. Improve acceptance criteria:
- Ensure acceptance criteria are specific, measurable, and testable for each user story.
- Review acceptance criteria as a team before starting development to avoid confusion.

9. Enhance system maintainability:
- Refactor code where necessary to improve readability and maintainability.
- Ensure all new code follows agreed naming conventions and is well-commented.

By implementing these improvements, we aim to deliver a more robust and maintainable system in the next iteration, with better team coordination and client satisfaction.
LLM tutor feedback:
This is a well-structured and thoughtful retrospective that addresses the key requirements of the assignment. You clearly identify what went well, what didn’t, and provide actionable steps for improvement, which aligns with the marking rubric’s expectations for critical reflection and process focus.

Strengths:
- Your reflection is specific and covers both technical and team process aspects (e.g., Git usage, stand-ups, documentation, and client communication).
- The “How can we improve?” section is detailed, with practical and relevant suggestions that show a good understanding of agile retrospectives and continuous improvement.
- You demonstrate critical thinking by not only listing issues but also proposing concrete solutions (e.g., assigning research topics, internal deadlines, and a testing lead).
- The addition of knowledge sharing and accountability measures shows a mature approach to team development.

Suggestions for further improvement:
- While your points are clear, you could further prioritise the most impactful improvements to focus on in the next iteration, to avoid spreading efforts too thin.
- Consider briefly reflecting on how previous retrospective actions were implemented and their outcomes, to close the feedback loop.
- Ensure that all process changes are realistic given your team’s capacity and iteration timeline.

Overall, this retrospective is comprehensive, well-organised, and demonstrates a strong understanding of both the technical and collaborative aspects of the project. Keep up the good work, and continue to iterate on your team processes for even better results in future iterations.

Sample question
Suppose you are a data scientist working for Monash Biomedicine Discovery Institute. Your team is conducting a biomedical experiment. There are three different results in the experiment, A1, A2, A3, and the probability of occurrence of each is 1/3. The experiment is repeated twice: X represents the number of occurrences of the result A1 in the 2 experiments, and Y represents the number of occurrences of the result A2 in the two experiments. Now it is your job to calculate the correlation coefficient between X and Y.

Solution
Let Z be the number of occurences of the result A3 then we have [Math: X+Y+Z=2].

According to the property of Variance, [Math: V(Y)=V(2-X-Z)=V(X+Z)=V(X)+V(Z)+2Cov(X,Z)]

Notice [Math: V(X)=V(Y)=V(Z)], [Math: Cov(X,Z)=Cov(X,Y)], hence we have [Math: V(X)=-2Cov(X,Y)]

Finally we plug those in the formula and we get
\begin{split}
Corr(X,Y)&=\frac{Cov(X,Y)}{\sqrt{V(X)}\sqrt{V(Y)}}\\
&=-\frac{Cov(X,Y)}{-2Cov(X,Y)}\\
&=\frac{1}{2}
\end{split}

Consider the following events, A = “each value appears at least once” and B = “the outcome is alternate in numbers (i.e., a specific face of the die cannot be observed to occur directly after it was observed on the previous roll but it may have occurred elsewhere in the sequence of rolls)”. What is p(A|B)? Are you likely to win the house? Solve this analytically (enumeration is not allowed) and experimentally by simulation, in each case printing the value found.



Answer

Question 4: PDF, CDF (2+2+3+3=10 Marks)
Suppose you are a data scientist working for NASA. You and your team are studying star annihilation. The latest research shows that the probability of star annihilation lasting more than t millon years is
\begin{equation}
\frac{225}{(t+15)^2}, t\ge0
\end{equation}
After that, the star will rapidly collapse into a black hole.
1. What is the probability density function (PDF) for t? What is the cumulative density function (CDF) for t?
2. Find the probability that a randomly selected star will annihilate within 3 million years.
3. Given that a star has been annihilated for 3 million years already, find the probability that it will annihilate for at least 5 more million years.
4. You observe that a large number of stars begin annihilating today. Find the number of years after which only 10% of these stars are expected to annihilate (the rest have completed annihilation and collapsed into black hole).



Answer
1.

Assume that the time of star annihilation is [Math: x].

[Math: \therefore P(x\leq t)=1-\frac{225}{(t+15)^2}, t\ge0]

[Math: \therefore] CDF is [Math: F_x(x)=1-\frac{225}{(x+15)^2}, x\ge0].

and PDF is [Math: f_x(x)=(1-\frac{225}{(x+15)^2})'=\frac{450}{(x+15)^3}, x\ge0].
2.The question is to calculate [Math: P(x\leq 3)]

[Math: \therefore P(x\leq 3)=F(3)=\frac{11}{36}].
3.Assume that a star has been annihilated for 3 million years already is [Math: A], and a star has been annihilated more than 8 million years is [Math: B].

[Math: \therefore P(A)=1-F(3)=\frac{225}{324}] 

  [Math: P(B)=1-F(8)=\frac{225}{529}]

[Math: \therefore P(B|A)=\frac{P(AB)}{P(A)}=\frac{P(B)}{P(A)}=\frac{324}{529}]
4.Assume the number of years is [Math: m].

[Math: \therefore 1-F(m)=0.1]

That is [Math: \frac {225}{(m+15)^2}=0.1]

[Math: \therefore m =  32.4341]

Question 5: Chebyshev’s inequality, Correlation (7+8=15 Marks)
1. Enceladus is the sixth-largest moon of Saturn. Enceladus is often called the protector of Saturn. Many meteorites that were supposed to fly to Saturn will be affected by the Enceladus's gravity and hit Enceladus. Now suppose that the probability of a meteorite being attracted by Enceladus and hitting Enceladus is 50%. When there are 1600 meteorites flying to Saturn, what is the probability that more than 400 meteorites and less than 1200 meteorites will hit Enceladus?
2. There are 2 white balls and 2 red balls in each of two boxes called box A and box B. First take one ball from box A, put it into box B after viewing the color, and then take one ball from box B. Let X and Y be the number of red balls taken from boxes A and B respectively, then what is the correlation coefficient between X and Y.



Answer
1.

Assume that the number of meteorites flying to Saturn is [Math: X],

So [Math: X \sim B(1600,0.5)]

According to Chebyshev's inequality

[Math:  X=1600, \mu =1600 \times 0.5 = 800]

[Math: \varepsilon = 400, \sigma^2 =400]

[Math: \therefore P(400\le X \le 1200) = P(|X-800|\le 400) = P({|X- \mu | <  \varepsilon }) = 1 - \frac{\sigma^2} {\varepsilon^2} = \frac{399}{400}] 

2.

[Math: P(X=0,Y=0)=\frac{1}{2}\times \frac{3}{5}=\frac{3}{10}]

[Math: P(X=0,Y=1)=\frac{1}{2}\times \frac{2}{5}=\frac{1}{5}]

[Math: P(X=1,Y=0)=\frac{1}{2}\times \frac{2}{5}=\frac{1}{5}]

[Math: P(X=1,Y=1)=\frac{1}{2}\times \frac{3}{5}=\frac{3}{10}]

[Math: \therefore E(X)=0\times 0.5+1\times 0.5=0.5].

[Math: E(Y)=0\times 0.5+1\times 0.5=0.5]

[Math: E(XY)=1\times 0.3 = 0.3]

[Math: cov(X,Y)=E(XY)-E(X)E(Y) = 0.05].

And [Math: D(X)=D(Y)=0.25]

[Math: \therefore \rho = \frac{cov(X,Y)}{\sqrt{D(X)} {\sqrt{D(Y)}}}=0.2]

Question 6: Distribution, Probability (4+6=10 Marks)
Suppose you are working for the European Space Agency and are studying meteors. After statistical investigation, 5 meteorites fly into the earth’s atmosphere every year. Now you want to find
1. What is the probability there will be at least 2 meteors flying into earth's atmosphere in the first half of 2021?
2. Assuming the event in question (a) occurs, what is the probability that there will be at least 4 meteors flying into earth's atmosphere over the first 9 months in the year 2021?



Answer
1.

Assume that the number of meteors flying into earth's atmosphere in the first half of 2021 is [Math: A].

[Math: \therefore P(A \geq 2) = 1-P(A < 2) = 1-P(A=1)-P(A=0)]=[Math:  1- C{5}^{4} \times (\frac{1}{2})^4 \times \frac{1}{2} - C{5}^{5}\times(\frac{1}{2})^5 ]= [Math: 1- \frac{5}{32} - \frac{1}{32}]=[Math: \frac{13}{16}]

2.

Assume that the probability at least 4 meteors flying into earth's atmosphere over the first 9 months in the year 2021 is [Math: B].

[Math:  P(B\geq4|A\geq2)=P(B=4|A=2)+P(B=5|A=2)+P(B=4|A=3)+P(B=5|A=3)+P(B=4|A=4)+P(B=5|A=4)+P(B=5|A=5)] 

= [Math: \frac{3}{8}\times\frac{5}{16} + \frac{1}{8} \times \frac{5}{16} + \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{16} + \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{5}{16} + \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{32} + \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{32} + \frac{1}{32} ]

=[Math: \frac{9}{16}]

Question 7: Distribution, PDF, CDF (3+2+5=10 Marks)
Assume you are a data scientist working for the Victoria Police. According to the report from the accident investigation department, Mitsubishi EVO X seems to have more accidents than the other vehicles and they want to know some extra information about it. You have a dataset of the number of accidents related to Mitsubishi EVO X reported weekly. The dataset is as follows.

| Time period    | Vehicle model     | Number of reported cases     
|  Week 1 2021 | Mitsubishi EVO X   | 9    |
|  Week 2 2021 | Mitsubishi EVO X   | 9    |
|  Week 3 2021 | Mitsubishi EVO X   |  7   |
|  Week 4 2021 | Mitsubishi EVO X   | 12    |
|  Week 5 2021 | Mitsubishi EVO X   | 8    |
|  Week 6 2021 | Mitsubishi EVO X   | 11    |
|  Week 7 2021 | Mitsubishi EVO X   | 8    |
|  Week 8 2021 | Mitsubishi EVO X   | 8    |

We take [Math: X] as the time interval between two accidents in the following calculation.

Based on this dataset, now it is your duty to find out 
1. PDF(X)
2. CDF(X) 
3. [Math: \mathbf{E}[X]]



Answer

1. It can be known from prior knowledge that the time interval between two accidents [Math: X \sim E(\lambda)]

[Math: \because] Obviously [Math: x>0]

[Math: \therefore PDF(X)=f(x)= \lambda e^{- \lambda x}].     Here [Math: \lambda] is unknown.

[Math: \lambda] is the number of occurrences of events in a unit time. It should be noted here that the unit time can be in different units such as seconds, minutes, hours, etc.

Let [Math: \lambda] be the number of car accidents in a week.

[Math: \therefore \lambda = \frac{9 + 9 + 7 + 12 + 8 + 11 + 8 + 8}{8} = 9].

[Math: \therefore PDF(X)=f(x)= 9e^{- 9x},x>0]  
2.

[Math: CDF(X)= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}f(x)dx = 1 - e^{- 9x}, x>0]

3.

[Math: E(X)= \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac {1}{9}]

Assignment weighting 15% - Lecturer in Charge: Minh Le
MonHeli is a thriving helicopter company. The company owns a range of helicopters whose
standard cost of hire may vary depending on the type of charter and the number of hours
flown. Each helicopter is identified by their call sign; much like a registration number
identifies a car. The total time each helicopter is flown is recorded to keep track of
maintenance schedules. The standard cost of hire for a helicopter depends on the
helicopter type however this may be varied over time and for certain clients (eg. frequent
flyers). Each of the helicopters has a fixed number of passenger seats available. The
company’s charter work is categorised as: scenic flight, transport, filming, geological survey,
fire mapping, fuel reduction burning, logging coupe regeneration burning, aerial seeding,
training, and search and rescue.
The company has a number of employees consisting of pilots, engineers and a secretary.
Details are kept of each employee’s name, address, contact number, Tax File Number,
salary and next of kin. As only qualified pilots are allowed to take charters, records are kept
of the pilots’ endorsements. An endorsement is the credential a pilot has to fly a certain type
of helicopter. Therefore, when a pilot is endorsed to fly a certain helicopter type (eg.
JetRanger) a record is kept of the pilot’s endorsement for this type of helicopter and the total
hours the pilot has flown in this helicopter type. This indicates the pilot’s level of experience
in this type of aircraft. For a pilot to have a current endorsement for a helicopter type they
must be reviewed annually and the date of their last review is kept for each of their
endorsements.
Charters can vary in time – from several days to part of an hour. A charter is flown by one
pilot using one helicopter. As clients only pay for the time spent flying, a record is kept of
every time the helicopter takes off and lands during a charter flight (a leg) - a charter may
consist of one or more legs. When a client initially makes a booking, the destination, the
estimated time of departure (ETD) and estimated time of arrival (ETA) are recorded for each
leg of the charter. For example, a client may book a helicopter to fly them from Gondor
Airport to Rivendell for a 10:00 am meeting, then leave at 12:00 noon to fly to Mount Doom
for 3 hours skiing and then back to Gondor Airport by 6:00 pm.

/*
Q1

List all endorsements held by pilots where the endorsements last annual
review date was after 31st March 2020.

Show the helicopter type number, employee number, employee lastname, employee
firstname and the date of the review.

This listing should be displayed in ascending order of the last annual review date.

Allow short or long form of month (Mon or Month)
*/

SELECT
    ht_nbr,
    emp.emp_nbr,
    emp_lname,
    emp_fname,
    to_char(end_last_annual_review, 'Dy dd Mon YYYY') AS review_date
FROM
         mh.employee emp
    JOIN mh.endorsement en
    ON emp.emp_nbr = en.emp_nbr
WHERE
    end_last_annual_review > TO_DATE('31-Mar-2020', 'dd-Mon-yyyy')
ORDER BY
    end_last_annual_review;

/*
Q2
List those charters where special requirements are specified.

Show the charter number, client number, client lastname, client firstname and
the special requirements specified.

This listing should be displayed in ascending order of the charter number.
*/

SELECT
    charter_nbr,
    c.client_nbr,
    client_lname,
    client_fname,
    charter_special_reqs
FROM
         mh.client c
    JOIN mh.charter ch
    ON c.client_nbr = ch.client_nbr
WHERE
    charter_special_reqs IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY
    charter_nbr;

/*
Q3

List the charter details for those charters which meet the following requirements:
- destination is Mount Doom, and
- the charter cost is less than $1000 per hour, or no special requirements are specified

Show the charter number, client lastname, client firstname and the charter cost per hour.
The client name should be listed in a single column called FULLNAME (sample output: Gandalf The Grey).

The listing should be displayed in the descending order of the client FULLNAME.

*/

SELECT DISTINCT
    ch.charter_nbr,
    ltrim(client_fname
          || ' '
          || client_lname) AS FULLNAME,
    charter_cost_per_hour
FROM
         mh.client c
    JOIN mh.charter ch
    ON c.client_nbr = ch.client_nbr
    JOIN mh.charter_leg cl
    ON ch.charter_nbr = cl.charter_nbr
    JOIN mh.location l
    ON cl.location_nbr_destination = l.location_nbr
WHERE
        upper(location_name) = upper('Mount Doom')
    AND ( charter_cost_per_hour < 1000
          OR charter_special_reqs IS NULL )
ORDER BY
    FULLNAME DESC;

/*
Q4

For each helicopter type for which there are at least two helicopters of that
type, list the total number of helicopters of that type.

Show the helicopter type number, helicopter type name and the number of
helicopters owned.

The listing should be displayed in the descending order of number of helicopters.

*/

SELECT
    ht.ht_nbr,
    ht_name,
    COUNT(*) AS total_no_helicopters
FROM
         mh.helicopter_type ht
    JOIN mh.helicopter h
    ON ht.ht_nbr = h.ht_nbr
GROUP BY
    ht.ht_nbr,
    ht_name
HAVING
    COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY
    total_no_helicopters DESC;

/*
Q5

For each location that has been used as the origin of a charter leg more than once,
list the location as well as the number of times it has been used as an origin

Show location number, location name and the number of times the location has
been used as an origin.

The listing should be displayed in the ascending order of the number of times a
location has been used as an origin.

*/

SELECT
    l.location_nbr,
    location_name,
    COUNT(*) AS usedasorgin
FROM
         mh.charter_leg cl
    JOIN mh.location l
    ON ( l.location_nbr = cl.location_nbr_origin )
GROUP BY
    l.location_nbr,
    location_name
HAVING
    COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY
    usedasorgin;

/*
Q6

List the number of hours flown for EVERY helicopter type.

Show helicopter type number, helicopter type name and the total number of hours flown. Types
which have no recorded hours should be shown as 0 hours flown.

The listing should be displayed in the ascending order of hours flown.

*/

SELECT
    ht.ht_nbr,
    ht_name,
    nvl(SUM(heli_hrs_flown), 0) AS hoursflown
FROM
    mh.helicopter_type ht
    LEFT OUTER JOIN mh.helicopter h
    ON ( ht.ht_nbr = h.ht_nbr )
GROUP BY
    ht.ht_nbr,
    ht_name
ORDER BY
    hoursflown;

/*
Q7

List all the completed charters that Frodo Baggins has flown, with the most recent
charters appearing at the top of the list. A completed charter means all legs have
been flown.

Show the charter number and the date/time of departure for leg one ( note: a
charter may span several days).

Note: the DISTINCT is not required in the subselect to identify completed charters

*/

SELECT
    c.charter_nbr,
    to_char(MIN(cl_etd), 'dd-Mon-yyyy hh24:mi') AS "Charter Date/Time"
FROM
    (
             mh.charter c
        JOIN mh.charter_leg    cl
        ON cl.charter_nbr = c.charter_nbr
    )
    JOIN mh.employee       em
    ON c.emp_nbr = em.emp_nbr
WHERE
        upper(emp_fname) = upper('Frodo')
    AND upper(emp_lname) = upper('Baggins')
    AND c.charter_nbr NOT IN (
        SELECT DISTINCT
            charter_nbr
        FROM
            mh.charter_leg
        WHERE
            cl_ata IS NULL
    )
GROUP BY
    c.charter_nbr
ORDER BY
    MIN(cl_etd) DESC;

/*
Q8

List those completed charters where the total cost is less than the average total
cost for all charters. The total cost for a charter is obtained by multiplying the
charter cost per hour with the actual duration obtained from the leg actual departure
and actual arrival times. A completed charter means all legs have been flown.

Show charter number, client number, client lastname,
client firstname, total charter cost. The total charter cost should be
rounded to two decimal digits and displayed with a $ symbol e.g. $1234.56

For this question only, if either client name is empty '-' should be displayed.

The listing should be displayed in the descending order of total charter cost.

=> Allow range for LPAD 12 - 16, depends on display font size used.
=> Allow NVL on client_lname although not needed due to data model

*/

SELECT
    cl.charter_nbr,
    c.client_nbr,
    client_lname,
    nvl(client_fname, '-')       AS client_fname,
    lpad(to_char((SUM((cl_ata - cl_atd) * 24 * charter_cost_per_hour)), '$9999.99'),
         16)                    AS totalchartercost
FROM
         mh.client c
    JOIN mh.charter        ch
    ON ( c.client_nbr = ch.client_nbr )
    JOIN mh.charter_leg    cl
    ON ( ch.charter_nbr = cl.charter_nbr )
WHERE
    ch.charter_nbr NOT IN (
        SELECT DISTINCT
            charter_nbr
        FROM
            mh.charter_leg
        WHERE
            cl_ata IS NULL
    )
GROUP BY
    cl.charter_nbr,
    c.client_nbr,
    client_lname,
    client_fname
HAVING
    SUM((cl_ata - cl_atd) * 24 * charter_cost_per_hour) < (
        SELECT
            AVG(SUM((cl_ata - cl_atd) * 24 * charter_cost_per_hour))
        FROM
                 mh.charter ch
            JOIN mh.charter_leg cl
            ON ( ch.charter_nbr = cl.charter_nbr )
        WHERE
            ch.charter_nbr NOT IN (
                SELECT DISTINCT
                    charter_nbr
                FROM
                    mh.charter_leg
                WHERE
                    cl_ata IS NULL
            )
        GROUP BY
            cl.charter_nbr
    )
ORDER BY
    totalchartercost DESC;

/*
Q9

Which charters have been able to depart at the time estimated for all
legs of its flight?

Show the charter number, pilot’s name and the client’s name.

Order the output by charter number

DISTINCT in sub selects not required, MINUS is a set operation

*/

SELECT
    charter_nbr,
    ltrim(emp_fname
          || ' '
          || emp_lname)       AS pilotname,
    ltrim(client_fname
          || ' '
          || client_lname)    AS clientname
FROM
         mh.charter
    NATURAL JOIN mh.employee
    NATURAL JOIN mh.client
WHERE
    charter_nbr IN (
        SELECT
            distinct charter_nbr
        FROM
            mh.charter_leg
        WHERE
            cl_atd IS NOT NULL
        MINUS
        SELECT
            distinct charter_nbr
        FROM
            mh.charter_leg
        WHERE
            cl_etd <> cl_atd
    )
ORDER BY
    charter_nbr;

/*
Q10

For each client, list the name of their favorite destination location/s and
how many times they have visited that destination. The clients favorite
destination will be the location/s they visit the most based on completed
legs.

Show the client number, client full name in one column, the name of the
destination location and the number of times the location has been visited
by the client.

Order the output by the client number. Where a client has several favourite
destinations order them by destination name.

*/

SELECT
    cl.client_nbr,
    ltrim(client_fname
          || ' '
          || client_lname) AS client_fullname,
    location_name AS destination,
    COUNT(*) AS number_of_time_visited
FROM
         mh.client cl
    JOIN mh.charter ch
    ON cl.client_nbr = ch.client_nbr
    JOIN mh.charter_leg chl
    ON ch.charter_nbr = chl.charter_nbr
    JOIN mh.location l
    ON chl.location_nbr_destination = l.location_nbr
WHERE
    cl_ata IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY
    cl.client_nbr,
    ltrim(client_fname
          || ' '
          || client_lname),
    location_name
HAVING
    COUNT(*) = (
        SELECT
            MAX(COUNT(*))
        FROM
                 mh.charter ch2
            JOIN mh.charter_leg chl2
            ON ch2.charter_nbr = chl2.charter_nbr
        WHERE
                ch2.client_nbr = cl.client_nbr
            AND cl_ata IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY
            ch2.client_nbr,
            location_nbr_destination
    )
ORDER BY
    client_nbr,
    destination;

    AND cl.cl_leg_nbr = (
        SELECT
            cl_leg_nbr
        FROM
            mh.charter_leg
        WHERE
            mod(cl_leg_nbr, 2) = 0
        FETCH FIRST 1 ROW ONLY
    );



/*
    Q8
*/
-- PLEASE PLACE REQUIRED SQL STATEMENT FOR THIS PART HERE
-- ENSURE your query has a semicolon (;) at the end of this answer

SELECT
    chr.charter_nbr,
    cl.client_nbr,
    cl.client_lname,
    cl.client_fname,
    (
        SELECT
            SUM(AVG(chr.charter_cost_per_hour))
        FROM
            mh.charter
    ) AS totalchartercost
FROM
         mh.charter chr
    JOIN mh.charter_leg    chl
    ON chr.charter_nbr = chl.charter_nbr
    JOIN mh.client         cl
    ON chr.client_nbr = cl.client_nbr
WHERE
    (
        SELECT
            SUM(AVG(chr.charter_cost_per_hour))
        FROM
            mh.charter
    ) < (
        SELECT
            SUM(chr.charter_cost_per_hour)
        FROM
            mh.charter
    )
GROUP BY
    chr.charter_nbr,
    cl.client_nbr,
    cl.client_lname,
    cl.client_fname
ORDER BY
    totalchartercost;
/*
    Q9
*/
-- PLEASE PLACE REQUIRED SQL STATEMENT FOR THIS PART HERE
-- ENSURE your query has a semicolon (;) at the end of this answer
SELECT
    ch.charter_nbr,
    em.emp_fname
    || ' '
    || emp_lname      AS pilotname,
    cl.client_fname
    || ' '
    || client_lname   AS clientname
FROM
         mh.charter ch
    JOIN mh.charter_leg    chr
    ON ch.charter_nbr = chr.charter_nbr
    JOIN mh.client         cl
    ON ch.charter_nbr = cl.client_nbr
    JOIN mh.employee       em
    ON ch.emp_nbr = em.emp_nbr
WHERE
    to_char(cl_etd, 'HH:MI:SS dd Mon yyyy') = to_char(cl_atd, 'HH:MI:SS dd Mon yyyy')
ORDER BY
    ch.charter_nbr;


/*
    Q10
*/
-- PLEASE PLACE REQUIRED SQL STATEMENT FOR THIS PART HERE
-- ENSURE your query has a semicolon (;) at the end of this answer

SELECT
    cl.client_nbr,
    cl.client_fname
    || ' '
    || cl.client_lname            AS fullname,
    lo.location_name,
    COUNT(lo.location_name)       AS nbr_time_visit
FROM
         mh.charter ch
    JOIN mh.client         cl
    ON ch.client_nbr = cl.client_nbr
    JOIN mh.charter_leg    chr
    ON ch.charter_nbr = chr.charter_nbr
    JOIN mh.location       lo
    ON chr.location_nbr_destination = lo.location_nbr
GROUP BY
    cl.client_nbr,
    cl.client_fname
    || ' '
    || cl.client_lname,
    lo.location_name
HAVING
    COUNT(lo.location_name) > 1
ORDER BY
    client_nbr,
    location_name;
LLM tutor feedback:
**Overall Feedback**

Thank you for your submission, John. You have made a good attempt at answering the SQL queries, and your code is generally well-structured and readable. However, there are several issues with SQL correctness, use of required constructs, and some misunderstandings of the assignment requirements. Below is specific feedback for each question:

---

**Q1:**  
- You should use `TO_DATE('31-Mar-2020', 'dd-Mon-yyyy')` for date comparison, not a string.  
- The date format in `TO_CHAR` should match the sample output (e.g., 'Dy dd Mon YYYY').  
- Good use of JOIN.

**Q2:**  
- Correct use of JOIN and filtering for special requirements.  
- Output columns and ordering are correct.

**Q3:**  
- The WHERE clause is overly complex and does not fully implement the logic:  
  - You need to check for destination 'Mount Doom' AND (cost < 1000 OR special requirements IS NULL).  
  - Cost comparison should be numeric, not string.  
  - The FULLNAME column should use `LTRIM` to avoid leading spaces.

**Q4:**  
- Logic is correct, but the alias for the count column should match the required output.  
- Use `COUNT(*)` instead of `COUNT(hl.heli_callsign)` for clarity.

**Q5:**  
- Correct logic and output.  
- Good use of HAVING and ordering.

**Q6:**  
- You are missing helicopter types with zero hours. Use a LEFT OUTER JOIN and `NVL` to show 0 for types with no helicopters.

**Q7:**  
- The query does not correctly identify completed charters or the first leg's departure time.  
- You should filter for charters where all legs have actual arrival times and use `MIN(cl_etd)` for leg one.

**Q8:**  
- The calculation of total charter cost is incorrect and does not follow the requirements.  
- You need to sum the actual time flown for each charter and compare to the average.

**Q9:**  
- The logic is incorrect. You should check that for all legs, `cl_etd = cl_atd` for a charter, not just compare formatted strings.

**Q10:**  
- The query does not correctly identify favorite destinations (the most visited per client).  
- You need to use a subquery to find the max count per client and filter accordingly.

---

**General Advice:**  
- Pay close attention to data types (dates, numbers vs. strings).  
- Use explicit ANSI JOINs as required.  
- Carefully read each question and match the output format exactly.  
- Test your queries with sample data if possible.

Keep practicing, and review the sample solutions and feedback to improve your SQL skills!

The Animal Doctors management have indicated:
● they do not require the clinic addresses or any phone numbers to be broken down into
component attributes
● they would like to be able to easily expand:
○ the vet specialisations, as they employ further vets with new specialisations, and
○ the types of animals, as they treat a new type which they have not previously seen
● the services which are provided during a visit are identified by a service code and have a
standard service cost which must be recorded as part of the database. Every visit incurs at
least one service charge. During a visit (treatment of a patient - the animal) the servicing vet
may vary the service standard cost depending on their professional judgement,
● the drugs which are provided during a visit have a standard cost which must be recorded as
part of the database, however like services this cost may be varied by the vet for a particular
visit
● when a drug is used as part of the visit (eg. an injection that the vet gives the patient in the
clinic) the frequency is not recorded (frequency is only used only for drugs given to the owner
to be administered to their pet following the visit eg. a twice daily antibiotic tablet), and
● management would like a count maintained, as part of the stored database data, which
indicates how many times a given specialist vet has serviced a visit in a clinic which is not
their home clinic.

LLM tutor feedback:
Thank you for your submission. You have made a good attempt at translating the requirements into a logical data model, and you have included the main entities such as PetOwner, Pet, Clinic, Vet, Service, Drug, Visit, and DrugUsage. Your use of primary keys (P *) and foreign keys (F *) is clear, and you have attempted to capture relationships such as ownership and service provision.

Strengths:
- Most core entities from the case study are present.
- You have included key attributes for each entity, and the use of prefixes (e.g., owner_, pet_) is consistent.
- The model shows an understanding of the need for foreign keys to represent relationships.

Areas for Improvement:
- The Visit entity appears to have some confusion in its attributes and foreign keys (e.g., visit_servicecost, visit_visitservicecost, visit_date1, pet_id1, ser_code1, cli_id1). It is unclear what these represent; attribute names should be clear and consistent.
- The relationships between entities (e.g., which vet performed which service during a visit, which drugs were administered, and the link between visits and services/drugs) are not fully explicit. Consider using associative entities (e.g., VisitService, VisitDrug) to capture many-to-many relationships and allow for variable costs per visit as required.
- The requirement to track how many times a specialist vet has serviced a visit at a non-home clinic is not clearly addressed. You may need an attribute or a separate table to record this.
- Some attributes (e.g., vet_Numberofnotatfamilyclinic) are unclear in purpose and naming.
- Attribute comments are not shown here; ensure these are included in your actual database implementation.

Overall, you are on the right track, but further refinement and clarification of relationships and attribute naming are needed to fully meet the assignment requirements.

FIT5190 Assignment 1: Research Writing Task 2 State the problem  
Anomaly Detection in Network Traffic Using Machine Learning  
Anomaly detection in network traffic is a critical task for ensuring the security and reliability of computer networks. With the increasing complexity and volume of network data, traditional rule-based detection methods often fail to identify novel or sophisticated attacks. The main problem is that existing approaches either generate too many false positives or are unable to detect previously unseen threats, which can lead to significant security breaches or unnecessary resource consumption.

To address these challenges, this research focuses on applying machine learning techniques to improve the accuracy and efficiency of anomaly detection in network traffic. The research will answer several key questions: First, which machine learning algorithms are most effective for detecting anomalies in high-dimensional network data? Second, how can feature selection and extraction methods be optimized to enhance detection performance? Third, what evaluation metrics should be used to assess the effectiveness of the proposed models? Finally, how can the system be adapted to detect new types of attacks in real-time? Addressing these questions is essential for developing a robust and adaptive anomaly detection system that can protect modern networks from evolving threats.
LLM tutor feedback:
Your submission provides a clear and concise statement of the problem, focusing on the limitations of traditional anomaly detection methods in network traffic and the need for improved machine learning approaches. The introduction effectively sets the context and highlights the significance of the issue, demonstrating a good understanding of current challenges in network security.

The second paragraph outlines relevant and specific research questions that are directly linked to the problem identified. These questions are well-formulated, significant, and answerable, covering algorithm selection, feature engineering, evaluation metrics, and adaptability to new threats. This shows a logical structure and a critical approach to the research topic.

To further strengthen your work, consider briefly referencing existing literature or notable studies to support your claims about the shortcomings of traditional methods and the promise of machine learning. This would demonstrate engagement with current research and fulfill the criterion of acknowledging the work of others. Additionally, you could clarify whether your focus is on supervised, unsupervised, or hybrid machine learning techniques, as this would add specificity to your research scope.

Overall, your writing is clear, concise, and well-organized, with a logical flow from problem statement to research questions. With minor enhancements, particularly in referencing and specificity, your submission would reach an even higher standard. Well done!

LLM tutor feedback:
Your submission demonstrates a strong understanding of both the Value Proposition Canvas (VPC) and Business Model Canvas (BMC) as applied to a technology-driven mental health solution. You clearly identify two primary customer segments—young adults seeking mental health support and mental health professionals/researchers—and tailor value propositions to each. The VPC is well-structured, with thoughtful articulation of customer gains, pains, and pain relievers, and you effectively link product features (e.g., AI chatbot, gamification, wearables integration) to user needs and motivations. The use of color-coding (blue/green) to distinguish value for each segment is a nice touch, though in text form, be sure to clarify these distinctions for clarity.

Your BMC is comprehensive, covering all nine building blocks with relevant details. Key partners, activities, and resources are well-chosen, and your revenue streams (freemium, subscriptions, partnerships, data licensing) are realistic for a digital health platform. The cost structure is also thoughtfully considered.

For further improvement, your analysis would benefit from more explicit critical evaluation of the choices made in each canvas block. For example, discuss why certain channels or customer relationships are prioritized, or the rationale behind your revenue model. Additionally, referencing any market research or data to support your assumptions (e.g., demand for mental health apps, user willingness to pay) would strengthen your argument and align with rubric expectations for “extensive backup data.”

Overall, this is a well-organized and insightful submission that demonstrates creativity and a solid grasp of entrepreneurial frameworks. With deeper critical analysis and supporting evidence, your work would reach an even higher standard. Well done!

The Animal Doctors management have indicated:
● they do not require the clinic addresses or any phone numbers to be broken down into
component attributes
● they would like to be able to easily expand:
○ the vet specialisations, as they employ further vets with new specialisations, and
○ the types of animals, as they treat a new type which they have not previously seen
● the services which are provided during a visit are identified by a service code and have a
standard service cost which must be recorded as part of the database. Every visit incurs at
least one service charge. During a visit (treatment of a patient - the animal) the servicing vet
may vary the service standard cost depending on their professional judgement,
● the drugs which are provided during a visit have a standard cost which must be recorded as
part of the database, however like services this cost may be varied by the vet for a particular
visit
● when a drug is used as part of the visit (eg. an injection that the vet gives the patient in the
clinic) the frequency is not recorded (frequency is only used only for drugs given to the owner
to be administered to their pet following the visit eg. a twice daily antibiotic tablet), and
● management would like a count maintained, as part of the stored database data, which
indicates how many times a given specialist vet has serviced a visit in a clinic which is not
their home clinic.

Task to complete:
1. Perform normalisation to 3NF for the data depicted in the sample AD reports. Note that only
one normalisation is required for the Visit Invoice, you have been provided with two samples
so you can appreciate some of the variety which occurs.
The approach you are required to use is the same approach as shown in the normalisation
tutorial solution. The normalisation must be carried out form by form, beginning by you
representing the document you are working on as a single UNF form and then moving
through 1NF, 2NF and 3NF.
During normalisation, you must:
○ Not add surrogate keys.
○ Include all attributes (you must not remove any attribute as derivable)
○ Clearly show UNF, 1NF, 2NF and 3NF.
○ Clearly identify the Primary Key in all relations.
○ Clearly identify all dependencies at the various normalisation stages (Partial at 1NF,
Transitive at 2NF and Full at 3NF). You should use the same notation as depicted in
the normalisation sample solutions, for example:
attr1 -> attr2, attr3
If none exist you must note this by stating:
No partial dependencies present and/or

LLM tutor feedback:
**Feedback on Normalisation Submission**

You have made a good attempt at normalising the two required forms (Pet Ownership Details and Visit Invoice) from UNF through to 3NF, and you have clearly indicated the progression through the normal forms. Your identification of primary keys and dependencies at each stage is generally clear, and you have avoided introducing surrogate keys during the normalisation process, as required.

**Strengths:**
- You have included all relevant attributes from the forms and maintained consistent attribute names.
- The stepwise breakdown from UNF to 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF is evident.
- Partial and transitive dependencies are identified and stated, and you have noted when none are present.
- The final 3NF relations are listed and show a logical separation of entities.

**Areas for Improvement:**
- Some dependency notations could be clearer. For example, in the Pet Ownership Details, the relationship between animals, owners, and vets could be more explicitly shown, especially regarding how a pet is linked to both an owner and a preferred vet.
- In the Visit Invoice, the distinction between a patient (animal) and the owner is not fully clear, and the use of "servicingId" and "servicingName" could be better explained (is this the vet or the visit?).
- The SERVICE and MEDICINE relations include patientID, but it would be clearer to show how these relate to the visit/invoice, as services and medicines are typically linked to a specific visit rather than just a patient.
- Some attribute groupings (e.g., drugQty and drugCost under drugID) may need further clarification, as drug cost can vary per visit.

**Suggestions:**
- Use more precise dependency notation (e.g., patientID, serviceCode → serviceCost) and clarify composite keys where appropriate.
- Consider adding brief explanations for your design choices, especially where the mapping from the form to relations is not straightforward.

Overall, this is a solid effort with room for refinement in dependency notation and entity relationships. Keep up the good work!

1. | 1. | Provide a succinct overview of the purpose of the proposed National Facial Biometric Matching Capability (NFBMC), the societal problems it aims to address, the services it will provide, and the benefits that could flow from its implementation (2 marks)
The National Facial Biometric Matching Capability (NFBMC) is designed to facilitate secure and efficient sharing of facial biometric information between government agencies. Its main purpose is to combat identity crime, support law enforcement, enhance national security, and improve the delivery of government services (Information Integrity Solutions, 2015). The Interoperability Hub (the Hub) acts as a central point for transmitting biometric and biographic data between agencies, without storing the data itself. The anticipated benefits include improved fraud prevention, more effective law enforcement, strengthened border security, streamlined service delivery, and increased public trust in identity credentials (Information Integrity Solutions, 2015).

==================== Page 2 ====================

2. | Explain who has carried out this PIA, on whose behalf and what its scope is (0.5 mark)
The Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) was conducted by Information Integrity Solutions Pty Ltd (IIS) for the Attorney-General’s Department (AGD). The scope of the PIA is limited to the Interoperability Hub and the governance framework for information sharing between agencies, focusing on the privacy implications of the information transmitted through the Hub (Information Integrity Solutions, 2015). | 
3. | Explain the intrinsic sensitivity of biometric information and the risks to individuals associated with the use of biometric matching techniques by government agencies? (1 mark)
Biometric information is inherently sensitive due to its uniqueness, permanence, and the depth of personal data it can reveal (Rahman, Verhaert, & Nyst, 2018). Unlike other identifiers, biometrics such as facial images, fingerprints, and DNA are unique to each individual and cannot be changed if compromised. The use of biometric matching by government agencies raises risks such as identity theft, wrongful identification, loss of privacy, reputational harm, and limited avenues for redress in case of errors or misuse (Information Integrity Solutions, 2015). | 
4. | This PIA features consultation with Federal and State Privacy Commissioners. Explain why they were key stakeholders to consult (0.5 mark)
Federal and State Privacy Commissioners are key stakeholders because they are responsible for overseeing privacy regulation and ensuring compliance with privacy laws (OAIC, 2020). Their input is essential in identifying privacy risks and ensuring that the NFBMC aligns with legal and ethical standards for handling sensitive biometric data. | 

==================== Page 3 ====================

5. | The NFBMC has adopted a Privacy by Design approach. For each Privacy by Design principle provide two examples of how components of the NFBMC or its design processes reflects the principle. (4 marks) Note: As a variety of mappings are possible, your answer should clearly demonstrate your understanding of all of the components of the NFBMC
Privacy by Design (PbD), as outlined by Cavoukian (2011), consists of seven foundational principles:  
1. Proactive not reactive; Preventive not Remedial  
a) The NFBMC has established strong governance and oversight mechanisms to monitor privacy risks.  
b) The Hub is housed in a secure facility and is not connected to cloud services, reducing exposure to external threats.  
2. Privacy as the Default Setting  
a) The Hub does not retain biometric or biographic data, only minimal transaction metadata.  
b) Operational documents and governance frameworks are designed to comply with the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs).  
3. Privacy embedded into design  
a) Privacy considerations are integrated into the Hub’s guiding principles and technical design.  
b) The use of cryptographic algorithms is considered to enhance data security.  
4. Full functionality – positive-sum, not zero-sum  
a) The Hub is designed to deliver all required functions while maximising privacy protections.  
b) Both functional and non-functional requirements are reviewed to ensure privacy is not compromised.  
5. End-to-End security – full lifecycle protection  
a) Privacy risks are assessed throughout the system’s lifecycle, including during regulator consultations.  
b) A framework is in place to maintain privacy protections over time.  
6. Visibility and transparency – keep it open  
a) The AGD has committed to publishing the PIA and related documents.  

==================== Page 4 ====================

b) The operation of the Hub is made transparent to the public.
7. Respect for user privacy – keep it user-centric
a) Individuals using face matching services are to be properly informed and their consent obtained.
b) Privacy commissioners have raised concerns about the accuracy of facial biometric technology, ensuring user interests are considered.

==================== Page 5 ====================

 | Information Flows (4 marks) | 
6. | 6. | Summarise the Hub’s information flows. (2 marks)
The Hub supports several key information flows (Information Integrity Solutions, 2015):  
1. Image retrieval: Agencies submit a person’s image and biographic data for matching; the Hub returns results based on matches found.  
2. Image-based verification: Agencies provide biographic data or unique identifiers; the Hub supplies matching facial images and identity information if available.  
3. Image-based identification: Agencies submit a facial image (with or without additional data); a human operator reviews the results, and the Hub returns match scores and associated identity information.  
4. Incremental updates and image galleries: Agencies send multiple images and biographic data to update watchlists or targets; the Hub processes and prompts accordingly.  
5. Occasional bulk data transfer: Agencies submit batches of images and data; the Hub identifies possible matches and returns relevant identity information.  
Human operators are involved in some processes, particularly for audit and verification purposes. |  | 
7. |  | Recommendation 1 is to apply the APPs to all the information the Hub collects, transmits or holds, regardless of whether or not it is legally deemed to be personal information. Explain the reasoning for this (2 marks)

==================== Page 6 ====================

Applying the APPs to all information handled by the Hub, regardless of its legal status as personal information, ensures a consistent and robust privacy framework (Information Integrity Solutions, 2015). This approach aligns with the Privacy Act and provides clear obligations for the AGD and participating agencies, reducing ambiguity and strengthening public trust. It also helps prevent privacy breaches and regulatory penalties by ensuring all data is treated with the highest standard of care (OAIC, 2021).

9. | 9. | This PIA was undertaken in 2015. Provide an update on where the development of each component of the National Facial Biometric Matching Capability is in 2021
As of 2021, publicly available information on the NFBMC’s development is limited, with most updates traceable to 2019. The progress can be summarised in three main areas:  
a) Policy Revisions: The NFBMC has updated the Face Identification Service (FIS) Access Policy, Face Verification Service (FVS) Access Policy, and the Face Matching Services Compliance Policy (NFBMC, 2018). These policies clarify agency requirements for accessing FIS and FVS, and the Compliance Policy ensures ongoing monitoring and enforcement of privacy and security standards (NFBMC, n.d.).  
b) Additional PIAs: The National Driver Licence Facial Recognition Solution (NDLFRS), a component of the NFBMC, has undergone its own PIA, identifying risks such as lack of transparency, security concerns in data sharing, data accuracy, and function creep (Information Integrity Solutions, 2019). IIS also conducted a PIA on the FIS in 2017, highlighting risks like inappropriate use, disclosure, consent, and accountability (Information Integrity Solutions, 2017). PIAs for the Australian Federal Police (AFP) and Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade (DFAT) access to the FVS have also been completed (Wilson, 2016).  
c) Government Response: The AGD has responded to issues raised in these PIAs, including the need for privacy statements, improvements to the Hub portal’s user interface, and enhancements to matching algorithms (Attorney-General’s Department, 2016). |  |

Task A: Investigating Natural Increase in Australia's population
In this task, you are required to visualise the relationship between the births, deaths, total
fertility rate (TFR), net overseas migration (Births) and net interstate migration (NIM) for the
different Australian states/territories, and gain insights on how these relations and trends
change over time. The data files used in this task were originally downloaded from Australian
Bureau of Statistics. We have extracted the data from the original files and transformed them
into a simpler format. Please download the data from Moodle:
● Births.csv - This file contains yearly data regarding the recorded number of births by
Australian state/territory of registration between 1977 and 2016.
● Deaths.csv -This file contains yearly data regarding the recorded number of deaths by
Australian state/territory of registration between 1977 and 2016.
● TFR.csv - This file contains yearly data on the recorded average number of births per
woman over her lifetime by each state/territory between 1971 and 2016.
● NOM.csv - This data file contains yearly data on the net gain or loss of population
through immigration (migrant arrivals) to Australia and emigration (migrant
departures) from Australia, for the period between 1977 and 2016.
● NIM.csv - This data file contains yearly data on the net gain or loss of population
through the movement of people from one state or territory to another, for the period
between 1977 and 2016.

### Answer 3

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
# Natural Growth in Australia's population
BirthEachYear <- Births %>% rowwise() %>%  mutate(Total_Birth= sum(c(NSW, ACT, QLD, SA, WA, TAS, VIC, NT)))
DeathEachYear <- Deaths %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(Total_Death= sum(c(NSW, ACT, QLD, SA, WA, TAS, VIC, NT)))
NaturalGrowthEachYear <- data.frame(BirthEachYear$Year,BirthEachYear$Total_Birth,DeathEachYear$Total_Death)
NaturalGrowthEachYear <- NaturalGrowthEachYear %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(NaturalGrowth = diff(c(DeathEachYear.Total_Death, BirthEachYear.Total_Birth)))
ggplot(data=NaturalGrowthEachYear, aes(x=BirthEachYear.Year, y=NaturalGrowth)) +
  geom_line() +
  xlab("Year") + ylab("Natural Growth") + ggtitle("Natural growth each year") +
  geom_point()
```

**Explanation** - Growth is defined as the total births subtracted by the total deaths for that year. It can be observed that the natural growth is significantly higher than the starting year. The shift to that has been with ups and downs throughout the span of 49 years. The growth was increased from 1977 up to around 1992 and then after that declined to a rock bottom again by the year 2004. But it then skyrocketed back and after 2010 it fluctuated in the range of 155k and 165k.

```{r}
data1 <- NOM %>%
```

### Answer 3

```{r}
# Filtering data based on relevance judge
relevant <- filter(twitter_data, twitter_data$relevanceJudge == 1)
relevant_entities <- relevant$'#entities'
non_relevant <- filter(twitter_data, twitter_data$relevanceJudge == 0)
non_relevant_entities <- non_relevant$'#entities'
# Calculations for relevanceJudge==1
mean_relevant <- mean(relevant_entities)
std_relevant <- sd(relevant_entities)
quartile1_relevant <- quantile(relevant_entities)[2]
median_relevant <- median(relevant_entities)
max_relevant <- max(relevant_entities)
# Calculations for relevanceJudge==0
mean_nonrelevant <- mean(non_relevant_entities)
std_nonrelevant <- sd(non_relevant_entities)
quartile1_nonrelevant <- quantile(non_relevant_entities)[2]
median_nonrelevant <- median(non_relevant_entities)
max_nonrelevant <- max(non_relevant_entities)
# Relevance (1)
sprintf("mean relevant - %f", mean_relevant)
sprintf("std relevant - %f", std_relevant)
sprintf("quantile relevant - %f", quartile1_relevant)
sprintf("median relevant - %f", median_relevant)
sprintf("max relevant - %f", max_relevant)
# Non Relevance (0)
sprintf("mean non relevant - %f", mean_nonrelevant)
sprintf("std non relevant - %f", std_nonrelevant)
sprintf("quantile non relevant - %f", quartile1_nonrelevant)
sprintf("median non relevant - %f", median_nonrelevant)
sprintf("max non relevant - %f", max_nonrelevant)
```

**Explanation** - The data of relevanceJudge column is separated to 0 and 1 (Non-relevance and relevance) which is then used to calculate different functions using pre defined libraries. It is notable that almost all the values are similar except the binary integer of quantile.

FIT9132 Introduction to Databases
2021 Semester 1
Assignment 2A - SQL - Centre Breeding (CB)
Assignment weighting 10% - Lecturer in Charge: Minh Le
As natural habitats disappear, it is increasingly difficult to ensure that all species can survive
in the wild. One solution is to use zoos, reserves and other types of organizations that keep
wild animals in captivity as conservation centres for various species. To assist in recording
the animals in captivity and their offspring, a Centre Breeding database is to be created in
which all centres will record the animals which they hold and the breeding events which take
place at the centres.
Database designers have considered the requirements and arrived at the following data
model:
Page 1 of 8


==================== Page 2 ====================

Model Details:
SPECIES
Records the details of the species, the name of the species, as well as its popular name and the
genus and family to which the species belongs. The relevant details for the Tasmanian Devil, for
instance, are as follows ...
Genus: Sarcophilus
Species: harrisii
Popular name: Tasmanian Devil
Family: DASYURIDAE
It is also important to know the species’ natural range, which is usually given as a description of the
geographic regions and natural habitats where a species is found in the wild.

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
spec_genus | The species genus
spec_name | The species name
spec_popular_name | The species popular name
spec_family | The species family name
spec_natural range | Description of the natural range of the species

ANIMAL
Records the details of all animals held at a particular centre

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
animal_id | The identifier for the animal
animal_sex | The animal sex (M or F)
brevent_id | If bred in captivity (i.e. at a centre), the id of the breeding event which produced the animal. Animals which have been captured from the wild will have no brevent_id assigned
center_id | The "home" center where the animal is normally located. Animals are sometimes located at other centres for a breeding event
spec_genus | The species genus for the animal
spec_name | The species name for the animal

Page 2 of 8


==================== Page 3 ====================

CENTRE
Records the details of all centres that keep animals in captivity (zoos, reserves and other
types of organizations)

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
centre_id | The identifier for the center
centre_name | The centre name
centre_address | The centre address
centre_director | The name of the centres director
centre_phone_no | The centres phone contact number

BREEDING_EVENT
Records the details of breeding events which have taken place in the centres

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
brevent_id | The identifier for the breeding event
brevent_date | The date on which the breeding event took place
mother_id | The animal_id of the animal who was the mother
father_id | The animal_id of the animal who was the father

-- Center Breeding SCHEMA
-- DROP TABLES 
DROP TABLE animal CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

DROP TABLE breeding_event CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

DROP TABLE centre CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

DROP TABLE species CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

-- CREATE TABLES
CREATE TABLE animal (
    animal_id   NUMBER(6) NOT NULL,
    animal_sex  CHAR(1) NOT NULL,
    brevent_id  NUMBER(6),
    centre_id   CHAR(6) NOT NULL,
    spec_genus  VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL,
    spec_name   VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL
);

ALTER TABLE animal
    ADD CONSTRAINT animal_sex_check CHECK ( animal_sex IN ( 'F', 'M' ) );

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.animal_id IS
    'Unique ID for this animal ';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.animal_sex IS
    'Sex of animal (M or F)';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.brevent_id IS
    'Unique identifier for breeding event';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.centre_id IS

-- (i)

drop sequence animal_seq;
drop sequence breedevent_seq;

create sequence animal_seq start with 500 increment by 1;

UPDATE animal
SET

        SELECT

        FROM
            centre
        WHERE
            centre_name = 'Kruger National Park'
    )
WHERE
    centre_id = (

        WHERE

    );

DELETE FROM centre
WHERE

1 Athlete work schedule (8 marks)
You are an athlete, who mostly works as a personal trainer but also competes in sporting com-
petitions. You can sometimes make more money from competing than from training, but you
need time to prepare and recover, which is time that you cannot spend working as a trainer, or
preparing for, competing in or recovering from any other competitions.
You want to maximise the amount of money you can earn by combining your job as a personal
trainer with participating in competitions. To do this, you will write a function
best_schedule(weekly_income, competitions).
1.1 Input
weekly_income is a list of non-negative integers, where weekly_income[i] is the amount of
money you will earn working as a personal trainer in week i.
competitions is a list of tuples, each representing a sporting competition. Each tuple contains
3 non-negative integers, (start_time, end_time, winnings).
start_time is the is the week that you will need to begin preparing for this competition (i.e.
the first week that you cannot do your regular job as a personal trainer, if you compete in this
competition).
end_time is the last week that you will need to spend recovering from this competition (i.e.
the last week that you cannot do your regular job as a personal trainer, if you compete in this
competition).
winnings is the amount of money you will win if you compete in this competition.
1.2 Output
best_schedulereturnsaninteger, whichisthemaximumamountofmoneythatcanbeearned.
1.3 Example
weekly_income = [3,7,2,1,8,4,5]
competitions = [(1,3,15),(2,2,8),(0,4,30),(3,5,19)]
print(best_schedule(weekly_income, competitions))
>>> 42
In the above example, the optimal schedule is to work as a trainer in weeks 0 and 1 (earning
3+7), then compete in the (2,2,8) event in week 2 (earning 8), then compete in the (3,5,19)
event from weeks 3 to 5 (earning 19), then work as a trainer in week 6 (earning 5).
1.4 Complexity
best_schedule should run in O(Nlog(N)) time and O(N) space, where N is the total number
of elements in weekly_income and competitions put together.

A salesperson lives on the coast. They travel to various cities along the coast to work. Some-
times they stay in a city for a day or a few days to work, and other times they simply pass
through on their way to another city.

The salesperson has an idea of how much money they can make by working in each city, for

finished quarantine), or they can travel to either adjacent city. Traveling always takes 1 day,
and since the cities are along a coast, each city has two adjacent cities, except for two cities on

home).

from typing import List, Tuple

def best_schedule(weekly_income: List[int], competitions: List[Tuple[int, int, int]]) -> int:
    """
    Given a list of weekly incomes and a list of competitions (each as a tuple of (start_time, end_time, winnings)),
    returns the maximum income that can be earned by optimally choosing when to work as a trainer and when to compete.

    :time complexity: O(NlogN), where N is the total number of weeks plus the number of competitions.
    :space complexity: O(N), where N is the total number of weeks plus the number of competitions.
    """
    if not weekly_income:
        return 0
    n = len(weekly_income)
    competitions.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])  # Sort competitions by end_time
    dp = [0] * n  # dp[i] = max income up to week i
    comp_idx = 0
    for week in range(n):
        # Option 1: work as a trainer this week
        if week == 0:
            max_income = weekly_income[0]
        else:
            max_income = dp[week-1] + weekly_income[week]
        # Option 2: finish a competition this week
        while comp_idx < len(competitions) and competitions[comp_idx][1] == week:
            start, end, win = competitions[comp_idx]
            if start == 0:
                comp_income = win
            else:
                comp_income = win + dp[start-1]
            if comp_income > max_income:
                max_income = comp_income
            comp_idx += 1
        dp[week] = max_income
    return dp[-1]

def best_itinerary(profit: List[List[int]], quarantine_time: List[int], home: int) -> int:
    """
    Given a profit table (profit[day][city]), quarantine times for each city, and a starting city (home),
    returns the maximum profit that can be earned by moving, quarantining, and working optimally.

    :time complexity: O(nd), where n is the number of cities and d is the number of days.
    :space complexity: O(nd), where n is the number of cities and d is the number of days.
    """
    n = len(quarantine_time)
    d = len(profit)
    if d == 0:
        return 0
    # dp1[day][city]: max profit if working in city on day (i.e., not in quarantine)
    # dp2[day][city]: max profit if arriving in city on day and starting quarantine
    dp1 = [[0]*n for _ in range(d+1)]
    dp2 = [[0]*n for _ in range(d+1)]
    for day in range(d-1, -1, -1):
        for city in range(n):
            # If not in quarantine, can work or move
            work = profit[day][city] + dp1[day+1][city] if day+1 <= d else 0
            move_left = dp2[day+1][city-1] if city > 0 and day+1 <= d else 0
            move_right = dp2[day+1][city+1] if city < n-1 and day+1 <= d else 0
            dp1[day][city] = max(work, move_left, move_right)
        for city in range(n):
            # If arriving in city and need to quarantine
            q = quarantine_time[city]
            if day+q < d:
                dp2[day][city] = dp1[day+q][city]
            else:
                dp2[day][city] = 0
    # On day 0, at home, can start working immediately
    return dp1[0][home]

FIT5088 INFORMATION AND KNOWLEDGE MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS  
ASSIGNMENT 2:  
IKM SYSTEM REPORT

Executive Summary  
Scope (Australia) Ltd is a non-profit organisation focused on supporting people with disabilities to live empowered and equal lives. As the organisation grows, it faces increasing challenges in managing and sharing knowledge efficiently across its many services and locations. This report outlines the process for developing and implementing a Knowledge Management (KM) strategy for Scope, referencing ISO 30401 and the Becerra-Fernandez & Sabherwal KM Solutions model. The report identifies key components of a KM strategy suitable for Scope, discusses the potential role of an internal wiki (using Guru) in supporting KM processes, and provides user stories to illustrate practical applications. A preliminary assessment of Guru is included, followed by recommendations for effective KM strategy development and implementation.

Table of Contents  
1. Introduction  
2. Discussion  
 2.1 Developing and Implementing a KM Strategy  
 2.2 Key Components of Scope’s KM Strategy  
 2.3 Role of Internal Wiki and User Stories  
 2.4 Preliminary Assessment of Guru  
3. Recommendations  
4. References  

1. Introduction  
Scope (Australia) Ltd is a leading disability service provider in Victoria, offering a range of services such as supported independent living, short-term accommodation, therapy, and lifestyle options (Scope, 2021). With over 100 service locations and thousands of clients, Scope’s operations are complex and information-intensive. As the organisation expands, it recognises the need for a robust Knowledge Management (KM) system to improve efficiency, reduce knowledge loss, and enhance service quality. This report examines how Scope can develop and implement a KM strategy, the role of internal wikis, and the suitability of Guru as a KM solution.

2. Discussion  
2.1 Developing and Implementing a KM Strategy  
2.1.1 Understanding Organisational Context and Needs  
The first step in developing a KM strategy is to understand the organisation’s context, including its mission, structure, and current KM practices. For Scope, this means recognising its role as a non-profit, its reliance on both staff and volunteers, and the diversity of its services. Identifying the specific knowledge needs—such as sharing best practices, onboarding new staff, and ensuring compliance—is essential. This aligns with ISO 30401, which emphasises understanding the organisation’s environment and stakeholders before designing KM processes (ISO, 2018).

2.1.2 Defining the Scope of the KM System  
Once the organisational context is clear, the next step is to define the scope of the KM system. This involves determining which knowledge assets are most critical, which processes need improvement, and what outcomes are expected. For Scope, priorities may include digitising paper-based records, improving access to policies and procedures, and facilitating knowledge sharing among geographically dispersed teams.

2.1.3 Developing the KM Strategy  
With the scope defined, a tailored KM strategy can be developed. According to the Becerra-Fernandez & Sabherwal model, key KM processes include knowledge creation, storage/retrieval, transfer, and application (Becerra-Fernandez & Sabherwal, 2015). For Scope, the strategy should address:  
- Acquiring new knowledge (e.g., capturing lessons learned from service delivery)  
- Applying existing knowledge (e.g., making procedures easily accessible)  
- Retaining knowledge (e.g., digitising and backing up records)  
- Updating or removing outdated knowledge (e.g., regular content reviews)

2.2 Key Components of Scope’s KM Strategy  
Based on Scope’s needs and the job description for the Knowledge Manager, the following components are central to the KM strategy:

2.2.1 Upgrading the KM System  
Scope’s current KM system may not fully support its needs. Upgrading the system should focus on improving usability, integrating with other organisational systems (e.g., HR, client management), and streamlining workflows. A user-friendly interface and clear guidance will help staff and volunteers adopt the system more easily.

2.2.2 Digitising Knowledge Processes  
Digitisation is crucial for retaining organisational knowledge and reducing reliance on paper. All key documents, procedures, and records should be digitised and stored in a central repository. This not only protects against knowledge loss but also enables remote access and easier updates.

2.2.3 Organising and Sharing Knowledge  
Organising knowledge into logical categories and making it easily searchable will improve efficiency. Sharing knowledge through collaborative platforms encourages a culture of learning and continuous improvement. For Scope, this could mean creating standard templates for service delivery, FAQs for volunteers, and best practice guides.

2.3 Role of Internal Wiki and User Stories  
An internal wiki is a collaborative platform where staff and volunteers can create, edit, and access organisational knowledge. Wikis are especially useful for non-profits like Scope, where knowledge is distributed among many people and turnover can be high (Bibbo et al., 2012).

2.3.1 Repository of Shared Knowledge  
The wiki serves as a central repository for policies, procedures, and practical tips. Its simple editing interface allows anyone in the organisation to contribute, ensuring that knowledge is kept up to date and relevant.

2.3.2 Platform for Knowledge Sharing  
Wikis facilitate collaboration by allowing multiple users to edit and comment on content. This supports peer learning and helps surface tacit knowledge that might otherwise be lost.

2.3.3 User Stories  
User Story 1:  
As a new volunteer at Scope, I use the internal wiki to access a step-by-step guide on providing short-term accommodation services. The guide includes tips from experienced volunteers, common challenges, and contact information for support. This helps me feel confident and reduces the need to ask repetitive questions.

User Story 2:  
As a team leader, I update the “Incident Reporting” page on the wiki with recent changes to procedures. Other staff members can comment on the changes, ask questions, or suggest improvements. This ensures everyone is working with the latest information and encourages continuous improvement.

Strengths of Wiki Solution:  
- Easy to use and update  
- Encourages collaboration and knowledge sharing  
- Reduces duplication and knowledge loss  
- Accessible from anywhere

Weaknesses:  
- Requires active participation to stay current  
- Risk of outdated or inaccurate information if not regularly reviewed  
- May need training for less tech-savvy users

2.4 Preliminary Assessment of Guru  
Guru is a cloud-based knowledge management platform designed to help organisations organise and share information efficiently (Guru, 2021). Its features align well with Scope’s needs:

Integration:  
Guru integrates with common tools like Microsoft Teams, Outlook, and Google Workspace, which are likely used by Scope. This supports seamless information flow across the organisation.

Intelligent Knowledge Delivery:  
Guru’s AI-powered suggestions can push relevant knowledge to users based on their context, reducing time spent searching for information. This is particularly useful for new staff or volunteers navigating complex processes.

Collaboration and Organisation:  
Guru supports collaborative editing, categorisation, and real-time updates. Its notification system ensures important updates are not missed, and duplicate content is minimised.

Cost and Accessibility:  
Guru offers special plans for non-profits, making it a cost-effective solution for Scope.

Limitations:  
- Success depends on user engagement and regular content updates  
- May require initial training and change management

3. Recommendations  
Based on the analysis, the following recommendations are made:

1. Engage Stakeholders Early  
Involve staff and volunteers in the design and implementation of the KM strategy. Their input will help ensure the system meets real needs and increase buy-in.

2. Adopt Guru as Part of the KM Solution  
Guru’s features align well with Scope’s requirements for integration, collaboration, and knowledge delivery. It is recommended to pilot Guru as the internal wiki platform, alongside other KM initiatives.

3. Provide Training and Ongoing Support  
Offer training sessions and clear documentation to help users adopt the new system. Establish a process for regular content review and updates to maintain information quality.

From a teaching strategy perspective, the workbooks provide you the formative learning experience, with close support from your peers and tutor. That you individually learn effectively and always ask questions among each other and your tutor when not sure;

User Requirements Info Provided
They have provided your team with the following baseline requirement info:
Current System Environment (IT News)
First System Solution
Currently, the Salesforce integrated covid-19 contact tracing and testing systems is being rolled out, to replace the first highly manual system that uses an IBM’s i2 platform, an Advanced Analytics and Intelligence Analysis system.  The first solution was a myriad of existing departmental and different vendor systems and manual processes that feed information into the IBM’s i2 platform. It was first thought that integration configuration would provide the contact tracing and testing data processing for the Victorian Government.
The Victorian government stopped using the IBM analytics tool as part of its first digital contact tracing system after its requirements changed and the system could not be reconfigured.
On October 12, 2020, during the inquiry hearings, IBM reported that it became clear to them that “the DHHS [Department of Health and Human Services] data analysis team required a system capable of alerting them to significant events or conditions that should be followed up by an analyst, in contrast to an analyst using the system to discover these conditions or events.” However there is no clarity in the inquiry report of where this misunderstanding might have occurred.
Second System Solution
Assume the following functional overview of the SalesForce’s system solution (The Guardian, Sep 2020;  (Victorian Premier’s Office, 30 Nov 2020):
Is a digitised system covering the whole contact tracing process – from test results to interviews, phone calls, and the management of cases and contacts.
Can consolidate and align many different platforms into one platform significantly reducing pen and paper manual data entry.”
End user teams can be based remotely because they will be able to access the data from anywhere in the state.
Sends text notifications automatically to people when they tested positive.
Fax machines and phone calls are still being used in GP practices where software does not link with department systems.
An IBM artificial intelligence product from the defence department will provide predictive modelling “that can help you pick up patterns that might not be necessarily obvious”.
Feeds Victorian data to the national benchmark data reporting system that shows Victoria performance against national benchmarks in reporting cases being contacted and interviewed within 24 hours, and close contacts being identified within 48 hours.
Provides an online dashboard for Covid cases in Victoria will soon contain information about outbreaks in health workplaces, and the 14-day rolling average will be included in data for metropolitan Melbourne and regional Victoria, given it is the metric used to decide the easing of restrictions.
Instead of adopting a proven and successfully working QR contact tracing app used by the NSW and ACT Governments, a new client installed QR coded contact tracing app is currently being developed and rolled out for businesses to check in patrons’ names and contact phone numbers.
The automated new system would run in parallel to the existing contact tracing efforts of 2,600 workers in the state to ensure a smooth transition. More staff could be added to help boost efforts at a later date.
