User Journey Mapping The State Government also wants the better system solution to be user experience centred, providing the following user journey map of what is expected of an end-to-end contact tracing system overview. More end users’ perspective of system functional and non-functional descriptions can be found in https://sbi.sydney.edu.au/contact-tracing/ and https://www.nsw.gov.au/covid-19/covid-safe/customer-record-keeping/obligations.   The contact tracing app for business and individual use-cases is required to operate on Android and Apple phones. Samples of more granular user experience linked functionalities are provided in this link https://au.pcmag.com/help/66802/heres-how-contact-tracing-will-work-on-iphones-and-android-phones   While the above user requirement resources are assumed to be official main documentation provided to your team, you can optionally choose to research more to find other clearer, more comprehensive user requirements, if needed.   Any changes to the functional scope implied from the official user documentations would need to formally be reviewed and approved by the State Government client.

System Platform Requirements
Consideration is being looked at for redeveloping the second system solution as a cloud platform solution, which allows the Victorian Government to commercialise and potentially export its third model of integrated covid-19 contact tracing and testing system solution as covid-19 “data centres on the fly”, allowing future revenues to offset the cost of system changes since Covid-19 pandemic started in early 2020.
The Client also mentioned that they intend to use a Robotics Process Automation (RPA) tool, notably the  UiPath app to allow both state and national covid-19 responders (such as covid-19 scientists, public health statisticians and other administrators) to develop and automate their own front-end applications, including specialised reports for more powerful data analytics mining capabilities without the need for programmers. As this is a new work productivity tool, all members of the project team and covid-19 responder-teams in other state and national covid-19 work related departments need to be trained in using this apps development tool.
The Client agrees to adopt the national cybersecurity guidelines provided by the Australian Cybersecurity Centre’s advice, especially the ones regarding Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) actors targeting Australian health sector organisations and COVID-19 essential services . Your team is expected to advise the integration of the appropriate cybersecurity recommendations into your proposed project plan.
These platform expectations mean the new program needs to take a DevOps methodology in defining its cloud platform system solution. The DevOps program will involve a number of projects that innovates this system solution as a range of independently deplorable services.

As natural habitats disappear, it is increasingly difficult to ensure that all species can survive in the
wild. One solution is to use zoos, reserves and other types of organizations that keep wild animals in
captivity as conservation centres for various species. To assist in recording the animals in captivity
and their offspring, a Centre Breeding database is to be created in which all centres will record the
animals which they hold and the breeding events which take place at the centres.
Database designers have considered the requirements and arrived at the following data model:
Page 1 of 8


==================== Page 2 ====================

Model Details:
SPECIES
Records the details of the species, the name of the species, as well as its popular name and the
genus and family to which the species belongs. The relevant details for the Tasmanian Devil, for
instance, are as follows ...
Genus: Sarcophilus
Species: harrisii
Popular name: Tasmanian Devil
Family: DASYURIDAE
It is also important to know the species’ natural range, which is usually given as a description of the
geographic regions and natural habitats where a species is found in the wild.

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
spec_genus | The species genus
spec_name | The species name
spec_popular_name | The species popular name
spec_family | The species family name
spec_natural range | Description of the natural range of the species

ANIMAL
Records the details of all animals held at a particular centre

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
animal_id | The identifier for the animal
animal_sex | The animal sex (M or F)
brevent_id | If bred in captivity (i.e. at a centre), the id of the breeding event which produced the animal. Animals which have been captured from the wild will have no brevent_id assigned
center_id | The "home" center where the animal is normally located. Animals are sometimes located at other centres for a breeding event
spec_genus | The species genus for the animal
spec_name | The species name for the animal

Records the details of all centres that keep animals in captivity (zoos, reserves and other
types of organizations)

BREEDING_EVENT
Records the details of breeding events which have taken place in the centres

Programming Exercise 1 - Sample Solution
FIT5147 Semester 1 2021
By Benjamin Lee and Kadek Satriadi
DATA CHECKING FOR ERRORS
At the first glance, we notice that there are rows with missing values on specific attributes such
as Date.Planted. We don’t want to remove these rows since they still hold important information
such as geospatial location. The first thing to do is checking for possible errors in the dataset.
The first error is an incorrect Longitude Latitude value. A tree being in the middle of Port Phillip
Bay is clearly incorrect. This row can be removed.


==================== Page 2 ====================

The second error is a tree that is apparently planted in the year 2061. As it is currently 2021,
this is also clearly incorrect and can be removed as well.


==================== Page 3 ====================

DATA ANALYSIS
Q1) How are Acacia, Agathis, Backhousia, Eucalyptus, and Magnolia distributed spatially?
Briefly explain the distribution in terms of their spatial clusterisation (visual judgement is enough)
and whether they are mainly on streets vs parks.
A map is most suitable for this. We plot each tree individually using its Longitude and Latitude
value, filtering by the five specified genera. To very easily distinguish between the different
genera, we create small multiples to isolate each one in its own separate map. We also map
colour to see whether or not the tree is located in the park or on a street.
From the map, we can clearly see that Acacia is the only tree genus that is planted on
streets—the southern street in particular. This genus is also planted inside of the park, where
they are all densely planted in a cluster nearby the aforementioned street.
Agathis, Eucalyptus, and Magnolia are planted in many different parts of the Fitzroy Gardens. Of
note is that Agathis and Eucalyptus also have specific areas in which they are more
concentrated: Agathis along what appears to be a walkway inside of the park; and Eucalyptus
next to what appears to be a lake.
Backhousia is the least spread out genus. Other than the two trees planted in the north, they are
all planted in a straight line to the south-east of the Garden, close to the same street that the
Acacia are planted.


==================== Page 4 ====================

Q2) How does the relative number of planted trees by the aforementioned genera (plural for
genus) vary in the year 2013, 2014, 2015, 2016, and 2017?
As we want to compare the relative number (i.e., proportions) of the five genera against each
other, it is best to use a normalised stacked bar chart. To normalise a stacked bar chart, we go
to the CNT(Genus) option in the Rows section, click Quick Table Calculation, and select Percent
of Total. Afterwards, go to the same CNT(Genus) option, click Compute Using, and select Cell.
We can clearly see that Acacia is the most commonly planted type of tree across the five
specified years. However, none were planted in 2015, in favour of planting more Agathis,
Eucalyptus, and Magnolia. Each of these three genera were planted separately in 2014, 2016,
and 2017 as well, although not to the same extent that Acacia were.
In contrast, Backhousia was only planted in 2013. That said, it comprised the majority of trees
(of our five specified genera) that were planted in that year, with Acacia being the only other
one.

Data Checking for Errors

At the beginning, I checked the dataset for any errors or inconsistencies. I noticed that some rows had missing values, for example in the Date.Planted column. Since these rows still contain useful information like geospatial coordinates, I decided not to remove them.

The first error I found was a tree with Longitude and Latitude values that placed it in Port Phillip Bay, which is not possible. I removed this row from the dataset.

The second error was a tree with a Date.Planted in the year 2061. Since the current year is 2021, this is not valid, so I removed this row as well.

==================== Page 2 ====================

Data Analysis

Q1) How are Acacia, Agathis, Backhousia, Eucalyptus, and Magnolia distributed spatially?

To answer this, I plotted the trees on a map using their Longitude and Latitude, filtering for the five genera. I used small multiples to show each genus on its own map, and used colour to indicate whether the tree was in a park or on a street.

From the maps, I observed that Acacia is the only genus planted on streets, especially the southern street. Acacia is also found in the park, where they are clustered near the same street.

Agathis, Eucalyptus, and Magnolia are spread throughout Fitzroy Gardens. Agathis and Eucalyptus have areas where they are more concentrated: Agathis along a walkway, and Eucalyptus near a lake.

Backhousia is the least spread out, with most of them planted in a straight line in the south-east of the Garden, close to the street where Acacia is found. Only two Backhousia trees are in the north.

==================== Page 3 ====================

Q2) How does the relative number of planted trees by the aforementioned genera vary in the year 2013, 2014, 2015, 2016, and 2017?

To compare the proportions of the five genera over these years, I used a normalised stacked bar chart. I calculated the percent of total for each genus per year.

From the chart, I saw that Acacia was the most commonly planted genus across the five years, except in 2015, when none were planted. In 2015, Agathis, Eucalyptus, and Magnolia were planted more. These three genera were also planted in 2014, 2016, and 2017, but not as much as Acacia.

Backhousia was only planted in 2013, and made up the majority of trees planted that year among the five genera, with Acacia being the only other one.

1 DNA fragments (9 marks)
You are maintaining a database for a study into drug resistance in hospitals. During the study,
you will receive bacterial DNA collected from patients. Each bacterial DNA sample con-
tains a drug resistant gene sequence at the start.
The researchers will need to query your database for various different drug resistant gene
sequences over the course of the study (details below), but you will also need to be able to
update the contents of the database with new bacterial DNA samples.
To solve this problem, you will need to create a class SequenceDatabase, to represent the
database. This class will need to have two methods, addSequence(s) and query(q).
As usual, you are welcome to create other functions/methods.
Note: DNA is typically represented using A, T, C and G. For the purpose of easy coding, we
will use A, B, C and D (since they have adjacent ASCII values).
1.1 Input
The input to addSequence is a single nonempty string of uppercase letters in uppercase [A-D].
The input to query is a single (possibly empty) string of uppercase letters in uppercase [A-D].
1.2 Output
addSequence(s) should not return anything. It should appropriately store s into the database
represented by the instance of SequenceDatabase. We define the frequency of a particular
string to be the number of times it has been added to the database in this way.
query(q) should return a string with the following properties:
• It must have q as a prefix
• It should have a higher frequency in the database than any other string with q as a
prefix
• If two or more strings with prefix q are tied for most frequent, return the lexicographically
least of them
If no such string exists, query should return None.
4

1.3 Example
db = SequenceDatabase()
db.addSequence("ABCD")
db.addSequence("ABC")
db.addSequence("ABC")
db.query("A")
>>> "ABC"
db.addSequence("ABCD")
db.query("A")
>>> "ABC"
db.addSequence("ABCD")
db.query("A")
>>> "ABCD"
de.query("B")
>>> None
Notethatthename"db"intheaboveexampleisarbitrary(i.e. theinstanceof SequenceDatabase
could be called anything)
1.4 Complexity
Remember that string comparison is not considered O(1) in this unit.
• The __init__ method of SequenceDatabase should run in O(1)
• addSequence(s) should run in O(len(s))
• query(q) should run in O(len(q)). Note that although you need to return a string longer
than len(q), since strings are pass-by-reference, returning a string of any length is O(1).
5


==================== Page 6 ====================

2 Open reading frames (8 marks)
In Molecular Genetics, there is a notion of an Open Reading Frame (ORF). An ORF is a
portion of DNA that is used as the blueprint for a protein. All ORFs start with a particular
sequence, and end with a particular sequence.
In this task, we wish to find all sections of a genome which start with a given sequence of
characters, and end with a (possibly) different given sequence of characters.
To solve this problem, you will need to create a class OrfFinder. This class will need a method,
find(start, end). Also note that the constructor for this class takes a string genome as a
parameter, unlike the class in Problem 1 (shown in the example below).
2.1 Input
genome is a single non-empty string consisting only of uppercase [A-D]. genome is passed as an
arguement to the __init__ method of OrfFinder (i.e. it gets used when creating an instance
of the class).
start and end are each a single non-empty string consisting of only uppercase [A-D].
2.2 Output
find returns a list of strings. This list contains all the substrings of genome which have start
as a prefix and end as a suffix. There is no particular requirement for the order of these strings.
start and end must not overlap in the substring (see the last two cases of the example below).
2.3 Example
genome1 = OrfFinder("AAABBBCCC")
genome1.find("AAA","BB")
>>> ["AAABB","AAABBB"]
genome1.find("BB","A")
>>>[]
genome1.find("AA","BC")
>>>["AABBBC","AAABBBC"]
genome1.find("A","B")
>>> ["AAAB","AAABB","AAABBB","AAB","AABB","AABBB","AB","ABB","ABBB"]
genome1.find("AA","A")
>>> ["AAA"]
#note that "AA" is not valid, since start and end would need to overlap
genome1.find("AAAB","BBB")
>>> []
# note that "AAABBB" is not valid, since start and end would need to overlap
6

2.4 Complexity
• The __init__ method of OrfFinder must run in O(N2) time, where N is the length of
genome.
• find must run in (len(start)+len(end)+U) time, where U is the number of characters
in the output list.
As an example of what the complexity for find means, consider a string consisting of N "B"s
2
followed by N "A"s.
2
If we call find("A","B"), the output is empty, so U is O(1). On the other hand, if we call
find("B", "A") then U is O(N2).

"""

Submitted by:
Rohan Patel

# TASK 1

class Node_Seq:
    def __init__(self):
        """
        Node_Seq class constructor.
        """
        self.index = 5
        self.freq = 0
        self.data = None
        self.link = [None] * 5
        self.leaf = False

class SequenceDatabase:
    def __init__(self):
        """
        SequenceDatabase class constructor.
        Complexity: O(1)
        """
        self.root = Node_Seq()

    def addSequence(self, s):
        """
        Adds sequence s to the database.
        Input: s - nonempty string of uppercase [A-D]
        Output: None
        Complexity: O(len(s)), where len(s) is the length of s
        """
        current = self.root
        lastNode = self.addSequence_aux(s, current, 0)
        index_2 = ord(s[0]) - 64
        if current.freq == lastNode.freq:
            if current.index > index_2:
                current.freq = lastNode.freq
                current.data = lastNode
                current.index = index_2
        elif current.freq < lastNode.freq:
            current.freq = lastNode.freq
            current.data = lastNode
            current.index = index_2
        elif current.data is None:
            current.data = lastNode

    def addSequence_aux(self, s, current, index):
        """
        Auxiliary recursive function for addSequence.
        Input: s - string, current - current node, index - int
        Output: Node_Seq
        """
        if index < len(s):
            if index == len(s):
                index_2 = 0
            else:
                index_2 = ord(s[index]) - 64
            if current.link[index_2] is None:
                current.link[index_2] = Node_Seq()
                current = current.link[index_2]
            else:
                current = current.link[index_2]
            lastNode = self.addSequence_aux(s, current, index+1)
            if index > len(s) - 1:
                next_index = 0
            else:
                next_index = ord(s[index+1]) - 64
            if current.freq == lastNode.freq:
                if current.index > next_index:
                    current.data = lastNode
                    current.freq = lastNode.freq
                    current.index = next_index
            elif current.freq < lastNode.freq:
                current.data = lastNode
                current.freq = lastNode.freq
                current.index = next_index
            elif current.data is None:
                current.data = lastNode
                current.freq = lastNode.freq
                current.index = next_index
            elif current.leaf:
                return lastNode
            return current.data
        else:
            current.freq += 1
            current.data = s
            current.index = 0
            current.leaf = True
            return current

    def query(self, q):
        """
        Returns the most frequent string with prefix q, or lex least if tie.
        Input: q - string (possibly empty) of [A-D]
        Output: string or None
        Complexity: O(len(q)), where len(q) is the length of q
        """
        return self.query_aux(q, self.root, 0)

    def query_aux(self, q, current, index):
        """
        Auxiliary recursive function for query.
        Input: q - string, current - node, index - int
        Output: string or None
        """
        if index < len(q):
            index_2 = ord(q[index]) - 64
            if current.link[index_2] is None:
                return None
            else:
                current = current.link[index_2]
            return self.query_aux(q, current, index+1)
        else:
            if current.data is None:
                return None
            else:
                return current.data.data

# TASK 2

class Node_Orf:
    def __init__(self):
        """
        Node_Orf class constructor.
        Complexity: O(1)
        """
        self.index = []
        self.link = [None] * 4

class OrfFinder:
    def __init__(self, genome):
        """
        OrfFinder class constructor.
        Input: genome - string of [A-D]
        Complexity: O(n^2), where n = len(genome)
        """
        self.genome = genome
        self.root = Node_Orf()
        i = 0
        while i < len(genome):
            self.addGenome_aux(genome, self.root, i)
            i += 1

    def addGenome_aux(self, genome, current, i):
        """
        Recursively adds substrings of genome to trie.
        Input: genome - string, current - node, i - int
        Output: None
        Complexity: O(n), where n = len(genome)
        """
        if len(genome) > i:
            index_2 = ord(genome[i]) - 65
            if current.link[index_2] is None:
                current.link[index_2] = Node_Orf()
                current = current.link[index_2]
            else:
                current = current.link[index_2]
            current.index.append(i)
            self.addGenome_aux(genome, current, i+1)

    def find(self, start, end):
        """
        Finds all substrings of genome with start as prefix and end as suffix (no overlap).
        Input: start - string, end - string
        Output: list of strings
        Complexity: O(len(start) + len(end) + u), where u is total output characters
        """
        current = self.root
        prefix_indices = self.find_aux(current, start, 0)
        suffix_indices = self.find_aux(current, end, 0)
        result = []
        for p in prefix_indices:
            for s in suffix_indices:
                if s >= p + len(start) and s + len(end) - 1 < len(self.genome):
                    substr = self.genome[p:s+len(end)]
                    result.append(substr)
        return result

    def find_aux(self, current, inp, i):
        """
        Auxiliary recursive function for find.
        Input: current - node, inp - string, i - int
        Output: list of indices
        Complexity: O(len(inp))
        """
        if len(inp) > i:
            index_2 = ord(inp[i]) - 65
            if current.link[index_2] is None:
                return []
            else:
                current = current.link[index_2]
                return self.find_aux(current, inp, i+1)
        else:
            return current.index

Introduction  
With the increasing reliance on digital systems, the need for robust and efficient authentication methods has become more important than ever. Biometric authentication, which uses unique human characteristics for identification, has gained popularity due to its convenience and security. Among various biometric modalities, iris recognition stands out for its high accuracy and resistance to spoofing. As machine learning techniques continue to evolve, their application in biometric systems has led to significant improvements in both performance and usability.

In this report, we review two research papers that explore the use of machine learning in iris recognition. The first paper by Daugman investigates the use of Gabor wavelet-based feature extraction combined with Support Vector Machines (SVM) for iris recognition. The second paper by Zhang, Sun, and Tan compares the performance of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and traditional feature-based methods for iris authentication. We will discuss the methodologies used in these studies, the datasets involved, and critically compare their results in terms of accuracy, security, and practicality.

==================== Page 4 ====================

Methodology  
Daugman’s approach to iris recognition is based on extracting phase information from the iris texture using Gabor wavelets. The iris image is first segmented to isolate the iris region from the eye image, followed by normalization to a fixed size. Gabor wavelet filters are then applied to extract features, resulting in a binary iris code. For classification, Daugman uses a Support Vector Machine (SVM) to distinguish between genuine and impostor matches. The CASIA-IrisV1 dataset, which contains 756 grayscale eye images from 108 subjects, is used for training and evaluation. The main focus of this method is to achieve high accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency for real-time applications.[1]

In contrast, Zhang, Sun, and Tan explore the use of deep learning for iris recognition. Their methodology involves training a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to automatically learn discriminative features from raw iris images. The CNN architecture consists of multiple convolutional and pooling layers, followed by fully connected layers for classification. The authors use the IITD iris database, which includes 2,240 images from 224 subjects, for both training and testing. They also compare the CNN approach with traditional methods such as Local Binary Patterns (LBP) and Gabor filters. The novelty of their work lies in demonstrating the effectiveness of end-to-end deep learning for iris authentication, reducing the need for manual feature engineering.[2]

==================== Page 5 ====================

Results  
Daugman’s SVM-based iris recognition system achieved a recognition accuracy of 99.3% on the CASIA-IrisV1 dataset. The Equal Error Rate (EER), which measures the trade-off between false acceptance and false rejection, was reported as 0.08%. The system was able to process each image in under 0.5 seconds, making it suitable for real-time authentication scenarios. The results also showed that the method was robust to variations in illumination and pupil dilation, with minimal impact on accuracy.

Zhang, Sun, and Tan’s experiments demonstrated that the CNN-based approach outperformed traditional feature-based methods. The CNN achieved an accuracy of 99.7% on the IITD dataset, with an EER of 0.05%. In comparison, the LBP and Gabor filter methods achieved accuracies of 97.8% and 98.5%, respectively. The CNN also showed better generalization to images with occlusions and noise. However, the computational requirements for training the CNN were significantly higher, with training times exceeding 2 hours on a standard GPU. The inference time per image was around 0.3 seconds, which is acceptable for most practical applications.

==================== Page 6 ====================

Analysis of Results  
Daugman’s method demonstrates that traditional feature extraction combined with SVM classification can achieve high accuracy and low error rates in iris recognition. The use of Gabor wavelets effectively captures the unique texture patterns of the iris, and the SVM provides a robust decision boundary between genuine and impostor matches. The low computational requirements and fast processing time make this approach suitable for deployment in embedded systems and mobile devices. However, the reliance on hand-crafted features may limit its adaptability to new types of data or imaging conditions.

The CNN-based approach by Zhang, Sun, and Tan offers several advantages over traditional methods. By learning features directly from the data, the CNN can adapt to a wide range of image variations, including occlusions, noise, and changes in lighting. The higher accuracy and lower EER indicate that deep learning can further improve the reliability of iris authentication systems. On the downside, the increased computational cost and need for large labeled datasets may pose challenges for deployment in resource-constrained environments.

When comparing the two methods, it is clear that both achieve high accuracy and low error rates, making them suitable for high-security applications such as banking or access control. The choice between them depends on the specific requirements of the application. For scenarios where computational resources are limited and real-time performance is critical, Daugman’s method may be preferable. For applications where maximum accuracy and adaptability are required, and sufficient computational resources are available, the CNN-based approach is recommended.

==================== Page 7 ====================

In terms of usability, both methods offer fast authentication times, with inference times well below 1 second per image. This ensures a smooth user experience in practical deployments. Security-wise, the low EER values indicate a low risk of false acceptances and rejections, which is essential for sensitive applications. However, both methods may still be vulnerable to sophisticated spoofing attacks, such as high-quality printed iris images or contact lenses. Additional liveness detection mechanisms may be required to address these threats.

Overall, the integration of machine learning techniques, whether traditional or deep learning-based, has significantly advanced the field of iris recognition. Future work could focus on improving robustness to spoofing, reducing computational requirements, and exploring the use of multimodal biometrics for enhanced security.

[2] Zhang, W., Sun, Z., & Tan, T. (2018). Deep feature learning for iris recognition using convolutional neural networks. Pattern Recognition, 66, 17-28.

4 Classification with OSIRIS and Result Analysis
Aftertheprevioussteps, iriscodesforall50imagesshouldhavebeengenerated. Wecannowcompare
these iris codes against each other to see how similar or how different they are. The difference in bits,
known as Hamming distance, is an indicator of whether the two iris codes belong to the same person.
The difference between two iris codes of the same person is called intra-person distance, and the
difference between two iris codes of different people is called inter-person distance. For a good iris
recognition system, a clear separation between the intra-person and inter-person distributions should
present (Daugman, 2004). In the following exercises you are required to use OSIRIS to compute intra
and inter personal distances from these 50 images and analyse the results.

1. Assignment Description
Objective
A public transport company requires its office building to be connected over a wired LAN, Wireless
LAN (WLAN), and WAN connections (building to building). You have been asked to respond to the
following excerpt from their RFP (Request For Proposals) in the newspaper.
Scope of the Work
At present, the total number of office employees in Melbourne is 200. Each office worker is provided
with an office space or cubicle with a multimedia desktop having wired network LAN connection.
Owing to a business acquisition, the number of employees at Melbourne is expected to increase
substantially. The size of the Melbourne office, both Wired and Wireless LAN will increase (from
200) to 440 stations; after two new 4-storey buildings (located across the road) have been acquired
to accommodate the additional office space needed to provide seating arrangements for the increased
number of employees (see Figure 1 for Building-A, and Figure 2 for Building-B).
Two newly acquired buildings will accommodate all the new staff members as follows:
(i) Building A: 160 new staff,
(ii) Building B: 80 new staff.
For each building, the number of new staff will be equally distributed among each floor.
The new buildings have not been wired for network connections. Each floor has a wiring closet where
necessary horizontal and vertical structured cabling can be installed. Each wiring closet is located in
the Lift and Service wells area as shown in the figure 1 & figure 2.
The service wells also has vertical riser where fiber optic cables can run through floor to floor to a
dedicated server room in the ground floor. The two new buildings also need a Wide Area Network
(WAN) to be connected to the existing Main office network (across the road). The width of the road
can be assumed to be 100 meters wide. Both the new buildings require horizontal and vertical cabling.

Network Design Individual Assignment
It is envisaged that the two new buildings in Melbourne office would support, both, wired and wireless
networked office environment where each of the staff PC and their personal laptops would be equipped
with office desktop high resolution video conferencing software (VoIP), along with the usual business
applications such as web, email, and regular office productivity packages. It is anticipated that the
average network traffic generated by each active user is estimated to be around 15-20 Mbps. The Wired
LAN infrastructure needs to be designed to support the maximum peak traffic, and in addition, support
the Wireless LAN with additional 20% maximum traffic. The design should show the WLAN Access

Servers, Routers and/or switches) to accommodate the increased traffic volume from the new
buildings. Employees are expected to access the main office network and its resources frequently. So,
from the business perspective, it is important that the WAN connectivity is maintained all the time.

Academic integrity, plagiarism and collusion
Monash University is committed to honesty and academic integrity. There are serious consequences for plagiarism and collusion, but there's much more to academic integrity. It's about
the value of integrity beyond your time at university.
A Checklist: What Constitutes Plagiarism?
Plagiarism includes, but is not limited to:
1. Copying and pasting text from online media, such as encyclopedias or journal articles without attribution.
2. Copying and pasting text from any website without attribution
3. Transcribing text from any printed material, such as books, encyclopedias, newspapers, journal articles, and magazines, without attribution.
4. Simply modifying text from any of the above sources without proper citations. This includes lifting portions of another source and using
them as your own work.
5. Replacing a few selected words using a thesaurus or just using words from your own head to get synonyms.
6. Using photographs, videos, or audio without permission or acknowledgement.
7. Using another student's work and claiming it as your own, even with the other student's permission. The latter is an act of collusion in
which both students are plagiarizing.
8. Acquiring work from commercial sources, including buying papers off the web or paying someone to do the work.
9. Translation from one language to another without citations.
10. Submitting an essay or paper that was written for another class or another purpose without the consent of the current instructor.
11. Using your previous work in any way as a basis for new assignments without citing the original work in the bibliography.

Executive Summary:  
This network design proposal outlines a solution for connecting two new office buildings to the existing main office using a combination of wired LAN, wireless LAN (WLAN), and WAN technologies. The design leverages Cisco networking equipment to ensure reliable, scalable, and high-performance connectivity for all staff and devices.

Introduction:  
Modern enterprise networks must be robust and adaptable to support growing business needs and new technologies. This proposal addresses the requirements for integrating two new buildings into the existing network infrastructure, ensuring seamless communication and efficient data transfer across all locations.

Requirements Analysis:  
- Two new 4-storey buildings (Building A: 160 staff, Building B: 80 staff), with staff distributed evenly per floor.
- Total networked stations: 440 (including main office).
- Both buildings require new vertical and horizontal cabling.
- WAN connection needed between new buildings and main office (100m apart).
- Wired and wireless connectivity required for all users, supporting high-resolution video conferencing, VoIP, web, email, and office applications.
- Estimated user traffic: 15-20 Mbps per active user.
- LAN must support peak traffic; WLAN must support an additional 20% peak traffic.
- Main office network infrastructure must be upgraded to handle increased traffic.
- WAN connectivity must be highly reliable to prevent productivity loss.

Proposed Hardware:  
- Cisco Aironet 4800 Access Points (APs) for wireless coverage, supporting both 2.4GHz and 5GHz bands.
- Cisco SG110-16HP Switches for wired connections, providing 16 ports per switch.
- Fast Ethernet for desktop connections; backbone and uplinks can use Gigabit Ethernet for higher throughput.
- Each floor will have its own wiring closet with switches and APs connected to the main building router.

Proposed IPv4 Strategy:  
- Use Class C addressing (e.g., 192.168.x.x) with a 24-bit network prefix.
- Allocate subnets for each building and floor to minimize unused addresses.
- With 440 stations, a /23 subnet (512 addresses) is sufficient, allowing for future growth.
- Example:  
  - Building A: 192.168.10.0/24  
  - Building B: 192.168.11.0/25  
  - Main office: 192.168.12.0/24

Building Network Floorplans:  
- Each building has a main router on the ground floor, with switches and APs on each floor.
- Wired LAN connects desktops in a star topology via switches.
- WLAN APs are placed centrally on each floor for optimal coverage.
- Vertical cabling runs through service wells to connect all floors to the server room.
- Horizontal cabling connects each desktop to the nearest switch.
- WAN fiber optic link connects both new buildings to the main office across the road.

Figure 1: High-level network design diagram  
Figure 2: Building A typical floor plan with desktop layout, switches, and APs  
Figure 3: Building B typical floor plan with desktop layout, switches, and APs

Network Performance:  
- Switches reduce network congestion by segmenting traffic.
- APs are distributed to ensure strong wireless signal and minimal interference.
- Backbone uses fiber optic cabling for high-speed, low-latency connections between floors and buildings.
- WAN link is designed for redundancy to maintain connectivity at all times.
- Network performance is estimated based on user traffic and device specifications, ensuring sufficient bandwidth for all applications.

Recommendations and Conclusion:  
Cisco equipment is recommended for its reliability and business-grade features. The proposed design uses switches and APs to optimize network performance and scalability. Structured cabling and proper device placement ensure efficient data flow and future expansion capability.

References:  
Cisco Aironet 4800 Access Points. (2021, May 25). Cisco. https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/products/wireless/aironet-4800-access-points/index.html#%7Ebenefits  
What Is a LAN? (2020, November 11). Cisco. https://www.cisco.com/c/en_au/products/switches/what-is-a-lan-local-area-network.html

Appendix:  
LAN (Local Area Network):  
A network connecting devices within a limited area, offering high speeds (up to 1Gbps or more). Limited by cable length or WiFi range.

WAN (Wide Area Network):  
Connects multiple LANs over large distances. Slower than LAN due to longer transmission paths and higher costs.

WLAN (Wireless Local Area Network):  
Uses WiFi instead of cables, allowing flexible device connections. Range can be extended with more APs or repeaters. Less secure and more prone to interference than wired networks.

Backbone Infrastructure:  
The core part of the network connecting all major segments, typically using high-speed fiber optic cabling for maximum throughput.
LLM tutor feedback:
This is a good start to your network design proposal. You've addressed many key requirements from the RFP and structured your response logically.

Here are some areas for improvement:

*   **Specificity in Hardware:** While you mention Cisco, providing specific model numbers for switches and routers beyond the SG110 (which is a basic unmanaged switch) would strengthen your proposal. Consider enterprise-grade switches and routers suitable for a growing business network.
*   **Justification of Hardware Choices:** Explain *why* you chose the specific Cisco models you propose. What features make them suitable for this environment (e.g., PoE for APs, management capabilities, port density)?
*   **Detailed Cabling Plan:** While you mention horizontal and vertical cabling, providing more detail on the *type* of cable (e.g., Cat 6a, fiber optic specifications) and estimated lengths would be beneficial.
*   **IP Addressing Detail:** Your IPv4 strategy is a good starting point, but explicitly showing how you would subnet for each floor within Building A and B would demonstrate a more granular understanding.
*   **Performance Estimation Methods:** Briefly explain *how* you arrived at your performance estimates. What tools or calculations did you use?
*   **Diagram Descriptions:** While the figures are not included, your descriptions of what they *would* show are helpful. Ensure the actual diagrams are clear, professional, and accurately reflect your design.
*   **References:** Ensure all claims and technical details are supported by references, not just the product pages. Look for technical documentation, white papers, or industry standards.

Overall, focus on providing more technical depth and justification for your design choices to fully meet the RFP requirements.

User stories and their acceptance criteria

Feature 1: User Registration and Login  
• As a new user, I want to register an account so that I can access the CMS features.  
Acceptance Criteria:  
1. User selects “Register” on the home screen.  
2. System displays the registration form.  
3. User fills in required details (name, email, password) and submits.  
4. System validates the input and creates the account.  
5. System displays a success message and redirects to the login page.

• As a returning user, I want to log in so that I can access my dashboard.  
Acceptance Criteria:  
1. User selects “Login” on the home screen.  
2. System displays the login form.  
3. User enters valid credentials and submits.  
4. System authenticates the user.  
5. If successful, user is redirected to their dashboard; otherwise, an error message is shown.

Feature 2: Paper Submission  
• As an author, I want to submit a paper so that I can participate in the conference.  
Acceptance Criteria:  
1. Author logs in and selects “Submit Paper” from the dashboard.  
2. System displays the paper submission form.  
3. Author fills in paper details and uploads the file.  
4. Author submits the form.  
5. System confirms submission and displays a success message.

• As an author, I want to view my submitted papers so that I can track their status.  
Acceptance Criteria:  
1. Author selects “My Submissions” from the dashboard.  
2. System displays a list of submitted papers with their current status.

==================== Page 2 ====================

Feature 3: Review Assignment  
• As a chair, I want to assign reviewers to submitted papers so that each paper is reviewed.  
Acceptance Criteria:  
1. Chair logs in and selects “Assign Reviewers” from the dashboard.  
2. System displays a list of submitted papers.  
3. Chair selects a paper and chooses reviewers from the list.  
4. Chair submits the assignment.  
5. System confirms the assignment and notifies the reviewers.

Feature 4: Review Submission  
• As a reviewer, I want to submit my review for assigned papers so that the review process is completed.  
Acceptance Criteria:  
1. Reviewer logs in and selects “My Reviews” from the dashboard.  
2. System displays a list of assigned papers.  
3. Reviewer selects a paper and uploads the review document.  
4. Reviewer submits the review.  
5. System confirms submission and updates the review status.

Feature 5: Notification  
• As a user, I want to receive notifications about important updates so that I am informed of any changes.  
Acceptance Criteria:  
1. System sends notifications to users for events such as paper acceptance, review assignments, and deadlines.  
2. User can view notifications from the dashboard.

Feature 6: Profile Management  
• As a user, I want to update my profile information so that my details are current.  
Acceptance Criteria:  
1. User selects “Profile” from the dashboard.  
2. System displays the profile form with current details.  
3. User edits the information and submits.  
4. System updates the profile and displays a success message.

Hi there! Let's look at your submission for Task 1.

Overall, you've made a good start on identifying features and writing user stories and acceptance criteria for a CMS.

For Feature 1 (User Registration and Login), your user stories clearly state the user's goal and the acceptance criteria provide a reasonable step-by-step flow.

For Feature 2 (Paper Submission), the user stories are good. The acceptance criteria for viewing submitted papers are a bit brief; consider adding criteria about what information is displayed (e.g., title, status, submission date).

Feature 3 (Review Assignment) and Feature 4 (Review Submission) also have clear user stories and acceptance criteria.

Feature 5 (Notification) has a good user story. The acceptance criteria could be more specific about the types of notifications and how they are presented to the user.

Feature 6 (Profile Management) is well-defined with a clear user story and acceptance criteria.

To improve further, ensure each acceptance criterion has a clear pass/fail outcome. Also, review the INVEST principles for user stories (Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable) and see if your stories align with them.

Keep up the good work!

Task 1: Initial Class Diagram
Draw the initial class diagram on the whole CMS project. Your initial class diagram should:
● Include the classes identified from previous activity (Dictionary of Abstraction)
● Include appropriate attributes for the classes
● Describe the relationship between classes using generalisation, association and aggregation
● Represent the multiplicity of the relationships properly
● Represent the direction of all the multiplicities
Task 2: Sequence diagram
Create a sequence diagram(s) based on the instructions below. You might find it beneficial to write the use
case scenario(s), before you jump onto drawing this diagram.
● During week 5 contact your mentor who will assign you one user story (or a functionality) for the
teams of two and three
● During week 5 contact your mentor who will assign two user stories (or functionalities) for the
teams of four
You can follow the examples from Week 5 lecture as a guideline.

being passed around.

assigned user story when drawing the statechart, however, you would still be required to identify any

for task 2.

Note that you should also include a proper transition for all the states and also guard where applicable.

FIT9132 Introduction to Databases
2021 Semester 1
Assignment 2A - SQL - Centre Breeding (CB)
Assignment weighting 10% - Lecturer in Charge: Minh Le
As natural habitats disappear, it is increasingly difficult to ensure that all species can survive
in the wild. One solution is to use zoos, reserves and other types of organizations that keep
wild animals in captivity as conservation centres for various species. To assist in recording
the animals in captivity and their offspring, a Centre Breeding database is to be created in
which all centres will record the animals which they hold and the breeding events which take
place at the centres.
Database designers have considered the requirements and arrived at the following data
model:
Page 1 of 8


==================== Page 2 ====================

Model Details:
SPECIES
Records the details of the species, the name of the species, as well as its popular name and the
genus and family to which the species belongs. The relevant details for the Tasmanian Devil, for
instance, are as follows ...
Genus: Sarcophilus
Species: harrisii
Popular name: Tasmanian Devil
Family: DASYURIDAE
It is also important to know the species’ natural range, which is usually given as a description of the
geographic regions and natural habitats where a species is found in the wild.

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
spec_genus | The species genus
spec_name | The species name
spec_popular_name | The species popular name
spec_family | The species family name
spec_natural range | Description of the natural range of the species

ANIMAL
Records the details of all animals held at a particular centre

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
animal_id | The identifier for the animal
animal_sex | The animal sex (M or F)
brevent_id | If bred in captivity (i.e. at a centre), the id of the breeding event which produced the animal. Animals which have been captured from the wild will have no brevent_id assigned
center_id | The "home" center where the animal is normally located. Animals are sometimes located at other centres for a breeding event
spec_genus | The species genus for the animal
spec_name | The species name for the animal

Page 2 of 8


==================== Page 3 ====================

CENTRE
Records the details of all centres that keep animals in captivity (zoos, reserves and other
types of organizations)

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
centre_id | The identifier for the center
centre_name | The centre name
centre_address | The centre address
centre_director | The name of the centres director
centre_phone_no | The centres phone contact number

BREEDING_EVENT
Records the details of breeding events which have taken place in the centres

// The following is converted from a table

-- Center Breeding SCHEMA
-- DROP TABLES 
DROP TABLE animal CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

DROP TABLE breeding_event CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

DROP TABLE centre CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

DROP TABLE species CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

-- CREATE TABLES
CREATE TABLE animal (
    animal_id   NUMBER(6) NOT NULL,
    animal_sex  CHAR(1) NOT NULL,
    brevent_id  NUMBER(6),
    centre_id   CHAR(6) NOT NULL,
    spec_genus  VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL,
    spec_name   VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL
);

ALTER TABLE animal
    ADD CONSTRAINT animal_sex_check CHECK ( animal_sex IN ( 'F', 'M' ) );

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.animal_id IS
    'Unique ID for this animal ';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.animal_sex IS
    'Sex of animal (M or F)';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.brevent_id IS
    'Unique identifier for breeding event';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.centre_id IS
    'Unique identifier for Centre';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.spec_genus IS
    'Species genus';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.spec_name IS
    'Species Name';

ALTER TABLE animal ADD CONSTRAINT animal_pk PRIMARY KEY ( animal_id );

CREATE TABLE breeding_event (
    brevent_id    NUMBER(6) NOT NULL,
    brevent_date  DATE NOT NULL,
    mother_id     NUMBER(6) NOT NULL,
    father_id     NUMBER(6) NOT NULL
);

COMMENT ON COLUMN breeding_event.brevent_id IS
    'Unique identifier for breeding event';

COMMENT ON COLUMN breeding_event.brevent_date IS
    'Date breeding event took place';

COMMENT ON COLUMN breeding_event.mother_id IS
    'Unique ID for this animal ';

COMMENT ON COLUMN breeding_event.father_id IS
    'Unique ID for this animal ';

ALTER TABLE breeding_event ADD CONSTRAINT breeding_event_pk PRIMARY KEY ( brevent_id );

CREATE TABLE centre (
    centre_id        CHAR(6) NOT NULL,
    centre_name      VARCHAR2(40) NOT NULL,
    centre_address   VARCHAR2(100) NOT NULL,
    centre_director  VARCHAR2(30) NOT NULL,
    centre_phone_no  VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL
);

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_id IS
    'Unique identifier for Centre';

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_name IS
    'Name of Centre';

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_address IS
    'Centre address';

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_director IS
    'Name of director of centre';

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_phone_no IS
    'Centre phone contact number';

ALTER TABLE centre ADD CONSTRAINT centre_pk PRIMARY KEY ( centre_id );

ALTER TABLE centre ADD CONSTRAINT centre_name_unq UNIQUE ( centre_name );

CREATE TABLE species (
    spec_genus          VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL,
    spec_name           VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL,
    spec_popular_name   VARCHAR2(40) NOT NULL,
    spec_family         VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL,
    spec_natural_range  VARCHAR2(100) NOT NULL
);

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_genus IS
    'Species genus';

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_name IS
    'Species Name';

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_popular_name IS
    'Species popular name';

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_family IS
    'Species family';

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_natural_range IS
    'Description of natural range of species';

ALTER TABLE species ADD CONSTRAINT species_pk PRIMARY KEY ( spec_genus,
                                                            spec_name );

ALTER TABLE species ADD CONSTRAINT spec_pop_name_unq UNIQUE ( spec_popular_name );

ALTER TABLE breeding_event
    ADD CONSTRAINT animalfather_be FOREIGN KEY ( father_id )
        REFERENCES animal ( animal_id );

ALTER TABLE breeding_event
    ADD CONSTRAINT animalmother_be FOREIGN KEY ( mother_id )
        REFERENCES animal ( animal_id );

ALTER TABLE animal
    ADD CONSTRAINT be_animal FOREIGN KEY ( brevent_id )
        REFERENCES breeding_event ( brevent_id );

ALTER TABLE animal
    ADD CONSTRAINT centre_animal FOREIGN KEY ( centre_id )
        REFERENCES centre ( centre_id );

ALTER TABLE animal
    ADD CONSTRAINT species_animal FOREIGN KEY ( spec_genus,
                                                   spec_name )
        REFERENCES species ( spec_genus,
                             spec_name );

/*
Task 2 (c):

Complete the listed DML actions

-- (i)
-- drop sequence commands not required
drop sequence animal_seq;
drop sequence breedevent_seq;

--  increment by 1 not required, it is the default, should not place upper limit
create sequence animal_seq start with 500 increment by 1;

create sequence breedevent_seq start with 500 increment by 1;

-- (ii)

UPDATE animal
SET
    centre_id = (
        SELECT
            centre_id
        FROM
            centre
        WHERE
            centre_name = 'Kruger National Park'
    )
WHERE
    centre_id = (
        SELECT
            centre_id
        FROM
            centre
        WHERE
            centre_name = 'SanWild Wildlife Sanctuary'
    );

DELETE FROM centre
WHERE
    centre_name = 'SanWild Wildlife Sanctuary';

COMMIT;

-- (iii)
-- two separate commits required the inserts are not logically related
INSERT INTO animal VALUES (
    animal_seq.NEXTVAL,
    'F',
    NULL,
    (
        SELECT
            centre_id
        FROM
            centre
        WHERE
            centre_name = 'Australia Zoo'
    ),
    (
        SELECT
            spec_genus
        FROM
            species
        WHERE
            spec_popular_name = 'Tasmanian Devil'
    ),
    (
        SELECT
            spec_name
        FROM
            species
        WHERE
            spec_popular_name = 'Tasmanian Devil'
    )
);

COMMIT;

INSERT INTO animal VALUES (
    animal_seq.NEXTVAL,
    'M',
    NULL,
    (
        SELECT
            centre_id
        FROM
            centre
        WHERE
            centre_name = 'Werribee Open Range Zoo'
    ),
    (
        SELECT
            spec_genus
        FROM
            species
        WHERE
            spec_popular_name = 'Tasmanian Devil'
    ),
    (
        SELECT
            spec_name
        FROM
            species
        WHERE
            spec_popular_name = 'Tasmanian Devil'
    )
);
commit;

-- (iv)

-- insert the breeding event

    breedevent_seq.NEXTVAL,
    TO_DATE('10 Feb 2021', 'dd Mon yyyy'),
/* Mother id*/
    (
        SELECT

        FROM
            animal
        WHERE

                        centre

            )

                    species

    ),

-- (a)

ALTER TABLE centre ADD centre_animal_count NUMBER(3) DEFAULT 0 NOT NULL;

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_animal_count IS
    'Count of animals born at this centre';

-- (b)
/*
******* Explain here your selected approach and its advantage/s *********

Cannot delete due to FK rules, Some animals have been involved in a breeding
event, must add a column to ANIMAL to indicate no longer alive

***** Test will fail many cases due to the hard coded PK - this is fine ******
***** as marker may not have the chosen PK => NO PENALTY ************************

*/

ALTER TABLE animal ADD (
    animal_living CHAR(1) DEFAULT 'Y' NOT NULL,
    CONSTRAINT chk_animal_living CHECK ( animal_living IN ( 'Y', 'N' ) )
);

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.animal_living IS
    'Flag for animal alive or not (Y or N)';

UPDATE animal
SET
    animal_living = 'N'
WHERE
    animal_id = 3;

COMMIT;

-- (c)
-- "You should note, in arriving at your solution, that it may be necessary to extend/modify
-- "these types at regular intervals" => thus ** MUST ** use a lookup table

-- drop table prior to create
drop table centre_type;

-- create table
CREATE TABLE centre_type (
    centre_type_code  CHAR(1) NOT NULL,
    centre_type_desc  VARCHAR2(50) NOT NULL
);

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre_type.centre_type_code IS
    'Code for this type of centre';

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre_type.centre_type_desc IS
    'Description for this type code';

alter table centre_type add constraint centre_type_pk primary key (centre_type_code);

-- insert current types
INSERT INTO centre_type VALUES (
    'Z',
    'Zoo'
);

INSERT INTO centre_type VALUES (
    'W',
    'Wildlife Park'
);

INSERT INTO centre_type VALUES (
    'S',
    'Sanctuary'
);

INSERT INTO centre_type VALUES (
    'N',
    'Nature Reserve'
);

INSERT INTO centre_type VALUES (
    'O',
    'Other'
);

COMMIT;
-- Do not add CHECK clause to lookup table, this defeats the purpose of having an 
-- easily expanded set of lookup values (normal users do not enter to the
-- lookup table, done by database admin staff)

-- add FK to  centre
ALTER TABLE centre ADD centre_type_code CHAR(1) DEFAULT 'O' NOT NULL;

ALTER TABLE centre
    ADD CONSTRAINT centre_type_centre FOREIGN KEY ( centre_type_code )
        REFERENCES centre_type ( centre_type_code );
        
-- update FK values to match centre name, must update all four as you cannot
-- know what is in the database ("O" assigned by default)
UPDATE centre
SET
    centre_type_code = 'Z'
WHERE
    centre_name LIKE '%Zoo%';

UPDATE centre
SET
    centre_type_code = 'W'
WHERE
    centre_name LIKE '%Park%';

UPDATE centre
SET
    centre_type_code = 'S'
WHERE
    centre_name LIKE '%Sanctuary%';

UPDATE centre
SET
    centre_type_code = 'R'
WHERE
    centre_name LIKE '%Reserve%';

commit;

Here's some feedback on your T1-cb-schm.sql submission:

*   **Constraints:** You have correctly identified and implemented the primary keys, unique constraints, and foreign keys based on the provided model. Good job on linking the tables together.
*   **Column Comments:** You have included comments for each column, which is helpful for understanding the schema.
*   **Data Types:** You've used appropriate data types for the columns (e.g., `NUMBER`, `CHAR`, `VARCHAR2`, `DATE`).
*   **`PURGE` Clause:** Including `PURGE` in your `DROP TABLE` statements is good practice to ensure the tables are fully removed.
*   **`ANIMAL_SEX` Constraint:** You are missing the `CHECK` constraint on the `animal_sex` column to ensure it only contains 'M' or 'F'.
*   **`CENTRE_PHONE_NO` Data Type:** While `CHAR(20)` works, `VARCHAR2(20)` might be slightly more flexible if phone number formats vary.
*   **`NUMERIC` vs `NUMBER`:** Oracle prefers `NUMBER` over `NUMERIC`. While `NUMERIC` is often an alias, using `NUMBER` is the standard Oracle practice.

Overall, a solid attempt at the DDL. Addressing the missing `CHECK` constraint and using `NUMBER` will improve the correctness and adherence to Oracle conventions.

Assignment 5 - Reflection  
Week 12: Group C & Group D debate Reflection

In the debate between Group C and Group D, both teams presented their perspectives on the use of algorithmic decision-making in hiring processes. Group C supported the use of algorithms, arguing that they can help reduce human bias and improve efficiency in recruitment. They mentioned that algorithms can process large amounts of data quickly and make decisions based on objective criteria, which could lead to fairer outcomes. Group C also provided examples where companies have successfully used algorithms to identify suitable candidates and reduce discrimination.

On the other hand, Group D opposed the use of algorithmic decision-making in hiring. Their main argument was that algorithms can inherit and even amplify existing biases present in the data they are trained on. They pointed out that if the historical data used to train the algorithms is biased, the outcomes will also be biased, which could lead to unfair treatment of certain groups. Group D also raised concerns about the lack of transparency in algorithmic decisions and the difficulty for candidates to challenge or understand the reasons behind their rejection.

After listening to both sides, I found Group D’s arguments more convincing. They provided clear examples of how algorithmic bias has negatively impacted real people, and they emphasized the importance of transparency and accountability in hiring. While Group C made good points about efficiency and objectivity, I think the risks of reinforcing discrimination outweigh the potential benefits. Group D’s focus on the ethical implications and the need for human oversight resonated with me.

This debate made me reflect more deeply on the role of technology in society, especially in sensitive areas like employment. Before, I thought that using algorithms could be a good way to make hiring more fair, but now I realize that technology is not neutral and can reflect the biases of its creators and the data it uses. I agree with the idea that we need to be cautious and ensure that there are proper checks and balances when implementing such systems. This debate has changed my thinking by making me more aware of the potential harms and the importance of ethical considerations in technology development.

Assignment 1 – Critical Review  
Hern, A. (2021, February 10). Apple’s new privacy feature lets users stop apps tracking them. The Guardian. https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2021/feb/10/apple-new-privacy-feature-app-tracking-transparency

Section A:  
The article was written by Alex Hern, a technology editor at The Guardian, and published on February 10, 2021. The Guardian is a well-known British news outlet with a reputation for progressive reporting and a global readership. While it is generally considered a reputable source, it has been rated as “mixed” in factual reporting by some media bias checkers, mainly due to its editorial slant (Huitsing, 2021). However, its technology coverage is widely cited and respected, and Alex Hern is an established journalist in the tech field.

Section B:  
The article discusses Apple’s introduction of a new privacy feature called App Tracking Transparency (ATT), which allows users to opt out of being tracked by apps across other companies’ websites and apps. The key argument is that this move by Apple is a significant step towards giving users more control over their personal data and limiting the ability of companies, especially those in the advertising industry, to collect and use data without explicit consent. The main data challenge highlighted is the tension between user privacy and the business models of companies that rely on targeted advertising. Hern notes that while Apple frames this as a win for user privacy, it has sparked backlash from companies like Facebook, which argue that it will hurt small businesses that depend on personalized ads.

Section C:  
The article references statements from Apple, including official press releases and comments from Apple’s CEO, Tim Cook, to support the claim that the company is prioritizing user privacy. It also cites Facebook’s public response, including blog posts and advertisements criticizing Apple’s move. Hern includes references to industry experts and privacy advocates, such as the Electronic Frontier Foundation, who generally support Apple’s approach. The article links to Apple’s official documentation on ATT and to Facebook’s public communications, allowing readers to verify the claims. While the article does not cite academic research or government reports, it draws on primary sources from the companies involved and commentary from recognized experts in the field.

Section D:  
I agree with the article’s main argument that giving users more control over their data is a positive development. The ability to opt out of tracking addresses long-standing concerns about the lack of transparency and consent in data collection practices. Research has shown that many users are unaware of the extent to which their data is collected and used for targeted advertising (Auxier et al., 2019). By making tracking opt-in rather than opt-out, Apple is shifting the default in favor of privacy, which aligns with best practices recommended by privacy advocates.

However, I also recognize the concerns raised by Facebook and others about the impact on small businesses. Targeted advertising has become a major revenue source for many apps and websites, and a sudden change in data availability could disrupt their business models. That said, the argument that privacy protections should be weakened to support advertising revenue is not convincing, especially when user rights are at stake. The article could have explored more deeply how alternative business models or less intrusive forms of advertising might address these concerns.

Section E:  
The most relevant principle from Beard and Longstaff’s (2018) Ethical Design Framework is the principle of Respect for Human Rights, particularly the right to privacy. The ATT feature is designed to protect users’ autonomy and dignity by giving them meaningful control over their personal information. This aligns with the framework’s emphasis on technologies that respect and uphold fundamental rights.

Another relevant principle is Net Benefit. Apple’s move can be seen as an attempt to maximize the social good by reducing the risks associated with widespread data tracking, such as manipulation, discrimination, and loss of trust. However, the framework also calls for consideration of unintended consequences. The potential negative impact on small businesses and the digital economy is a factor that should be weighed, as Beard and Longstaff suggest that ethical design requires balancing benefits and harms.

Finally, the principle of Responsibility is important. Apple is taking responsibility for the privacy of its users, but the article raises questions about the responsibilities of other actors, such as app developers and advertisers. The framework helps to clarify that ethical technology design is not just about technical features, but about the broader ecosystem and the obligations of all stakeholders.

Your assignment demonstrates a clear understanding of the article’s content, effectively summarizing Apple’s privacy feature and its societal implications. You appropriately identify the key arguments, sources, and data challenges, notably the tension between user privacy and targeted advertising. Your critical engagement is evident in your agreement with the privacy benefits and recognition of the economic concerns raised by Facebook, supported by relevant research from Pew.

Your application of Beard and Longstaff’s Ethical Design Framework is well-articulated, especially regarding Respect for Human Rights and Responsibility. You thoughtfully connect the principles to the article’s context, highlighting the importance of balancing societal benefits with potential harms.

However, the analysis could be strengthened by deeper critique of the evidence sources—such as questioning the neutrality of corporate statements or exploring the broader societal impacts beyond small businesses. Additionally, integrating more academic or policy research could enhance the critical depth.

Your writing is clear, well-structured, and correctly cited, demonstrating good academic integrity. To improve, consider explicitly discussing how the framework’s principles might guide future technological design decisions or policy responses.

Overall, a solid, insightful response that aligns well with the assignment criteria. Keep developing your critical perspective and deepen engagement with diverse evidence sources for an even more comprehensive analysis.

Introduction  
This report outlines the deliverables for Iteration 2 for our client Jane Smith. The main goal is to enhance the online booking platform to improve user experience and streamline staff operations. The report includes:  
- Overview of users for Iteration 2 (personas)  
- Current iteration overview, including high-level user stories and value for the client  
- Acceptance testing documentation for the client/product owner  
- Links to Trello, client communications, and other artefacts

Overview of Users  
The platform’s primary users are adults aged 25-50, including both frequent travelers and staff managing bookings. The client aims to expand to younger users and improve internal efficiency.

Emily Carter:  
A 29-year-old marketing executive. She travels frequently for both work and leisure. She values quick, mobile-friendly booking and clear information about her reservations. She is tech-savvy and expects a seamless experience.  
Quote: “I want to book my trips in minutes, not hours.”

Liam Turner:  
A 41-year-old father of three, working as a high school teacher. He plans family holidays during school breaks. He prefers websites that are easy to navigate and provide detailed package information.  
Quote: “I need to see all the options for my family at a glance.”

Sophie Lin:  
A 24-year-old university student. She travels with friends and looks for group discounts. She is active on social media and likes to share her experiences online.  
Quote: “I love finding deals and sharing my trips with friends.”

David Kim:  
A 38-year-old customer support staff member. He manages customer queries and booking issues. He wants a dashboard to track and resolve support tickets efficiently.  
Quote: “A clear system helps me help our customers faster.”

Rachel Wong:  
A 33-year-old content manager. She updates website content and promotions. She prefers a simple CMS that doesn’t require technical skills.  
Quote: “I need to update offers quickly without touching code.”

Iteration Overview  
Booking Management  
- Customers can view, edit, and cancel bookings  
- Staff can manage and update booking statuses  
- Booking confirmation emails sent automatically

Payment Integration  
- Customers can pay using credit card, PayPal, or Apple Pay  
- Payment status visible in user dashboard  
- Refunds processed through the platform

Content Management System (CMS)  
- Admin and content managers can add/edit travel packages  
- Promotional banners and offers can be updated without coding  
- Images and descriptions easily managed

Customer Support Dashboard  
- Staff can view and respond to customer support tickets  
- Ticket status (open, pending, resolved) tracked  
- Customers receive notifications when their issue is updated

Social Media Integration  
- Website links to Facebook, Instagram, and Twitter  
- Customers can share bookings on social media

Summer FIT5057 Formative Learning & Assessment
The Concepts behind using Workbooks in Assignments 1 and 2
In many units, critical and reflective thinking and literacy skills are expected and often not explicitly taught. FIT5057 is unique because it also offers students practice drills to develop their critical thinking and literacy skills when preparing for the major assignment 1 and 2 work.
Through these practice drills via Workbooks’ tasks, you will be guided & supported to:
Learn by applying the key PM concepts and techniques you need to write a project plan and understand the practice implications and real-world case studies of theoretical PM concepts.
Learn by applying the techniques that make up research, critical thinking, reading and writing skills, which collectively are your employability skills.
Learn by hands-on experience the real implications of continuous teamplay, time management, issues reporting and collaborative resolution with peers and your tutor.
Reflecting on these practice drills will place you in a better position to understand the relevant PM info you need and identify the skill-based techniques you need to apply to complete all the 3 summative assignments.
From a teaching strategy perspective, the workbooks provide you the formative learning experience, with close support from your peers and tutor. The major assignments provide the summative evidence that you are able to understand and apply the relevant PM concepts and the appropriate PM, research, critical thinking, reading and writing techniques to respond effectively to the given assignments.
If you often skip tutorials and consults, you will expose yourself to ineffective formative learning and be prepared for stressful and painful consequences  when completing the assignments near submission deadlines.
For Assignment 2, your attitude and capacity to work with other people from diverse backgrounds and different abilities can also determine the challenges you may face in groupwork and influence the quality of not just your own, but your peers’ contributions in completing the assignment work. You may also make or break relationships, which may enhance or block your future career developments as working with, relating to people from diverse backgrounds and abilities are important soft skills you need to lead and motivate people when you aim for senior management and leadership career paths.
There are 5 success factors for doing well in assignments:
That you individually learn effectively and always ask questions among each other and your tutor when not sure;
That you regular attend tutorials and consultations, and keep up to date with lecture lessons;
That you engage in effective group collaboration and communications;
That you practice effective time management and have succession plans in case some members become unavailable during the semester.
That you are able to respect each other despite coming from different backgrounds or having different learning abilities; and work positively with everyone regardless of any people issues you encounter.

Before attending tutorial class and doing any workbook tasks, we expect you to firstly view the online lecture resources, to grasp understanding of the concepts and techniques you will need to know when doing tutorial and workbook exercises. Without this pre-class preparation, your learning and our teaching is at high risk of being ineffective.
Each workbook will come with attached readings. You are allowed to reuse the given readings in your assignments as references, if appropriate. While you will learn research methods in this subject, the provided workbook readings are designed to direct you to find other similar, hence relevant resources for  developing the sought answers to workbook questions. Rather than spending hours searching for references, the references are supplied to help students focus more time on learning, understanding and practising applying the relevant PM concepts and techniques needed in the assignment work. If you do not attempt the workbook work effectively or on time, your actions are likely to also affect the quality of your assignment outcomes.

User Requirements Info Provided
They have provided your team with the following baseline requirement info:
Current System Environment (IT News)
First System Solution
Currently, the Salesforce integrated covid-19 contact tracing and testing systems is being rolled out, to replace the first highly manual system that uses an IBM’s i2 platform, an Advanced Analytics and Intelligence Analysis system.  The first solution was a myriad of existing departmental and different vendor systems and manual processes that feed information into the IBM’s i2 platform. It was first thought that integration configuration would provide the contact tracing and testing data processing for the Victorian Government.
The Victorian government stopped using the IBM analytics tool as part of its first digital contact tracing system after its requirements changed and the system could not be reconfigured.
On October 12, 2020, during the inquiry hearings, IBM reported that it became clear to them that “the DHHS [Department of Health and Human Services] data analysis team required a system capable of alerting them to significant events or conditions that should be followed up by an analyst, in contrast to an analyst using the system to discover these conditions or events.” However there is no clarity in the inquiry report of where this misunderstanding might have occurred.
Second System Solution
Assume the following functional overview of the SalesForce’s system solution (The Guardian, Sep 2020;  (Victorian Premier’s Office, 30 Nov 2020):
Is a digitised system covering the whole contact tracing process – from test results to interviews, phone calls, and the management of cases and contacts.
Can consolidate and align many different platforms into one platform significantly reducing pen and paper manual data entry.”
End user teams can be based remotely because they will be able to access the data from anywhere in the state.
Sends text notifications automatically to people when they tested positive.
Fax machines and phone calls are still being used in GP practices where software does not link with department systems.
An IBM artificial intelligence product from the defence department will provide predictive modelling “that can help you pick up patterns that might not be necessarily obvious”.
Feeds Victorian data to the national benchmark data reporting system that shows Victoria performance against national benchmarks in reporting cases being contacted and interviewed within 24 hours, and close contacts being identified within 48 hours.
Provides an online dashboard for Covid cases in Victoria will soon contain information about outbreaks in health workplaces, and the 14-day rolling average will be included in data for metropolitan Melbourne and regional Victoria, given it is the metric used to decide the easing of restrictions.
Instead of adopting a proven and successfully working QR contact tracing app used by the NSW and ACT Governments, a new client installed QR coded contact tracing app is currently being developed and rolled out for businesses to check in patrons’ names and contact phone numbers.
The automated new system would run in parallel to the existing contact tracing efforts of 2,600 workers in the state to ensure a smooth transition. More staff could be added to help boost efforts at a later date.

The contact tracing app for business and individual use-cases is required to operate on Android and Apple phones.
