Any changes to the functional scope implied from the official user documentations would need to formally be reviewed and approved by the State Government client.

System Platform Requirements
Consideration is being looked at for redeveloping the second system solution as a cloud platform solution, which allows the Victorian Government to commercialise and potentially export its third model of integrated covid-19 contact tracing and testing system solution as covid-19 “data centres on the fly”, allowing future revenues to offset the cost of system changes since Covid-19 pandemic started in early 2020.
The Client also mentioned that they intend to use a Robotics Process Automation (RPA) tool, notably the  UiPath app to allow both state and national covid-19 responders (such as covid-19 scientists, public health statisticians and other administrators) to develop and automate their own front-end applications, including specialised reports for more powerful data analytics mining capabilities without the need for programmers. As this is a new work productivity tool, all members of the project team and covid-19 responder-teams in other state and national covid-19 work related departments need to be trained in using this apps development tool.
The Client agrees to adopt the national cybersecurity guidelines provided by the Australian Cybersecurity Centre’s advice, especially the ones regarding Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) actors targeting Australian health sector organisations and COVID-19 essential services . Your team is expected to advise the integration of the appropriate cybersecurity recommendations into your proposed project plan.
These platform expectations mean the new program needs to take a DevOps methodology in defining its cloud platform system solution. The DevOps program will involve a number of projects that innovates this system solution as a range of independently deplorable services.

As natural habitats disappear, it is increasingly difficult to ensure that all species can survive in the
wild. One solution is to use zoos, reserves and other types of organizations that keep wild animals in
captivity as conservation centres for various species. To assist in recording the animals in captivity
and their offspring, a Centre Breeding database is to be created in which all centres will record the
animals which they hold and the breeding events which take place at the centres.
Database designers have considered the requirements and arrived at the following data model:
Page 1 of 8


==================== Page 2 ====================

Model Details:
SPECIES
Records the details of the species, the name of the species, as well as its popular name and the
genus and family to which the species belongs. The relevant details for the Tasmanian Devil, for
instance, are as follows ...
Genus: Sarcophilus
Species: harrisii
Popular name: Tasmanian Devil
Family: DASYURIDAE
It is also important to know the species’ natural range, which is usually given as a description of the
geographic regions and natural habitats where a species is found in the wild.

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
spec_genus | The species genus
spec_name | The species name
spec_popular_name | The species popular name
spec_family | The species family name
spec_natural range | Description of the natural range of the species

ANIMAL
Records the details of all animals held at a particular centre

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
animal_id | The identifier for the animal
animal_sex | The animal sex (M or F)
brevent_id | If bred in captivity (i.e. at a centre), the id of the breeding event which produced the animal. Animals which have been captured from the wild will have no brevent_id assigned
center_id | The "home" center where the animal is normally located. Animals are sometimes located at other centres for a breeding event
spec_genus | The species genus for the animal
spec_name | The species name for the animal

Page 2 of 8


==================== Page 3 ====================

CENTRE
Records the details of all centres that keep animals in captivity (zoos, reserves and other
types of organizations)

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
centre_id | The identifier for the center
centre_name | The centre name
centre_address | The centre address
centre_director | The name of the centres director
centre_phone_no | The centres phone contact number

BREEDING_EVENT
Records the details of breeding events which have taken place in the centres

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
brevent_id | The identifier for the breeding event
brevent_date | The date on which the breeding event took place
mother_id | The animal_id of the animal who was the mother
father_id | The animal_id of the animal who was the father

LLM tutor feedback:
Your submission demonstrates a good understanding of key concepts, but there are areas for improvement:

1. **Sequence Creation**: Correctly created the sequence `animal_breeding_event_sequence` starting at 500, which aligns with the requirements.

2. **Centre Deletion and Animal Transfer**: Your approach to transferring animals from 'SAF30' to 'SAF10' by inserting into `animal` with `centre_id` and then deleting the old centre is conceptually sound. However, the insert statement is incomplete; it only inserts `centre_id` without other necessary attributes like `animal_id`, `animal_sex`, etc. Also, the insert statement's `select` clause is invalid because it only selects a string `'SAF10'` from the `animal` table, which doesn't make sense. You should instead insert new animals with proper attributes, possibly copying existing data or creating new entries.

3. **Adding Wild Animals**: You correctly added wild animals with `brevent_id` as NULL, indicating they are captured from the wild. The data is appropriate.

4. **Breeding Event and Offspring**: You correctly inserted a breeding event and two offspring animals. However, the `brevent_id` for the offspring should be unique and consistent with your sequence, not hardcoded as '1'. Also, the `animal_id` values are hardcoded; for better practice, use the sequence to generate IDs dynamically.

Overall, focus on ensuring your insert statements include all necessary attributes, and avoid hardcoding primary key values. Use sequences for IDs consistently, and double-check the logic for transferring animals between centres.

Your submission demonstrates a solid understanding of both shell commands and R for data analysis. You correctly identified the delimiter as a comma and the total number of columns and rows, and your approach to filtering columns and searching for specific terms is appropriate. The use of `awk`, `grep`, and `cut` commands shows good command-line proficiency.

In R, you effectively read the dataset, extracted year and month, and created clear visualizations for headline trends and emoji counts. Your code for identifying the year with the highest headlines and analyzing emoji trends is well-structured. The addition of the ‘comment_emotion’ column based on emoji counts is a good approach, and your bar chart effectively illustrates positive versus negative comments over time.

Your topic analysis using characteristic terms and the subsequent line chart provides valuable insights into headline content trends. The consistent use of `dplyr` and `ggplot2` functions indicates good familiarity with tidyverse tools.

However, some responses lack detailed justifications or explanations, especially for the interpretation of the plots and findings. Including brief insights or observations directly in your report would strengthen your analysis. Also, ensure that all parts of the assignment are explicitly addressed, such as the justification for identifying invalid headlines or more detailed explanations of trends.

Overall, your work is accurate, well-organized, and demonstrates good technical skills. Adding more interpretative commentary would elevate your submission further.

The Animal Doctors management have indicated:
● they do not require the clinic addresses or any phone numbers to be broken down into
component attributes
● they would like to be able to easily expand:
○ the vet specialisations, as they employ further vets with new specialisations, and
○ the types of animals, as they treat a new type which they have not previously seen
● the services which are provided during a visit are identified by a service code and have a
standard service cost which must be recorded as part of the database. Every visit incurs at
least one service charge. During a visit (treatment of a patient - the animal) the servicing vet
may vary the service standard cost depending on their professional judgement,
● the drugs which are provided during a visit have a standard cost which must be recorded as
part of the database, however like services this cost may be varied by the vet for a particular
visit
● when a drug is used as part of the visit (eg. an injection that the vet gives the patient in the
clinic) the frequency is not recorded (frequency is only used only for drugs given to the owner
to be administered to their pet following the visit eg. a twice daily antibiotic tablet), and
● management would like a count maintained, as part of the stored database data, which
indicates how many times a given specialist vet has serviced a visit in a clinic which is not
their home clinic.
Animal Doctor have provided several forms, depicted below, which they make use of, showing some
of the data they wish to record:
● a sample 'Pet Ownership Details' which shows pets owned by a specific owner, and the
owner’s preferred vet, and
● two samples 'Visit Invoice'.

Task to complete:
1. Perform normalisation to 3NF for the data depicted in the sample AD reports. Note that only
one normalisation is required for the Visit Invoice, you have been provided with two samples
so you can appreciate some of the variety which occurs.
The approach you are required to use is the same approach as shown in the normalisation
tutorial solution. The normalisation must be carried out form by form, beginning by you
representing the document you are working on as a single UNF form and then moving
through 1NF, 2NF and 3NF.
During normalisation, you must:
○ Not add surrogate keys.
○ Include all attributes (you must not remove any attribute as derivable)
○ Clearly show UNF, 1NF, 2NF and 3NF.
○ Clearly identify the Primary Key in all relations.
○ Clearly identify all dependencies at the various normalisation stages (Partial at 1NF,
Transitive at 2NF and Full at 3NF). You should use the same notation as depicted in
the normalisation sample solutions, for example:
attr1 -> attr2, attr3
If none exist you must note this by stating:
No partial dependencies present and/or
No transitive dependencies present
○ If required, carry out attribute synthesis.
The attribute names used in your normalisation and those on your subsequent logical model
must be the same.

Assignment description:
Assignment 2A:   Workbook 2
Reading & Project Planning Practice
 for Assignment 2B
Summer FIT5057 Formative Learning & Assessment
The Concepts behind using Workbooks in Assignments 1 and 2
In many units, critical and reflective thinking and literacy skills are expected and often not explicitly taught. FIT5057 is unique because it also offers students practice drills to develop their critical thinking and literacy skills when preparing for the major assignment 1 and 2 work.
Through these practice drills via Workbooks’ tasks, you will be guided & supported to:
Learn by applying the key PM concepts and techniques you need to write a project plan and understand the practice implications and real-world case studies of theoretical PM concepts.
Learn by applying the techniques that make up research, critical thinking, reading and writing skills, which collectively are your employability skills.
Learn by hands-on experience the real implications of continuous teamplay, time management, issues reporting and collaborative resolution with peers and your tutor.
Reflecting on these practice drills will place you in a better position to understand the relevant PM info you need and identify the skill-based techniques you need to apply to complete all the 3 summative assignments.
From a teaching strategy perspective, the workbooks provide you the formative learning experience, with close support from your peers and tutor. The major assignments provide the summative evidence that you are able to understand and apply the relevant PM concepts and the appropriate PM, research, critical thinking, reading and writing techniques to respond effectively to the given assignments.
If you often skip tutorials and consults, you will expose yourself to ineffective formative learning and be prepared for stressful and painful consequences  when completing the assignments near submission deadlines.
For Assignment 2, your attitude and capacity to work with other people from diverse backgrounds and different abilities can also determine the challenges you may face in groupwork and influence the quality of not just your own, but your peers’ contributions in completing the assignment work. You may also make or break relationships, which may enhance or block your future career developments as working with, relating to people from diverse backgrounds and abilities are important soft skills you need to lead and motivate people when you aim for senior management and leadership career paths.
There are 5 success factors for doing well in assignments:
That you individually learn effectively and always ask questions among each other and your tutor when not sure;
That you regular attend tutorials and consultations, and keep up to date with lecture lessons;
That you engage in effective group collaboration and communications;
That you practice effective time management and have succession plans in case some members become unavailable during the semester.
That you are able to respect each other despite coming from different backgrounds or having different learning abilities; and work positively with everyone regardless of any people issues you encounter.
Instructions for using each workbook:
Pre-Class Readings
Before attending tutorial class and doing any workbook tasks, we expect you to firstly view the online lecture resources, to grasp understanding of the concepts and techniques you will need to know when doing tutorial and workbook exercises. Without this pre-class preparation, your learning and our teaching is at high risk of being ineffective.
Each workbook will come with attached readings. You are allowed to reuse the given readings in your assignments as references, if appropriate. While you will learn research methods in this subject, the provided workbook readings are designed to direct you to find other similar, hence relevant resources for  developing the sought answers to workbook questions. Rather than spending hours searching for references, the references are supplied to help students focus more time on learning, understanding and practising applying the relevant PM concepts and techniques needed in the assignment work. If you do not attempt the workbook work effectively or on time, your actions are likely to also affect the quality of your assignment outcomes.

Current System Environment (IT News)
First System Solution
Currently, the Salesforce integrated covid-19 contact tracing and testing systems is being rolled out, to replace the first highly manual system that uses an IBM’s i2 platform, an Advanced Analytics and Intelligence Analysis system.  The first solution was a myriad of existing departmental and different vendor systems and manual processes that feed information into the IBM’s i2 platform. It was first thought that integration configuration would provide the contact tracing and testing data processing for the Victorian Government.
The Victorian government stopped using the IBM analytics tool as part of its first digital contact tracing system after its requirements changed and the system could not be reconfigured.
On October 12, 2020, during the inquiry hearings, IBM reported that it became clear to them that “the DHHS [Department of Health and Human Services] data analysis team required a system capable of alerting them to significant events or conditions that should be followed up by an analyst, in contrast to an analyst using the system to discover these conditions or events.” However there is no clarity in the inquiry report of where this misunderstanding might have occurred.
Second System Solution
Assume the following functional overview of the SalesForce’s system solution (The Guardian, Sep 2020;  (Victorian Premier’s Office, 30 Nov 2020):
Is a digitised system covering the whole contact tracing process – from test results to interviews, phone calls, and the management of cases and contacts.
Can consolidate and align many different platforms into one platform significantly reducing pen and paper manual data entry.”
End user teams can be based remotely because they will be able to access the data from anywhere in the state.
Sends text notifications automatically to people when they tested positive.
Fax machines and phone calls are still being used in GP practices where software does not link with department systems.
An IBM artificial intelligence product from the defence department will provide predictive modelling “that can help you pick up patterns that might not be necessarily obvious”.
Feeds Victorian data to the national benchmark data reporting system that shows Victoria performance against national benchmarks in reporting cases being contacted and interviewed within 24 hours, and close contacts being identified within 48 hours.
Provides an online dashboard for Covid cases in Victoria will soon contain information about outbreaks in health workplaces, and the 14-day rolling average will be included in data for metropolitan Melbourne and regional Victoria, given it is the metric used to decide the easing of restrictions.
Instead of adopting a proven and successfully working QR contact tracing app used by the NSW and ACT Governments, a new client installed QR coded contact tracing app is currently being developed and rolled out for businesses to check in patrons’ names and contact phone numbers.
The automated new system would run in parallel to the existing contact tracing efforts of 2,600 workers in the state to ensure a smooth transition. More staff could be added to help boost efforts at a later date.

Question 1  
A stakeholder is any individual, group, or organization that may affect, be affected by, or perceive itself to be affected by a decision, activity, or outcome of a project (PMBOK, 2017). Stakeholders can have varying levels of influence and interest in the project.

Question 2  
The stakeholder management process in PMBOK includes four main steps. First, identify stakeholders to determine their interests, involvement, and potential impact on the project (PMBOK, 2017). Second, plan stakeholder engagement by developing strategies to effectively involve stakeholders based on their needs and expectations (PMBOK, 2017). Third, manage stakeholder engagement, which involves communicating and working with stakeholders to meet their needs and address issues (PMBOK, 2017). Finally, monitor stakeholder engagement by tracking relationships and adjusting strategies as needed (PMBOK, 2017).

Question 3  
Identifying project stakeholders is important because it helps the project team understand who will be impacted by the project, what their interests and expectations are, and how they can influence the project (PMBOK, 2017). This process ensures that the team can plan appropriate engagement and communication strategies.

A PMBOK knowledge area is a defined aspect of project management described by its knowledge requirements, processes, and practices (PMBOK, 2017). There are 10 knowledge areas:  
1. Project Integration Management  
2. Project Scope Management  
3. Project Schedule Management  
4. Project Cost Management  
5. Project Quality Management  
6. Project Resource Management  
7. Project Communications Management  
8. Project Risk Management  
9. Project Procurement Management  
10. Project Stakeholder Management  

Question 10  
Enterprise Architecture (EA) defines the structure and operation of an organization, guiding project goals and product planning. In Scrum, the product backlog lists all work items needed for the project. EA helps prioritize backlog items based on business goals and strategy, ensuring that the most valuable products are developed first. This alignment ensures that the Scrum team delivers products that support the organization’s objectives.

Appendix A MADA Graduate Research Team System
Preliminary System Description
The MADA Graduate Research Team has been looking after PhD students for
many years. Charli is the manager of the team and has provided the following
preliminary information about the system s/he would like you to build for the
team. Charli has provided the following preliminary system description, and sis
looking forward to meeting your team.
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
The MADA Graduate Research Team look after all the PhD students in our
Faculty. We currently have 78 PhD students. Our main goal is to support our
PhD students so that they successfully complete their PhDs.
A PhD student is either full-time (up to 3 years) or a part-time (up to 6 years),
and is allocated one or 2 supervisors to assist them during their PhD.
Throughout this time, regular and thorough reviews of progress help keep
candidates and supervisors on track. To assist with this review process formal
progress meetings called milestones are compulsory for all PhD students.
There are three milestones at set times during a student’s PhD:
• Confirmation
• Progress Review (Mid Candidature Review)
• Final Review (Pre Submission Seminar)
These milestone meetings are with the PhD candidate, the supervisor and a
panel of 3 expert academics. We currently have a manual system and a few
different spreadsheets to manage this process. It is chaotic and very time
consuming and it is very difficult to manage. At busy times milestone meetings
are delayed and we often have no idea of where we are in the process. There
are lots of issues with staff and candidates not being notified about the
milestone meetings in a timely way, and the information required to check
progress is all over the place – often in the email inbox of staff.
We want to be able to automate and streamline the process as much as
possible so that the whole milestone meeting process from start to finish is
very smooth, including when a candidate does not meet the requirements and
we have to organise another milestone meeting. There are so many aspects –
organising the meeting date, time, location and confirming availability of all
attendees, selecting suitable panel members, making sure all the admin
aspects of the meeting (catering, tech set up, etc.) are done, sending emails
and reminders and managing cancellations to name just a few.

FIT2001 – Assignment 1: Requirements Gathering
We want all the documentation and feedback associated with a milestone
meeting to be in one spot online. Students should be able to upload all the
required documentation for a milestone meeting, supervisors should be able to
add any comments, and panel members should also be able to add comments
and their final decision as to the meeting outcome. All the stakeholders should
have access to the system for what they need to do with it. We also really want
help with managing panel members suggestions from the supervisor and the
student and the selection of suitable panel members.
We don’t currently have any overall reporting or analysis of the progress of our
PhD candidates across our Faculty, but we would really like this feature in
future. It would also be interesting to see this progress breakdown by
Supervisor, to see if there are issues with particular supervisors.

Questions Prepared for Interview:
- Can you describe the main challenges you face with the current manual system for managing PhD milestones?

- What is your preferred method for notifying staff and students about meetings? Would you like options beyond email, such as SMS?
- When should supervisors and panel members be able to add comments or upload documents—before, during, or after meetings?
- Are there any restrictions or preferences for how panel members are selected for each milestone meeting?
- What kind of reporting or analytics would be most useful for you and your team?
- Are there any privacy or access control concerns for different types of users (students, supervisors, panel members, admin staff)?
- Would you like the system to handle logistics such as room bookings, catering, and technical setup?

Notes Summary from Interview:
- Automated email reminders for milestone meetings are a high priority.
- Students should be able to propose preferred meeting times, with supervisors confirming availability.
- The system should automate matching panel members to students, using area of expertise and ensuring no panel member exceeds 12 meetings per year.
- Students, supervisors, and panel members should have role-based access to upload documents and add comments.
- All documentation and feedback for each milestone should be stored in one place online.
- The system should manage meeting logistics (time, location, catering, tech setup).
- Panel members can approve or reject candidates, with feedback.
- If a milestone is unsatisfactory, the system should notify the student immediately and help arrange a follow-up meeting.
- Supervisors can suggest panel members.
- Reporting on student progress and supervisor performance is a desired future feature.

Email Correspondence with Client:
Three emails were exchanged: the agenda, a reminder, and a summary. The agenda and reminder were sent before the interview to outline the meeting topics and confirm details. The summary was sent after the interview to recap key points and allow the client to clarify any misunderstandings.

Stakeholder Analysis

Manager (Charli) – We placed Charli in the High power/High interest quadrant. As the manager and project sponsor, Charli is responsible for the success of the system and is highly invested in its outcome. Regular updates and involvement in key decisions are essential.

PhD Candidates – PhD candidates are in the High interest/Low power quadrant. The system is designed to support them, so their interest is high, but they have little influence over the system’s design or implementation. They should be kept informed and given opportunities for feedback.

Supervisors – Supervisors are also in the High interest/Low power quadrant. They play a key role in managing students and participating in milestone meetings, but do not have decision-making power over the system. They should be kept updated and consulted as needed.

Expert Academics (Panel Members) – We placed expert academics in the High power/High interest quadrant. They have significant influence over milestone outcomes and are directly involved in the process. Their feedback and engagement are important for the system’s success.

==================== Page 6 ====================

Administrative Staff – We placed admin staff in the Low power/High interest quadrant. They are responsible for organising logistics and supporting the process, so they have a strong interest in a streamlined system, but limited power over decisions.

IT Support – IT support staff are in the Low power/Low interest quadrant. They may be involved in technical setup and maintenance, but have minimal influence or direct interest in the system’s day-to-day use.

Assumptions
- We assumed that all users will have access to the internet and basic digital literacy.
- We assumed that the number of PhD students will remain relatively stable.
- We assumed that all milestone meetings will continue to follow the current three-stage structure.

LLM tutor feedback:
This is a solid and well-structured requirements gathering response. The interview questions are relevant, comprehensive, and demonstrate an understanding of the system’s core functionalities, such as automation, notifications, document management, and stakeholder roles. Including the notes summary and email correspondence shows good evidence of communication and planning.

Your stakeholder analysis is clear and aligns well with the system’s context. Placing Charli, students, supervisors, and academics in high-interest quadrants reflects their importance, while correctly identifying admin staff and IT support’s lower influence demonstrates good stakeholder understanding. Your reasoning for placement is concise and appropriate.

However, consider explicitly linking your stakeholder analysis to the power/interest matrix with a brief explanation for each placement, which would strengthen your analysis. Also, while the interview questions are relevant, adding questions about privacy/security concerns or future scalability could enhance completeness.

Overall, your submission demonstrates good grasp of requirements gathering and stakeholder analysis principles. Keep focusing on explicitly justifying your stakeholder placements and expanding on potential future system features or constraints. Well done!

LLM tutor feedback:
Your normalization process demonstrates a good understanding of the steps from UNF to 3NF, correctly identifying dependencies and dependencies types. You appropriately separated entities such as OWNER, VET, ANIMAL, and related visit and service tables, which aligns with best practices. Notably, you identified the transitive dependency vet_id → vet_name and addressed it by creating a separate VET table, which is correct.

However, some improvements are needed. In the Pet Ownership document, the attribute vet_name is stored in the OWNER table, which violates normalization principles; vet_name should be stored only in the VET table, with OWNER referencing vet_id. Similarly, in the Visit Invoice, storing vet_name directly in the INVOICE table is redundant; it should be stored only in the VET table, with references from the visit record. Also, the partial dependency of owner_id → owner_fname, owner_lname, owner_address is correctly identified, but the final 3NF model should explicitly show that owner_id is the primary key for OWNER, and similarly for other entities.

Your attribute naming is consistent, but ensure that attribute prefixes (like owner_, animal_, vet_) are used throughout for clarity, especially in the logical model. Also, explicitly marking primary keys (PK) and foreign keys (FK) in your diagrams and documentation will improve clarity.

Overall, your approach is solid, but pay closer attention to avoiding redundancy and ensuring that all attributes are stored in the correct entities, with dependencies properly represented.

Objective:
The objective of this assignment is to gain familiarity with classification models using R.
You will be using a modified version of the Kaggle competition data: Predict next-day rain in
Australia. https://www.kaggle.com/jsphyg/weather-dataset-rattle-package, but predicting
whether or not the following day will be cloudy. The data contains a number of
meteorological observations as attributes, and the class attribute “CloudTomorrow”. Details
of the decision attributes follow the assignment description.
You are expected to use R for your analysis, and may use any R package. Clear your
workspace, set the number of significant digits to a sensible value, and use ‘WAUS’ as the
default data frame name for the whole data set. Read your data into R using the following
code:
rm(list = ls())
WAUS <- read.csv("CloudPredict2021.csv")
L <- as.data.frame(c(1:49))
set.seed(88888888) # Your Student ID is the random seed
L <- L[sample(nrow(L), 10, replace = FALSE),] # sample 10 locations
WAUS <- WAUS[(WAUS$Location %in% L),]
WAUS <- WAUS[sample(nrow(WAUS), 2000, replace = FALSE),] # sample 2000 rows
We want to obtain a model that may be used to predict whether it is going to be cloudy
tomorrow for 10 locations in Australia.

Summary  
The WAUS dataset initially contained 10,000 rows across 49 locations. After applying the assignment’s sampling procedure (using the student ID as the random seed), 2,000 rows were selected from 10 random locations. Following data exploration and pre-processing, the dataset was reduced to 1,462 rows after removing columns with excessive missing values and omitting rows with NAs. This cleaned dataset was then split into a 70% training set (1,023 rows) and a 30% test set (439 rows).

Q1  
Figure 1.1  
The proportion of cloudy days to clear days in my dataset is 0.287, meaning 28.7% of the days are classified as cloudy. This was calculated by counting the number of rows where CloudTomorrow == 1 and dividing by the total number of rows (2,000). Using the describe() function from the psych package, I obtained summary statistics for all predictor variables.

The dataset spans from 2008 to 2019, with a concentration of records in 2013. The temperature ranges from -5.8°C to 45.2°C. Rainfall varies from 0 mm to 72.4 mm, indicating the presence of outliers. Both Humidity9am and Humidity3pm have high standard deviations, suggesting a wide spread of humidity values. The Evaporation and Sunshine columns have very low counts of non-NA values (n), with more than half of their entries missing.

1

==================== Page 2 ====================

Based on the summary statistics, I decided to omit the Evaporation and Sunshine columns from further analysis due to their high proportion of missing values (over 1,000 NAs each). Removing these columns first, then omitting rows with any remaining NAs, resulted in a dataset of 1,462 records, which is a reasonable size for analysis. If I had instead removed all rows with NAs while keeping these columns, the dataset would have been reduced to fewer than 600 records, which is too small.

Q2  
The pre-processing steps were as follows:  
- Remove the Evaporation and Sunshine columns from the WAUS dataframe.  
- Omit all rows with any remaining NA values.  
- Convert the Location column to a factor, as it is categorical and should not be treated as numeric by tree-based models.  
- Convert CloudTomorrow to a factor for classification.

The best values for each metric are highlighted. For accuracy, sensitivity, and AUC, higher is better; for false alarm, lower is better. Sensitivity and false alarm were calculated from the confusion matrices using the formulas:  
Sensitivity = True Positive / (True Positive + False Negative)  
False Alarm = False Positive / (True Negative + False Positive)

The Random Forest classifier has the highest AUC and lowest false alarm rate, and the highest accuracy. However, its sensitivity is lower than the Decision Tree. The Decision Tree has the highest sensitivity but a higher false alarm rate. Overall, Random Forest is the best single classifier.

calculate_accuracy = function(confusion_matrix) {
  confusion_matrix = as.data.frame(confusion_matrix)
  values = confusion_matrix$Freq
  accuracy = (values[1] + values[4]) / sum(values)
  return(round(accuracy, 4))
}
calculate_sensitivity = function(confusion_matrix) {
  confusion_matrix = as.data.frame(confusion_matrix)
  values = confusion_matrix$Freq
  sensitivity = values[4] / (values[2] + values[4])
  return(round(sensitivity, 4))
}
calculate_false_alarm = function(confusion_matrix) {
  confusion_matrix = as.data.frame(confusion_matrix)
  values = confusion_matrix$Freq
  false_alarm = values[3] / (values[1] + values[3])
  return(round(false_alarm, 4))
}

Expectation of content
Hints for writing a business report versus an essay
• Business reports and academic essay are very different. Their objectives, format and
the expectation of the reader are different.
• Business reports focus on the practical application of the theory - providing insights
and recommendations rather than explaining the theory itself. They are concise, very
practical, usually worded in the first person (I / we recommend… etc) and focused on
identifying and solving a problem rather than discussing the theories behind the
problem / recommendations.
Hints for Structuring Your Report
• Title page
• Exec summary
• Table of contents
• Introduction and purpose of report
• Insights and discussion
• Recommendations
• Conclusion
For this business report, we would expect no more than 7 to 10 pages:
• 3-4 pages (title page, exec. summary, contents, introduction and purpose)
• 4-6 pages main body with conclusions and recommendations.
• You may have references (but remember these are not usual in business reports).
Hints for Writing Your Report
• Remember the audience and purpose of a business report. The report should be
focused on the problem, insights and recommendations on how to address the problem.
The reader should be able to walk away with practical and tangible actions they can
implement.
• The theories discussed in Professional Practice should be used to understand and
develop the key issues and recommendations however the theories themselves should
not be specifically raised in the business report.


==================== Page 4 ====================

• The executive summary should act as a stand alone document that can be read
separately from the report. A good executive summary assumes the reader will not read
further and therefore includes a succinct summary of the key points and
recommendations.
• The report should clearly include the purpose of the report (either in the introduction or
in a separate section.
• Don’t gloss over the recommendations. It should be clear to the reader why they are
important and what tangible actions the reader should take to implement them.
• Put recommendations in a separate section so they don’t get “lost” in the report.
• Keep the business report very succinct, short and punchy. Use lots of bullet points and
sub headings to link ideas.
• Remember your audience: in this case, a senior executive in your company, not your
Monash tutor. Ensure you keep this is mind when deciding the content and level of
detail in your report:
• What would a senior executive want to achieve from reading your report (insights into
the problem and recommendations on how to address it, for example);
• What is their existing knowledge of the topic (it’s their company); and
• What is the appropriate level of detail given what they want to do with the report (high
level insights with recommendations that are sufficiently explained so they can
readily implement them rather than technical or extensive background details).

With the expansion of our company into new international markets, the formation of global multidisciplinary teams is now a strategic priority. The integration of teams from Australia, India, China, and the US presents both opportunities and challenges.  
The main purposes of this report are:  
 To provide insights into the key characteristics of high-performing teams.  
 To identify potential challenges that may impact the performance of new global teams.  
 To recommend practical actions for management to address these challenges.  
Building high-performing global teams will give the company a competitive advantage. The focus of this report is to equip senior management with the necessary understanding and recommendations to ensure the success of these teams.  
2 Insights and discussion  
2.1 Key characteristics of a high-performing team  
Based on my experience and industry best practices, high-performing teams share several common characteristics:  
1. Clear objectives and shared vision. Every team member understands the team’s goals and is committed to achieving them. Alignment on purpose is essential for motivation and direction.  
2. Open and transparent communication. Team members are encouraged to share ideas, feedback, and concerns openly. This reduces misunderstandings and builds trust.  
- 4 -  


==================== Page 5 ====================

3. Effective leadership and defined roles. A strong leader provides direction, supports team members, and ensures that responsibilities are clearly assigned. Each member knows their role and how it contributes to the team’s success.  
4. Mutual trust and respect. High-performing teams are built on trust. Members respect each other’s expertise and perspectives, which fosters collaboration and innovation.  
5. Adaptability and continuous improvement. The best teams are flexible and willing to adapt to changing circumstances. They regularly review their processes and seek ways to improve.  
6. Supportive team culture. A positive environment where members feel valued and supported leads to higher engagement and better results.  
7. Recognition and empowerment. Team members are empowered to make decisions and are recognized for their contributions, which increases motivation and ownership.  
While these characteristics are well-known, implementing them in a global context requires special attention to the unique challenges of distributed and multicultural teams.  
- 5 -  


==================== Page 6 ====================

2.2 Potential challenges  
Forming global multidisciplinary teams introduces several challenges that can impact performance:  
1. Language barriers. Differences in language proficiency and communication styles can lead to misunderstandings and slow down collaboration.  
2. Cultural differences. Team members from different countries may have varying approaches to hierarchy, decision-making, and conflict resolution. Without awareness, these differences can cause friction.  
3. Time zone differences. Coordinating meetings and project timelines across multiple time zones can reduce efficiency and delay decision-making.  
4. Varying attitudes towards authority. Some cultures prefer clear hierarchies, while others value flat structures. Misalignment can lead to confusion and reduced morale.  
5. Compliance with local laws and regulations. Operating in multiple countries requires understanding and adhering to different legal requirements, especially regarding data privacy and employment laws.  
6. Technology and collaboration tools. Inconsistent use of tools or lack of training can hinder effective teamwork.  
Recognizing these challenges is the first step towards building effective global teams.  
- 6 -  


==================== Page 7 ====================

3 Recommendations  
To address the challenges and build high-performing global teams, I recommend the following actions:  
1. Implement comprehensive onboarding and cross-cultural training. Provide training on language skills, cultural awareness, and legal compliance to help team members adapt quickly and work effectively together.  
2. Foster a strong sense of team identity and belonging. Encourage regular team-building activities, both virtual and in-person where possible, to build trust and connection among members.  
3. Promote clear communication standards and efficient collaboration tools. Establish guidelines for meetings, documentation, and feedback. Invest in reliable communication platforms and ensure all team members are trained to use them.  
4. Leverage local expertise and external partners. Work with local HR specialists and external consultants to ensure compliance with local regulations and to provide support for remote employees.  
By taking these steps, the company can overcome the main obstacles to global team performance and accelerate the integration process.  
- 7 -  


==================== Page 8 ====================

4 Conclusion  
Global multidisciplinary teams are essential for the company’s continued growth and competitiveness. By understanding the key characteristics of high-performing teams, anticipating the challenges of global collaboration, and implementing targeted recommendations, senior management can ensure that new teams are set up for success. With the right support and strategies, our global teams will deliver strong results and drive the company forward.

As natural habitats disappear, it is increasingly difficult to ensure that all species can survive in the
wild. One solution is to use zoos, reserves and other types of organizations that keep wild animals in
captivity as conservation centres for various species. To assist in recording the animals in captivity
and their offspring, a Centre Breeding database is to be created in which all centres will record the
animals which they hold and the breeding events which take place at the centres.
Database designers have considered the requirements and arrived at the following data model:

SPECIES
Records the details of the species, the name of the species, as well as its popular name and the
genus and family to which the species belongs. The relevant details for the Tasmanian Devil, for
instance, are as follows ...
Genus: Sarcophilus
Species: harrisii
Popular name: Tasmanian Devil
Family: DASYURIDAE
It is also important to know the species’ natural range, which is usually given as a description of the
geographic regions and natural habitats where a species is found in the wild.

FIT2094 Introduction to Databases 2021 Semester 1 Assignment 1A - Conceptual Model - Animal Doctors (AD) Learning Outcomes: 2, 3 (see Unit Preview) Assignment weighting 5% Assignment marked out of 100 and released as a grade out of 5 Your task for this assignment is to design a database which can be used to support the activities of a veterinary practice called Animal Doctors. Animal Doctors has a number of clinics distributed across the suburbs. For each clinic, they record the clinic's id (which is used to identify the clinic), the clinic's name, its address and the clinic's contact phone number. The practice has a number of veterinary surgeons (vets) who work in these clinics, a vet is assigned to one clinic as their home (or base) clinic. To be able to function, a clinic must have at least one vet assigned to it as the vet’s base clinic. Some of the Animal Doctors vets are specialists in areas such as oncology, cardiology, dermatology etc. These specialist vets, as well as having a home clinic (their base clinic) where they accept general visits, also rove around all clinics when their specialist skills are required. The details Animal Doctors record about a vet are their vet id (used to identify a vet), the vet's given name and family name, the vet's contact phone number and the date they were first employed by Animal Doctors. If the vet is a specialist vet then their area of specialisation is recorded (specialist vets only specialise in one particular area). Pet owners, who are each assigned a unique owner id, have their given name and family name recorded by the practice. The practice also records the owner's contact phone number. Each owner may have one or more pets; for each pet the practice records a unique animal id, the animal's name, the year the animal was born and the type of animal they are, for example a cat. Each animal is only recorded as being owned by one owner. When an animal needs veterinary attention (such as annual injections) or is ill, the owner brings their pet to one of the practice's clinics to be attended to by a vet - this is called a visit. The date and time of the visit, the clinic being visited and the attending vet are recorded (this attending vet may be one based at this particular clinic, i.e. this is their home clinic, or the visit may need the services of a roving specialist vet). In addition, the Page 1 of 5

// The following is converted from a table
practice records the length of the visit and any notes that the vet needs to make about the condition of the pet. Some visits require further follow up visits, the system needs to record, for these subsequent visits, which visit generated the follow up. A given visit may require multiple follow up visits to address an issue identified in the initial diagnosis. For example the pet may have an infected wound from a fight. The first visit for this issue results in antibiotics being given and the wound being stitched. Follow up visits may be required for example to remove the stitches, check the wound healing, provide further antibiotic injections etc. During a visit the attending vet may need to prescribe drugs for the animal. The practice identifies a drug by a unique drug id, and also records the drug's name and the drugs usage instructions (for example "1 ml/3Kg of body weight given once or twice a day"). When a drug is prescribed during a visit, the actual drug dose and frequency of administration are recorded. FIT3171 Introduction to Databases 2021 Semester 1 Assignment 1A - Conceptual Model - Animal Doctors (AD) Learning Outcomes: 2, 3, 4 (see Unit Preview) Assignment weighting 5% Assignment marked out of 100 and released as a grade out of 5 Your task for this assignment is to design a database which can be used to support the activities of a veterinary practice called Animal Doctors. Animal Doctors has a number of clinics distributed across the suburbs. For each clinic, they record the clinic's id (which is used to identify the clinic), the clinic's name, its address and the clinic's contact phone number. The practice has a number of veterinary surgeons (vets) who work in these clinics, a vet is assigned to one clinic as their home (or base) clinic. To be able to function, a clinic must have at least one vet assigned to it as the vet’s base clinic. Some of the Animal Doctors vets are specialists in areas such as oncology, cardiology, dermatology etc. These specialist vets, as well as having a home clinic (their base clinic) where they accept general visits, also rove around all clinics when their specialist skills are required. The details Animal Doctors record about a vet are their vet id (used to identify a vet), the vet's given name and family name, the vet's contact phone number and the date they were first employed by Animal Doctors. If the vet is a specialist vet then their area of specialisation is recorded (specialist vets only specialise in one particular area). Pet owners, who are each assigned a unique owner id, have their given name and family name recorded by the practice. The practice also records the owner's contact phone number. Each owner may have one or more pets; for each pet the practice records a unique animal id, the animal's name, the year the animal was born and the type of animal they are, for example a cat. Each animal is only recorded as being owned by one owner. When an animal needs veterinary attention (such as annual injections) or is ill, the owner brings their pet to one of the practice's clinics to be attended to by a vet - this is called a visit. The date and time of the visit, the clinic being visited and the attending vet are recorded (this attending vet may be one based at this particular clinic, i.e. this is their home clinic, or the visit may need the services of a roving specialist vet). In addition, the Page 1 of 5

practice records the length of the visit and any notes that the vet needs to make about the condition of the pet. Some visits require further follow up visits, the system needs to record, for these subsequent visits, which visit generated the follow up. A given visit may require multiple follow up visits to address an issue identified in the initial diagnosis. For example the pet may have an infected wound from a fight. The first visit for this issue results in antibiotics being given and the wound being stitched. Follow up visits may be required for example to remove the stitches, check the wound healing, provide further antibiotic injections etc. During a visit the attending vet may need to prescribe drugs for the animal. The practice identifies a drug by a unique drug id, and also records the drug's name and the drugs usage instructions (for example "1 ml/3Kg of body weight given once or twice a day"). When a drug is prescribed during a visit, the actual drug dose and frequency of administration are recorded. FIT9132 Introduction to Databases
2021 Semester 1
Assignment 2A - SQL - Centre Breeding (CB)
Assignment weighting 10% - Lecturer in Charge: Minh Le
As natural habitats disappear, it is increasingly difficult to ensure that all species can survive
in the wild. One solution is to use zoos, reserves and other types of organizations that keep
wild animals in captivity as conservation centres for various species. To assist in recording
the animals in captivity and their offspring, a Centre Breeding database is to be created in
which all centres will record the animals which they hold and the breeding events which take
place at the centres.
Database designers have considered the requirements and arrived at the following data
model:
Page 1 of 8

Model Details:
SPECIES
Records the details of the species, the name of the species, as well as its popular name and the
genus and family to which the species belongs. The relevant details for the Tasmanian Devil, for
instance, are as follows ...
Genus: Sarcophilus
Species: harrisii
Popular name: Tasmanian Devil
Family: DASYURIDAE
It is also important to know the species’ natural range, which is usually given as a description of the
geographic regions and natural habitats where a species is found in the wild.

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
spec_genus | The species genus
spec_name | The species name
spec_popular_name | The species popular name
spec_family | The species family name
spec_natural range | Description of the natural range of the species

ANIMAL
Records the details of all animals held at a particular centre

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
animal_id | The identifier for the animal
animal_sex | The animal sex (M or F)
brevent_id | If bred in captivity (i.e. at a centre), the id of the breeding event which produced the animal. Animals which have been captured from the wild will have no brevent_id assigned
center_id | The "home" center where the animal is normally located. Animals are sometimes located at other centres for a breeding event
spec_genus | The species genus for the animal
spec_name | The species name for the animal

Page 2 of 8

CENTRE
Records the details of all centres that keep animals in captivity (zoos, reserves and other
types of organizations)

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
centre_id | The identifier for the center
centre_name | The centre name
centre_address | The centre address
centre_director | The name of the centres director
centre_phone_no | The centres phone contact number

BREEDING_EVENT
Records the details of breeding events which have taken place in the centres

// The following is converted from a table
Attribute | Explanation
brevent_id | The identifier for the breeding event
brevent_date | The date on which the breeding event took place
mother_id | The animal_id of the animal who was the mother
father_id | The animal_id of the animal who was the father

CRITERIA FOR MARKING
Submissions will be graded on:
● the correct application of relational database principles,
● the correct handling of transactions and the setting of appropriate transaction
boundaries i.e. correct placement of commits, and
● the correct application of SQL statements and constructs to:
○ populate tables,
○ modify existing data in tables, and
○ modify the "live" database structure to meet the expressed requirements

file, create an SQL schema file which can be used to create this database in

-- Center Breeding SCHEMA
-- DROP TABLES 
DROP TABLE animal CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

DROP TABLE breeding_event CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

DROP TABLE centre CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

DROP TABLE species CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

-- CREATE TABLES
CREATE TABLE animal (
    animal_id   NUMBER(6) NOT NULL,
    animal_sex  CHAR(1) NOT NULL,
    brevent_id  NUMBER(6),
    centre_id   CHAR(6) NOT NULL,
    spec_genus  VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL,
    spec_name   VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL
);

ALTER TABLE animal
    ADD CONSTRAINT animal_sex_check CHECK ( animal_sex IN ( 'F', 'M' ) );

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.animal_id IS
    'Unique ID for this animal ';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.animal_sex IS
    'Sex of animal (M or F)';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.brevent_id IS
    'Unique identifier for breeding event';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.centre_id IS
    'Unique identifier for Centre';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.spec_genus IS
    'Species genus';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.spec_name IS
    'Species Name';

ALTER TABLE animal ADD CONSTRAINT animal_pk PRIMARY KEY ( animal_id );

CREATE TABLE breeding_event (
    brevent_id    NUMBER(6) NOT NULL,
    brevent_date  DATE NOT NULL,
    mother_id     NUMBER(6) NOT NULL,
    father_id     NUMBER(6) NOT NULL
);

COMMENT ON COLUMN breeding_event.brevent_id IS
    'Unique identifier for breeding event';

COMMENT ON COLUMN breeding_event.brevent_date IS
    'Date breeding event took place';

COMMENT ON COLUMN breeding_event.mother_id IS
    'Unique ID for this animal ';

COMMENT ON COLUMN breeding_event.father_id IS
    'Unique ID for this animal ';

ALTER TABLE breeding_event ADD CONSTRAINT breeding_event_pk PRIMARY KEY ( brevent_id );

CREATE TABLE centre (
    centre_id        CHAR(6) NOT NULL,
    centre_name      VARCHAR2(40) NOT NULL,
    centre_address   VARCHAR2(100) NOT NULL,
    centre_director  VARCHAR2(30) NOT NULL,
    centre_phone_no  VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL
);

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_id IS
    'Unique identifier for Centre';

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_name IS
    'Name of Centre';

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_address IS
    'Centre address';

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_director IS
    'Name of director of centre';

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_phone_no IS
    'Centre phone contact number';

ALTER TABLE centre ADD CONSTRAINT centre_pk PRIMARY KEY ( centre_id );

ALTER TABLE centre ADD CONSTRAINT centre_name_unq UNIQUE ( centre_name );

CREATE TABLE species (
    spec_genus          VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL,
    spec_name           VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL,
    spec_popular_name   VARCHAR2(40) NOT NULL,
    spec_family         VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL,
    spec_natural_range  VARCHAR2(100) NOT NULL
);

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_genus IS
    'Species genus';

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_name IS
    'Species Name';

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_popular_name IS
    'Species popular name';

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_family IS
    'Species family';

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_natural_range IS
    'Description of natural range of species';

ALTER TABLE species ADD CONSTRAINT species_pk PRIMARY KEY ( spec_genus,
                                                            spec_name );

ALTER TABLE species ADD CONSTRAINT spec_pop_name_unq UNIQUE ( spec_popular_name );

ALTER TABLE breeding_event
    ADD CONSTRAINT animalfather_be FOREIGN KEY ( father_id )
        REFERENCES animal ( animal_id );

ALTER TABLE breeding_event
    ADD CONSTRAINT animalmother_be FOREIGN KEY ( mother_id )
        REFERENCES animal ( animal_id );

ALTER TABLE animal
    ADD CONSTRAINT be_animal FOREIGN KEY ( brevent_id )
        REFERENCES breeding_event ( brevent_id );

ALTER TABLE animal
    ADD CONSTRAINT centre_animal FOREIGN KEY ( centre_id )
        REFERENCES centre ( centre_id );

ALTER TABLE animal
    ADD CONSTRAINT species_animal FOREIGN KEY ( spec_genus,
                                                   spec_name )
        REFERENCES species ( spec_genus,
                             spec_name );

-- (i)
-- drop sequence commands not required
drop sequence animal_seq;
drop sequence breedevent_seq;

--  increment by 1 not required, it is the default, should not place upper limit
create sequence animal_seq start with 500 increment by 1;

create sequence breedevent_seq start with 500 increment by 1;

-- (ii)

UPDATE animal
SET
    centre_id = (
        SELECT
            centre_id
        FROM
            centre
        WHERE
            centre_name = 'Kruger National Park'
    )
WHERE
    centre_id = (
        SELECT
            centre_id
        FROM
            centre
        WHERE
            centre_name = 'SanWild Wildlife Sanctuary'
    );

DELETE FROM centre
WHERE
    centre_name = 'SanWild Wildlife Sanctuary';

COMMIT;

-- two separate commits required the inserts are not logically related

    'F',

        WHERE

    (

);

Cannot delete due to FK rules, Some animals have been involved in a breeding

***** Test will fail many cases due to the hard coded PK - this is fine ******

*/

ALTER TABLE animal ADD (
    animal_living CHAR(1) DEFAULT 'Y' NOT NULL,
    CONSTRAINT chk_animal_living CHECK ( animal_living IN ( 'Y', 'N' ) )
);

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.animal_living IS
    'Flag for animal alive or not (Y or N)';

UPDATE animal
SET
    animal_living = 'N'
WHERE
    animal_id = 3;

-- (c)
-- "You should note, in arriving at your solution, that it may be necessary to extend/modify
-- "these types at regular intervals" => thus ** MUST ** use a lookup table

-- drop table prior to create
drop table centre_type;

-- create table
CREATE TABLE centre_type (
    centre_type_code  CHAR(1) NOT NULL,
    centre_type_desc  VARCHAR2(50) NOT NULL
);

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre_type.centre_type_code IS
    'Code for this type of centre';

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre_type.centre_type_desc IS
    'Description for this type code';

alter table centre_type add constraint centre_type_pk primary key (centre_type_code);

    'Nature Reserve'
);

    'Other'
);

-- Do not add CHECK clause to lookup table, this defeats the purpose of having an 
-- easily expanded set of lookup values (normal users do not enter to the
-- lookup table, done by database admin staff)

-- add FK to  centre
ALTER TABLE centre ADD centre_type_code CHAR(1) DEFAULT 'O' NOT NULL;

ALTER TABLE centre
    ADD CONSTRAINT centre_type_centre FOREIGN KEY ( centre_type_code )
        REFERENCES centre_type ( centre_type_code );

Introduction  
Cosmic Property is a company owned by Damian that specializes in producing reports for long term maintenance plans of major building components. This report is to communicate how the implementation of the new website will occur after the building process is finished.  
The purpose of this implementation plan is to outline the steps for deploying the new website to the web hosting server and to enable the client to take over and maintain the website independently.  
The audience includes Damian Markese as the website owner, and the target users of the new website (building owners, building managers, business owners, business managers, building investors, and landlords).  
The implementation scope covers hardware and software preparation, installation, documentation, data conversion, user training, acceptance testing, go live, post-implementation review, and handover of technical/systems documentation.  
Limitations and constraints include the possibility that the website may not be fully deployed in the first iteration, as not all requested features may be available initially.

Topic: Anomaly Detection in Network Traffic

A complete sentence to summarise what you are undertaking in your research in 20 words or less.
This research investigates and compares various anomaly detection techniques to identify abnormal patterns in network traffic data.

A paragraph for elaborating your research by extending the sentence in (1) into structured (“linked”) sentences.
Anomaly detection is a critical task in network security, aiming to identify unusual patterns that may indicate cyber threats or attacks. In this study, we apply three different anomaly detection methods, including statistical analysis, clustering algorithms, and deep learning-based approaches, to network traffic datasets. Firstly, we use statistical techniques to establish baseline behaviour and flag deviations as potential anomalies. However, these methods may not capture complex patterns in high-dimensional data. Therefore, we also implement clustering algorithms, such as k-means, to group similar traffic and detect outliers. Finally, we explore deep learning models, specifically autoencoders, to learn representations of normal traffic and identify anomalies based on reconstruction errors. The dataset used contains millions of network flow records with various features like source IP, destination IP, and packet size. We compare the performance of these methods using metrics such as precision, recall, and F1-score to assess their effectiveness in real-world scenarios.

1 WLAN Network Design and Security
For this task, you will perform a WLAN site survey. Your task is to produce a map of (part of)
a building that gives an overview of the wireless networks that are available, as well as an
analysis of the network.
What you will need: a Wi-Fi-enabled laptop (some smartphones also work, see below), and
a place to scan. You can perform a survey of your home, of an office space, of parts of the
Monash campus, or inside a shopping Centre. If you don’t own a suitable device that you
could use for this activity, please try to borrow one from a friend, or contact us to figure out
an alternative.
1.1 Survey (30 marks)
Create a mapof theplace youwanttosurvey. A simplefloor-planwill besufficient,it doesn’t
have to be perfectly to scale (see belowfor an example).Your surveyshould coveranareaof
at least 40squaremeters (e.g. 4x10metersor two-storey buildingsof6x5each).Be creative
– thesurveycanincludehallwaysor outsideareas.Be suretotake theanalysis inpart1.2into
account, by designing your survey to include walls, doors etc. It will be easier to write
something interesting in part 1.2.
Furthermore, your survey must include at least three Wi-Fi Access Points (WAP). These
can be your own, but can also include neighbors’ APs. If you are scanning in a commercial
area or on campus, you should be able to see enough APs. If you want, you can create an
additional AP with a phone (using “Personal hotspot” or “Tethering” features).
For the survey, use a WLAN sniffing tool (see below) at- least eight different locationson
your map. For each location, record the technicalcharacteristics of all visible APs. Depending
on the scanning tool you use, you can record (printscreen) features such as the Network
Name, MAC Address, Signal Strength, Security, 802.11 Version(s) Supported, Band (2.4 or 5
GHz) andChannel(s) used.
On the map you should indicate the location of the access points and the locations
where you took measurements. For the access points, use the actual location if you know it,
or an approximation based on the observed signal strength (e.g. ifit’syour neighbor’saccess
point and you don’t know exactly where it is). For each measurement point, you can either
add the characteristics directly into the map, or create a separate table with the details. You
can submit several maps if you choose to enter data directly intothe maps,or asinglemap if
you use additional tables. Include the screen-shots (print screen of WLAN sniffing tool data
display) in the appendix section of your report asa proof ofyour datasource. Createthemap
yourself, do not use the mapping features available in some commercial(i.e.paid)WLAN
sniffing tools.

1.2 Report (30 marks)
Write a report (word limit 600) on your observations analyzing the data collected in the
previous step. The report does not have to follow any particular structure (i.e. simply
answering the following questions is enough,youdon’tneed anexecutive summary,tableof
contents etc.). Your analysis should investigate the following aspects:
• Channel Occupancy: Are different access points competing on the same channels? Are
they configured to use overlapping channels? Is roamingavailable? Determine the
overlap that has been implemented to enable roaming. Etc. (6 marks)
• Interference:Effect of other sources of signals (microwave oven, reflected signals from
walls, doors etc. (6 marks)
• Coverage: Do the access points sufficiently cover the desired area? Could the placement
or configuration be improved? (6 marks)
• Attenuation: How does different material affect signal strength and noise? Do you notice
any difference in attenuation for different APs? How about attenuation caused by your
own body? Can you measure and reflect the result? (6 marks)
• One more aspectof your own choice. A few suggestions: (6 marks)
– explain signal-to-noise ratio either using measurednoise or by assuming that
noise is slightly below the weakest signal your scanning software can detect.
– measure the download and upload speeds in different locations.
– describe how to extrapolate or interpolate locationsof APs from signal strengths.
Important: Describe your findings and explain them with some technical detail with
diagrams (i.e., not only say what you found, but also how you performed the analysis or
why you think the network is behaving that way). Maximum word limit for Part-1: 600
words.

1.2 WAP Report

Of the three access points, only AP2 and AP3 were using the 2.4 GHz band, but they were on different channels (1 and 11), so there was no direct channel overlap. AP1 was on the 5 GHz band and did not interfere with the others. Roaming was technically available between AP1 and AP2 since they are from the same router, but my device did not switch automatically during the tests, probably because the signal from AP1 was still strong enough in most locations.

Interference was most noticeable in the kitchen area, where the microwave oven is located. When the microwave was running, the signal strength of AP2 dropped by about 10 dBm, and the connection speed was unstable. Walls and doors also had a clear effect: the thick wall between the living room and bedroom caused a significant drop in signal strength for both AP1 and AP2. AP3, being a mobile hotspot, was only active for some tests and was placed in the study room. Its signal dropped quickly outside the room, especially with the door closed.

Coverage was generally good for AP1 and AP2, covering all main rooms except the far end of the bathroom, where the signal was weak. AP3 only covered the study and part of the hallway. If I wanted to improve coverage, moving the main router closer to the center of the apartment (currently in the living room) would help even out the signal, especially for the bedroom and bathroom.

Attenuation was strongest through the bathroom wall, which is tiled and thicker than the others. Signal strength dropped by about 20 dBm compared to the same distance through a plaster wall. I tried measuring the effect of my body by standing between the laptop and AP2; the signal dropped by 2-3 dBm, but the result was inconsistent and sometimes within the margin of error of the app.

For my own aspect, I measured download speeds at each location using Speedtest. The highest speeds were in the living room near AP1 (up to 90 Mbps), while the lowest were in the bathroom (around 15 Mbps). The mobile hotspot (AP3) was much slower, maxing out at 12 Mbps even in the same room. This shows that signal strength and speed are closely related, but also depend on the AP’s capabilities and backhaul connection.

2. Cyber Security

Article 6

A security issue was discovered involving home and small business routers. Attackers exploited weak default passwords and outdated firmware to gain unauthorized access to routers. Once inside, they could change DNS settings, redirect users to malicious websites, or use the routers as part of a botnet for further attacks. The problem was widespread, affecting many brands and models, and was made worse by users not updating firmware or changing default credentials. The attack was first noticed when users reported strange internet behavior and security researchers traced the cause back to compromised routers. The main risk is that attackers can intercept or manipulate internet traffic, leading to data theft or further malware infections.

The affected systems are consumer and small business routers, including models from TP-Link, D-Link, Netgear, and others, especially those with default credentials or outdated firmware.

The problem was discovered by security researchers investigating reports of DNS hijacking. They published their findings in a security blog and notified vendors. The issue was found through targeted research and analysis of network traffic, using tools like Shodan to find vulnerable devices.

This issue is very serious because routers are the gateway to home and business networks. If compromised, attackers can control all network traffic, steal sensitive data, or launch attacks on other devices. Exploiting the weakness usually requires only knowledge of the default login credentials or access to the router’s web interface, which is often exposed to the internet. The consequences include loss of privacy, financial theft, and being part of larger cyberattacks.

On a technical level, routers should be updated regularly, default passwords must be changed, and remote management should be disabled unless necessary. Users need to be educated about the risks of leaving devices unpatched and using weak passwords. On a policy level, manufacturers should enforce stronger security defaults, such as forcing password changes on first use and providing automatic firmware updates. ISPs and governments could also provide guidance and possibly require minimum security standards for consumer networking equipment.

Article:

Krebs, B. (2020). Why You Should Reboot Your Router — and Update Its Firmware. Krebs on Security. Retrieved 24 May 2021, from https://krebsonsecurity.com/2020/03/why-you-should-reboot-your-router-and-update-its-firmware/.

Journals:

Week 6

This week’s lab focused on how operating systems interact with hardware, especially I/O devices. We compared BIOS and UEFI, learning that UEFI is more modern and supports larger drives and more features. We also looked at how motherboards and chipsets work together, and the difference between Northbridge and Southbridge. The rest of the lab was about the assignment, especially how to use MARIE for input/output and subroutines.

Week 7

We worked in groups to discuss operating system concepts. We learned the difference between a process and a program, and the different process states. User mode is what the user interacts with, while kernel mode is the OS’s internal operations. We explored Linux system calls and checked running processes in Task Manager. I found out my laptop has 4 cores and 16GB RAM.

Week 8

This week was about network layers and protocols. We learned about encapsulation, decapsulation, and the different addresses used at each layer. We also used Wireshark for the first time to capture packets and see how data moves across the network. It was interesting to see the actual HTTP requests and responses.

Week 9

We discussed the assignment in detail, especially the WLAN survey and how to analyze the data. The workshop covered email protocols and how images are sent. We used Wireshark again to capture packets and see IP addresses. I learned that MAC addresses are not visible beyond the local network.

Week 10

We learned about routers and how they forward packets. Static routing is manual, while dynamic routing lets routers build tables automatically. We talked about distance vector and link state routing. We also discussed subnets and how to find MAC addresses using ARP.

==================== Page 4 ====================

Week 11

This week was about encryption. We learned about Caesar, monoalphabetic, and Vigenere ciphers, and why they are not secure. We used frequency analysis to break simple ciphers. We also discussed public and private key encryption, and how RSA works with prime numbers. The practical part was interesting because we could see how easy it is to break weak ciphers.

Week 12

We looked at HTTP and HTTPS protocols, and how certificates work. HTTPS uses TLS to encrypt data, making it more secure than HTTP. We learned how certificates are created and verified, and how the private key is used to sign data. I finished the class by filling out the SETU survey.

Appendix

Screenshots of the WLAN scanning tool readings are attached in the appendix, showing the data collected at each measurement location. The screenshots are numbered according to the measurement points and show the signal strength, channel, and other details for each access point.

1 Liquid Trading (10 marks)
In this task, you are a trader who specialises in valuable liquids. You have come to a new town,
and your goal is to trade with the local people in order to maximise the value of the liquid you
have.
Each person in the town is willing to make various trades at various ratios. For example, one
person might trade water for mercury at a rate of 1L water for 0.01L mercury. Since you are
from the big city, you know the prices of each of these liquids, and so you can determine which
trades are worthwhile and which are not.
You are only in the town for a limited time, and trading takes time, so you have a maximum
number of trades you can conduct before you need to move on, though you may choose to trade
fewer times than this maximum. You also only have one container (with unlimited capacity!),
and you cannot mix liquids or they will become worthless, so you must trade all of your current
liquid whenever you make a trade.
To solve this problem you will write a function, best_trades(prices, starting_liquid,
max_trades, townspeople)
1.1 Input
Liquids each have an ID. There are n liquids, and each one has a unique ID from the range
[0..n-1].
prices is an array of length n, where prices[i] is the value of 1L of the liquid with ID i.
starting_liquidistheIDoftheliquidyouarrivewith. Youalwaysstartwith1Lofthisliquid.
townspeople is a list of non-empty lists. Each interior list corresponds to the trades offered
by a particular person. The interior lists contain 3 element tuples, (give, receive, ratio).
Each tuple indicates that this person is willing to be given liquid with ID give in exchange
for liquid with ID receive at the given ratio. For example, (2,5,1.5) indicates that you can
give liquid with ID 2, and receive 1.5 times as much liquid with ID 5. Note that people are not
necessarily willing to perform these trades in both directions, i.e. this example does not mean
that you can give liquid 5 and receive liquid 2.
For each liquid, there will be at least one townsperson who is willing to trade for that liquid.
1.2 Output
best_trades should return the maximum value that you can obtain after performing at most
max_trades trades.
