==================== Page 1 ====================

FIT1049 Assignment 1: Understanding the IT role and profession
INTRODUCTION
This assignment will provide you with an opportunity to research a particular professional role of interest to you, where
you would be able to use the IT expertise you gain at university. By completing this assignment, you will have a realistic
understanding of the role of your choice, the current industry landscape surrounding the role you chose, and the
attributes and/or skills you would typically need in order to be employable and successful in the role. It may also be
useful to note that a very similar research process, if not the same, will ensue when you are looking for a job in a specific
field, although that will likely focus on a particular organisation whose job you are applying for.
On completion of this assignment, you will be demonstrating the unit’s Learning Outcomes 1, 3 and 5:
1. Describe a variety of roles for IT professional and the personal, social, ethical and legal impacts arising from their
work;
3. Effectively participate in a range of two way oral and written communication forms using appropriate intrapersonal
and interpersonal communication skills and technologies;
5. Gather information, critically evaluate the material and use correct citation techniques when writing the material into
an appropriate format.
INSTRUCTIONS
To get started, choose a particular professional role of your interest, in which you would be able to use the IT expertise
you have gained at university. For example, you could choose:
● a more ‘traditional’ IT role, such as Business Analyst, Software Developer, IT Security Consultant, or Game
Developer;
● a role that is not always explicitly associated with IT, such as Industrial Designer, Police Officer, or
Astrophysicist;
● a role within a particular industry/field of practice, such as ‘Data Scientist in Finance’ or ‘Forensic IT Specialist in
Law Enforcement’.
Please consult your Tutor on role selection. Once you and your tutor agree on the role, you will conduct research on:
● The role: What do the role’s typical duties and responsibilities include? What kind of
qualifications/accreditations would one need for this role? Who are the typical and/or common employers?
● The industry and profession: What makes the role relevant in the contemporary industry landscape? How has
the role evolved recently, and in which direction is it predicted to further evolve in future (especially in light of
the current pandemic situation)? What would a common career progression for the role be?
● Required skills and attributes: What kind of skills and/or attributes would you typically need in order to be
employable and successful in the role (including the required IT-specific skills, and also general or non-technical
skills if relevant), and why? Consider how the desirable and/or essential skills set for this role may have changed
since the current pandemic began.
The tutorial activities in Week 3 will guide you through a systematic research process, with which you can address these
questions consistently and comprehensively. Based on the research you conducted in Week 3, you will then be
communicating the outcomes in two formats, that are:
Part A: written report – 10%
Part B: five-minute virtual presentation – 10%
TOTAL: 20%
Details of submission methods are provided below under their respective sections.

Part A – Written report
Due Date: Sunday the 28th March 2021 (i.e. the end of Week 4)
Task: Based on all of the research you have conducted, document your research outcomes in a report format. As we will
discuss in the Week 3 tutorial, your research (and hence the report) will need to be based on a balanced and well-
curated selection of sources, for which you will be required to conduct a thorough analysis of the sources you have
gathered. Furthermore, your report must include the APA 6th style of citation and referencing, with a list of references
at the end.
Your report must be no longer than three pages (excluding a list of references, however). As a guideline, your report is
expected to cover:
● a brief description of the role of your choice;
● a balanced overview of the current industry landscape surrounding the role you chose; and
● an overview of the attributes and/or skills you would typically need in order to be employable and successful in
the role (including the required IT-specific skills, and also general or non-technical skills if relevant)
You will also be provided with more detailed instructions on how to format a report in the Week 3 virtual tutorial, but
you may also refer to this resource.
Submission method: Your report must be submitted online via Moodle as a PDF document. Penalties of 10% off per day
apply for late submission.
Criteria for assessment:
Your worksheet will be assessed based on the criteria below, and the marking sheet is designed to reflect these criteria.
It is recommended that you use the ‘Assignment 1 Part A: Written Report Assessment Sheet’ available below in this
sheet as a guide to ensure you have addressed all the criteria prior to submission.
1. Depth of analysis of the chosen IT related role and its sources
2. Extent of research done
3. Quality of writing (including spelling and grammar)
4. Appropriate citation of sources using APA 6th referencing style
Part B – Oral presentation
Due date: Tutorial class, Week 6 (unless otherwise scheduled)
Task: Your task is to give a 5 minute oral presentation. The purpose of the presentation is to share the findings from
your research with your Tutor and a Lead Tutor. Your presentation must be supported by a visual presentation tool.
While you may use any presentation tool (such as PowerPoint, Google Slides, Keynote or Prezi), it is your responsibility
to check that the tool you choose will function in the venue/platform. For those presenting online via Zoom, you must
also activate your video stream to provide a ‘self-view’, which will allow the audience to observe your non-verbal
communications skills during the presentation. As a guideline, your presentation should include:
● an introduction to the presentation;
● a brief description of the role of your choice, and its context;
● a balanced overview of the current industry landscape surrounding the role you chose;
● an overview of the attributes and/or skills you would typically need in order to be employable and successful in
the role; and
● a conclusion to the presentation.

The Role of Data Scientist in the Healthcare Industry  
FIT1049: Assignment 1 Part A  
[Student Name] [Student ID]


==================== Page 2 ====================

Introduction  
This report discusses the role of data scientists in the healthcare industry. A data scientist is an IT professional who uses analytical, statistical, and programming skills to collect, analyze, and interpret large data sets (IBM, 2020). In healthcare, data scientists play a crucial role in improving patient outcomes, optimizing hospital operations, and supporting medical research by leveraging data-driven insights (Davenport & Kalakota, 2019).

Healthcare generates vast amounts of data from electronic health records (EHRs), medical imaging, genomics, and wearable devices (Ristevski & Chen, 2018). The ability to extract meaningful information from this data is essential for evidence-based decision-making and innovation in the industry. As such, data scientists are increasingly in demand in healthcare organizations, research institutions, and health technology companies (Glassdoor, 2021).

Body  
Key skills and attributes to be employable/successful as a data scientist in healthcare  
Data scientists in healthcare require a combination of technical, analytical, and domain-specific skills. Firstly, a strong foundation in programming languages such as Python or R is essential for data manipulation and analysis (IBM, 2020). Knowledge of machine learning algorithms and statistical modeling is also critical, as these are used to develop predictive models for disease diagnosis, patient risk stratification, and treatment recommendations (Davenport & Kalakota, 2019).

In addition to technical skills, familiarity with healthcare data standards and privacy regulations, such as HIPAA, is important to ensure compliance and ethical handling of sensitive patient information (Ristevski & Chen, 2018). Data scientists must also be able to work with unstructured data, such as clinical notes and medical images, which requires experience with natural language processing (NLP) and image analysis techniques (IBM, 2020).

Communication and collaboration are key non-technical skills for data scientists in healthcare. They often work in multidisciplinary teams with clinicians, IT professionals, and administrators, so the ability to explain complex analytical results to non-technical stakeholders is vital (Davenport & Kalakota, 2019). Problem-solving, critical thinking, and attention to detail are also important attributes for success in this role.

The COVID-19 pandemic has further highlighted the importance of data science in healthcare, with increased demand for skills in epidemiological modeling, real-time data visualization, and public health analytics (Glassdoor, 2021). As a result, adaptability and a willingness to learn new tools and techniques are increasingly valued by employers.

Qualifications and typical employers  
A bachelor’s degree in computer science, statistics, mathematics, or a related field is typically required for entry-level data scientist roles, with many positions preferring a master’s or PhD, especially in healthcare (IBM, 2020). Additional certifications in data science or healthcare analytics can be advantageous. Typical employers include hospitals, health insurance companies, pharmaceutical firms, government health agencies, and health tech startups (Glassdoor, 2021).


==================== Page 3 ====================

A balanced overview of the current industry landscape surrounding data scientists in healthcare  
The healthcare industry is undergoing a digital transformation, with data science at the forefront of this change. The adoption of electronic health records, telemedicine, and wearable health devices has led to an explosion of healthcare data (Ristevski & Chen, 2018). Data scientists are needed to make sense of this data and drive improvements in patient care, operational efficiency, and medical research.

According to Glassdoor (2021), data scientist was ranked among the top jobs in the United States, with healthcare being one of the fastest-growing sectors for data science roles. The demand for data scientists in healthcare is expected to continue rising as organizations seek to leverage data for personalized medicine, predictive analytics, and population health management (Davenport & Kalakota, 2019).

The role of data scientists in healthcare has evolved rapidly in recent years. Initially, their work focused on descriptive analytics and reporting. However, there is now a shift towards more advanced analytics, including machine learning, artificial intelligence (AI), and real-time data processing (IBM, 2020). The COVID-19 pandemic accelerated this trend, with data scientists playing a key role in tracking the spread of the virus, modeling healthcare resource needs, and supporting vaccine development (Glassdoor, 2021).

Despite the growing opportunities, there are challenges in the industry, such as data privacy concerns, data quality issues, and the need for better integration of data from multiple sources (Ristevski & Chen, 2018). Data scientists must navigate these challenges while ensuring that their work adheres to ethical and legal standards.

Career progression for data scientists in healthcare typically starts with roles such as data analyst or junior data scientist, progressing to senior data scientist, data science manager, or chief data officer. Some may also move into specialized areas such as bioinformatics, clinical informatics, or health AI research (Davenport & Kalakota, 2019).

Conclusion  
In summary, data scientists play a vital role in the healthcare industry by transforming data into actionable insights that improve patient care and operational efficiency. The role requires a blend of technical, analytical, and communication skills, as well as an understanding of healthcare regulations and data privacy. With the ongoing digital transformation of healthcare and the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, the demand for skilled data scientists is expected to remain strong, making it a promising career path for IT graduates.

Faculty of Information Technology

Assignment 4 – Business Report
Individual
Submission: Please submit your report to Moodle.
Value: The assignment is worth 15% of the total marks for the unit.
Due Date: Friday, Week 12 10pm to Moodle (grades returned in week 14)
Aims
• The assignment is designed to demonstrate your ability to:
• Identify and address the relevant professional practice issues raised in a given
situation
• Critically evaluate how to plan and structure a more complex communication and
produce an effective communication of professional quality.
• Assess the needs of a specific audience and develop a communication which
meets those needs.
Background
The final piece of assessment for FIT 5122 is a formal written business report reflecting on
your Professional Practice learning (through the readings, activities and assessment items
that you have completed).
The assignment
You will write a formal business report to a senior executive audience in your organization
addressing the request from your Director below.
Your task
You are currently a team leader in an Australian based software development company.
Your company recently acquired a competitor with offices based in India, China and the US.
The company is planning to establish new global multidisciplinary teams consisting of team
members across each of the countries. The teams will be working on existing and new
products. Senior Management are keen to ensure that the new teams become productive as
soon as possible. They have asked you to prepare a business report outlining:
1. Your insights into the key characteristics of a high performing team;
2. The potential challenges that may prevent the new global teams from performing;
and
3. Your recommendations for how the company should address these potential
challenges.
Note: Your business report should be primarily based around the key concepts discussed in
Professional Practice to demonstrate your understanding and ability to apply the knowledge
learnt during the semester.


==================== Page 2 ====================

Deliverables
Your business report is to be submitted to Moodle by 10pm Friday week 12.
Extensions and late submissions
Please note, University policy stipulates that no assignments can have a due date during
SWOTVAC or the weekend prior. As a result, extensions to the business report due date
can only be granted IF exceptional circumstances apply. Please refer to the University’s
policy on what constitutes special consideration and how to apply. A draft of material
prepared up to that point should also be available for your tutor’s consideration if requested.
Late assignments will be penalised 10% per day as per University policy.
Marking guidelines:
Mark allocation:

• Executive summary: Out of 10 marks | o The executive summary acts as a stand alone document o The reader can clearly understand the purpose of the report, key issues (and why they are important) and actionable recommendations from the summary
• Introduction: Out of 10 marks | o Purpose and objective of the report is clear
• Content: Out of 60 marks | o The report includes the relevant Professional Practice issues and points are well made, practical and insightful. There is a depth of refection in the content. o 10 Marks: Insights into high performing teams are explained concisely and support the reader’s understanding of the challenges and recommendations o 25 Marks: Challenges are explained so the reader can understand what the implications / problems would be for the company o 25 Marks: Recommendations are succinct, useful and delivered in a way that the reader can readily implement.

Marking guidelines:
• FAIL: Inappropriate structure (for example essay format). Content is focused on
theory rather than practical application. A number of obvious points are missing. Poor
or no executive summary (does act as a stand alone document).
• PASS: Poor structure. Content is mostly focused on theory with minimal practical
application but some good points. Poor executive summary (does act as a stand
alone document).


==================== Page 3 ====================

• CREDIT: Reads like a business report. Good structure. Content is mostly focused on
practical application rather than theory. Content demonstrates an understanding of
the relevant Professional Practice issues. Challenges and recommendations are
practical and useful. Executive summary mostly acts as a stand alone document.
• DISTINCTION: Reads like a business report. Excellent concise structure. Points are
well considered and show an indepth understanding of the topics discussed in
Professional Practice. Challenges and recommendations are insightful as well as
practical and useful. Executive summary acts as a stand alone document.
• HIGH DISTINCTION: As for distinction but with unique insights about the challenges
and recommendations.
Expectation of content
Hints for writing a business report versus an essay
• Business reports and academic essay are very different. Their objectives, format and
the expectation of the reader are different.
• Business reports focus on the practical application of the theory - providing insights
and recommendations rather than explaining the theory itself. They are concise, very
practical, usually worded in the first person (I / we recommend… etc) and focused on
identifying and solving a problem rather than discussing the theories behind the
problem / recommendations.
Hints for Structuring Your Report
• Title page
• Exec summary
• Table of contents
• Introduction and purpose of report
• Insights and discussion
• Recommendations
• Conclusion
For this business report, we would expect no more than 7 to 10 pages:
• 3-4 pages (title page, exec. summary, contents, introduction and purpose)
• 4-6 pages main body with conclusions and recommendations.
• You may have references (but remember these are not usual in business reports).
Hints for Writing Your Report
• Remember the audience and purpose of a business report. The report should be
focused on the problem, insights and recommendations on how to address the problem.
The reader should be able to walk away with practical and tangible actions they can
implement.
• The theories discussed in Professional Practice should be used to understand and
develop the key issues and recommendations however the theories themselves should
not be specifically raised in the business report.


==================== Page 4 ====================

• The executive summary should act as a stand alone document that can be read
separately from the report. A good executive summary assumes the reader will not read
further and therefore includes a succinct summary of the key points and
recommendations.
• The report should clearly include the purpose of the report (either in the introduction or
in a separate section.
• Don’t gloss over the recommendations. It should be clear to the reader why they are
important and what tangible actions the reader should take to implement them.
• Put recommendations in a separate section so they don’t get “lost” in the report.
• Keep the business report very succinct, short and punchy. Use lots of bullet points and
sub headings to link ideas.
• Remember your audience: in this case, a senior executive in your company, not your
Monash tutor. Ensure you keep this is mind when deciding the content and level of
detail in your report:
• What would a senior executive want to achieve from reading your report (insights into
the problem and recommendations on how to address it, for example);
• What is their existing knowledge of the topic (it’s their company); and
• What is the appropriate level of detail given what they want to do with the report (high
level insights with recommendations that are sufficiently explained so they can
readily implement them rather than technical or extensive background details).
Plagiarism
Students should also be aware of the plagiarism and collusion policies established by FIT
and Monash University. This policy can be
found at: http://www.infotech.monash.edu.au/resources/student/assignments/policies.ht ml.

Executive Summary  
Purpose  
Following the recent acquisition of a multinational competitor, our software development company is forming new global multidisciplinary teams to work on both existing and new products. This report aims to:  
• Identify the key characteristics of a high performing team  
• Analyse the potential challenges that may prevent the new teams from performing  
• Recommend practical solutions to address these challenges  

Challenges  
While the diversity of skills and backgrounds in the new teams offers many advantages, it also introduces potential challenges if not managed effectively. This report examines the team from three perspectives: multiculturalism, multidisciplinary collaboration, and standardisation. The strengths and weaknesses in each area are discussed, followed by recommendations to address the identified issues.  

Recommendations

==================== Page 4 ====================

Introduction  
Our company’s recent acquisition of a multinational competitor has led to the formation of new global multidisciplinary teams. These teams will be responsible for enhancing existing products and developing new ones. The global nature of the teams means members will come from different countries, bringing diverse backgrounds and cultures, which may impact communication. Additionally, the multidisciplinary aspect means members have varied educational and professional expertise, requiring time to understand each other’s strengths.  

This report will outline the characteristics of high performing teams, analyse the opportunities and challenges facing the new teams, and provide recommendations to address these challenges.  
1  


==================== Page 5 ====================

Insights into High Performing Teams  
High performing teams not only enhance individual capabilities but also unlock the collective potential of the group. Such teams drive productivity, foster innovation, solve problems efficiently, and deliver high-quality outcomes. The key characteristics of a high performing team include:  
1. Clear purpose: All members understand the project’s goals, target audience, and current progress.  
2. Defined roles and responsibilities: Each member knows their responsibilities and how they contribute to the team’s objectives.  
3. Trust and collaboration: Team members build trust, recognise each other’s strengths and weaknesses, and work together effectively.  
4. Effective communication: The team establishes clear communication channels and resolves conflicts in a healthy manner.  
5. Consistent standards: The team follows agreed-upon standards for both development and communication, ensuring alignment and accountability.  
2  


==================== Page 6 ====================

Discussion and Challenges  
This section analyses the new teams in relation to the characteristics of high performing teams, highlighting both opportunities and potential challenges. Recommendations are provided in the next section.  

Multiculturalism  
The teams are multicultural, with members from different countries, cultures, languages, and work styles. This diversity can benefit product development by bringing varied perspectives and innovative ideas. Members’ familiarity with different markets also supports global product positioning.  

However, if not managed well, these strengths can become challenges. For example, using a single working language may disadvantage non-native speakers, affecting communication efficiency. Differences in work hours and habits across regions can also impact productivity. Miscommunication and conflicting work styles may lead to misunderstandings and conflict within the team.  
3  


==================== Page 7 ====================

Multidisciplinary Collaboration  
The teams are multidisciplinary, with members possessing different expertise and problem-solving approaches. Leveraging these strengths can reduce time and costs, improving overall efficiency. Team members can also learn from each other, enhancing both individual and team capabilities.  

However, these benefits depend on mutual understanding and effective leadership. If the team leader is not aware of each member’s strengths, tasks may be assigned inefficiently, leading to delays and increased costs. For example, assigning a web developer to a user interface task outside their expertise can hinder progress.  

Standardisation  
Consistent standards are essential for high performing teams. Clear development and communication standards help members understand their roles and responsibilities. They also provide guidance on when and how to communicate, and how to resolve conflicts constructively.  

Given the global and multidisciplinary nature of the teams, establishing clear standards is critical. Without them, the team may become disorganised, and conflicts may escalate.  
4  


==================== Page 8 ====================

Recommendations  
Based on the analysis above, the following recommendations are proposed to address the potential challenges facing the new teams:  

• Foster effective communication practices, including clear social guidelines. Establishing a positive and inclusive communication culture from the outset is essential. Social guidelines should be agreed upon by all team members, such as avoiding slang during work and respecting time zone differences to prevent late-night disturbances. Team members should also support each other in overcoming language barriers.  

• Encourage constructive conflict resolution. In a cross-cultural and multidisciplinary team, misunderstandings and conflicts are inevitable. Team leaders and members should be trained to resolve conflicts positively, listening to different viewpoints and discussing differences calmly rather than arguing.  

• Implement daily standup meetings. Regular standup meetings help team members understand each other’s work habits and strengths. These meetings promote mutual understanding, clarify roles, and provide opportunities for feedback and learning.  

5  


==================== Page 9 ====================

• Develop and enforce consistent standards for development and communication. With team members from different offices, it is important to adopt a unified development approach, such as Agile, to streamline project management. Establishing a single communication channel ensures that everyone knows where to go for support and how to escalate issues.  

6  


==================== Page 10 ====================

Conclusion  
The new teams consist of members from a recently acquired competitor, each bringing recognised skills and experience. However, differences in culture, expertise, and work habits present challenges in team formation and performance. The main challenges identified are:  
• Multicultural communication: ensuring effective communication across cultures  
• Multidisciplinary integration: improving understanding of each member’s capabilities  
• Standardisation: managing teams efficiently with clear guidelines  

To address these, the following actions are recommended:  
• Enhance communication skills and mutual respect among team members  
• Promote positive conflict resolution strategies  
• Hold daily meetings to build understanding  
• Implement uniform standards for team management  

By following these recommendations, the new global multidisciplinary teams can become productive and deliver successful outcomes.  
7

To enhance the report further, the student could consider providing more specific examples or case studies to support their recommendations and strengthen their analysis. Overall, a strong effort in addressing the requirements of the assignment. Well done!

ASSIGNMENT - PART E [20%]
INDIVIDUAL WEEKLY JOURNAL


Brief
In this stage of the assignment you will demonstrate the individual research, learning and decision-making undertaken as part of your contribution to the team project.


Successful completion of this assessment demonstrates the learning outcomes:


* Work in small teams to plan and execute a creative brief
* Evaluate software procedures for digital audio/visual design

This journal requires regular contributions made on a weekly basis, which will be assessed upon final submission at the end of the semester.


Task
Each student is required to complete weekly journal entries describing and reflecting on design and development processes of all assignment project stages. This journal will also serve as a record of your contribution to team decision-making.


This journal is not process documentation - do not simply provide step-by-step breakdowns of how you created your assignment deliverables.


The journal should demonstrate how you make decisions and apply knowledge.


   * Identification and analysis of new work to be done.
   * Team and individual decisions regarding project direction and work allocation
   * How you applied relevant knowledge from lectures and studio classes
   * Independent research you undertook and what you learned from it
   * Reflection and analysis of previous weeks’ planning and outcomes


At the start of each week, you must start a new journal entry - previously completed entries should not be edited. If you feel that a previously completed entry no longer reflects your project’s direction, you can discuss reasoning and changes in the current week’s entry.


Each entry can be up to 500 words in length. Bullet-point lists and short notes are allowed. Visual aids are also encouraged and do not contribute to word limit.


You will be able to choose your 10 best journal entries to submit for marking.
Deliverables
Throughout the semester, your journal entries must be created and maintained in a Google Doc created using your Monash University account and shared with your tutor. This will allow your tutor to review and assess your document’s version history.


Final submission at the end of the semester will be in the form of an exported DOCX file containing 10 journal entries that you would like to submit for marking. The exported file will be uploaded to Moodle for similarity checking (via TurnItIn) and grading.


   * Create a Google Doc at the start of the semester
   * Create a new Google Doc, or copy the template provided on Moodle
   * Name the document: “FIT2091 - Weekly Journal - [Your Name]”
   * Share the document with your tutor’s email address and assign “editor” permission.
   * Submit an exported DOCX at the end of semester
   * Identify your 10 best journal entries that you would like to have assessed
   * For journal entries that you do not want assessed, highlight the text and set the colour to grey.
   * Export your entire journal as a single DOCX file


Failure to submit either of these deliverables may result in this assessment task being marked as an incomplete submission.


Assessment Criteria
The 10 journal entries selected for submission in the final upload will be assessed using the following criteria.


   * Problem identification and reflection
   * Discussion of creative and technical goals and/or problems
   * Discussion of weekly outcomes or opportunities for improvement
   * Research and applied knowledge
   * Consideration and application of lecture and studio content
   * Evidence of further individual research
   * Planning and teamwork contributions
   * Discussion of team and individual work allocation
   * Consideration of pre-production and production processes
   * Journal presentation
   * Use of visual aids and referencing where appropriate
   * Clarity and structure of journal entries

Introduction  
This report outlines the planned deliverables for Iteration 1 of our project for Dr. Smith’s “Urban Green”. It includes user personas, a summary of the current iteration, user stories and acceptance criteria, and links to supporting documentation. The personas are designed to represent the main users of the platform. The current iteration overview describes the main features to be delivered. User stories and acceptance criteria provide more detail on the specific functions to be implemented.

Personas  
Alex (Urban Gardener)  
Alex is a young professional living in an apartment in the city. He is passionate about sustainability and wants to grow his own herbs and vegetables, but has limited space and time. Alex is looking for a simple way to track his plants and get reminders for watering and care. He values clear instructions and easy-to-use interfaces.

Quote:  
“I want to keep my balcony garden thriving, but I need reminders and tips that fit my busy schedule.”

==================== Page 1 ====================

Faculty of Information Technology
FIT5122 Professional Practice
Assignment 4 – Business Report
Individual
Submission: Please submit your report to Moodle.
Value: The assignment is worth 15% of the total marks for the unit.
Due Date: Friday, Week 12 10pm to Moodle (grades returned in week 14)
Aims
• The assignment is designed to demonstrate your ability to:
• Identify and address the relevant professional practice issues raised in a given
situation
• Critically evaluate how to plan and structure a more complex communication and
produce an effective communication of professional quality.
• Assess the needs of a specific audience and develop a communication which
meets those needs.
Background
The final piece of assessment for FIT 5122 is a formal written business report reflecting on
your Professional Practice learning (through the readings, activities and assessment items
that you have completed).
The assignment
You will write a formal business report to a senior executive audience in your organization
addressing the request from your Director below.
Your task
You are currently a team leader in an Australian based software development company.
Your company recently acquired a competitor with offices based in India, China and the US.
The company is planning to establish new global multidisciplinary teams consisting of team
members across each of the countries. The teams will be working on existing and new
products. Senior Management are keen to ensure that the new teams become productive as
soon as possible. They have asked you to prepare a business report outlining:
1. Your insights into the key characteristics of a high performing team;
2. The potential challenges that may prevent the new global teams from performing;
and
3. Your recommendations for how the company should address these potential
challenges.
Note: Your business report should be primarily based around the key concepts discussed in
Professional Practice to demonstrate your understanding and ability to apply the knowledge
learnt during the semester.


==================== Page 2 ====================

Deliverables
Your business report is to be submitted to Moodle by 10pm Friday week 12.
Extensions and late submissions
Please note, University policy stipulates that no assignments can have a due date during
SWOTVAC or the weekend prior. As a result, extensions to the business report due date
can only be granted IF exceptional circumstances apply. Please refer to the University’s
policy on what constitutes special consideration and how to apply. A draft of material
prepared up to that point should also be available for your tutor’s consideration if requested.
Late assignments will be penalised 10% per day as per University policy.

Expectation of content
Hints for writing a business report versus an essay
• Business reports and academic essay are very different. Their objectives, format and
the expectation of the reader are different.
• Business reports focus on the practical application of the theory - providing insights
and recommendations rather than explaining the theory itself. They are concise, very
practical, usually worded in the first person (I / we recommend… etc) and focused on
identifying and solving a problem rather than discussing the theories behind the
problem / recommendations.
Hints for Structuring Your Report
• Title page
• Exec summary
• Table of contents
• Introduction and purpose of report
• Insights and discussion
• Recommendations
• Conclusion
For this business report, we would expect no more than 7 to 10 pages:
• 3-4 pages (title page, exec. summary, contents, introduction and purpose)
• 4-6 pages main body with conclusions and recommendations.
• You may have references (but remember these are not usual in business reports).
Hints for Writing Your Report
• Remember the audience and purpose of a business report. The report should be
focused on the problem, insights and recommendations on how to address the problem.
The reader should be able to walk away with practical and tangible actions they can
implement.
• The theories discussed in Professional Practice should be used to understand and
develop the key issues and recommendations however the theories themselves should
not be specifically raised in the business report.


==================== Page 4 ====================

• The executive summary should act as a stand alone document that can be read
separately from the report. A good executive summary assumes the reader will not read
further and therefore includes a succinct summary of the key points and
recommendations.
• The report should clearly include the purpose of the report (either in the introduction or
in a separate section.
• Don’t gloss over the recommendations. It should be clear to the reader why they are
important and what tangible actions the reader should take to implement them.
• Put recommendations in a separate section so they don’t get “lost” in the report.
• Keep the business report very succinct, short and punchy. Use lots of bullet points and
sub headings to link ideas.
• Remember your audience: in this case, a senior executive in your company, not your
Monash tutor. Ensure you keep this is mind when deciding the content and level of
detail in your report:
• What would a senior executive want to achieve from reading your report (insights into
the problem and recommendations on how to address it, for example);
• What is their existing knowledge of the topic (it’s their company); and
• What is the appropriate level of detail given what they want to do with the report (high
level insights with recommendations that are sufficiently explained so they can
readily implement them rather than technical or extensive background details).
Plagiarism
Students should also be aware of the plagiarism and collusion policies established by FIT
and Monash University. This policy can be
found at: http://www.infotech.monash.edu.au/resources/student/assignments/policies.ht ml.

Executive Summary  
High-performing teams are essential for any organization aiming to achieve its business goals and maintain a competitive edge, especially in a global context. This business report outlines the key characteristics of high-performing teams, analyzes the main challenges that may arise when forming new global multidisciplinary teams, and provides practical recommendations to address these challenges.  
The key characteristics of high-performing teams are:  
1. Leveraging the unique strengths of each team member;  
2. Fostering a shared sense of value and purpose among all members;  
3. Building a safe and trusting environment where everyone feels supported.  
Potential challenges for the new global teams include:  
- Communication barriers and misunderstandings;  
- Cultural and regional differences in work styles;  
- Unclear roles, objectives, or expectations;  
- Diverse personalities and resistance to change.  
To address these challenges, the following recommendations are made:  
1. Identify and utilize individual strengths;  
2. Promote a culture of shared values and collaboration;  
3. Encourage open and transparent communication.

==================== Page 4 ====================

Introduction and Purpose of Report  
Introduction  
The importance of high-performing teams has become even more significant in today’s rapidly changing business environment. As organizations expand globally and embrace digital transformation, the ability to build and manage effective teams is a key factor for success. As stated by Reid Hoffman, cofounder of LinkedIn, “No matter how brilliant your mind or strategy, if you’re playing a solo game, you’ll always lose out to a team.”  
Our company is forming new global multidisciplinary teams with members from Australia, India, China, and the US, working on both existing and new products. As a team leader, my responsibility is to ensure these teams become productive quickly.  
Purpose  
The purpose of this business report is to:  
1. Identify the key characteristics of high-performing teams;  
2. Analyze the potential challenges that may hinder the performance of new global teams;  
3. Provide recommendations to address these challenges and support team success.  


==================== Page 5 ====================

Insights and Discussion  
Insights  
The key characteristics of a high-performing team can be summarized as follows:  
1. Leveraging the unique strengths of each team member;  
2. Creating an environment where everyone feels their values are recognized and shared;  
3. Building a safe and trusting atmosphere where team members support each other.  
The first characteristic is understanding and utilizing the unique strengths and talents of each team member. Often, individuals may not recognize their own strengths, so it is important for leaders to help identify and match these strengths to suitable roles and responsibilities. This not only increases individual engagement but also enhances overall team performance.  
The second characteristic is fostering a sense of shared value and purpose. When team members feel that their contributions are valued and that they are part of something meaningful, they are more likely to be motivated and committed to the team’s goals. Positive feedback and recognition play a key role in reinforcing this sense of value.  
The third characteristic is psychological safety and trust. Research, such as Google’s study on team effectiveness, shows that psychological safety is the most important factor for team success. When team members trust each other and feel safe to express ideas, ask for help, or raise concerns, the team is more likely to innovate and perform at a high level.  


==================== Page 6 ====================

Discussion  
Given that our new teams are global and multidisciplinary, several challenges may impact their performance:  
- Communication barriers and misunderstandings: Differences in language, time zones, and communication styles can lead to incomplete information or misinterpretation, which can hinder collaboration.  
- Cultural and regional differences: Team members from different countries may have different approaches to work, time management, and problem-solving. For example, some cultures may prefer direct communication, while others rely on context and subtext.  
- Unclear roles and objectives: Without clear division of labor and well-defined goals, team members may have different expectations, leading to confusion and misalignment.  
- Diverse personalities and resistance to change: Differences in personality, work ethic, and openness to new ways of working can create friction and slow down team progress.  
These challenges can lead to conflicts within the team. While some level of constructive conflict can stimulate discussion and improvement, unresolved or negative conflict can damage trust and reduce performance. Addressing these issues early is critical for building a high-performing team.  


==================== Page 7 ====================

Recommendations  
Based on the insights and challenges discussed, the following recommendations are proposed to support the success of the new global multidisciplinary teams:  
1. Identify and utilize individual strengths:  
   - Conduct team-building activities to help members understand each other’s skills and strengths;  
   - Assign roles and responsibilities that align with individual talents;  
   - Provide opportunities for members to showcase their expertise.  
2. Promote a culture of shared values and collaboration:  
   - Establish clear team goals and a shared vision;  
   - Recognize and celebrate individual and team achievements;  
   - Encourage mutual respect and appreciation among team members.  
3. Encourage open and transparent communication:  
   - Set up regular meetings and communication channels;  
   - Provide cross-cultural training to increase awareness of different communication styles and work habits;  
   - Foster an environment where team members feel comfortable sharing ideas and feedback.  
4. Address conflicts constructively:  
   - Implement clear processes for resolving disagreements;  
   - Encourage open discussion of issues before they escalate;  
   - Support team members in finding common ground and solutions.  


==================== Page 8 ====================

Conclusion  
To build and maintain high-performing global teams, the following actions are essential:  
1. Clearly define the team’s mission and purpose to align everyone’s efforts;  
2. Establish regular and effective communication mechanisms, both formal and informal, to keep everyone informed and engaged;  
3. Recognize that some conflict, especially around goals and processes, can be beneficial if managed well, but relationship conflicts should be minimized;  
4. Foster a culture of trust, respect, and collaboration to support team success.  
By focusing on these areas, the company can help new global teams overcome challenges and achieve high performance.
LLM tutor feedback:
The student's report demonstrates a good understanding of the assignment requirements and key concepts.  The structure is largely appropriate for a business report, with a clear executive summary, introduction, discussion, and recommendations.  The report is concise and avoids excessive theoretical discussion, focusing on practical application.

However, the report could be strengthened by:

* **More in-depth analysis of challenges:** While the report identifies potential challenges, a deeper exploration of their *implications* for the company is needed.  For example, how might communication barriers affect project timelines or quality?  How might cultural differences impact team dynamics?  Quantifiable examples would further enhance the analysis.

* **More specific recommendations:**  The recommendations are good starting points, but they lack concrete details.  For example, "conduct team-building activities" is vague.  What *type* of activities?  How will these activities be measured for effectiveness?  Adding specific examples and measurable outcomes would make the recommendations more actionable.

* **Stronger connection to Professional Practice:** While the report touches on relevant concepts, a more explicit connection to the specific theories and frameworks discussed in FIT5122 would elevate the analysis.  Briefly mentioning relevant models or concepts (e.g., Tuckman's stages of group development) would demonstrate a deeper understanding of the subject matter.

* **Executive Summary:** While the executive summary is present, it could be more impactful by summarizing the *key findings* of the report, not just the recommendations.  It should also highlight the *importance* of the issues raised.

Overall, the report is a solid foundation.  With more in-depth analysis of challenges, more specific recommendations, and a stronger connection to Professional Practice concepts, the report would achieve a higher grade.

The Animal Doctors management have indicated:
● they do not require the clinic addresses or any phone numbers to be broken down into
component attributes
● they would like to be able to easily expand:
○ the vet specialisations, as they employ further vets with new specialisations, and
○ the types of animals, as they treat a new type which they have not previously seen
● the services which are provided during a visit are identified by a service code and have a
standard service cost which must be recorded as part of the database. Every visit incurs at
least one service charge. During a visit (treatment of a patient - the animal) the servicing vet
may vary the service standard cost depending on their professional judgement,
● the drugs which are provided during a visit have a standard cost which must be recorded as
part of the database, however like services this cost may be varied by the vet for a particular
visit
● when a drug is used as part of the visit (eg. an injection that the vet gives the patient in the
clinic) the frequency is not recorded (frequency is only used only for drugs given to the owner
to be administered to their pet following the visit eg. a twice daily antibiotic tablet), and
● management would like a count maintained, as part of the stored database data, which
indicates how many times a given specialist vet has serviced a visit in a clinic which is not
their home clinic.

The Concepts behind using Workbooks in Assignments 1 and 2
In many units, critical and reflective thinking and literacy skills are expected and often not explicitly taught. FIT5057
is unique because it also offers students practice drills to develop their critical thinking and literacy skills when
preparing for the major assignment 1 and 2 work.
Through these practice drills via Workbooks’ tasks, you will be guided & supported to:
1. Learn by applying the key PM concepts and techniques you need to write a project plan and understand
the practice implications and real-world case studies of theoretical PM concepts.
2. Learn by applying the techniques that make up research, critical thinking, reading and writing skills, which
collectively are your employability skills.
3. Learn by hands-on experience the real implications of continuous teamplay, time management, issues
reporting and collaborative resolution with peers and your tutor.
Reflecting on these practice drills will place you in a better position to understand the relevant PM info you need
and identify the skill-based techniques you need to apply to complete all the 3 summative assignments.
From a teaching strategy perspective, the workbooks provide you the
formative learning experience, with close support from your peers and
tutor. The major assignments provide the summative evidence that you
are able to understand and apply the relevant PM concepts and the
appropriate PM, research, critical thinking, reading and writing
techniques to respond effectively to the given assignments.
If you often skip tutorials and consults, you will expose yourself to
ineffective formative learning and be prepared for stressful and painful
consequences when completing the assignments near submission
deadlines.
For Assignment 2, your attitude and capacity to work with other people from diverse backgrounds and different
abilities can also determine the challenges you may face in groupwork and influence the quality of not just your
own, but your peers’ contributions in completing the assignment work. You may also make or break relationships,
which may enhance or block your future career developments as working with, relating to people from diverse
backgrounds and abilities are important soft skills you need to lead and motivate people when you aim for senior
management and leadership career paths.
There are 5 success factors for doing well in assignments:
1. That you individually learn effectively and always ask questions among each other and your tutor when not
sure;
2. That you regular attend tutorials and consultations, and keep up to date with lecture lessons;
3. That you engage in effective group collaboration and communications;
4. That you practice effective time management and have succession plans in case some members become
unavailable during the semester.
5. That you are able to respect each other despite coming from different backgrounds or having different
learning abilities; and work positively with everyone regardless of any people issues you encounter.

Instructions:
The aim of this workbook is to guide you to understand the research, critical reading and writing techniques
involved in literature search, information selection for analysis to respond to given inquiry questions. Instructions
for applying these techniques are given in the later part of this workbook.
Each Student is to write a 1,000 words report (excluding Reference List) that answers the following questions:

// The following is converted from a table
1. Explain what is meant by DevOps as a concept and as a practice model.
2. Explain what is meant by Enterprise Architecture as a practice model.
3. Discuss the integration of DevOps in Project Management Work.
4. Discuss the integration of EA in Project Management Work.
5. Summarise by visualising the integration of DevOps, EA in Project Management.
6. Conclude why, as a future project manager, you need to understand the implications of DevOps and EA in
project management work.

Your answer should show an introduction, body and conclusion that relate to your question of choice. No executive
summary is needed. The writing format is a report, which means you need to have meaningful cascading headings
that explain what is the purpose and scope of your report; the key findings as instructed and a conclusion as
instructed.

Introduction  
In today’s fast-changing business environment, software and web technologies play a crucial role in driving organizational growth and competitiveness. Companies are under pressure to deliver products faster, with higher quality, and to adapt quickly to market changes. This has led to the emergence of concepts like DevOps and Enterprise Architecture (EA), which are increasingly important in project management. This report will explain the concepts of DevOps and EA, discuss their integration into project management, and highlight their significance for future project managers.

The concept of DevOps  
DevOps is a term formed from “development” and “operations.” It does not have a single, universally accepted definition, but generally refers to a set of practices, tools, and a cultural philosophy that brings together software development and IT operations (Capgemini, 2016). The main goal of DevOps is to shorten the development lifecycle, increase deployment frequency, and deliver high-quality software reliably. DevOps emphasizes collaboration between developers and operations teams, and relies heavily on automation to streamline processes.

The application of DevOps  
DevOps addresses several key challenges in software delivery, such as complex deployment processes, error-prone operations, siloed teams, and the need for both rapid change and system stability (Capgemini, 2016). Capgemini (2016) identifies three main areas for implementing DevOps:

1. Cultural Change  
DevOps requires a shift in organizational culture, promoting collaboration across traditionally separate teams. Performance evaluation and rewards should be aligned to encourage shared goals and teamwork.

2. DevOps Lifecycle  
The DevOps lifecycle covers the entire process from development to deployment. It starts with coding, followed by various testing phases (unit, functional, non-functional), then user and operational acceptance testing. Once these are complete, the system goes live. This cycle is continuous, with ongoing improvements and feedback.

3. Automation Tools  
DevOps relies on automation for building, testing, and deploying software. Using a single repository, environments can be created and managed automatically, reducing manual intervention and errors.

In traditional project management, different teams handle different stages of the process, often leading to miscommunication and delays. DevOps breaks down these silos by involving operations staff early in development, allowing them to understand the system architecture and contribute to deployment planning. Developers also participate in deployment, providing input on optimization and troubleshooting.

The concept and application of Enterprise Architecture  
Enterprise Architecture (EA) originated as a way for IT to align with changing business strategies (González-Rojas, López, & Correal, 2017). EA is a management framework that helps organizations optimize business processes, improve efficiency, and enhance information security by integrating information systems (Bojinov, 2016).

Application of Enterprise Architecture  
EA focuses on planning, modeling, and guiding the implementation of business strategies. Project management (PM), on the other hand, is about achieving specific project goals through planning and execution (Kaine, 2017). For organizations to successfully implement business strategies, EA and PM must be integrated. Project portfolio management (PPM) provides a high-level view of an organization’s strategy and depends on a solid EA to analyze relationships within the project portfolio (Quartel, Steen, & Lankhorst, 2012). Techniques like roadmapping help align EA and PPM, ensuring IT initiatives support business goals (Gellweiler, 2020). Integrating EA and PM enables organizations to execute projects that deliver strategic value.

Summarising the integration of DevOps and EA in PM  
Both DevOps and EA focus on improving collaboration and communication across departments. Integrating these concepts into project management is now common practice. DevOps connects all relevant stakeholders, while EA clarifies roles and responsibilities within the organization. This integration is especially important in large organizations, where dependencies between departments are complex.

For example, in an SAP implementation project, EA provides a map of business processes and identifies key users and workflows. This helps the project team gather requirements and design solutions that fit the organization’s needs. DevOps enables continuous integration and delivery, allowing the team to share updates, conduct testing, and deploy features efficiently. Feedback from users and engineers is quickly incorporated, helping to identify and resolve issues early (Roy, n.d.). By combining DevOps and EA, project teams can better understand client requirements and respond effectively.

Conclusion  
As organizations expand and globalize, implementing systems like ERP has become increasingly important. Project managers must act as bridges between different functional areas (Rebecca, 2017). Understanding and applying DevOps and EA is essential for project managers to deliver successful projects. Failed or delayed projects can be costly, but DevOps and EA help managers evaluate proposals, ensure effective communication, and keep all stakeholders aligned. These methodologies break down barriers between teams and promote timely issue resolution (DEVOPS REF). As a result, DevOps and EA are becoming key tools in modern project management.

Application Concept

Introduction

The proposed mobile application is a Community Board Game Events App. Board gaming is a popular hobby, but finding local events, meetups, and like-minded players can be challenging, especially for newcomers or people who have recently moved to a new area. Traditional methods such as Facebook groups or word-of-mouth are often fragmented and unreliable. This app aims to centralise information about board game events, allow users to organise and join meetups, and facilitate communication between board gamers in their local area.

The app will allow users to discover upcoming board game events on a map, RSVP to events, create their own events, and chat with other attendees. Users can also maintain a profile listing their favourite games and interests, making it easier to find compatible gaming groups. The app will use location services to show nearby events and push notifications to remind users of upcoming meetups.

Target Audience

The target audience includes board game enthusiasts of all ages, from casual players to hobbyists, as well as people new to an area looking to make friends through board gaming. It is also aimed at event organisers, such as board game cafes, clubs, and individuals who want to host public or private gaming sessions. The app is designed to be accessible to both tech-savvy users and those less familiar with technology, with a focus on a simple and intuitive interface.

The app addresses the need for a centralised, easy-to-use platform for discovering and organising board game events, reducing the friction of finding groups and making new connections in the hobby.


==================== Page 3 ====================

Application Functionality

Key features of the application include:

Event Discovery
- Users can view a map of upcoming board game events in their area.
- Events are displayed as pins on the map, with details such as time, location, games being played, and organiser info.
- Users can filter events by date, distance, or game type.

Event Creation and Management
- Users can create new events, specifying details such as location (using map picker), date/time, games, and maximum number of attendees.
- Organisers can edit or cancel events, and manage RSVPs.

RSVP and Attendance
- Users can RSVP to events, see who else is attending, and receive reminders.
- Attendees can check in at the event using a QR code generated by the app.

User Profiles
- Each user has a profile with their name, photo, favourite games, and a short bio.
- Users can update their profile and set preferences for notifications and privacy.

Chat and Messaging
- Each event has a group chat for attendees to coordinate details, share updates, or ask questions.
- Users can also send direct messages to other users.

Notifications
- Users receive push notifications for event reminders, new messages, and updates to events they are attending.

Persistent Storage and Cloud Sync
- All user data, events, and messages are stored in the cloud, allowing access from any device after login.

Map and Location Services
- The app uses the device’s location to show nearby events and suggest relevant meetups.

Web Service Integration
- The app fetches board game data (e.g., images, descriptions) from BoardGameGeek’s API to help organisers list games for their events.


==================== Page 4 ====================

Competition and Innovation

Competitor Analysis

1. Meetup
Meetup is a popular app for organising and joining local events, including board game meetups. It offers event creation, RSVPs, group chats, and notifications. However, it is a general-purpose platform and not tailored to board gaming. Searching for board game events can be cumbersome, and there is no integration with board game databases or features specific to the hobby (e.g., listing games, matching by interests).

Strengths:
- Large user base
- Robust event management features

Weaknesses:
- Not specific to board gaming
- Limited filtering for game types
- No integration with board game data

2. Board Game Atlas
Board Game Atlas is a website and app focused on board games, including a tool for finding local game stores and events. It allows users to search for events and stores, but the event creation and RSVP features are limited. The interface is more focused on game discovery and price comparison than community events.

Strengths:
- Board game database integration
- Some event discovery features

Weaknesses:
- Limited event management
- No chat or messaging
- Less focus on local community building

Innovation

This app differentiates itself by being purpose-built for board gamers, with features such as:
- Integration with board game databases for easy event setup and discovery
- Map-based event discovery with real-time filtering
- In-app group chat for each event
- QR code check-in for attendance tracking
- User profiles focused on gaming interests
- Push notifications tailored to event reminders and updates

By focusing on the needs of the board gaming community, the app provides a smoother, more relevant experience than general event apps.


==================== Page 5 ====================

Feasibility and Technology

iOS Capabilities and Frameworks

- UIKit: For building the app’s user interface, including navigation, forms, and custom views.
- MapKit: To display events on a map, allow users to pick locations, and show directions.
- Core Location: To access the user’s current location and suggest nearby events.
- Firebase: For authentication (sign up, login), real-time database (storing events, messages, profiles), and cloud storage (profile images, event photos).
- Push Notifications: Using Firebase Cloud Messaging for event reminders and chat updates.
- AVFoundation: For scanning QR codes at event check-in.
- URLSession: For integrating with the BoardGameGeek API to fetch game data.
- TableView and CollectionView: For displaying lists of events, attendees, and messages.
- Gesture Recognisers: For swipe actions (e.g., deleting events, RSVP).
- Local Notifications: For reminders when the app is in the background.

Technical Feasibility

All required features can be implemented using standard iOS frameworks and Firebase. MapKit and Core Location are well-supported for map and location features. Firebase provides scalable backend services for authentication, data storage, and messaging. QR code scanning is supported via AVFoundation. Integration with external APIs (BoardGameGeek) is feasible using URLSession and JSON parsing.

Potential challenges include managing real-time chat performance, handling privacy for location data, and ensuring smooth integration between map and event data. However, these are manageable with current iOS technologies.


==================== Page 6 ====================

Interface Design and Storyboard Mock-ups

Navigation Hierarchy

- Launch Screen: App logo and loading indicator.
- Login/Signup: Email/password authentication, option for Google/Apple sign-in.
- Home (Tab Bar Controller):
    - Map: Shows pins for nearby events, search/filter bar at top.
    - Events List: List of upcoming events, filter and sort options.
    - My Events: Events the user is attending or organising.
    - Messages: List of event chats and direct messages.
    - Profile: User profile, settings, and preferences.

Storyboard Mock-ups (Descriptions)

1. Map Screen
- Map with event pins; tapping a pin shows event details in a bottom sheet.
- Filter button to select date, distance, or game type.

2. Event Details
- Event name, organiser, date/time, location (with map preview), list of games, attendee list (avatars), RSVP button.
- Button to join event chat.
- QR code for check-in (for attendees).

3. Event Creation
- Form with fields for event name, date/time picker, location picker (map), games (searchable from BoardGameGeek), max attendees, description.
- Option to upload event image.

4. Chat Screen
- Group chat for each event, with message bubbles, timestamps, and image sharing.
- Direct messaging interface for private chats.

5. Profile Screen
- User photo, name, bio, favourite games (with images), edit button.
- Settings for notifications, privacy, and account management.

Design Considerations

- Follows Apple Human Interface Guidelines: clear navigation, large touch targets, readable fonts, and consistent iconography.
- Uses tab bar for main navigation, modal sheets for event creation and details.
- Map interactions use standard gestures (pinch, pan, tap).
- Accessibility: supports Dynamic Type, VoiceOver, and high-contrast mode.

Assignment description:
==================== Page 1 ====================

Faculty of Information Technology
FIT5122 Professional Practice
Assignment 4 – Business Report
Individual
Submission: Please submit your report to Moodle.
Value: The assignment is worth 15% of the total marks for the unit.
Due Date: Friday, Week 12 10pm to Moodle (grades returned in week 14)
Aims
• The assignment is designed to demonstrate your ability to:
• Identify and address the relevant professional practice issues raised in a given
situation
• Critically evaluate how to plan and structure a more complex communication and
produce an effective communication of professional quality.
• Assess the needs of a specific audience and develop a communication which
meets those needs.
Background
The final piece of assessment for FIT 5122 is a formal written business report reflecting on
your Professional Practice learning (through the readings, activities and assessment items
that you have completed).
The assignment
You will write a formal business report to a senior executive audience in your organization
addressing the request from your Director below.
Your task
You are currently a team leader in an Australian based software development company.
Your company recently acquired a competitor with offices based in India, China and the US.
The company is planning to establish new global multidisciplinary teams consisting of team
members across each of the countries. The teams will be working on existing and new
products. Senior Management are keen to ensure that the new teams become productive as
soon as possible. They have asked you to prepare a business report outlining:
1. Your insights into the key characteristics of a high performing team;
2. The potential challenges that may prevent the new global teams from performing;
and
3. Your recommendations for how the company should address these potential
challenges.
Note: Your business report should be primarily based around the key concepts discussed in
Professional Practice to demonstrate your understanding and ability to apply the knowledge
learnt during the semester.


==================== Page 2 ====================

Deliverables
Your business report is to be submitted to Moodle by 10pm Friday week 12.
Extensions and late submissions
Please note, University policy stipulates that no assignments can have a due date during
SWOTVAC or the weekend prior. As a result, extensions to the business report due date
can only be granted IF exceptional circumstances apply. Please refer to the University’s
policy on what constitutes special consideration and how to apply. A draft of material
prepared up to that point should also be available for your tutor’s consideration if requested.
Late assignments will be penalised 10% per day as per University policy.
Marking guidelines:
Mark allocation:

// The following is converted from a table
• Structure: Out of 20 marks | o Reads like a business report with a practical rather than theoretical focus. o The structure and formatting are appropriate for a business report - headings and bullet points are used to keep the report succinct o The report is appropriately pitched to a senior management audience o English expression and language is appropriate and professional
• Executive summary: Out of 10 marks | o The executive summary acts as a stand alone document o The reader can clearly understand the purpose of the report, key issues (and why they are important) and actionable recommendations from the summary
• Introduction: Out of 10 marks | o Purpose and objective of the report is clear
• Content: Out of 60 marks | o The report includes the relevant Professional Practice issues and points are well made, practical and insightful. There is a depth of refection in the content. o 10 Marks: Insights into high performing teams are explained concisely and support the reader’s understanding of the challenges and recommendations o 25 Marks: Challenges are explained so the reader can understand what the implications / problems would be for the company o 25 Marks: Recommendations are succinct, useful and delivered in a way that the reader can readily implement.

Marking guidelines:
• FAIL: Inappropriate structure (for example essay format). Content is focused on
theory rather than practical application. A number of obvious points are missing. Poor
or no executive summary (does act as a stand alone document).
• PASS: Poor structure. Content is mostly focused on theory with minimal practical
application but some good points. Poor executive summary (does act as a stand
alone document).


==================== Page 3 ====================

• CREDIT: Reads like a business report. Good structure. Content is mostly focused on
practical application rather than theory. Content demonstrates an understanding of
the relevant Professional Practice issues. Challenges and recommendations are
practical and useful. Executive summary mostly acts as a stand alone document.
• DISTINCTION: Reads like a business report. Excellent concise structure. Points are
well considered and show an indepth understanding of the topics discussed in
Professional Practice. Challenges and recommendations are insightful as well as
practical and useful. Executive summary acts as a stand alone document.
• HIGH DISTINCTION: As for distinction but with unique insights about the challenges
and recommendations.
Expectation of content
Hints for writing a business report versus an essay
• Business reports and academic essay are very different. Their objectives, format and
the expectation of the reader are different.
• Business reports focus on the practical application of the theory - providing insights
and recommendations rather than explaining the theory itself. They are concise, very
practical, usually worded in the first person (I / we recommend… etc) and focused on
identifying and solving a problem rather than discussing the theories behind the
problem / recommendations.
Hints for Structuring Your Report
• Title page
• Exec summary
• Table of contents
• Introduction and purpose of report
• Insights and discussion
• Recommendations
• Conclusion
For this business report, we would expect no more than 7 to 10 pages:
• 3-4 pages (title page, exec. summary, contents, introduction and purpose)
• 4-6 pages main body with conclusions and recommendations.
• You may have references (but remember these are not usual in business reports).
Hints for Writing Your Report
• Remember the audience and purpose of a business report. The report should be
focused on the problem, insights and recommendations on how to address the problem.
The reader should be able to walk away with practical and tangible actions they can
implement.
• The theories discussed in Professional Practice should be used to understand and
develop the key issues and recommendations however the theories themselves should
not be specifically raised in the business report.


==================== Page 4 ====================

• The executive summary should act as a stand alone document that can be read
separately from the report. A good executive summary assumes the reader will not read
further and therefore includes a succinct summary of the key points and
recommendations.
• The report should clearly include the purpose of the report (either in the introduction or
in a separate section.
• Don’t gloss over the recommendations. It should be clear to the reader why they are
important and what tangible actions the reader should take to implement them.
• Put recommendations in a separate section so they don’t get “lost” in the report.
• Keep the business report very succinct, short and punchy. Use lots of bullet points and
sub headings to link ideas.
• Remember your audience: in this case, a senior executive in your company, not your
Monash tutor. Ensure you keep this is mind when deciding the content and level of
detail in your report:
• What would a senior executive want to achieve from reading your report (insights into
the problem and recommendations on how to address it, for example);
• What is their existing knowledge of the topic (it’s their company); and
• What is the appropriate level of detail given what they want to do with the report (high
level insights with recommendations that are sufficiently explained so they can
readily implement them rather than technical or extensive background details).
Plagiarism
Students should also be aware of the plagiarism and collusion policies established by FIT
and Monash University. This policy can be
found at: http://www.infotech.monash.edu.au/resources/student/assignments/policies.ht ml.
