1. Introduction and Purpose of Report  
InnovateSoft has recently acquired “CodeSphere”, an international company with offices in India, China, and the US. Research and experience show that diverse teams can be more effective and innovative. The company is now forming new global multidisciplinary teams with members from each country, who will be responsible for both existing and new products.  
The purpose of this report is to provide senior executives with information and recommendations for establishing and sustaining high-performing global teams. The report will first discuss the key characteristics of high-performing teams, then outline the main challenges these teams may face, and finally provide recommendations to address these challenges.  
4  


==================== Page 5 ====================

2. Insights and Discussion  
2.1. Key Characteristics of a High-Performing Team  
A high-performing team is made up of motivated and skilled individuals working towards shared goals. While every team is unique, high-performing teams share several common characteristics:  
• Clear purpose and goals  
The team’s purpose and goals are well defined and understood by all members. Everyone knows what needs to be achieved and how their work contributes to the overall objectives.  
• Clear expectations and roles  
Each team member’s role and responsibilities are clearly defined, reducing confusion and conflict. This helps keep the team organized and accountable.  
• Understanding of each other’s strengths and weaknesses  
Team members appreciate each other’s skills and are aware of areas where support may be needed. This helps the team use everyone’s strengths effectively.  
• Open and clear communication  
Effective communication is essential for team success. High-performing teams have clear channels and expectations for communication, and know how to handle conflicts constructively.  
• Opportunities for participation  
All team members are encouraged to contribute and participate. Their input is valued and recognized, which increases engagement.  
• Trust and openness  
Trust is the foundation of a high-performing team. Team members respect each other and feel comfortable sharing ideas and taking risks.  
5  


==================== Page 6 ====================

2.2. Potential Challenges for Global Teams  
• Lack of trust  
Trust takes time to develop, especially in new teams where members have not worked together before. In global teams, building trust is harder due to limited face-to-face interaction. This can lead to lower engagement and productivity.  
• Communication barriers  
Global teams face challenges such as time zone differences, language barriers, and limited opportunities for informal communication. Virtual meetings can lead to misunderstandings, and language differences can make it harder to communicate clearly.  
• Cultural differences  
Team members from different countries may have different values, communication styles, and expectations. For example, Australia and the US are low-context cultures (direct communication), while India and China are high-context cultures (more indirect communication). These differences can lead to misunderstandings and conflict.  
• Inability to manage conflict  
Conflicts can arise in any team, but are more likely in global teams due to virtual communication and cultural differences. Without strong personal connections, conflicts can escalate and harm team morale.  
• Lack of goal clarity  
If team members are unclear about the team’s objectives, they may not be fully engaged or committed.  
• Poorly defined roles and responsibilities  
Unclear roles can lead to confusion, duplicated work, and conflict, reducing the team’s effectiveness.  
6  


==================== Page 7 ====================

3. Recommendations  
To address the challenges identified above, the following recommendations are made:  
• Careful selection of team members  
Choose team members based on their skills, experience, and fit with the team’s objectives. Keep teams small and flexible where possible.  
• Regular virtual meetings (bi-weekly) using video conferencing  
Hold regular video meetings so team members can see each other and build relationships. Encourage everyone to share updates and discuss issues. Rotate meeting times to accommodate different time zones, and provide recordings for those who cannot attend.  
• Periodic in-person meetings or events  
Organize face-to-face meetings or events when possible. Meeting in person helps build stronger relationships and trust.  
• Team building activities  
Arrange team building activities, both online and in person, to help team members get to know each other and work together more effectively.  
• Training for team members  
Provide training on communication skills, conflict resolution, team roles, and cross-cultural awareness. This will help team members understand and work through differences.  
• Clear definition of goals and roles  
Set clear goals and define each team member’s role and responsibilities. Provide a basic structure and process for the team to follow, especially for new members.  
8  


==================== Page 8 ====================

4. Conclusion  
In summary, forming high-performing global teams at InnovateSoft will bring both opportunities and challenges. The main challenges are lack of trust, communication barriers, cultural differences, unclear goals, and poorly defined roles. To overcome these, it is important to select the right team members, define goals and roles clearly, communicate regularly, organize team building and in-person meetings, and provide training. By following these recommendations, the new global teams will be better positioned to succeed.  
10

As natural habitats disappear, it is increasingly difficult to ensure that all species can survive in the
wild. One solution is to use zoos, reserves and other types of organizations that keep wild animals in
captivity as conservation centres for various species. To assist in recording the animals in captivity
and their offspring, a Centre Breeding database is to be created in which all centres will record the
animals which they hold and the breeding events which take place at the centres.
Database designers have considered the requirements and arrived at the following data model:

Records the details of the species, the name of the species, as well as its popular name and the
genus and family to which the species belongs. The relevant details for the Tasmanian Devil, for
instance, are as follows ...
Genus: Sarcophilus
Species: harrisii
Popular name: Tasmanian Devil
Family: DASYURIDAE
It is also important to know the species’ natural range, which is usually given as a description of the
geographic regions and natural habitats where a species is found in the wild.

Assignment weighting 15% - Lecturer in Charge: Minh Le
MonHeli is a thriving helicopter company. The company owns a range of helicopters whose
standard cost of hire may vary depending on the type of charter and the number of hours
flown. Each helicopter is identified by their call sign; much like a registration number
identifies a car. The total time each helicopter is flown is recorded to keep track of
maintenance schedules. The standard cost of hire for a helicopter depends on the
helicopter type however this may be varied over time and for certain clients (eg. frequent
flyers). Each of the helicopters has a fixed number of passenger seats available. The
company’s charter work is categorised as: scenic flight, transport, filming, geological survey,
fire mapping, fuel reduction burning, logging coupe regeneration burning, aerial seeding,
training, and search and rescue.
The company has a number of employees consisting of pilots, engineers and a secretary.
Details are kept of each employee’s name, address, contact number, Tax File Number,
salary and next of kin. As only qualified pilots are allowed to take charters, records are kept
of the pilots’ endorsements. An endorsement is the credential a pilot has to fly a certain type
of helicopter. Therefore, when a pilot is endorsed to fly a certain helicopter type (eg.
JetRanger) a record is kept of the pilot’s endorsement for this type of helicopter and the total
hours the pilot has flown in this helicopter type. This indicates the pilot’s level of experience
in this type of aircraft. For a pilot to have a current endorsement for a helicopter type they
must be reviewed annually and the date of their last review is kept for each of their
endorsements.
Charters can vary in time – from several days to part of an hour. A charter is flown by one
pilot using one helicopter. As clients only pay for the time spent flying, a record is kept of
every time the helicopter takes off and lands during a charter flight (a leg) - a charter may
consist of one or more legs. When a client initially makes a booking, the destination, the
estimated time of departure (ETD) and estimated time of arrival (ETA) are recorded for each
leg of the charter. For example, a client may book a helicopter to fly them from Gondor
Airport to Rivendell for a 10:00 am meeting, then leave at 12:00 noon to fly to Mount Doom
for 3 hours skiing and then back to Gondor Airport by 6:00 pm.

/*
Q1

List all endorsements held by pilots where the endorsements last annual
review date was after 31st March 2020.

Show the helicopter type number, employee number, employee lastname, employee
firstname and the date of the review.

This listing should be displayed in ascending order of the last annual review date.

Allow short or long form of month (Mon or Month)
*/

SELECT
    ht_nbr,
    emp.emp_nbr,
    emp_lname,
    emp_fname,
    to_char(end_last_annual_review, 'Dy dd Mon YYYY') AS review_date
FROM
         mh.employee emp
    JOIN mh.endorsement en
    ON emp.emp_nbr = en.emp_nbr
WHERE
    end_last_annual_review > TO_DATE('31-Mar-2020', 'dd-Mon-yyyy')
ORDER BY
    end_last_annual_review;

/*
Q2
List those charters where special requirements are specified.

Show the charter number, client number, client lastname, client firstname and
the special requirements specified.

This listing should be displayed in ascending order of the charter number.
*/

SELECT
    charter_nbr,
    c.client_nbr,
    client_lname,
    client_fname,
    charter_special_reqs
FROM
         mh.client c
    JOIN mh.charter ch
    ON c.client_nbr = ch.client_nbr
WHERE
    charter_special_reqs IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY
    charter_nbr;

/*
Q3

List the charter details for those charters which meet the following requirements:
- destination is Mount Doom, and
- the charter cost is less than $1000 per hour, or no special requirements are specified

Show the charter number, client lastname, client firstname and the charter cost per hour.
The client name should be listed in a single column called FULLNAME (sample output: Gandalf The Grey).

The listing should be displayed in the descending order of the client FULLNAME.

*/

SELECT DISTINCT
    ch.charter_nbr,
    ltrim(client_fname
          || ' '
          || client_lname) AS FULLNAME,
    charter_cost_per_hour
FROM
         mh.client c
    JOIN mh.charter ch
    ON c.client_nbr = ch.client_nbr
    JOIN mh.charter_leg cl
    ON ch.charter_nbr = cl.charter_nbr
    JOIN mh.location l
    ON cl.location_nbr_destination = l.location_nbr
WHERE
        upper(location_name) = upper('Mount Doom')
    AND ( charter_cost_per_hour < 1000
          OR charter_special_reqs IS NULL )
ORDER BY
    FULLNAME DESC;

/*
Q4

For each helicopter type for which there are at least two helicopters of that
type, list the total number of helicopters of that type.

Show the helicopter type number, helicopter type name and the number of
helicopters owned.

The listing should be displayed in the descending order of number of helicopters.

*/

SELECT
    ht.ht_nbr,
    ht_name,
    COUNT(*) AS total_no_helicopters
FROM
         mh.helicopter_type ht
    JOIN mh.helicopter h
    ON ht.ht_nbr = h.ht_nbr
GROUP BY
    ht.ht_nbr,
    ht_name
HAVING
    COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY
    total_no_helicopters DESC;

/*
Q5

For each location that has been used as the origin of a charter leg more than once,
list the location as well as the number of times it has been used as an origin

Show location number, location name and the number of times the location has
been used as an origin.

The listing should be displayed in the ascending order of the number of times a
location has been used as an origin.

*/

SELECT
    l.location_nbr,
    location_name,
    COUNT(*) AS usedasorgin
FROM
         mh.charter_leg cl
    JOIN mh.location l
    ON ( l.location_nbr = cl.location_nbr_origin )
GROUP BY
    l.location_nbr,
    location_name
HAVING
    COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY
    usedasorgin;

/*
Q6

List the number of hours flown for EVERY helicopter type.

Show helicopter type number, helicopter type name and the total number of hours flown. Types
which have no recorded hours should be shown as 0 hours flown.

The listing should be displayed in the ascending order of hours flown.

*/

SELECT
    ht.ht_nbr,
    ht_name,
    nvl(SUM(heli_hrs_flown), 0) AS hoursflown
FROM
    mh.helicopter_type ht
    LEFT OUTER JOIN mh.helicopter h
    ON ( ht.ht_nbr = h.ht_nbr )
GROUP BY
    ht.ht_nbr,
    ht_name
ORDER BY
    hoursflown;

/*
Q7

List all the completed charters that Frodo Baggins has flown, with the most recent
charters appearing at the top of the list. A completed charter means all legs have
been flown.

Show the charter number and the date/time of departure for leg one ( note: a
charter may span several days).

Note: the DISTINCT is not required in the subselect to identify completed charters

*/

SELECT
    c.charter_nbr,
    to_char(MIN(cl_etd), 'dd-Mon-yyyy hh24:mi') AS "Charter Date/Time"
FROM
    (
             mh.charter c
        JOIN mh.charter_leg    cl
        ON cl.charter_nbr = c.charter_nbr
    )
    JOIN mh.employee       em
    ON c.emp_nbr = em.emp_nbr
WHERE
        upper(emp_fname) = upper('Frodo')
    AND upper(emp_lname) = upper('Baggins')
    AND c.charter_nbr NOT IN (
        SELECT DISTINCT
            charter_nbr
        FROM
            mh.charter_leg
        WHERE
            cl_ata IS NULL
    )
GROUP BY
    c.charter_nbr
ORDER BY
    MIN(cl_etd) DESC;

/*
Q8

List those completed charters where the total cost is less than the average total
cost for all charters. The total cost for a charter is obtained by multiplying the
charter cost per hour with the actual duration obtained from the leg actual departure
and actual arrival times. A completed charter means all legs have been flown.

Show charter number, client number, client lastname,
client firstname, total charter cost. The total charter cost should be
rounded to two decimal digits and displayed with a $ symbol e.g. $1234.56

For this question only, if either client name is empty '-' should be displayed.

The listing should be displayed in the descending order of total charter cost.

=> Allow range for LPAD 12 - 16, depends on display font size used.
=> Allow NVL on client_lname although not needed due to data model

*/

SELECT
    cl.charter_nbr,
    c.client_nbr,
    client_lname,
    nvl(client_fname, '-')       AS client_fname,
    lpad(to_char((SUM((cl_ata - cl_atd) * 24 * charter_cost_per_hour)), '$9999.99'),
         16)                    AS totalchartercost
FROM
         mh.client c
    JOIN mh.charter        ch
    ON ( c.client_nbr = ch.client_nbr )
    JOIN mh.charter_leg    cl
    ON ( ch.charter_nbr = cl.charter_nbr )
WHERE
    ch.charter_nbr NOT IN (
        SELECT DISTINCT
            charter_nbr
        FROM
            mh.charter_leg
        WHERE
            cl_ata IS NULL
    )
GROUP BY
    cl.charter_nbr,
    c.client_nbr,
    client_lname,
    client_fname
HAVING
    SUM((cl_ata - cl_atd) * 24 * charter_cost_per_hour) < (
        SELECT
            AVG(SUM((cl_ata - cl_atd) * 24 * charter_cost_per_hour))
        FROM
                 mh.charter ch
            JOIN mh.charter_leg cl
            ON ( ch.charter_nbr = cl.charter_nbr )
        WHERE
            ch.charter_nbr NOT IN (
                SELECT DISTINCT
                    charter_nbr
                FROM
                    mh.charter_leg
                WHERE
                    cl_ata IS NULL
            )
        GROUP BY
            cl.charter_nbr
    )
ORDER BY
    totalchartercost DESC;

/*
Q9

Which charters have been able to depart at the time estimated for all
legs of its flight?

Show the charter number, pilot’s name and the client’s name.

Order the output by charter number

DISTINCT in sub selects not required, MINUS is a set operation

*/

SELECT
    charter_nbr,
    ltrim(emp_fname
          || ' '
          || emp_lname)       AS pilotname,
    ltrim(client_fname
          || ' '
          || client_lname)    AS clientname
FROM
         mh.charter
    NATURAL JOIN mh.employee
    NATURAL JOIN mh.client
WHERE
    charter_nbr IN (
        SELECT
            distinct charter_nbr
        FROM
            mh.charter_leg
        WHERE
            cl_atd IS NOT NULL
        MINUS
        SELECT
            distinct charter_nbr
        FROM
            mh.charter_leg
        WHERE
            cl_etd <> cl_atd
    )
ORDER BY
    charter_nbr;

/*
Q10

For each client, list the name of their favorite destination location/s and
how many times they have visited that destination. The clients favorite
destination will be the location/s they visit the most based on completed
legs.

Show the client number, client full name in one column, the name of the
destination location and the number of times the location has been visited
by the client.

Order the output by the client number. Where a client has several favourite
destinations order them by destination name.

*/

SELECT
    cl.client_nbr,
    ltrim(client_fname
          || ' '
          || client_lname) AS client_fullname,
    location_name AS destination,
    COUNT(*) AS number_of_time_visited
FROM
         mh.client cl
    JOIN mh.charter ch
    ON cl.client_nbr = ch.client_nbr
    JOIN mh.charter_leg chl
    ON ch.charter_nbr = chl.charter_nbr
    JOIN mh.location l
    ON chl.location_nbr_destination = l.location_nbr
WHERE
    cl_ata IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY
    cl.client_nbr,
    ltrim(client_fname
          || ' '
          || client_lname),
    location_name
HAVING
    COUNT(*) = (
        SELECT
            MAX(COUNT(*))
        FROM
                 mh.charter ch2
            JOIN mh.charter_leg chl2
            ON ch2.charter_nbr = chl2.charter_nbr
        WHERE
                ch2.client_nbr = cl.client_nbr
            AND cl_ata IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY
            ch2.client_nbr,
            location_nbr_destination
    )
ORDER BY
    client_nbr,
    destination;

        emp.emp_fname = 'Frodo'
    AND emp.emp_lname = 'Baggins'
    AND ch.charter_nbr NOT IN (
        SELECT charter_nbr FROM mh.charter_leg WHERE cl_ata IS NULL
    )
GROUP BY
    ch.charter_nbr
ORDER BY
    charter_departure DESC;

/*
    Q8
*/
-- PLEASE PLACE REQUIRED SQL STATEMENT FOR THIS PART HERE
-- ENSURE your query has a semicolon (;) at the end of this answer
SELECT
    ch.charter_nbr,
    cl.client_nbr,
    NVL(cl.client_lname, '-') AS client_lname,
    NVL(cl.client_fname, '-') AS client_fname,
    TO_CHAR(ROUND(SUM((clg.cl_ata - clg.cl_atd) * 24 * ch.charter_cost_per_hour), 2), '$9999.99') AS totalchartercost
FROM
         mh.charter ch
    JOIN mh.client cl ON ch.client_nbr = cl.client_nbr
    JOIN mh.charter_leg clg ON ch.charter_nbr = clg.charter_nbr
WHERE
    ch.charter_nbr NOT IN (
        SELECT charter_nbr FROM mh.charter_leg WHERE cl_ata IS NULL
    )
GROUP BY
    ch.charter_nbr,
    cl.client_nbr,
    cl.client_lname,
    cl.client_fname
HAVING
    SUM((clg.cl_ata - clg.cl_atd) * 24 * ch.charter_cost_per_hour) < (
        SELECT AVG(total)
        FROM (
            SELECT SUM((clg2.cl_ata - clg2.cl_atd) * 24 * ch2.charter_cost_per_hour) AS total
            FROM mh.charter ch2
                JOIN mh.charter_leg clg2 ON ch2.charter_nbr = clg2.charter_nbr
            WHERE ch2.charter_nbr NOT IN (
                SELECT charter_nbr FROM mh.charter_leg WHERE cl_ata IS NULL
            )
            GROUP BY ch2.charter_nbr
        )
    )
ORDER BY
    totalchartercost DESC;

/*
    Q9
*/
-- PLEASE PLACE REQUIRED SQL STATEMENT FOR THIS PART HERE
-- ENSURE your query has a semicolon (;) at the end of this answer
SELECT
    ch.charter_nbr,
    emp.emp_fname
    || ' '
    || emp.emp_lname AS pilotname,
    cl.client_fname
    || ' '
    || cl.client_lname AS clientname
FROM
         mh.charter ch
    JOIN mh.employee emp ON ch.emp_nbr = emp.emp_nbr
    JOIN mh.client cl ON ch.client_nbr = cl.client_nbr
WHERE
    ch.charter_nbr IN (
        SELECT clg.charter_nbr
        FROM mh.charter_leg clg
        GROUP BY clg.charter_nbr
        HAVING MIN(clg.cl_etd - clg.cl_atd) = 0 AND MAX(clg.cl_etd - clg.cl_atd) = 0
    )
ORDER BY
    ch.charter_nbr;

/*
    Q10
*/
-- PLEASE PLACE REQUIRED SQL STATEMENT FOR THIS PART HERE
-- ENSURE your query has a semicolon (;) at the end of this answer
SELECT
    cl.client_nbr,
    cl.client_fname
    || ' '
    || cl.client_lname AS clientname,
    l.location_name,
    cnt.num_visits
FROM
         mh.client cl
    JOIN mh.charter ch ON cl.client_nbr = ch.client_nbr
    JOIN (
        SELECT
            ch2.client_nbr,
            clg2.location_nbr_destination,
            COUNT(*) AS num_visits
        FROM
                 mh.charter ch2
            JOIN mh.charter_leg clg2 ON ch2.charter_nbr = clg2.charter_nbr
        WHERE
            clg2.cl_ata IS NOT NULL
        GROUP BY
            ch2.client_nbr,
            clg2.location_nbr_destination
    ) cnt ON cl.client_nbr = cnt.client_nbr
    JOIN mh.location l ON cnt.location_nbr_destination = l.location_nbr
WHERE
    cnt.num_visits = (
        SELECT MAX(cnt2.num_visits)
        FROM (
            SELECT
                ch3.client_nbr,
                clg3.location_nbr_destination,
                COUNT(*) AS num_visits
            FROM
                     mh.charter ch3
                JOIN mh.charter_leg clg3 ON ch3.charter_nbr = clg3.charter_nbr
            WHERE
                clg3.cl_ata IS NOT NULL
            GROUP BY
                ch3.client_nbr,
                clg3.location_nbr_destination
        ) cnt2
        WHERE cnt2.client_nbr = cl.client_nbr
    )
ORDER BY
    cl.client_nbr,
    l.location_name;

As natural habitats disappear, it is increasingly difficult to ensure that all species can survive in the
wild. One solution is to use zoos, reserves and other types of organizations that keep wild animals in
captivity as conservation centres for various species. To assist in recording the animals in captivity
and their offspring, a Centre Breeding database is to be created in which all centres will record the
animals which they hold and the breeding events which take place at the centres.
Database designers have considered the requirements and arrived at the following data model:
Page 1 of 8


==================== Page 2 ====================

Model Details:
SPECIES
Records the details of the species, the name of the species, as well as its popular name and the
genus and family to which the species belongs. The relevant details for the Tasmanian Devil, for
instance, are as follows ...
Genus: Sarcophilus
Species: harrisii
Popular name: Tasmanian Devil
Family: DASYURIDAE
It is also important to know the species’ natural range, which is usually given as a description of the
geographic regions and natural habitats where a species is found in the wild.

spec_name | The species name

spec_family | The species family name

ANIMAL
Records the details of all animals held at a particular centre

// The following is converted from a table

center_id | The "home" center where the animal is normally located. Animals are sometimes located at other centres for a breeding event

LLM tutor feedback:
The student's SQL script for Task 2(b) is mostly correct, but needs improvement in several areas.  The insertion of 12 animals and 4 breeding events is good, and the data appears to meet the constraints (wild-caught animals, no Tasmanian Devils to AUS10/AUS20, dates within range).  However, the script lacks crucial elements for a robust solution.

The use of hardcoded primary keys (animal_id and brevent_id) is a significant issue.  The assignment explicitly requires the use of sequences for these values.  The script should be modified to use sequences for automatic primary key generation.

The script should also be wrapped in a single transaction block using `BEGIN TRANSACTION` and `COMMIT` statements.  This ensures data integrity and consistency.

The script should include error handling (e.g., checking for constraint violations) to make it more robust.  The current script lacks this crucial aspect.

Finally, the script should be more efficient.  Instead of separate INSERT statements for each animal, consider using a single INSERT statement with multiple rows.  This can improve performance.

Overall, the student demonstrates a basic understanding of SQL insertion, but needs to improve their understanding of transactions, sequences, and error handling.  The script needs significant revision to meet the assignment requirements fully.

Small Business Website Redesign
Create a high-fidelity mock-up image of a proposed website redesign for a local business. Write a short
report to document the redesign, providing theory-based justifications for important changes made.
Website Redesign Guidelines
• Create a high-fidelity mock-up image only - do not code a working webpage.
• Redesign the homepage of the website only - the entire homepage, no other pages.
• You can do either a desktop or mobile redesign - choose one only.
• Choose an appropriate tool to create a redesign mock-up that is of presentable quality.
• Any site content and design elements can be altered, with appropriate documented justification.
• Unnecessary features can be removed, missing features can be added.
• Regardless of local language, your redesign mock-up must be presented in English language.
Report Guidelines
• The report must be between 500 and 1000 words.
• Justify decisions responsible for significant visual design changes that you have made.
• Consider reasons for changes made to page layout and navigation menus.
• Justify any changes made to the site’s content and other information architecture issues.
• You may include reference images and diagrams – these do not contribute to the word count.
• Include a separate page for references – this does not contribute to the word count.
Marking Criteria (15%)
• Implementation of redesign mock-up
• Identification and critique of design issues
• Consideration of client and target demographic needs
• Evidence of research, justification of design decisions
• Report structure, formatting, and references

other unavoidable circumstance, please contact your tutor to apply for special consideration.

Choosing a local business website
The redesign must be for a real local small business with an existing website. Find a site that would
benefit from a redesign! If you can immediately find 5 things wrong with the site, it may be a good choice.
• Local: The business is located in the city/state/province in which you currently live.
• Small: The business must not be a well-known brand. It must operate in one city only.
• Existing: The business’ existing website needs to be currently accessible online.
If you are having difficulty finding a suitable business site to redesign, try using Google Maps (or other map
service) to search for different types of businesses in your local area.
Researching your site re-design
Your redesign should attempt to solve real problems found in the existing site.
• Content that is not useful, inappropriate, or missing
• Visual design and branding that does not suit the business or audience
• Information architecture problems that make content hard to locate
• Usability and technical issues that make the site difficult to use
The most important redesign decisions you make should be supported with appropriate reasoning that
takes into consideration:
• Client business goals
• Target demographic preferences and user goals
• Research into competing websites

Virtual student submission:
Report on website redesign

Introduction  
The website chosen for redesign is for a local business, The Little Bookshop, focusing on desktop users. The Little Bookshop is an independent bookstore located in the city, specializing in curated selections of children’s books, young adult fiction, and literary gifts. The business aims to foster a love of reading in the local community, with a target audience of parents, teachers, and young readers, primarily families and educators in the area.  
For the redesign, I compared the current site to other independent bookstores such as Readings (Melbourne), The Younger Sun, and Booktopia, to identify best practices and areas for improvement.

Identification and critique of design issues – Structure  
The original homepage of The Little Bookshop features a cluttered layout, with a dense sidebar menu listing all book categories, events, and contact information. The navigation is overwhelming, especially for first-time visitors, and the most important content (featured books, store events) is pushed down the page.  
In the redesign, the navigation was simplified into a horizontal menu at the top, with dropdowns for main categories (Books, Events, About, Contact). This change follows the convention seen on Readings and Booktopia, making it easier for users to find what they need. The sidebar was removed to allow more space for featured content and to reduce visual clutter.  
The homepage structure was reorganized to prioritize a large hero image carousel at the top, showcasing new arrivals and upcoming events, followed by sections for featured books and staff picks. This approach draws attention to key offerings and aligns with user expectations for a modern bookstore website.

Identification and critique of design issues – Content  
The original site contains lengthy paragraphs about the store’s history and mission at the top of the homepage, which can deter users looking for books or event information. In the redesign, this content was condensed into a brief welcome message, with a link to a dedicated “About” page for those interested in learning more.  
The events section was previously buried at the bottom of the homepage. In the new design, upcoming events are highlighted in a dedicated section near the top, with clear calls to action to RSVP or learn more. This change reflects the importance of community engagement for the business and makes it easier for users to participate.  
The original homepage also lacked a search bar, which is essential for users looking for specific titles or authors. A prominent search bar was added to the top right of the page, following the standard seen on other bookstore sites.  
Additionally, the original site did not feature any customer reviews or testimonials. In the redesign, a small section for customer testimonials was added below the featured books, providing social proof and building trust with new visitors.

Identification and critique of design issues – Visual design  
The original visual design used a muted beige and brown color palette, with small serif fonts and minimal imagery. While this gives a cozy feel, it can appear outdated and lacks visual interest. In the redesign, the color scheme was updated to include brighter accent colors (teal and coral) for buttons and highlights, while retaining a warm, inviting background.  
Large, high-quality images of books and in-store events were added to make the site more visually engaging. The font was changed to a clean, modern sans-serif for improved readability, especially for younger users and those with visual impairments.  
Accessibility was also considered by ensuring sufficient color contrast for text and interactive elements, following guidelines from the W3C Web Accessibility Initiative.

LLM tutor feedback:
The student's report demonstrates a good understanding of website redesign principles.  The critique of the original site's issues is solid, focusing on clutter, navigation, and content organization.  The proposed redesign addresses these issues effectively, with a clear rationale for changes to layout, navigation, and content prioritization.  The justification for the visual design changes is also well-reasoned, referencing the need for a more modern and engaging aesthetic.

However, the report could be strengthened by:

* **More specific examples:** While the student mentions competing sites, deeper analysis of *why* those sites are effective would enhance the justification.  For instance, instead of just saying the navigation is improved, explain *how* the new navigation structure is more intuitive based on user behavior observed on competitor sites.
* **Quantifiable data:**  If possible, incorporating data on user behavior on the original site (e.g., bounce rates, time on page) would provide stronger evidence for the need for change.
* **Target demographic analysis:**  The report mentions the target demographic, but a more detailed analysis of their needs and preferences would strengthen the justification for design choices.  For example, how do the proposed changes cater to the needs of parents, teachers, and young readers?
* **Accessibility considerations:** While the report mentions accessibility, a more in-depth discussion of specific accessibility guidelines (e.g., WCAG) and how the redesign addresses them would be beneficial.
* **Client goals:**  The report touches on client goals, but a clearer articulation of how the redesign aligns with those goals would strengthen the argument.  What are the specific business objectives the redesign aims to achieve?

Overall, the report is well-structured and demonstrates a good understanding of the assignment requirements.  By incorporating the suggestions above, the student can further enhance the depth and persuasiveness of the arguments.

LLM tutor feedback:
The student's submission shows a good attempt at normalizing the data and creating a logical model.  However, there are several areas needing improvement.

Normalization: While the 3NF tables are presented, the crucial dependency diagrams are missing.  Without these, it's difficult to assess the correctness of the normalization process.  The submission lacks the step-by-step UNF, 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF transformations for each form, as required.  This is a significant deficiency.

Logical Model: The logical model is a good start, but lacks crucial details.  The use of Crow's Foot notation is correct, but the lack of data types and sizes is a problem.  The comments for attributes are a positive step, but they should be more descriptive and explain the purpose of each attribute.  The inclusion of a surrogate key is also good.  However, the choice of which relation to add a surrogate key to needs justification.  The check clauses/lookup tables are not clearly identified.  The relationships between tables are not fully specified (cardinality and connectivity).

Schema File: The schema file (ad_schema.sql) is a critical component.  The submission states it includes DROP table commands, but this needs verification.  The spool/echo commands are also important to capture the output.  The header comments are good practice.  However, the submission lacks the actual schema file content.

Overall: The student needs to meticulously complete the normalization process, including dependency diagrams, and provide a more comprehensive logical model with data types, sizes, and fully specified relationships.  The schema file and its output are also essential parts of the submission.  The missing dependency diagrams and the lack of data type information in the logical model are major issues that need to be addressed.

FIT3174 IT STRATEGY AND GOVERNANCE
Semester 1, 2021
Assignment 2: Evaluating IT Systems Failure: A Case Study
Value: This assignment is worth 10% of your final assessment in the unit.
Type of Assignment: Individual
Submission Date: Friday, Week 11 (21st May 2021), 11:55 pm
Submission of the Assignments: You are required to submit your assignment to
Moodle/Turnitin. The file names should contain Unit Code, assignment number and your
student ID number, similar to the following example:
FIT3174A2_StudentID.docx OR FIT3174A2_StudentID.pdf
Learning outcomes:
Demonstrate the understanding of IT governance, and how IT projects can be
successfully completed with appropriate attention to IT critical success factors,
implementation issues and IT governance. Discuss the applications of the well-known IT
governance frameworks commonly used in organisations.
Broad Assignment Aims
Students are presented with a case study to:
(a) critically review the organisation’s need for good IT governance,
(b) critically evaluate the implementation experience of the organisation and suggest
ways to improve its IT governance by drawing on the theoretical constructs involved in
IT governance frameworks like COBIT,
(c) demonstrate how a chosen IT governance framework can affect the organisation’s
corporate and IT strategies, thereby influencing the values and risks associated with IT-
enabled investments.
Students are required to derive the strategic motivations, barriers, impact, and lessons of
the case organisation in implementing IT governance frameworks like COBIT, relating
them to the IT governance scholarly literature.
Report Outline:
A recommended report outline is as follows:
• Title page (with assignment title, student name, ID, unit code, tutors name)
• Table of contents
• Executive summary or Abstract
• Introduction section, e.g. purpose, scope
• Body (responses to the assignment tasks)
o a critical review of the case study
o evaluate the applications of IT governance frameworks
• Conclusion

ABSTRACT  
This report analyses the Boeing 737 MAX project failure through the lens of IT governance frameworks, focusing on implementation issues, critical success factors, and how frameworks like COBIT and ITIL could have addressed the problems.

TASK 1  
Problem Analysis  
The Boeing 737 MAX project, which resulted in two major crashes in 2018 and 2019, highlights significant governance and project management failures. Three key implementation issues identified from the case are:

1. Inadequate risk assessment and management  
Boeing’s rush to compete with Airbus led to the rapid development of the 737 MAX, incorporating larger engines and the MCAS software to address aerodynamic changes. However, the risks associated with these changes, especially the reliance on a single Angle of Attack (AoA) sensor for MCAS, were not properly assessed or mitigated. The lack of comprehensive risk analysis meant that critical safety issues were overlooked, contributing to the fatal accidents.

2. Poor communication and lack of transparency  
There was a breakdown in communication between engineers, management, and regulatory bodies. Engineers raised concerns about the MCAS system, but these were not effectively communicated to decision-makers or the FAA. Additionally, Boeing did not provide adequate information or training to airlines and pilots regarding the new system, leading to confusion and inability to respond to MCAS malfunctions during flight.

3. Misaligned organizational culture and priorities  
After the merger with McDonnell Douglas, Boeing’s culture shifted from engineering excellence to prioritizing cost-cutting and shareholder value. This change in priorities led to decisions that compromised safety, such as minimizing pilot training requirements and downplaying the need for simulator training. The focus on short-term financial gains over long-term safety and reputation was a critical factor in the project’s failure.

Solution Development  
The Boeing 737 MAX project could have succeeded if key IT critical success factors were addressed:

1. Effective risk management  
Implementing a robust risk management process would have identified the potential hazards associated with the MCAS system and the new engine configuration. Regular risk assessments and mitigation strategies could have prevented the deployment of unsafe systems.

2. Strong communication and stakeholder engagement  
Ensuring open channels of communication between engineers, management, regulators, and end-users (pilots and airlines) is essential. Early and transparent sharing of information about system changes and potential risks would have enabled better decision-making and preparedness.

3. Comprehensive training and change management  
Providing thorough training and clear documentation for pilots and maintenance crews on the new MCAS system would have equipped them to handle malfunctions. Change management processes should have included stakeholder input and feedback to ensure readiness for operational changes.

Solution development principles  
Good governance was lacking in the Boeing 737 MAX project, as evidenced by the FAA’s reliance on Boeing’s self-certification and the company’s reluctance to address known issues. Effective governance would have enforced accountability, transparency, and adherence to safety standards. The absence of independent oversight and a culture of responsibility allowed critical issues to persist. Companies that prioritize governance, such as by recalling unsafe products or halting sales until issues are resolved, demonstrate resilience and maintain trust. In Boeing’s case, the lack of governance led to loss of life, financial losses, and reputational damage.

Task 2  
COBIT principles  
Applying the COBIT 2019 framework’s six principles could have mitigated the Boeing 737 MAX project’s failures:

1. Provide Stakeholder Value  
Boeing failed to deliver long-term value to stakeholders by prioritizing speed and cost over safety. COBIT emphasizes aligning IT initiatives with stakeholder needs and value creation, which would have shifted focus to safety and reliability.

2. Holistic Approach  
A holistic view would have integrated technical, organizational, and regulatory perspectives, ensuring that changes to the aircraft were evaluated in the context of the entire system, not just isolated components.

3. Dynamic Governance System  
COBIT’s principle of adaptability would have encouraged Boeing to respond to emerging risks and feedback, rather than rigidly adhering to initial plans despite evidence of problems.

4. Governance Distinct from Management  
Clear separation between governance (oversight, policy-setting) and management (execution) was lacking. COBIT stresses this distinction, which could have ensured independent review and accountability.

5. Tailored to Enterprise Needs  
Boeing’s governance should have reflected its legacy of engineering excellence, rather than adopting a one-size-fits-all approach focused on cost-cutting.

COBIT design factors  
Three relevant design factors for Boeing:

1. Enterprise strategy and goals  
Boeing’s strategy shifted towards rapid market response and cost reduction. Governance should have ensured that safety and quality remained core objectives, with clear alignment between strategy and operational goals.

2. Risk profile  
Boeing operated in a high-risk industry. Governance systems should have prioritized risk identification, assessment, and mitigation, with regular reviews and updates as new information emerged.

3. Regulatory requirements  
Aviation is heavily regulated. Governance design should have incorporated compliance with external standards and ensured independent verification, rather than relying on self-certification.

COBIT objectives and components  
Four governance/management objectives and relevant components:

1. Ensured risk optimization  
- Processes: Implement structured risk assessment and mitigation processes throughout the project lifecycle.  
- Information: Ensure accurate, timely sharing of risk-related information across all levels.

2. Managed innovation  
- Organizational structures: Foster a culture that encourages innovation while maintaining safety standards.  
- People, skills, and competencies: Invest in training and development to support innovative solutions.

3. Managed quality  
- Principles, policies, procedures: Establish and enforce quality control procedures for all project phases.  
- Organizational structures: Assign clear responsibility for quality assurance, independent of project delivery teams.

4. Ensured governance framework setting and maintenance  
- People, skills, and competencies: Appoint leaders with relevant technical expertise and a commitment to governance.  
- Processes: Regularly review and update governance frameworks to reflect lessons learned and industry best practices.

Task 3  
COBIT 2019 Alternative: ITIL Framework  
The IT Infrastructure Library (ITIL) framework focuses on managing IT services to align with business and customer needs throughout the service lifecycle.

Key concepts:  
- Purpose: ITIL aims to optimize IT service delivery and support, ensuring that IT contributes value to the business.  
- Coverage: ITIL covers service strategy, design, transition, operation, and continual improvement.  
- Application: ITIL is applied to manage IT processes, improve efficiency, and enhance customer satisfaction.

Differences from COBIT 2019:  
- COBIT provides a comprehensive governance and management framework for all IT activities, focusing on alignment with business goals and risk management.  
- ITIL is more operational, emphasizing service management and delivery rather than overall governance.

Applying ITIL to Boeing 737 MAX:  
If Boeing had applied ITIL principles, the MCAS system would have been managed as a critical IT service. ITIL’s focus on continual improvement and feedback would have encouraged ongoing monitoring and refinement of the system. Collaboration between engineers, IT, and end-users (pilots) would have been promoted, ensuring that issues were identified and addressed promptly. ITIL’s emphasis on training and knowledge management would have ensured that pilots were adequately prepared to handle new systems, reducing the risk of operational failures.

By integrating ITIL’s service management approach with COBIT’s governance framework, Boeing could have achieved both effective oversight and operational excellence, addressing the implementation issues and supporting critical success factors identified in Task 1.

For instance, linking the lack of pilot training to a specific critical success factor (e.g., user acceptance) would enhance the analysis. Overall, the report needs more in-depth analysis and concrete examples to support the arguments.  Stronger connections between the theoretical frameworks (COBIT, ITIL) and the specific events of the Boeing case are crucial for a higher grade.  Ensure that the chosen frameworks are applied in a way that demonstrates a clear understanding of their strengths and limitations in the context of the case.  Finally, the report should be more concise and focused on the key issues.

Attribute 16, Humidity9am: humidity (percent) at 9am
Attribute 17, Humidity3pm: humidity (percent) at 3pm
Attribute 18, Pressure9am: atmospheric pressure (hpa) reduced to mean
sea level at 9am
Attribute 19, Pressure3pm: atmospheric pressure (hpa) reduced to mean
sea level at 3pm
Attribute 20, Temp9am: temperature (degrees C) at 9am
Attribute 21, Temp3pm: temperature (degrees C) at 3pm
Attribute 22, RainToday: boolean: 1 if precipitation (mm) in the 24
hours to 9am exceeds 1mm, otherwise 0
Attribute 23, CloudyTomorrow: the target variable. If the average
fraction of sky obscured by cloud at 9am and 3pm is greater than 4
"oktas", the day is classified as cloudy. Otherwise the day is
classified as not cloudy. (Oktas record how many eigths of the sky are
obscured by cloud.)

This is the original website before redesign. At first glance, the website looks outdated and not very user-friendly. The color scheme is dull, and there is a lot of unused space which makes the homepage look empty. The navigation bar is not clear, and the menu items are not easy to find. There is also no clear call-to-action, so visitors may not know what to do when they arrive on the site.

From a user’s perspective, the homepage does not highlight the main services of the business. Important information like opening hours, contact details, and location are hard to find. The images used are low quality and do not represent the business well. There is also no section for customer reviews or testimonials, which could help build trust with new customers.

The layout is not well organized. The sections are not separated clearly, and the text is not aligned properly. The font size is inconsistent, and some text is hard to read because of the background color. There is also no search function, which makes it difficult for users to find specific information quickly. Overall, the website does not make a good first impression and could cause visitors to leave quickly.


==================== Page 2 ====================

After redesigning the website, I made several changes to improve the user experience and visual appeal. I kept the business logo at the top left corner to maintain brand identity. Next to the logo, I placed a clear navigation menu with main sections: Home, Services, About Us, Reviews, and Contact. I also added a search bar on the right side of the navigation, so users can easily find what they need.

The main banner now features a high-quality image that represents the business, with a bold headline and a call-to-action button (“Book Now”) to encourage visitors to take action. Below the banner, I created three main sections: Services Overview, Customer Reviews, and Location & Contact Info.

For the Services section, I used icons and short descriptions to make it easy for users to understand what the business offers. The Customer Reviews section features a few highlighted testimonials with customer photos to build trust. The Location & Contact Info section includes a map, address, phone number, and a contact form for quick inquiries.

I chose a modern color scheme with blue and white as the main colors, which looks clean and professional. The font is Arial, size 12 for body text and 16 bold for headings, to ensure readability. I also made sure there is enough spacing between sections to avoid clutter and make the homepage look organized.


==================== Page 3 ====================

One of the main problems with the original website was the lack of clear structure. In my redesign, I separated the homepage into distinct sections with clear headings. This helps users quickly find the information they are looking for. I also added a sticky navigation bar, so the menu is always visible as users scroll down the page.

The original site did not have any customer reviews, which are important for building trust. By adding a reviews section, I make it easier for new customers to see positive feedback from others. I also included a prominent call-to-action button in the banner, which was missing before. This guides users towards booking a service, which is a main goal for the business.

The images on the original site were not relevant or high quality. I replaced them with professional photos that match the business and make the site look more appealing. I also made sure all text is easy to read by using a simple font and good contrast between text and background.

For the contact section, I added a map and a simple contact form. This makes it easier for users to find the business location and get in touch. The search bar in the navigation helps users find specific information quickly, which improves usability.

Overall, my redesign focuses on making the homepage more user-friendly, visually appealing, and effective at achieving the business goals.

Overall impression  
You have clearly spotted key pain-points of the original site and explained the major visual/layout improvements you introduced. The redesign choices you list (hero image + CTA, sectional layout, sticky nav, reviews, map, modern palette) align well with current best practice.

Implementation of mock-up (≃3/5)  
Without seeing the JPG it is hard to judge fidelity, but your description suggests sound hierarchy and clearer navigation. Make sure the submitted image shows the entire scrolling homepage at realistic width, with consistent spacing and typographic scale.

Identification & critique of design issues (2/5)  
You mention dull colours, poor images, unclear nav, etc., yet the critique remains surface-level. Strengthen it by mapping each problem to a principle (e.g. lack of visual hierarchy → Gestalt proximity; small contrast → WCAG legibility; hidden menu items → Jakob Nielsen’s visibility of system status).

Client & target demographic (1/3)  
Little evidence is given about who the business serves or its goals beyond “booking a service”. Report structure & references (1.5/3)  
Current text is ~450 words—slightly under the 500-word minimum. Expand while adding clear sub-headings (Original Issues, Design Goals, Key Changes, Justifications, Conclusion). Image URLs are listed, but use a reference style (APA/Harvard) and add any literature/competitor sources you consult.

• Deepen critique with design/IA theory.

• Provide properly formatted references.

With these enhancements you can lift the report from a descriptive overview to a research-grounded professional rationale.

Current Progress  
During weeks 5 to 8, our team has made steady progress on the project. We have completed the main features of our web application and integrated the backend services. The system is now able to process user input and generate the expected output. We have also started preparing for user testing by setting up a test environment and drafting a feedback form. In addition, we have begun working on the technical documentation and interim report.

Issues Faced and Actions Taken  
One of the main challenges we encountered was related to deploying our backend service. Initially, we planned to use AWS EC2 for hosting, but due to budget constraints and limited free tier resources, we had to look for alternatives. After discussing with the team, I suggested using the Monash Faculty server, and I contacted the IT support team to request access. The process took a few days, but eventually we were granted access, which allowed us to deploy and test our application in a more stable environment.

Another issue was with the integration of the third-party API for data processing. The API had some undocumented changes which caused errors in our application. I spent some time debugging and reading through the API’s GitHub issues page. After identifying the problem, I updated our code to handle the new response format. I also documented the changes and shared them with the team to avoid similar issues in the future.

Project Progress (Week 5 – Week 8)  
We had a meeting with our supervisor to present our current progress and get feedback. The supervisor suggested improving the user interface to make it more intuitive, especially for first-time users. Based on this, I worked on redesigning some UI components and added tooltips to guide users through the process. I also implemented additional validation on the input forms to prevent invalid data from being submitted.

Our team completed the interim presentation during this period. We collaborated using Google Slides and divided the presentation sections among ourselves. We decided to record the presentation over Zoom, which made it easier to coordinate and allowed us to review the recording before submission. I was responsible for compiling the final video and uploading it to Moodle.

I also contributed to the technical documentation by writing the setup guide for the backend service. This involved detailing the installation steps, dependencies, and configuration required to run the service. I found this activity useful as it helped clarify some of the deployment steps that were previously unclear.

To prepare for user testing, I created a Google Form for collecting feedback and shared it with the team for review. We agreed on the questions to include and finalized the form. We also set up a schedule for inviting users to test the application and provide feedback.

Reflections  
Overall, I think the activities during this period were productive. The decision to switch hosting platforms was necessary and ultimately beneficial, as it provided a more reliable environment for our application. The process of debugging the API integration was challenging but rewarding, as it improved my problem-solving skills and understanding of third-party services. Working on the UI improvements also gave me a better appreciation for user experience design.

What to Do Next  
In the next four weeks, we plan to conduct user testing and collect feedback to identify areas for improvement. I will be responsible for monitoring the test environment and assisting users if they encounter any issues. After collecting enough feedback, I will help analyze the results and contribute to the test report. We will also continue working on the final documentation and ensure that all code is properly commented and organized. Additionally, we will schedule regular meetings to track progress and address any new challenges that arise.

Dear Jia Hao,

You have provided a clear, concise account of weeks 5–8 that covers progress, obstacles, and next-step intentions. The entry is well-structured and easy to read (good headings, logical flow) and demonstrates initiative (e.g., sourcing Monash servers, redesigning UI, preparing testing material).

What works well
• Relevance: All requested areas—technical progress, issues, planning—are addressed, so you comfortably satisfy the “relevant submission” band.

Opportunities to deepen reflection
1. Move beyond “what happened” to “so what/now what”.

Presentation tips
• A short introductory sentence giving overall sentiment (e.g., “A challenging yet rewarding fortnight…”) can create narrative cohesion.

Overall, you are on track for a solid mark in the 6-7 range. Incorporating the above layers of analysis and explicit learning take-aways would push the journal toward the 8-9 “deep reflection” band.

Keep up the good work!

FIT5205 Assignment 1: Critical Review

Who has written this article? Where has it been published? What is the expertise/reputation of the publication venue? When was it published?

The article was written by Karen Hao, a senior AI reporter at MIT Technology Review. It was published on MIT Technology Review’s website on February 19, 2021. MIT Technology Review is a well-established magazine that has been reporting on technology and its impacts since 1899. It is affiliated with the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, which is a globally recognized institution in science and technology. The publication is considered reputable and credible, especially in the field of emerging technologies.

What is the article about? Summarise the key argument(s) in your own words and identify key data challenges.

The article discusses the use of artificial intelligence (AI) in hiring processes, focusing on how AI-powered tools are being used by companies to screen job applicants. The author argues that while these tools are marketed as objective and efficient, they often perpetuate existing biases and can lead to unfair outcomes. The article highlights several key points:

Claim 1: AI hiring tools are not as objective as they seem.
1.1 The algorithms are trained on historical data, which often reflects existing biases in hiring practices, such as gender or racial discrimination.

Claim 2: Lack of transparency and accountability in AI systems.
2.1 Many companies using these tools do not fully understand how the algorithms work, making it difficult to identify or correct biased outcomes.

Claim 3: The use of AI in hiring can have significant negative impacts on job seekers.
3.1 Candidates may be unfairly screened out due to factors unrelated to their qualifications, such as their tone of voice or facial expressions during video interviews.

Claim 4: There is a need for better regulation and oversight.
4.1 The article suggests that without proper guidelines, the use of AI in hiring could worsen inequality in the job market.

The key data challenges identified include the quality and representativeness of training data, the opacity of algorithmic decision-making, and the difficulty in auditing or challenging automated decisions.

What evidence (e.g. academic research, government report, company statement, etc.) is referred to, described, and/or otherwise used to support the key argument(s)?

The article draws on a mix of sources to support its arguments. It references academic research on algorithmic bias, such as studies showing that AI systems can replicate and even amplify human prejudices. It also cites statements from AI ethics experts and interviews with job applicants who have experienced AI-driven hiring processes. Additionally, the article mentions company statements from vendors of AI hiring tools, who claim their products are fair and unbiased, but often provide little evidence to back up these claims. There are also references to government reports and policy discussions about the need for regulation in this area.

Where do you agree and where do you disagree with the key arguments? What evidence supports your views?

I agree with the author’s main argument that AI hiring tools are not inherently objective and can perpetuate bias if not carefully designed and monitored. The reliance on historical data is a significant issue, as it can encode past discriminatory practices into the algorithm. For example, a study by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) found that facial recognition algorithms had higher error rates for people of color, which suggests that similar issues could arise in AI hiring tools that analyze video interviews.

I also agree that the lack of transparency is a major problem. If companies cannot explain how their AI systems make decisions, it is impossible for candidates to challenge unfair outcomes. This lack of accountability undermines trust in the hiring process.

However, I think the article could have acknowledged that AI tools, if properly designed and regulated, have the potential to reduce some forms of bias by standardizing the evaluation process. For instance, structured interviews and skills assessments can be more consistent than unstructured human interviews. But this potential can only be realized if there is careful oversight and continuous monitoring for bias.

Which principle(s) of Beard & Longstaff’s Ethical Design Framework are most relevant to the issues discussed in this article? How does this framework help to evaluate the article’s key arguments?

The issues discussed in the article can be analyzed using several principles from Beard & Longstaff’s Ethical Design Framework. The most relevant principles are PR00: Ought Before Can, PR03: Responsibility, and PR05: Fairness.

PR00: Ought Before Can is about considering what should be done, not just what can be done with technology. The article questions whether it is ethical to use AI in hiring when the technology is not fully understood and may cause harm. This principle encourages us to think critically about the purpose and consequences of deploying AI in sensitive areas like employment.

PR03: Responsibility emphasizes the need for those who design and deploy AI systems to anticipate potential harms and take responsibility for their impacts. The article points out that many companies do not fully understand the tools they are using, which leads to a lack of accountability when things go wrong. Applying this principle would mean companies need to be more proactive in assessing and mitigating risks.

PR05: Fairness is directly related to the concerns about bias and discrimination in AI hiring tools. The article provides examples of how these systems can produce unfair outcomes, especially for marginalized groups. The framework’s focus on fairness supports the argument that AI systems must be designed and tested to ensure they do not disadvantage certain groups.

Overall, the Beard & Longstaff framework helps to evaluate the article’s key arguments by providing a structured way to assess the ethical implications of AI in hiring. It highlights the importance of considering not just technical feasibility, but also social and moral responsibilities.

==================== Page 1 ====================

Question 1:
a) Written only: Explain what a storage hierarchy is and its performance impact.
A storage hierarchy is a layered structure of memory and storage devices, ranked by their speed, cost, and capacity. At the top of the hierarchy are the fastest and most expensive types of memory, like CPU registers and cache, which are small in size but have very low access times. As we move down the hierarchy, we encounter main memory (RAM), then secondary storage like SSDs and HDDs, and finally archival storage like magnetic tapes, which are slowest but cheapest per byte. The performance impact is that the CPU can access frequently used data quickly if it is in the higher levels of the hierarchy, improving overall system speed, while less frequently used data is stored in slower, larger, and cheaper storage.

b) Written and in class: Explain the concepts of temporal and spatial locality.
Temporal locality: This refers to the tendency of a program to access the same memory locations repeatedly within a short period of time. If data has been accessed recently, it is likely to be accessed again soon.
Spatial locality: This is the tendency of a program to access memory locations that are close to each other within a short time frame. If a particular memory address is accessed, nearby addresses are likely to be accessed soon after.

c) Written only: Explain the operation of a simple directly mapped cache design.
A directly mapped cache is a cache structure where each block of main memory maps to exactly one cache line. The cache is organized into rows, each with a tag, a valid bit, and the data. The memory address is split into two parts: the index (LOW bits) and the tag (HIGH bits). The index selects which cache line to look at, and the tag is compared with the tag stored in that line. If the tags match and the valid bit is set, it is a cache hit and the data is used. If not, it is a cache miss, and the required block is fetched from main memory, replacing the existing data in that cache line.

==================== Page 2 ====================

If the valid bit is not set, it means the cache line is empty and needs to be filled from main memory. On a cache miss, the new data is loaded into the cache, and the tag and valid bit are updated. This design is simple but can lead to conflicts if multiple memory blocks map to the same cache line.

d) Written and in class: What is the purpose of a cache tag?
A cache tag is used to identify which block of main memory is currently stored in a particular cache line. It helps the cache controller determine if the data in a cache line corresponds to the requested memory address. If the tag matches the address and the valid bit is set, it is a cache hit.

Question 2:
a) Written and in class: Explain operation of a set associative cache.
A set associative cache is a compromise between a fully associative and a directly mapped cache. The cache is divided into sets, and each set contains multiple lines (ways). A memory address maps to a specific set using the index bits, but within that set, any line can be used to store the data. The tag is compared with all the lines in the set, and if any match, it is a cache hit. If none match, a cache miss occurs and one of the lines in the set is replaced (using a replacement policy like LRU). This reduces conflicts compared to a direct mapped cache.

b) Written and in class: Explain operation of a Harvard or split cache.
A Harvard or split cache architecture separates the cache into two parts: one for instructions and one for data. The instruction cache stores program instructions, while the data cache stores program data. This allows simultaneous access to instructions and data, improving performance. Each cache can be optimized for its specific access patterns, and instruction fetches do not interfere with data accesses.

==================== Page 3 ====================

By splitting the cache, the CPU can fetch instructions and data at the same time, which increases throughput. The instruction cache is optimized for sequential access, while the data cache is optimized for random access and read/write operations.

c) Written and in class: Explain operation of a multilevel cache (L1+L2; L1+L2+L3 [N/A]).
A multilevel cache system uses multiple layers of cache (L1, L2, and sometimes L3) to improve performance. L1 cache is the smallest and fastest, located closest to the CPU. L2 is larger and slower, and L3 (if present) is even larger and slower. When the CPU needs data, it first checks the L1 cache. If the data is not found (miss), it checks the L2 cache, and so on. If the data is not in any cache, it is fetched from main memory. When data is found in a lower-level cache, it is usually copied up to the higher-level cache for faster future access.

Question 3:
a) Written and in class: Explain the difference between write through and write back cache policies.

Write Through Cache Policy | Write Back Cache Policy
-------------------------- | -----------------------
Every write to the cache is immediately written to main memory | Writes are only made to the cache at first; main memory is updated later
Simple to implement, but can cause high memory traffic | More complex, but reduces memory traffic
Data in memory is always up to date | Data in memory may be stale until the cache line is written back (flushed)
No need for dirty bits | Requires dirty bits to track modified cache lines

b) Written only: Explain estimation methods for calculating cache performance from hit and miss ratios.

==================== Page 4 ====================

To estimate cache performance, we use the hit ratio (percentage of accesses found in the cache) and miss ratio (percentage not found). The effective memory access time (EAT) can be calculated using these ratios and the access times for cache and main memory.

For example, for a write through cache:
EAT = (Hit Ratio) * (Cache Access Time) + (Miss Ratio) * (Main Memory Access Time)

If there are separate read and write ratios, the formula can be expanded to account for the percentage of reads and writes, and their respective access times. For write back caches, the formula is similar, but write hits only update the cache, not main memory, so the access time for write hits is just the cache time.

By plugging in the hit and miss ratios, and the access times for cache and memory, we can estimate the average time to access memory.

Question 4:
a) Written only: Explain the difference between a CPU cache and an internal disk cache in a hard disk or solid state disk.

CPU Cache | Internal Disk Cache
---------- | ------------------
Located between CPU and main memory | Located inside the disk (HDD or SSD)
Very fast, small capacity (KBs to MBs) | Slower, larger capacity (MBs to GBs)
Stores frequently used instructions/data for the CPU | Buffers data being read from or written to the disk
Accessed by the CPU directly | Managed by the disk controller, not directly by the CPU
Optimized for speed and low latency | Optimized for throughput and reducing disk access times

The survey was conducted on the premises of my own house, with 2 of the access points being mine and the last one being a neighbours to the north of me. For context, AP1 is my home router, AP2 is my phone hotspot in my room and AP3 is my neighbours WiFi.  
Channel Occupancy: All Access points are running on different channels 149, 6 and 4 respectively. There may be some overlap between 6 and 4. It’s set to prioritise channels 1 and 8 on auto mode. This was done mainly through the application NetSpot.  
• Interference: Walls, particularly brick walls seem to hinder the signal strength significantly. This is demonstrated in measurement site 7 in relation to access point 1 in which the signal strength is slightly higher than sites inside the building, despite being ~3 meters from the building.

• Coverage: For AP1 [My Home Wifi] : Being situated in the corner, approximately 75% of the coverage lies outside the home, making coverage on the opposite end of the house [Site 1] weaker for no good reason. Optimal positioning of the access point would ideally be in the centre of the building for an even distribution.  
For AP2 [phone hotspot] it seems to have a larger reach than expected, considering site 4’s distance from access point 2, it maintains a fairly average connection. More than expected considering a phone would only likely be a meter or so from the desired device.  
For AP3, it is unapplicable. The router is somewhere in another persons house, however the fact I was able to get a connection from a fair distance suggests an impractical placement as it has no reason to reach my residence.  
• Attenuation: Brick walls definitely impede signal strength as seen by site 7, the walls in this residence do not however. They are a typical plaster wall, quite thin and as such shouldn’t provide much in the way of resistance. Laying on top of my phone saw no visible difference in signal strength.  
• One more aspect of your own choice – extrapolation and interpolation of AP from signals.  
Routers will emit signals in an almost circular pattern, as such by comparing signal strengths in different locations, the measurement sites closer to the site will be stronger and the ones further away will be weaker. Trial and error will allow you to map where the signals are strong and weak, slowly tracking where the AP is much like a game of battle ship.  
2. Cyber Security [350 Word Max] – Dutch Covid-19 Patient Data Leak  
2.1 Article Summary [ 200 Words ]  
Call centre employees for the Dutch Municipal Health Service had unrestricted access to the Dutch Health Ministry’s records and used their privilege to steal the information of Dutch citizens. This information, including their social security numbers, was then being sold online for malicious purposes. 2 men were arrested and their computers have been seized.  
2.2 System Identification [ 50 Words ]  
The system likely responsible for this incident is likely the patient database itself, unrestricted access made it very easy to take pictures with phones and spread them. It’s infeasible to monitor all employees in their homes, so the system should have made information harder to access.  
2.3 Problem Description [ 100 Words ]


==================== Page 4 ====================

Employees of call centres were given unrestricted access to highly sensitive information on thousands of citizens. Much of the information is of personal nature such as phone numbers, addresses and names. The ease of access coupled with employees being unmonitored made this information very vulnerable to exploitation. This problem resulted from oversight and an unreasonable amount of trust in their employees.  
2.4 Evaluate Significance and Seriousness.  
2.4.1 – Seriousness of Weakness  
The seriousness is of a high degree, the information available in the database is enough to find out who and where someone is. The addition of their social security number being available makes it very easy to blackmail someone or commit identity fraud, easily destroying someone’s life.  
2.4.2 – How to Exploit the Weakness  
To exploit this information, one could easily impersonate someone to a bank over the phone as all their identifying information is available. Alternatively, you could threaten to leak their information to malicious third parties and extort the individual for money. The exploit itself is just information access, so one might get a job in the ministry to get this information.  
2.4.3 – The Consequences of Exploitation  
The consequences relating to sensitive information being leaked are mainly related to identity fraud. Crimes committed in ones name that can ruin someone’s life, or credit cards opened under their name that can ruin their credit.  
Reactions :  
a) Technical Implications  
The ‘exploit’ in this situation is taking photos with a phone, so the appropriate reaction would be to require employees to request access to information before being able to view it  
b) Human Behavioural Implications  
Reactions in human behaviour aren’t present, exploitation is triggered by greed predominately. That aspect of human behaviour is not likely to change any time soon.  
c) Policy Implications.  
Policy should be changed to better reflect the sensitivity of the information, a spreadsheet with everything on it is hardly secure. Better storage should be considered, and policies for information withdrawal should be implemented.

Overall comments  
You have met most structural requirements: 3 APs, ≥8 test points, two data tables with all key parameters, a short WLAN analysis, a cyber-security report and the reflective journal.  The submission therefore satisfies the basic Pass criteria.  The following points indicate where marks are likely to be lost and how you could raise them next time.

WLAN survey (Part 1a)  
• The text implies a floor-plan but the report does not reference scale, construction materials or annotate the measurement/penetration paths.  Ensure every wall/door is drawn and labelled so the reader can link signal variations to obstacles.  
• Consider adding an extra column for frequency (2.4/5 GHz) and channel width (20/40/80 MHz) because these affect overlap.  
• Raw scanner screen-shots (required “proof of data source”) are missing from the appendix.

WLAN analysis (Part 1b)  
• Channel-occupancy discussion is too brief: explain why channels 4 & 6 interfere (they overlap by 50 % of their spectrum) and mention why using channel 149 (5 GHz) largely avoids 2.4 GHz congestion.  
• Interference and attenuation are asserted but not quantified.  Quote signal levels before/after a brick wall, or show a simple bar chart.  Comment on the body-loss experiment even if the difference is 0 dB.  
• Extra aspect: give a worked example of trilateration or SNR calculation to demonstrate technical depth.

Cyber-security report

• Discovery/publication needs a reference (e.g., Dutch newspaper “de Volkskrant”, 25 Jan 2021).  Cite all external sources in-text and in a reference list to satisfy academic-integrity expectations.

Presentation  
Proof-reading would remove minor grammar issues (e.g., “a fair distance suggests an impractical placement”).  Headings and numbered subsections would improve readability.

With tighter linkage between evidence and explanation, fuller referencing and clearer diagrams you would be heading toward Distinction / HD territory.
