Background
The final piece of assessment for FIT 5122 is a formal written business report reflecting on
your Professional Practice learning (through the readings, activities and assessment items
that you have completed).
The assignment
You will write a formal business report to a senior executive audience in your organization
addressing the request from your Director below.
Your task
You are currently a team leader in an Australian based software development company.
Your company recently acquired a competitor with offices based in India, China and the US.
The company is planning to establish new global multidisciplinary teams consisting of team
members across each of the countries. The teams will be working on existing and new
products. Senior Management are keen to ensure that the new teams become productive as
soon as possible. They have asked you to prepare a business report outlining:
1. Your insights into the key characteristics of a high performing team;
2. The potential challenges that may prevent the new global teams from performing;
and
3. Your recommendations for how the company should address these potential
challenges.
Note: Your business report should be primarily based around the key concepts discussed in
Professional Practice to demonstrate your understanding and ability to apply the knowledge
learnt during the semester.

can only be granted IF exceptional circumstances apply. Please refer to the University’s

Expectation of content
Hints for writing a business report versus an essay
• Business reports and academic essay are very different. Their objectives, format and
the expectation of the reader are different.
• Business reports focus on the practical application of the theory - providing insights
and recommendations rather than explaining the theory itself. They are concise, very
practical, usually worded in the first person (I / we recommend… etc) and focused on
identifying and solving a problem rather than discussing the theories behind the
problem / recommendations.
Hints for Structuring Your Report
• Title page
• Exec summary
• Table of contents
• Introduction and purpose of report
• Insights and discussion
• Recommendations
• Conclusion
For this business report, we would expect no more than 7 to 10 pages:
• 3-4 pages (title page, exec. summary, contents, introduction and purpose)
• 4-6 pages main body with conclusions and recommendations.
• You may have references (but remember these are not usual in business reports).
Hints for Writing Your Report
• Remember the audience and purpose of a business report. The report should be
focused on the problem, insights and recommendations on how to address the problem.
The reader should be able to walk away with practical and tangible actions they can
implement.
• The theories discussed in Professional Practice should be used to understand and
develop the key issues and recommendations however the theories themselves should
not be specifically raised in the business report.


==================== Page 4 ====================

• The executive summary should act as a stand alone document that can be read
separately from the report. A good executive summary assumes the reader will not read
further and therefore includes a succinct summary of the key points and
recommendations.
• The report should clearly include the purpose of the report (either in the introduction or
in a separate section.
• Don’t gloss over the recommendations. It should be clear to the reader why they are
important and what tangible actions the reader should take to implement them.
• Put recommendations in a separate section so they don’t get “lost” in the report.
• Keep the business report very succinct, short and punchy. Use lots of bullet points and
sub headings to link ideas.
• Remember your audience: in this case, a senior executive in your company, not your
Monash tutor. Ensure you keep this is mind when deciding the content and level of
detail in your report:
• What would a senior executive want to achieve from reading your report (insights into
the problem and recommendations on how to address it, for example);
• What is their existing knowledge of the topic (it’s their company); and
• What is the appropriate level of detail given what they want to do with the report (high
level insights with recommendations that are sufficiently explained so they can
readily implement them rather than technical or extensive background details).
Plagiarism
Students should also be aware of the plagiarism and collusion policies established by FIT
and Monash University. This policy can be
found at: http://www.infotech.monash.edu.au/resources/student/assignments/policies.ht ml.

This business report is written in response to the company’s recent acquisition and the formation of new global teams across Australia, China, India, and the United States. The report identifies the main challenges that may prevent these teams from becoming high-performing, and provides practical recommendations to address them. The three key challenges are communication, intercultural, and teamwork issues. In communication, language barriers, body language differences, and time zone issues are highlighted. For intercultural, the focus is on conflicts arising from different cultural backgrounds. In teamwork, the main concerns are the difficulties of online collaboration and varying leadership styles. The recommendations include clear communication strategies, cultural understanding, and the selection of multicultural team leaders to enhance team performance and efficiency.


==================== Page 4 ====================

Introduction and purpose  
With the company’s recent acquisition of a competitor in India, China, and the United States, we are now moving towards establishing global multidisciplinary teams. As a team leader, I recognize the opportunities and challenges that come with this expansion. The purpose of this business report is to analyze the main challenges that may affect the productivity of these new teams and to provide actionable recommendations to help the company build high-performing global teams as quickly as possible.

Insights and discussion  
The new global teams will consist of members from Australia, China, India, and the US. Based on my analysis, the main challenges to becoming a high-performing team are Communication, Intercultural, and Teamwork.

Communication  
Communication is a critical factor in team success. Effective communication is the foundation of a high-performing team, but global teams face several unique challenges.

Language  
Language differences are a major issue. Members from China and India may not be as comfortable with English as those from Australia and the US. Accents and varying levels of English proficiency can lead to misunderstandings and misinterpretations, which can slow down team progress.

Body language  
Body language is another important aspect. Different cultures use body language in different ways, which can easily cause confusion. For example, an Indian team member may shake their head to indicate agreement, but this gesture could be misunderstood by someone from China or Australia.

Time difference  
Time zone differences also create challenges. Scheduling meetings that suit all team members can be difficult. For example, a meeting time that is convenient for Australia may be late at night in the US and early morning in India, making it hard for everyone to participate effectively.


==================== Page 5 ====================

Intercultural  
With team members from different countries, cultural differences are inevitable and can lead to conflicts.

Polychronic / Monochronic cultures  
China and India are generally polychronic cultures, where relationships and flexibility are valued over strict adherence to schedules. In contrast, Australia and the US are more monochronic, focusing on punctuality and task completion. These differences can cause misunderstandings about priorities and work styles.

High / low context cultures  
China and India are high-context cultures, where communication is often indirect and much is left unsaid. Australia and the US are low-context cultures, where communication is direct and explicit. This can lead to confusion, especially when giving feedback or discussing mistakes. High-context members may avoid direct criticism, while low-context members may be more straightforward.

These cultural differences can result in conflicts and misunderstandings, which can negatively impact team performance.

Teamwork  
Teamwork is essential for any team, but global teams face additional challenges.

Online teamwork  
Due to geographical distance and the impact of COVID-19, most collaboration will be online. This can reduce the effectiveness of teamwork, as it is harder to build relationships and understand each other’s work. Online meetings can also lead to distractions and reduced engagement.

Leadership styles  
Different countries have different expectations of leadership. Authoritarian leadership may cause resistance, while a hands-off approach may lead to lack of direction. Finding the right leadership style is crucial for team success.


==================== Page 6 ====================

Recommendations  
Based on the challenges identified above, I have the following recommendations for each area.

Communication  
- For language barriers, encourage members from Australia and the US to help clarify and summarize discussions, especially when members from China and India are speaking.  
- Promote clear and simple communication, focusing on the main points and objectives.  
- For body language misunderstandings, encourage team members to ask for clarification if something is unclear, and to confirm understanding after meetings.  
- To address time zone issues, allow teams in the same country to meet separately and then share key points during a global meeting at a time that works for everyone. This makes better use of limited overlapping hours and improves communication efficiency.

Intercultural  
- Encourage all team members to learn about each other’s cultures and customs to reduce misunderstandings.  
- Combine the strengths of different cultures. For example, encourage direct communication from US and Australian members, while also valuing relationship-building from Chinese and Indian members.  
- Use social contracts that all team members agree to, setting clear expectations and rules for collaboration.  
- When conflicts arise, focus on identifying the root cause and use “I” statements to express concerns without direct criticism.

Teamwork  
- Select team leaders who have experience working in multiple cultures, or who have a good understanding of other countries’ cultures.  
- Train leaders in agile leadership, where the leader is part of the team and helps resolve conflicts and facilitate communication, rather than just giving orders.  
- Encourage leaders to motivate and support team members, and to ensure that everyone is involved in decision-making and task completion.


==================== Page 7 ====================

Conclusion  
In summary, building high-performing global teams requires addressing challenges in communication, intercultural understanding, and teamwork. Each of these areas presents specific obstacles that can hinder team performance. By implementing the recommendations above, the company can help teams overcome these challenges and work more efficiently together. Achieving high performance in global teams is an ongoing process that requires continuous effort and adaptation, but with the right strategies, we can ensure the success of our global business operations.

1 Athlete work schedule (8 marks)
You are an athlete, who mostly works as a personal trainer but also competes in sporting com-
petitions. You can sometimes make more money from competing than from training, but you
need time to prepare and recover, which is time that you cannot spend working as a trainer, or
preparing for, competing in or recovering from any other competitions.
You want to maximise the amount of money you can earn by combining your job as a personal
trainer with participating in competitions. To do this, you will write a function
best_schedule(weekly_income, competitions).
1.1 Input
weekly_income is a list of non-negative integers, where weekly_income[i] is the amount of
money you will earn working as a personal trainer in week i.
competitions is a list of tuples, each representing a sporting competition. Each tuple contains
3 non-negative integers, (start_time, end_time, winnings).
start_time is the is the week that you will need to begin preparing for this competition (i.e.
the first week that you cannot do your regular job as a personal trainer, if you compete in this
competition).
end_time is the last week that you will need to spend recovering from this competition (i.e.
the last week that you cannot do your regular job as a personal trainer, if you compete in this
competition).
winnings is the amount of money you will win if you compete in this competition.
1.2 Output
best_schedulereturnsaninteger, whichisthemaximumamountofmoneythatcanbeearned.
1.3 Example
weekly_income = [3,7,2,1,8,4,5]
competitions = [(1,3,15),(2,2,8),(0,4,30),(3,5,19)]
print(best_schedule(weekly_income, competitions))
>>> 42
In the above example, the optimal schedule is to work as a trainer in weeks 0 and 1 (earning
3+7), then compete in the (2,2,8) event in week 2 (earning 8), then compete in the (3,5,19)
event from weeks 3 to 5 (earning 19), then work as a trainer in week 6 (earning 5).
1.4 Complexity
best_schedule should run in O(Nlog(N)) time and O(N) space, where N is the total number
of elements in weekly_income and competitions put together.

2 Sales itinerary (9 marks)
A salesperson lives on the coast. They travel to various cities along the coast to work. Some-
times they stay in a city for a day or a few days to work, and other times they simply pass
through on their way to another city.
Since Covid-19, each city has instituted a policy of having travelers quarantine, but only if
they want to stay in the city. If they are just passing through, they can continue on their way
without quarantining. Also, each city asks visitors to quarantine for a different amount of time.
The salesperson has an idea of how much money they can make by working in each city, for
each day. They need to decide which cities to travel to, and which cities to work in, in order
to make the most money.
Each day, the salesperson can either work for the day in their current city (assuming they have
finished quarantine), or they can travel to either adjacent city. Traveling always takes 1 day,
and since the cities are along a coast, each city has two adjacent cities, except for two cities on
the ends of the coast, which only have 1.
To solve this problem, you will write a function best_itinerary(profit, quarantine_time,
home).
2.1 Input
We think of the n cities as being numbered 0...n-1. In one day, the salesperson can travel
from city i to either city i+1 or i-1. From city 0 they can only travel to city 1, and from city
n-1 they can only travel to city n-2.
profit is a list of lists. All interior lists are length n. Each interior list represents a differ-
entday. profit[d][c]istheprofitthatthesalespersonwillmakebyworkingincitycondayd.
quarantine_time is a list of non-negative integers. quarantine_time[i] is the number of
days city i requires visitors to quarantine before they can work there.
home is an integer between 0 and n-1 inclusive, which represents the city that the salesperson
starts in. They can start working in this city without needing to quarantine on the first day.
If they leave and come back later, they will need to quarantine.
2.2 Output
best_itinerary returns an integer, which is the maximum amount of money that can be
earned by the salesperson.

LLM tutor feedback:
best_schedule
+ Clear DP idea (profit up to each week), overall O(W+C) time and O(W) space – meets the required bound.
– Timeline stops at len(weekly_income)−1. Competitions whose end-time ≥ len(weekly_income) are silently discarded, so optimal schedules extending past the income list length are missed.
– weekly_income after the last competition week is implicitly 0; extend the DP length to max(last_week_from_income, last_end_in_comp)+1 instead of trimming competitions.
– profit[1] is computed with max over competitions that finish in week 0, but you do not check that their start_time is 0; a contest (1,0,…) is impossible yet still read. A cleaner base case is profit[0]=0 and loop from i=1.
– Sorting competitions by end-time and iterating once avoids the extra comp_by_end array and clarifies O(N log N) requirement.

best_itinerary
+ Uses a 2-D DP table indexed by day and city; complexity O(nd) as requested.
– The quarantine rule is misapplied: quarantine is needed only if the salesperson intends to WORK in the city, not to pass through. In your code a traveller who wants to go 0→2 must still spend q[1] days in city 1, which is incorrect and can lose profit.
  • Fix: allow “travel” transitions that add one day and land in the neighbour with no quarantine; start counting quarantine only when the person decides to stay and not move further.
– dp state does not differentiate “in quarantine” vs “free to work”, so once a city is chosen you cannot move again during the quarantine period. A common fix is to store two states per cell: best profit when free_to_work and best profit when still_quarantining(k).
– You limit the answer to dp[days][*]; if the last action on day d<days is “travel”, the profit earned earlier is still valid but will not be stored in dp[days]. Propagate best_so_far to every remaining day or take max over all days.

Style / documentation
– Provide docstrings for both functions and explain the DP recurrence.
– Inline comments should describe the meaning of each state and major update.

Overall: solid attempt on best_schedule; revisit timeline handling. best_itinerary needs a revised state definition to respect pass-through travel and quarantine semantics.

$stmt = $conn->prepare("UPDATE credential SET nickname=?, email=?, address=?, PhoneNumber=? WHERE ID=?");  
$stmt->bind_param("ssssi", $input_nickname, $input_email, $input_address, $input_phonenumber, $id);  
$stmt->execute();  
Now, injecting malicious input (e.g., ‘,salary=20) is treated as a string, not as SQL code.

DATA CHECKING FOR ERRORS
At the first glance, we notice that there are rows with missing values on specific attributes such
as Date.Planted. We don’t want to remove these rows since they still hold important information
such as geospatial location. The first thing to do is checking for possible errors in the dataset.
The first error is an incorrect Longitude Latitude value. A tree being in the middle of Port Phillip
Bay is clearly incorrect. This row can be removed.


==================== Page 2 ====================

The second error is a tree that is apparently planted in the year 2061. As it is currently 2021,
this is also clearly incorrect and can be removed as well.


==================== Page 3 ====================

DATA ANALYSIS
Q1) How are Acacia, Agathis, Backhousia, Eucalyptus, and Magnolia distributed spatially?
Briefly explain the distribution in terms of their spatial clusterisation (visual judgement is enough)
and whether they are mainly on streets vs parks.
A map is most suitable for this. We plot each tree individually using its Longitude and Latitude
value, filtering by the five specified genera. To very easily distinguish between the different
genera, we create small multiples to isolate each one in its own separate map. We also map
colour to see whether or not the tree is located in the park or on a street.
From the map, we can clearly see that Acacia is the only tree genus that is planted on
streets—the southern street in particular. This genus is also planted inside of the park, where
they are all densely planted in a cluster nearby the aforementioned street.
Agathis, Eucalyptus, and Magnolia are planted in many different parts of the Fitzroy Gardens. Of
note is that Agathis and Eucalyptus also have specific areas in which they are more
concentrated: Agathis along what appears to be a walkway inside of the park; and Eucalyptus
next to what appears to be a lake.
Backhousia is the least spread out genus. Other than the two trees planted in the north, they are
all planted in a straight line to the south-east of the Garden, close to the same street that the
Acacia are planted.


==================== Page 4 ====================

Q2) How does the relative number of planted trees by the aforementioned genera (plural for
genus) vary in the year 2013, 2014, 2015, 2016, and 2017?
As we want to compare the relative number (i.e., proportions) of the five genera against each
other, it is best to use a normalised stacked bar chart. To normalise a stacked bar chart, we go
to the CNT(Genus) option in the Rows section, click Quick Table Calculation, and select Percent
of Total. Afterwards, go to the same CNT(Genus) option, click Compute Using, and select Cell.
We can clearly see that Acacia is the most commonly planted type of tree across the five
specified years. However, none were planted in 2015, in favour of planting more Agathis,
Eucalyptus, and Magnolia. Each of these three genera were planted separately in 2014, 2016,
and 2017 as well, although not to the same extent that Acacia were.
In contrast, Backhousia was only planted in 2013. That said, it comprised the majority of trees
(of our five specified genera) that were planted in that year, with Acacia being the only other
one.

DATA CHECKING FOR ERRORS  
Upon initial inspection of the dataset, I noticed that some rows have missing values, particularly in the Date.Planted column. Since these rows still contain valuable geospatial information, I decided not to remove them. The first step was to check for errors in the data.

The first error I found was a tree with Longitude and Latitude values that placed it in the middle of Port Phillip Bay, which is not possible. I removed this row from the dataset.

The second error was a tree with a Date.Planted value of 2061. As the current year is 2021, this is clearly a mistake, so I removed this row as well.

==================== Page 2 ====================

DATA ANALYSIS

Q1) How are Acacia, Agathis, Backhousia, Eucalyptus, and Magnolia distributed spatially?  
To answer this, I created maps plotting each tree’s Longitude and Latitude, filtering for the five genera: Acacia, Agathis, Backhousia, Eucalyptus, and Magnolia. I used small multiples to show each genus on a separate map, and coloured the points to indicate whether the tree was in a park or on a street.

From the maps, I observed that Acacia is the only genus planted on streets, especially along the southern street, and also forms a dense cluster inside the park near that street. Agathis, Eucalyptus, and Magnolia are spread throughout Fitzroy Gardens, with Agathis concentrated along a walkway and Eucalyptus near a lake. Backhousia is the least spread out, mostly planted in a straight line in the south-east of the Garden, with two exceptions in the north.

==================== Page 3 ====================

Q2) How does the relative number of planted trees by the aforementioned genera vary in 2013, 2014, 2015, 2016, and 2017?  
To compare the proportions of the five genera over these years, I used a normalised stacked bar chart. I calculated the percent of total for each genus per year.

The chart shows that Acacia was the most commonly planted genus across the five years, except in 2015 when none were planted and Agathis, Eucalyptus, and Magnolia were planted more. These three genera were also planted in other years, but not as much as Acacia. Backhousia was only planted in 2013, making up the majority of trees for that year among the five genera, with Acacia being the only other genus planted then.

3. Scenario Pick  
Privacy-Preserving E-Auction

3.1 Plaintext Algorithm Description  
The protocol I propose enables multiple bidders to participate in an e-auction without revealing their individual bids to the auctioneer or to each other. The auctioneer only learns the winning bid and the winner’s identity. Each bidder secret-shares their bid among two non-colluding servers. The servers jointly compute the maximum bid and the winner using secure computation techniques, specifically additive secret sharing and Yao’s Garbled Circuits.

3.1.1 Offline Protocol (Bid Secret Sharing)  
Input: Each bidder’s bid value b_i, Bidders (as Senders), Servers S0 and S1 (as Receivers)  
Output: [b_i] ← Share(b_i), where [b_i] = (b_i0, b_i1) and b_i = b_i0 + b_i1 mod q

Each bidder executes:  
- For their bid b_i, generate a random value r_i in Z_q  
- Set b_i0 = r_i, b_i1 = b_i - r_i mod q  
- Send b_i0 to S0, b_i1 to S1

Servers S0 and S1 receive shares from all bidders.

3.1.2 Online Protocol (Secure Maximum Computation)  
Input: Shares of all bids from all bidders, Servers S0 and S1  
Output: Maximum bid value and winner’s index

Servers execute:  
- For each bidder, locally hold their share of the bid  
- Use Yao’s Garbled Circuit protocol to jointly compute the maximum among all bids and the corresponding winner index, without revealing any other information  
- Output the result to the auctioneer

Bidders learn only the auction result (winner and winning bid).

==================== Page 2 ====================

3.2 Protocol Overview

3.2.1 Involved Parties  
- n Bidders: Each wants to submit a private bid  
- 2 Non-colluding Servers (S0, S1): Jointly compute the auction result  
- Auctioneer: Receives only the winner and winning bid

3.2.2 Security & Privacy Goals  
- Bid privacy: No party (including servers and auctioneer) learns any bid except the winning bid  
- Correctness: The protocol outputs the correct winner and winning bid  
- Collusion resistance: As long as the two servers do not collude, privacy is preserved

Assumptions:  
1. Bidders are honest-but-curious (semi-honest)  
2. Servers are non-colluding and semi-honest  
3. Communication channels are secure

(1) Security Analysis  
- If an adversary corrupts one server, they only see random shares, which reveal nothing about the actual bids  
- The use of Yao’s Garbled Circuit ensures that during the maximum computation, no server learns the actual bid values, only the final output  
- Even if the auctioneer colludes with one server, they cannot reconstruct the bids

(2) Formal Security  
A protocol securely realizes the auction functionality F if for every adversary A attacking the real protocol, there exists a simulator S in the ideal world such that no environment Z can distinguish between the real and ideal executions except with negligible probability.

==================== Page 3 ====================

3.3 Protocol Design

3.3.1 Additive Secret Sharing  
Each bidder splits their bid into two random shares and sends one to each server. This ensures that neither server can reconstruct the bid alone.

Protocol:  
For each bidder i:  
- Choose random r_i ∈ Z_q  
- Set b_i0 = r_i, b_i1 = b_i - r_i mod q  
- Send b_i0 to S0, b_i1 to S1

Security Guarantee:  
- Each share is uniformly random; knowing only one share gives no information about the bid

3.3.2 Yao’s Garbled Circuit for Maximum Computation  
Servers S0 and S1 use Yao’s protocol to compute the maximum bid and winner index.

Protocol:  
- S0 acts as the garbler, S1 as the evaluator  
- S0 constructs a garbled circuit for the MAX and ARGMAX functions over the n bids  
- S0 and S1 use Oblivious Transfer (OT) to input their shares into the circuit  
- S1 evaluates the circuit and obtains the output (max bid, winner index)  
- Output is sent to the auctioneer

Security Guarantee:  
- Yao’s protocol ensures that S1 learns nothing except the output  
- S0 learns nothing about S1’s inputs  
- The garbled circuit hides all intermediate values

3.3.3 Oblivious Transfer (OT)  
Used for secure input of shares into the garbled circuit.

Protocol:  
- For each input bit, S0 prepares two keys  
- S1 uses OT to select the key corresponding to its input bit  
- Security: S0 does not learn S1’s input, S1 learns only the key for its input

==================== Page 4 ====================

3.4 Protocol Analysis

Since I was unable to implement the full protocol, I refer to the analysis in Huang et al. (2012) and Lindell & Pinkas (2009).

Offline Phase:  
- Each bidder sends two shares per bid (one to each server)  
- Communication cost: O(n) per bidder  
- Computation cost: negligible (random number generation and modular arithmetic)

Online Phase:  
- Servers run Yao’s Garbled Circuit for MAX/ARGMAX over n inputs  
- Communication cost: O(n * k), where k is the security parameter (circuit size grows linearly with n)  
- Computation cost: O(n * k) for garbling and evaluating the circuit

For a small number of bidders (e.g., n < 100), the protocol is practical. For large n, circuit size and communication may become a bottleneck.

3.5 Implementation and Evaluation

I was not able to finish a full implementation, but I wrote Python scripts for additive secret sharing and a simple maximum computation (not secure). For the garbled circuit, I found an open-source implementation at https://github.com/ojroques/garbled-circuit, which could be adapted for the MAX function.

Additive Sharing Script:  
- Splits a bid into two random shares  
- Validated that the sum of shares reconstructs the original bid

Evaluation:  
- For 10 bidders, additive sharing and reconstruction takes <0.001 sec per bid  
- Garbled circuit evaluation (from literature) takes ~1 sec for n=10, k=128

Appendix I  
Additive Sharing Script (Python)

```python
import random

def share_bid(bid, q=1000):
    r = random.randint(0, q-1)
    s0 = r
    s1 = (bid - r) % q
    return s0, s1

def reconstruct_bid(s0, s1, q=1000):
    return (s0 + s1) % q

# Example
bid = 350
s0, s1 = share_bid(bid)
print("Shares:", s0, s1)
print("Reconstructed:", reconstruct_bid(s0, s1))
```

LLM tutor feedback:
Hi [Student name],

Thanks for submitting your normalization work for Assignment 1B.

Your normalization of the "Pet Ownership Details" form is excellent! You've correctly identified the repeating groups, decomposed the relations through 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF, and accurately noted the dependencies at each stage.

For the "Visit Invoice" normalization, while your final 3NF structure is correct, there are issues in the intermediate steps (1NF and 2NF) that need attention:

1.  **1NF Primary Keys:** In your 1NF `Service` and `Drug` relations, the primary keys should be composite keys: `(visit_id, service_code)` for `Service` and `(visit_id, drug_code)` for `Drug`. This is because a single visit can include multiple services/drugs, and a single service/drug can be part of multiple visits. The combination uniquely identifies a specific service or drug instance within a visit.
2.  **Partial Dependencies:** Because the 1NF primary keys were identified incorrectly as single attributes, you missed the partial dependencies. With the correct composite keys:
    *   In `Service(visit_id, service_code, ...)`, `service_code` partially determines `service_desc` and `service_cost`.
    *   In `Drug(visit_id, drug_code, ...)`, `drug_code` partially determines `drug_name` and `drug_cost`.
3.  **2NF Structure:** Identifying these partial dependencies correctly would lead to the creation of separate `Service` and `Drug` master tables in 2NF, leaving the linking tables (`Visit_Service`, `Visit_Drug`) with the attributes fully dependent on the composite keys.

It's great that you arrived at the correct 3NF relations, but ensuring the process (identifying keys and dependencies accurately at each step) is correct is crucial for demonstrating understanding.

Keep up the good work, and pay close attention to identifying primary keys, especially composite ones, early in the normalization process.

LLM tutor feedback:
Okay, let's look at your Business Model Canvas for MindEase.

It's great that you've clearly structured your submission according to the nine building blocks of the BMC and addressed each one. You've identified relevant customer segments, considered different channels, and outlined potential revenue streams and cost structures for your health-tech venture.

To enhance this, consider adding more specific detail and demonstrating the connections *between* the blocks more explicitly. For example:
*   **Customer Segments & Value Proposition:** How does the value proposition specifically address the unique needs of *each* distinct customer segment (e.g., individuals vs. practitioners vs. corporate HR)?
*   **Key Resources & Activities:** How do your key resources (like licensed professionals) directly enable key activities (like content creation or therapy sessions)?
*   **Cost & Revenue:** While you've listed costs, briefly consider the feasibility. Is $12k realistic for app development? How do the projected revenues align with these costs and the proposed pricing?

Remember, Assignment 3 Part A also requires a 1500-word report analysing the rationale behind each canvas block and critiquing your business model. This BMC provides a solid foundation for that analysis, where you can elaborate on the 'why' behind your choices and demonstrate the model's viability in greater depth, as highlighted in the rubric.

Keep refining these connections and details to build a truly robust and convincing business model!

1 WLAN Network Design and Security
For this task, you will perform a WLAN site survey. Your task is to produce a map of (part of)
a building that gives an overview of the wireless networks that are available, as well as an
analysis of the network.
What you will need: a Wi-Fi-enabled laptop (some smartphones also work, see below), and
a place to scan. You can perform a survey of your home, of an office space, of parts of the
Monash campus, or inside a shopping Centre. If you don’t own a suitable device that you
could use for this activity, please try to borrow one from a friend, or contact us to figure out
an alternative.
1.1 Survey (30 marks)
Create a mapof theplace youwanttosurvey. A simplefloor-planwill besufficient,it doesn’t
have to be perfectly to scale (see belowfor an example).Your surveyshould coveranareaof
at least 40squaremeters (e.g. 4x10metersor two-storey buildingsof6x5each).Be creative
– thesurveycanincludehallwaysor outsideareas.Be suretotake theanalysis inpart1.2into
account, by designing your survey to include walls, doors etc. It will be easier to write
something interesting in part 1.2.
Furthermore, your survey must include at least three Wi-Fi Access Points (WAP). These
can be your own, but can also include neighbors’ APs. If you are scanning in a commercial
area or on campus, you should be able to see enough APs. If you want, you can create an
additional AP with a phone (using “Personal hotspot” or “Tethering” features).
For the survey, use a WLAN sniffing tool (see below) at- least eight different locationson
your map. For each location, record the technicalcharacteristics of all visible APs. Depending
on the scanning tool you use, you can record (printscreen) features such as the Network
Name, MAC Address, Signal Strength, Security, 802.11 Version(s) Supported, Band (2.4 or 5
GHz) andChannel(s) used.
On the map you should indicate the location of the access points and the locations
where you took measurements. For the access points, use the actual location if you know it,
or an approximation based on the observed signal strength (e.g. ifit’syour neighbor’saccess
point and you don’t know exactly where it is). For each measurement point, you can either
add the characteristics directly into the map, or create a separate table with the details. You
can submit several maps if you choose to enter data directly intothe maps,or asinglemap if
you use additional tables. Include the screen-shots (print screen of WLAN sniffing tool data
display) in the appendix section of your report asa proof ofyour datasource. Createthemap
yourself, do not use the mapping features available in some commercial(i.e.paid)WLAN
sniffing tools.


==================== Page 3 ====================

1.2 Report (30 marks)
Write a report (word limit 600) on your observations analyzing the data collected in the
previous step. The report does not have to follow any particular structure (i.e. simply
answering the following questions is enough,youdon’tneed anexecutive summary,tableof
contents etc.). Your analysis should investigate the following aspects:
• Channel Occupancy: Are different access points competing on the same channels? Are
they configured to use overlapping channels? Is roamingavailable? Determine the
overlap that has been implemented to enable roaming. Etc. (6 marks)
• Interference:Effect of other sources of signals (microwave oven, reflected signals from
walls, doors etc. (6 marks)
• Coverage: Do the access points sufficiently cover the desired area? Could the placement
or configuration be improved? (6 marks)
• Attenuation: How does different material affect signal strength and noise? Do you notice
any difference in attenuation for different APs? How about attenuation caused by your
own body? Can you measure and reflect the result? (6 marks)
• One more aspectof your own choice. A few suggestions: (6 marks)
– explain signal-to-noise ratio either using measurednoise or by assuming that
noise is slightly below the weakest signal your scanning software can detect.
– measure the download and upload speeds in different locations.
– describe how to extrapolate or interpolate locationsof APs from signal strengths.
Important: Describe your findings and explain them with some technical detail with
diagrams (i.e., not only say what you found, but also how you performed the analysis or
why you think the network is behaving that way). Maximum word limit for Part-1: 600
words.
1.3 Tools
● You can use e.g. Acrylic Wifi (https://www.acrylicwifi.com/en/) for Windows
● NetSpot (http://www.netspotapp.com) for Mac OS and Windows, &
● LinSSID or wavemon for Linux.
● If you have an Android smartphone, apps like Wifi Analyzer can also be used.
● On iOS, WiFi scanning apps do not provide enough detail, so iPhones won’t be
suitable for this task.
● Microsoft Visio
● Open source Wireshark packet capture tool for analysis.
● For drawing the site maps, any drawing tool shouldwork, for example LucidChart,
or even presentation tools such as PowerPoint, Keynoteor Google Slides.
● Scans of hand-drawn maps are acceptable if they areneat and easily readable.

2.1 Summary
(Wi-Fi Devices as Physical Object Sensors)
https://www.theregister.com/2021/03/31/wifi_devices_monitoring/
The IEEE is working on a new Wi-Fi standard called 802.11bf, which will allow Wi-Fi devices to act as sensors, not just for communication. This means Wi-Fi signals can be used to detect the presence, movement, and even gestures of people and objects, by analyzing how the signals are affected. This technology, called SENS, could be used for things like smart homes, security, and health monitoring. However, there are concerns about privacy and security, because these sensors could collect sensitive information without people knowing. The standard is still being developed, and there are also technical challenges like interference with other wireless devices and how to share the radio spectrum.

2.2 Identity
The affected systems are Wi-Fi sensor networks using the upcoming IEEE 802.11bf standard, which will include many low-power Wi-Fi devices acting as sensors in homes, offices, and public spaces.

2.3 Describe the Problem
The privacy and security risks were identified by Francesco Restuccia, a researcher at Northeastern University, through targeted research. He published a paper showing that SENS can infer sensitive information like keystrokes and gestures without user consent. The interference issue was also discussed in technical articles about spectrum sharing.

2.4 Estimate the Seriousness
The introduction of Wi-Fi sensing (SENS) is a major change for wireless networks. Technically, it allows for new applications like motion detection and health monitoring, but it also creates new risks. To exploit the privacy weakness, an attacker would only need access to the Wi-Fi network or be able to monitor the signals, which is not very difficult in many environments. The consequences could be serious: people’s movements, activities, and even what they type could be tracked without their knowledge. This could lead to privacy violations, stalking, or data theft.

On a technical level, stronger encryption and access controls are needed to prevent unauthorized sensing. Device manufacturers should also provide clear indicators when sensing is active. In terms of human behavior, users need to be aware that Wi-Fi can be used for more than just internet access, and should be careful about where and how they use Wi-Fi devices. On a policy level, new regulations may be needed to protect privacy, such as requiring consent for sensing and limiting what data can be collected. Without these protections, the risks could outweigh the benefits of the new technology.

1 Liquid Trading (10 marks)
In this task, you are a trader who specialises in valuable liquids. You have come to a new town,
and your goal is to trade with the local people in order to maximise the value of the liquid you
have.
Each person in the town is willing to make various trades at various ratios. For example, one
person might trade water for mercury at a rate of 1L water for 0.01L mercury. Since you are
from the big city, you know the prices of each of these liquids, and so you can determine which
trades are worthwhile and which are not.
You are only in the town for a limited time, and trading takes time, so you have a maximum
number of trades you can conduct before you need to move on, though you may choose to trade
fewer times than this maximum. You also only have one container (with unlimited capacity!),
and you cannot mix liquids or they will become worthless, so you must trade all of your current
liquid whenever you make a trade.
To solve this problem you will write a function, best_trades(prices, starting_liquid,
max_trades, townspeople)
1.1 Input
Liquids each have an ID. There are n liquids, and each one has a unique ID from the range
[0..n-1].
prices is an array of length n, where prices[i] is the value of 1L of the liquid with ID i.
starting_liquidistheIDoftheliquidyouarrivewith. Youalwaysstartwith1Lofthisliquid.
townspeople is a list of non-empty lists. Each interior list corresponds to the trades offered
by a particular person. The interior lists contain 3 element tuples, (give, receive, ratio).
Each tuple indicates that this person is willing to be given liquid with ID give in exchange
for liquid with ID receive at the given ratio. For example, (2,5,1.5) indicates that you can
give liquid with ID 2, and receive 1.5 times as much liquid with ID 5. Note that people are not
necessarily willing to perform these trades in both directions, i.e. this example does not mean
that you can give liquid 5 and receive liquid 2.
For each liquid, there will be at least one townsperson who is willing to trade for that liquid.
1.2 Output
best_trades should return the maximum value that you can obtain after performing at most
max_trades trades.

to our destination as cheaply as possible. However, there is a way we can make some money on
our way. We can pick up an item from one particular city, and deliver it to another particular
city. We want to determine whether it will be cheaper to perform this delivery during our
journey, or just go directly to our destination.
Tosolvethisproblem,youwillwriteafunctionopt_delivery(n, roads, start, end, delivery)

n is the number of cities. The cities are numbered [0..n-1].

between cities u and v. w is the cost of traveling along that road, which is always non-negative.
Note that roads can be traveled in either direction, and the cost is the same. There is at most
1 road between any pair of cities. roads will represent a simple, connected graph
start and end are each an integer in the range [0..n-1]. They represent the city you start

delivery is a tuple containing 3 values. The first value is the city where we can pick up the
item (from now on called the "pickup city"). The second value is the city where we can deliver
the item (from now on called the "delivery city"). The third value is the amount of money we
can make if we deliver the item from the pickup city to the delivery city.

repeatedly delivering the item for a profit).
2.2 Output
opt_delivery returns a tuple containing 2 elements. The first element is the cost of travelling
from the start city to the end city. This cost includes the profit we make from the delivery, if

The second element of the tuple is a list of integers. This list represents the cities we need to

twice.
Note that it is possible for a city to be any combination of the start, end, pickup or delivery
city. So we might need to start and end in the same place (start = end), or we might need to
deliver to the place we are going (end = delivery), or any other combination.

"""
This file contains graph implementation
Created by Zichuan Zhang
Last modified on 28/5
"""
from typing import *
import heapq

class Graph:
    def __init__(self, vertex: int):
        """
        This function initializes a graph with a given number of vertices.
        :param vertex: number of vertices
        """
        self.graph = [None] * vertex

    def add_edge(self, edge: Tuple[int, int, int]):
        """
        This function adds an undirected edge into the graph.
        :param edge: edge to insert (u, v, w)
        """
        u, v, w = edge
        if self.graph[u] is None:
            self.graph[u] = [(v, w)]
        else:
            self.graph[u].append((v, w))
        if self.graph[v] is None:
            self.graph[v] = [(u, w)]
        else:
            self.graph[v].append((u, w))

    def find_edges(self, vertex: int) -> List[Tuple[int, int]]:
        """
        This function finds edges from a specific vertex.
        :param vertex: vertex to find
        :return: return the found edges
        """
        return self.graph[vertex]


def best_trades(prices, starting_liquid, max_trades, townspeople):
    """
    This function uses BFS to find the max value
    :param prices: list of prices
    :param starting_liquid: liquid ID
    :param max_trades: maximum trade it can perform
    :param townspeople: list of available trade
    :return: maximum value
    :time-complexity: O(T*M) where T is available trades and M is max_trades
    """
    graph = Graph(len(prices))
    for people in townspeople:
        for trade in people:
            graph.add_edge(trade)

    n = len(prices)
    # dp[liquid][trades] = max amount of liquid we can have of type 'liquid' after 'trades' trades
    dp = [[0.0] * (max_trades + 1) for _ in range(n)]
    dp[starting_liquid][0] = 1.0

    for t in range(max_trades):
        for liquid in range(n):
            if dp[liquid][t] > 0:
                trades = graph.find_edges(liquid)
                if trades is not None:
                    for trade in trades:
                        next_liquid, ratio = trade
                        if dp[next_liquid][t+1] < dp[liquid][t] * ratio:
                            dp[next_liquid][t+1] = dp[liquid][t] * ratio

    max_value = 0
    for liquid in range(n):
        for t in range(max_trades + 1):
            value = dp[liquid][t] * prices[liquid]
            if value > max_value:
                max_value = value
    return max_value


def dijkstra(n, graph, start):
    """
    This function implements Dijkstra's algorithm to find shortest paths from start.
    :param n: number of vertices
    :param graph: adjacency list
    :param start: starting vertex
    :return: (dist, prev) where dist[i] is min cost to i, prev[i] is previous node in path
    :time-complexity: O(RlogN) where R is number of roads, N is number of cities
    """
    dist = [float('inf')] * n
    prev = [-1] * n
    visited = [False] * n
    dist[start] = 0
    heap = [(0, start)]
    while heap:
        d, u = heapq.heappop(heap)
        if visited[u]:
            continue
        visited[u] = True
        edges = graph.find_edges(u)
        if edges is not None:
            for v, w in edges:
                if not visited[v] and dist[v] > d + w:
                    dist[v] = d + w
                    prev[v] = u
                    heapq.heappush(heap, (dist[v], v))
    return dist, prev

def reconstruct_path(prev, start, end):
    """
    This function reconstructs the path from start to end using prev array.
    :param prev: previous node array
    :param start: start node
    :param end: end node
    :return: list of nodes in path
    """
    path = []
    curr = end
    while curr != -1:
        path.append(curr)
        if curr == start:
            break
        curr = prev[curr]
    path.reverse()
    return path

def opt_delivery(n, roads, start, end, delivery):
    """
    This function finds the minimum cost path from start to end, possibly making a delivery.
    :param n: number of cities
    :param roads: list of roads (u, v, w)
    :param start: start city
    :param end: end city
    :param delivery: (pickup, dropoff, profit)
    :return: (min_cost, path)
    :time-complexity: O(RlogN) where R is number of roads, N is number of cities
    """
    graph = Graph(n)
    for edge in roads:
        graph.add_edge(edge)

    pickup, dropoff, profit = delivery

    # Path without delivery
    dist1, prev1 = dijkstra(n, graph, start)
    cost_no_delivery = dist1[end]
    path_no_delivery = reconstruct_path(prev1, start, end)

    # Path with delivery: start -> pickup -> dropoff -> end
    dist_s, prev_s = dijkstra(n, graph, start)
    dist_p, prev_p = dijkstra(n, graph, pickup)
    dist_d, prev_d = dijkstra(n, graph, dropoff)

    cost_with_delivery = dist_s[pickup] + dist_p[dropoff] + dist_d[end] - profit

    # Reconstruct path for delivery
    path = []
    if cost_with_delivery < cost_no_delivery:
        # start -> pickup
        path1 = reconstruct_path(prev_s, start, pickup)
        # pickup -> dropoff
        path2 = reconstruct_path(prev_p, pickup, dropoff)
        # dropoff -> end
        path3 = reconstruct_path(prev_d, dropoff, end)
        # Merge paths, avoid duplicate nodes
        if len(path1) > 0:
            path.extend(path1)
        if len(path2) > 1:
            path.extend(path2[1:])
        if len(path3) > 1:
            path.extend(path3[1:])
        return (int(cost_with_delivery), path)
    else:
        return (int(cost_no_delivery), path_no_delivery)

*   Using a `Graph` class here works, but since trades are inherently directed and tied to specific people, you could also have potentially built the necessary structure directly from `townspeople` without the formal `Graph` class if you preferred.

**Optional Delivery (`opt_delivery`):**
*   Using Dijkstra's algorithm multiple times to find the costs of the direct path (`start` -> `end`) and the delivery path (`start` -> `pickup` -> `dropoff` -> `end`) is an efficient and correct strategy.
*   Your Dijkstra implementation using `heapq` is standard and achieves the required O(R log N) complexity.
*   The path reconstruction logic, especially how you combine the segments for the delivery path, is well-handled.
*   The overall complexity correctly meets the O(R log N) requirement.

**General Points:**
*   Your documentation is good, with clear function descriptions and complexity analyses. Adding the complexity for `reconstruct_path` (O(N) in the worst case) would make it even more complete.
*   You've successfully avoided forbidden data structures like dictionaries and sets.

Overall, this is a solid submission demonstrating a good understanding of graph algorithms and complexity analysis. Keep up the excellent work!

Assignment description:
==================== Page 1 ====================

Assignment 1
FIT5225 2021 SM1
iWebLens:
Creating and Deploying an Image Object Detection
Web Service within a Containerised Environment
1 Synopsis and Background
This project aims at building a web-based system that we call it iWebLens. It allows end-users to send
an image to a web service hosted in a Docker container and receive a list of objects detected in their
uploaded image. The project makes use of YOLO (You only look once) library, a state-of-the-art real-time
object detection system, and OpenCV (Open-Source Computer Vision Library) to perform required image
operations/transformations. Both YOLO and OpenCV are python-based open-source computer vision and
machine learning software libraries. The web service will be hosted as container in a Kubernetes cluster.
Kubernetes is used as the container orchestration system. The object detection web service is also de-
signed to be a RESTful API that can use Python’s FLASK library. We are interested in examining the
performance of iWebLens by varying the rate of requests sent to the system (demand) and the number of
existing Pods within the Kubernetes cluster (resources).
This assignment has the following objectives:
• Writing a python web service that accepts images in JSON object format, uses YOLO and OpenCV
to process images, and returns a JSON object with a list of detected objects.
• Building a Docker Image for the object detection web service.
• Creating a Kubernetes cluster on a virtual machine (instance) in the Nectar cloud.
• Deploying a Kubernetes service to distribute inbound requests among pods that are running the
object detection service.
• Testing the system under different load and number of pods.
You can focus on these objectives one after the other to secure partial marks.
2 The web service - [20 Marks]
You are supposed to develop a RESTful API that allows the client to upload images to the server. You can
use Flask to build your web service and any port over 1024. You Flask server should be multi-threaded
to be able to service multiple clients concurrently. Each image is sent to the web service using an HTTP
POST request containing a JSON object including a unique id, e.g., UUID and base64 encoded image (The
client script sending images to the web service is given to you). An image is binary data, so you cannot
directly insert it into JSON. You should convert the image to a textual representation that can then be
used as a normal string. The most common way to encode image into text is using base64 method. A
sample JSON request used to send an image can be as follows:
1


==================== Page 2 ====================

{
"id":"06e8b9e0-8d2e-11eb-8dcd-0242ac130003",
"image":"YWRzZmFzZGZhc2RmYXNkZmFzZGYzNDM1MyA7aztqMjUzJyBqaDJsM2 ..."
}
The web service creates a thread per request and uses YOLO and OpenCV python libraries to detect
objects in the image. A sample code for object detection using YOLO and OpenCV is given to you which
works as a console application. You can leverage this script and build a web service using flask and also
handle base64 decoding. For each image (request), your web service returns a JSON object with a list of
all objects detected in that image as follows:
{
"id":"The id from the client request",
"objects": [
{
"label": "human/book/cat/...",
"accuracy": a real number between 0-1,
"rectangle": {
"height": number,
"left": number,
"top": number,
"width": number
}
}
...
]
}
The “id” is the same id sent by the client along with the image. This is used to associate an asynchronous
response with the request at the client side. The “label” represents type of object detected, e.g., cat, book,
etc. “Accuracy” is a value representing the precision in object detection and rectangle is a JSON object
showing the position of a box around the object in the image. A sample response is shown below:
{
"id": "2b7082f5-d31a-54b7-a46e-5e4889bf69bd",
"objects": [
{
"label": "book",
"accuracy": 0.7890481352806091,
"rectangle": {"height": 114, "left": 380, "top": 363, "width": 254}
},
{
"label": "cat",
"accuracy": 0.6877481352806091,
"rectangle": {"height": 114, "left": 180, "top": 63, "width": 254}
}
]
}
You only need to build the server-side RESTful API. We provide the client script (iWebLens_client.py
file) that is designed to invoke the REST API with a different number of requests. Please make sure your
web service is fully compatible with requests sent by the given client script.
2


==================== Page 3 ====================

Due to limited resources in Nectar, we use the yolov3-tiny framework to develop a fast and reliable
RESTful API for object detection. You utilise a pre-trained network weights (no need to train the object
detection program yourself). For your reference, a sample network weights for yolov3-tiny at https:
//pjreddie.com/media/files/yolov3-tiny.weights and required configuration files and more infor-
mation can be found at https://github.com/pjreddie/darknet and https://github.com/pjreddie/
darknet/tree/master/cfg. Note that this network is trained on COCO dataset (http://cocodataset.
org/#home). We provided you with a sample group of images (128 images in inputfolder) from this
dataset and you shall use it for testing. For your reference, the full dataset can be found at http:
//images.cocodataset.org/zips/test2017.zip. Please extract the given client.zip file and you can
find inputfolder and iWebLens_client.py along with a readme file explaining how you can use them.
You can invoke the program as follows:
python iWebLens_client.py <inputfolder> <endpoint> <num_threads>
Here, inputfolder represents the folder that contains 128 images for the test. The endpoint is the
REST API URL and num_threads indicates the total number of threads sending requests to the server
concurrently. Please refer to the client script iWebLens_client.py and ReadMe.txt file for more details.
Here is a sample:
python iWebLens_client.py inputfolder/ http://118.138.43.2:5000/api/object_detection 16
3 Dockerfile - [10 Marks]
Docker builds images by reading the instructions from a file known as Dockerfile. Dockerfile is a text file
that contains all ordered commands needed to build a given image. You are supposed to build a Dockerfile
that includes all the required instructions to build your Docker image. You can find Dockerfile reference
documentation here: https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/builder/
4 Kubernetes Cluster - [20 Marks]
The default quota of your personal project (pt-) in Nectar only allows you to use 2 VCPUs. A real
Kubernetes cluster with multiple physical nodes requires more resources. You are tasked to install a
cluster on a single VM. For this purpose, you are going to use Kind, a tool for running local Kubernetes
clusters on a single machine using Docker container nodes, to create a Kubernetes cluster with 1 controller
and 1 worker node that run on the same VM (Choose the m3.small flavor). Make sure you only create
one cluster and it is configured properly.
Kind uses Docker to set up and initialise a Kube cluster for you; therefore, you need to create a VM
that uses Nectar Ubuntu 18.04 with Docker as its boot image and then follow Kind’s quick start guide
that is available here: https://kind.sigs.k8s.io/docs/user/quick-start.
5 Kubernetes Service - [20 Marks]
After you have a running Kubernetes cluster, you need to create service and deployment configurations
that will in turn create and deploy required pods in the cluster. The official documentation of Kubernetes
contains various resources on how to create pods from a Docker image, set CPU and/or memory limitations
and the steps required to create a deployment for your pods using selectors. Please make sure you set CPU
request and CPU limit to “0.5” for each pod. Initially, you will start with a single pod to test your web
3


==================== Page 4 ====================

service and gradually increase the number of pods to 3 in the Section 6. The preferred way of achieving
this is by creating replica sets and scaling them accordingly.
Finally, you need to expose your deployment in order to communicate with the web service that is
running inside your pods. You need to call the object detection service from your computer (PC or
Desktop); hence you can leverage Nodeport capabilities of kubernetes to expose your deployment.
The Nectar pt- project restricts access to many ports and limits access for some IP ranges even if you
open those ports to the public in your security group. It is recommended that you map a well-known port
(for example 80 or 8080) from your Nectar instance to your Kubernetes service port to avoid any issue.
6 Experiments - [30 Marks]
Your next objective is to test your system under a varying number of threads in the client with a different
number of resources (pods). When the system is up and running, you will run experiments to test the
impact of num of threads in the client and number of pods (available resources) in the cluster on the
response time of the service. Response time of a service is the period between when an end-user makes
a request until a response is sent back to the end-user. The iWebLens_client.py script automatically
measures the average response time for you and prints it at the end of its execution.
The number of pods must be scaled to 1, 2, and 3. Since the amount of CPU allocated to each pod is
limited, by increasing the number of pods, you will increase the amount of resources that is available to
your web service. You will also vary the number of threads in the client to analyse the impact of increasing
the load on the overall average response time of the service. To do so, you vary the num_threads argument
of iWebLens_client.py script to 1, 6, 11, 16, 21, 26, and 31. This way you will run a total of 3×7 = 21
experiments. For each run, 128 images will be sent to the server and average response time is collected.
To make your collected data points more reliable, you should run each experiment multiple times (at least
3 times), calculate and report the mean of average response time.
Your task is to create a 2-D line plot with 3 lines each for different number of pods (1, 2, and 3), the
x-axis represents the number of threads (1, 6, 11, 16, 21, 26, and 31), and the y-axis represents the mean of
the average response time in seconds. In your report, discuss this plot and justify your observations. Please
make sure you are using correct labels for the plot. To automate your experimentation and collect data
points, you can write a script that automatically varies the parameters for the experiments and collects
data points.
7 Report
Your report must be a maximum of 2 pages. You need to include the following in your report:
• A single plot showing the average response time of the web service versus the number of pods for a
different values of interval.
• Maximum of 500 words explaining trends and justifying observations in your experiments.
Use 12pt Times font, single column, 1-inch margin all around. Put your full name, your tutor name,
and student number at the top of your report.

The plot above illustrates the average response time of the web service as the number of client threads increases, for different numbers of pods (1, 2, and 3). Each line represents a different pod configuration, and the x-axis shows the number of threads used by the client, while the y-axis shows the mean average response time in seconds.

From the graph, it is clear that as the number of threads increases, the response time also increases for all pod configurations. When the number of threads is low (1 to 6), the response time remains relatively low and stable across all pod counts. However, as the number of threads increases beyond 11, the response time starts to rise more sharply, especially for the single pod configuration. This suggests that the system can handle a moderate level of concurrency efficiently, but performance degrades as the load increases.

Comparing the lines for different pod counts, we can see that increasing the number of pods from 1 to 2 and then to 3 generally reduces the response time for the same number of threads. This is because more pods mean more resources are available to handle concurrent requests, allowing the system to process more images in parallel. However, the improvement from adding more pods becomes less significant at higher thread counts, indicating that other bottlenecks (such as CPU limits, network bandwidth, or the object detection processing itself) start to dominate.

The sharp increase in response time after a certain number of threads (around 16 and above) for all pod configurations can be explained by resource saturation. Each pod is limited to 0.5 CPU, so when the number of concurrent requests exceeds the total available CPU across all pods, requests start to queue up, leading to longer response times. This effect is more pronounced for a single pod, but still visible even with three pods.

In summary, increasing the number of pods helps to reduce response time under higher loads, but there are diminishing returns due to resource limits and other system bottlenecks. The system performs best with a moderate number of threads and pods, but performance drops as the load increases beyond the available resources. Other factors such as network latency and the efficiency of the object detection code may also impact the observed response times.

FIT2094 Introduction to Databases 2021 Semester 1 Assignment 1A - Conceptual Model - Animal Doctors (AD) Learning Outcomes: 2, 3 (see Unit Preview) Assignment weighting 5% Assignment marked out of 100 and released as a grade out of 5 Your task for this assignment is to design a database which can be used to support the activities of a veterinary practice called Animal Doctors. Animal Doctors has a number of clinics distributed across the suburbs. For each clinic, they record the clinic's id (which is used to identify the clinic), the clinic's name, its address and the clinic's contact phone number. The practice has a number of veterinary surgeons (vets) who work in these clinics, a vet is assigned to one clinic as their home (or base) clinic. To be able to function, a clinic must have at least one vet assigned to it as the vet’s base clinic. Some of the Animal Doctors vets are specialists in areas such as oncology, cardiology, dermatology etc. These specialist vets, as well as having a home clinic (their base clinic) where they accept general visits, also rove around all clinics when their specialist skills are required. The details Animal Doctors record about a vet are their vet id (used to identify a vet), the vet's given name and family name, the vet's contact phone number and the date they were first employed by Animal Doctors. If the vet is a specialist vet then their area of specialisation is recorded (specialist vets only specialise in one particular area). Pet owners, who are each assigned a unique owner id, have their given name and family name recorded by the practice. The practice also records the owner's contact phone number. Each owner may have one or more pets; for each pet the practice records a unique animal id, the animal's name, the year the animal was born and the type of animal they are, for example a cat. Each animal is only recorded as being owned by one owner. When an animal needs veterinary attention (such as annual injections) or is ill, the owner brings their pet to one of the practice's clinics to be attended to by a vet - this is called a visit. The date and time of the visit, the clinic being visited and the attending vet are recorded (this attending vet may be one based at this particular clinic, i.e. this is their home clinic, or the visit may need the services of a roving specialist vet). In addition, the Page 1 of 5

FIT3171 Introduction to Databases 2021 Semester 1 Assignment 1A - Conceptual Model - Animal Doctors (AD) Learning Outcomes: 2, 3, 4 (see Unit Preview) Assignment weighting 5% Assignment marked out of 100 and released as a grade out of 5 Your task for this assignment is to design a database which can be used to support the activities of a veterinary practice called Animal Doctors. Animal Doctors has a number of clinics distributed across the suburbs. For each clinic, they record the clinic's id (which is used to identify the clinic), the clinic's name, its address and the clinic's contact phone number. The practice has a number of veterinary surgeons (vets) who work in these clinics, a vet is assigned to one clinic as their home (or base) clinic. To be able to function, a clinic must have at least one vet assigned to it as the vet’s base clinic. Some of the Animal Doctors vets are specialists in areas such as oncology, cardiology, dermatology etc. These specialist vets, as well as having a home clinic (their base clinic) where they accept general visits, also rove around all clinics when their specialist skills are required. The details Animal Doctors record about a vet are their vet id (used to identify a vet), the vet's given name and family name, the vet's contact phone number and the date they were first employed by Animal Doctors. If the vet is a specialist vet then their area of specialisation is recorded (specialist vets only specialise in one particular area). Pet owners, who are each assigned a unique owner id, have their given name and family name recorded by the practice. The practice also records the owner's contact phone number. Each owner may have one or more pets; for each pet the practice records a unique animal id, the animal's name, the year the animal was born and the type of animal they are, for example a cat. Each animal is only recorded as being owned by one owner. When an animal needs veterinary attention (such as annual injections) or is ill, the owner brings their pet to one of the practice's clinics to be attended to by a vet - this is called a visit. The date and time of the visit, the clinic being visited and the attending vet are recorded (this attending vet may be one based at this particular clinic, i.e. this is their home clinic, or the visit may need the services of a roving specialist vet). LLM tutor feedback:
Hi Wang Jia Hui,

Thanks for submitting your work for Assignment 1A. You've made a good start by identifying the main entities involved in the Animal Doctors system, such as VET, OWNER, PET, CLINIC, VISIT, DRUG, and PRESCRIPTION. You've also listed many of the required attributes for these entities.

However, the submission requires a conceptual model diagram (ERD using Crow's Foot notation) and, for FIT3171, a UML diagram, rather than text lists. Representing the model visually is crucial for clarity.

Here are a few specific points to consider for your model:

1.  **Attributes vs. Relationships:** In your entity lists (Page 1), terms like "roves around", "assigned", "owned by", "has", "make", "attend", "follow up", "generates", and "contains" represent relationships between entities, not attributes within them. These should be shown as connecting lines with cardinalities in an ERD/UML diagram.
2.  **VISIT Key:** Using `vis_date`, `vis_time`, and `pet_id` as the composite key for VISIT might not guarantee uniqueness. Consider if adding `clinic_id` is necessary, or if an assumption about uniqueness is needed (which should be documented). Remember, surrogate keys are not allowed for this conceptual model.
3.  **Cardinalities:**
    *   The relationship between VET and CLINIC (base assignment) should reflect that a clinic must have *at least one* vet assigned (1..*), not exactly one. A vet is assigned to exactly one base clinic (1).
    *   The relationship between DRUG and PRESCRIPTION implies a drug can be prescribed many times (0..*), while a prescription uses exactly one drug (1). Your UML notation `Prescription "1" --- uses --- "1" Drug` needs adjustment on the Drug side.
4.  **Specialist Vets:** You've included `vet_specialisation`. This attribute should likely be optional (nullable) as not all vets are specialists. The "roves around" relationship you listed might be unnecessary; the fact that any vet (including specialists) can attend a visit at any clinic (as per the VISIT entity's relationships) already covers this behaviour.

Focus on drawing the ERD (and UML if required) using the correct notation (Crow's Foot/UML multiplicities) and ensure all relationships and cardinalities accurately reflect the business rules described.

Keep up the effort!

Assignment 2 – Design Mock-up and Report
Small Business Website Redesign
Create a high-fidelity mock-up image of a proposed website redesign for a local business. Write a short
report to document the redesign, providing theory-based justifications for important changes made.
Website Redesign Guidelines
• Create a high-fidelity mock-up image only - do not code a working webpage.
• Redesign the homepage of the website only - the entire homepage, no other pages.
• You can do either a desktop or mobile redesign - choose one only.
• Choose an appropriate tool to create a redesign mock-up that is of presentable quality.
• Any site content and design elements can be altered, with appropriate documented justification.
• Unnecessary features can be removed, missing features can be added.
• Regardless of local language, your redesign mock-up must be presented in English language.
Report Guidelines
• The report must be between 500 and 1000 words.
• Justify decisions responsible for significant visual design changes that you have made.
• Consider reasons for changes made to page layout and navigation menus.
• Justify any changes made to the site’s content and other information architecture issues.
• You may include reference images and diagrams – these do not contribute to the word count.
• Include a separate page for references – this does not contribute to the word count.
Marking Criteria (15%)
• Implementation of redesign mock-up
• Identification and critique of design issues
• Consideration of client and target demographic needs
• Evidence of research, justification of design decisions
• Report structure, formatting, and references
In-class Participation Milestones (2% × 2)
1. Attend the week 6 lab session and inform your tutor of the site that you have chosen to redesign.
• Refer to the “choosing a local business website” guidelines on the next page
2. Complete peer assessment of 3 redesigns submitted by other students before the end of week 9.
If you are unable to attend the lab session for a participation milestone due to an unexpected hardship or
other unavoidable circumstance, please contact your tutor to apply for special consideration.
Due: Week 8, Friday 8pm
• Submit your homepage redesign on Moodle as a JPG image.
• Submit your written report on Moodle as a DOCX or PDF file.

Choosing a local business website
The redesign must be for a real local small business with an existing website. Find a site that would
benefit from a redesign! If you can immediately find 5 things wrong with the site, it may be a good choice.
• Local: The business is located in the city/state/province in which you currently live.
• Small: The business must not be a well-known brand. It must operate in one city only.
• Existing: The business’ existing website needs to be currently accessible online.
If you are having difficulty finding a suitable business site to redesign, try using Google Maps (or other map
service) to search for different types of businesses in your local area.
Researching your site re-design
Your redesign should attempt to solve real problems found in the existing site.
• Content that is not useful, inappropriate, or missing
• Visual design and branding that does not suit the business or audience
• Information architecture problems that make content hard to locate
• Usability and technical issues that make the site difficult to use
The most important redesign decisions you make should be supported with appropriate reasoning that
takes into consideration:
• Client business goals
• Target demographic preferences and user goals
• Research into competing websites
• Observation of current website design trends
Choosing your design tool
Your redesign must be in the form of a high-fidelity mock-up – not a low-fidelity wireframe. Do not code a
working webpage. No marks are awarded in this assessment for coding.
You do not need to use professional user interface design tools. Choose a tool that you are comfortable
working with that will allow you to produce a clear and presentable design with relative ease.
Some suggestions:
• UI Design software
o Canva, Moqups (online design tools)
o Figma, Invision Studio (free professional UI design tools)
o Adobe XD or Sketch (commercial professional UI design tools)
• Image editing software
o Draw.io (or other online diagramming tools)
o Photopea.com, Paint.net (free graphics editors)
o Adobe Photoshop or Adobe Illustrator (professional graphics editors)
• Office productivity software
o Microsoft PowerPoint, Google Slides (recommended)
