Introduction  
This report is about the redesign of a real local business website. The business I chose is “Green Leaf Florist” located in Glen Waverley, VIC 3150. I have visited this florist before and found their flowers are fresh and beautiful. When I checked their website, I noticed several issues that could be improved, so I decided to use it for this assignment.

Problem Recognition  
The current website is not user-friendly. When customers first visit the homepage (see F1 in references), it is hard to find the main products. The navigation bar is small and not clear, and the “Shop Now” button is hidden at the bottom. There is too much text on the homepage, which makes it confusing for new users, especially older customers who may not be familiar with online shopping. The images of flowers are not high quality and do not attract attention. Also, there is no clear information about delivery options or store location on the homepage. The website does not have a section for special offers or featured bouquets, which are important for attracting customers. The background is plain white, which looks boring and does not match the brand of a florist.

Evaluation and Research  
A good florist website should have a clear layout, with easy access to main categories like “Bouquets”, “Plants”, and “Gifts”. According to research, websites with high-quality images and clear call-to-action buttons have higher conversion rates (Smith 2019). I also looked at other florist websites, such as “Fig & Bloom” and “Flowers Across Melbourne”, which use large, colourful images and simple navigation. They also highlight special offers and best sellers on the homepage. Studies show that customers prefer websites with visual appeal and easy navigation (Lee 2017). The use of soft colours and floral patterns can make the website more attractive and match the business brand. It is also important to have contact information and delivery details visible on the homepage.

Design  
Based on the problems above, I redesigned the homepage to make it more user-friendly and visually appealing. The top of the page has the “Green Leaf Florist” logo and a horizontal navigation bar with clear labels: “Home”, “Shop”, “Special Offers”, “Delivery Info”, “Gallery”, and “Contact”. The “Shop” menu has dropdown options for “Bouquets”, “Plants”, and “Gifts”. The “Special Offers” section is new and shows discounts or seasonal bouquets. The “Delivery Info” page gives details about delivery areas and times.

The main part of the homepage has a large banner image of fresh flowers with a “Shop Now” button in the centre, making it easy for customers to start shopping. Below the banner, there are three featured products with high-quality images and prices. There is also a section for “Best Sellers” and “Customer Favourites”, so customers can quickly see popular items. On the right side, I added a small box with store location, opening hours, and a phone number for easy contact. The background uses a soft green colour with a subtle floral pattern, which matches the florist theme and makes the website more inviting.

I removed unnecessary text and made the layout simple, so customers can find what they want quickly. The images are larger and clearer, and the “Shop Now” button is easy to find. The navigation bar is at the top and stays visible when scrolling. I also added a “Gallery” page to show photos of flower arrangements, which helps build trust with customers.

Summary  
A clear and attractive homepage is important for a small business like Green Leaf Florist. The redesign solves the problems of confusing layout, poor navigation, and lack of visual appeal. By using high-quality images, clear navigation, and matching the brand with soft colours, the website will attract more customers and make it easier for them to order flowers. This will help the business stand out from competitors and improve customer satisfaction.

(Reference images are attached as F1 and F2. The mock-up image is submitted separately.)

The Animal Doctors management have indicated:
● they do not require the clinic addresses or any phone numbers to be broken down into
component attributes
● they would like to be able to easily expand:
○ the vet specialisations, as they employ further vets with new specialisations, and
○ the types of animals, as they treat a new type which they have not previously seen
● the services which are provided during a visit are identified by a service code and have a
standard service cost which must be recorded as part of the database. Every visit incurs at
least one service charge. During a visit (treatment of a patient - the animal) the servicing vet
may vary the service standard cost depending on their professional judgement,
● the drugs which are provided during a visit have a standard cost which must be recorded as
part of the database, however like services this cost may be varied by the vet for a particular
visit
● when a drug is used as part of the visit (eg. an injection that the vet gives the patient in the
clinic) the frequency is not recorded (frequency is only used only for drugs given to the owner
to be administered to their pet following the visit eg. a twice daily antibiotic tablet), and
● management would like a count maintained, as part of the stored database data, which
indicates how many times a given specialist vet has serviced a visit in a clinic which is not
their home clinic.
Animal Doctor have provided several forms, depicted below, which they make use of, showing some
of the data they wish to record:
● a sample 'Pet Ownership Details' which shows pets owned by a specific owner, and the
owner’s preferred vet, and
● two samples 'Visit Invoice'.

Task to complete:
1. Perform normalisation to 3NF for the data depicted in the sample AD reports. Note that only
one normalisation is required for the Visit Invoice, you have been provided with two samples
so you can appreciate some of the variety which occurs.
The approach you are required to use is the same approach as shown in the normalisation
tutorial solution. The normalisation must be carried out form by form, beginning by you
representing the document you are working on as a single UNF form and then moving
through 1NF, 2NF and 3NF.
During normalisation, you must:
○ Not add surrogate keys.
○ Include all attributes (you must not remove any attribute as derivable)
○ Clearly show UNF, 1NF, 2NF and 3NF.
○ Clearly identify the Primary Key in all relations.
○ Clearly identify all dependencies at the various normalisation stages (Partial at 1NF,
Transitive at 2NF and Full at 3NF). You should use the same notation as depicted in
the normalisation sample solutions, for example:
attr1 -> attr2, attr3
If none exist you must note this by stating:
No partial dependencies present and/or
No transitive dependencies present
○ If required, carry out attribute synthesis.
The attribute names used in your normalisation and those on your subsequent logical model
must be the same.

case study and the normalisations you carried out in step 1 above, prepare a logical level
design for the Animal Doctors database.
○ The logical model must be drawn using the Oracle Data Modeler. The information
engineering or Crow’s foot notation must be used in drawing the model. Your logical
model must not show datatypes.
○ All relations depicted on this model must be in 3NF
○ You are required to add at least one surrogate key to your design (you are free to
select the most appropriate relation to make this change in)

the table structure, not simply comments in the schema file).
○ Check clauses/look up tables must be applied to attributes where appropriate.

nickname please edit the legend panel to show your actual name
○ Note that your GIT repository must clearly indicate your development history with
multiple commits/pushes as you work on your model.
3. Generate the schema for the database in Oracle Data Modeler and use the schema to
create the database in your Oracle account. The only edits you are permitted to carry out to
the generated schema file is to add header comment/s containing your details (student
name/id) and the commands to turn on and off spool/echo for your script. Ensure you:
○ Capture the output of the run of your schema statements using the spool command.
○ Ensure your script includes drop table statements at the start of the script.
○ Name the schema file as ad_schema.sql.

LLM tutor feedback:
Hi there,

Well done on generating and running the schema script for the Animal Doctors database. It's great to see that the script executed successfully, creating all the necessary tables, constraints, and comments.

**Strengths:**

*   **Successful Execution:** Your script ran without errors, which is excellent.
*   **Requirements Met:** You've included essential elements like `DROP TABLE` statements, attribute comments, primary keys, foreign keys, and check constraints (`PET` table).
*   **Lookup Tables:** The `ANIMAL` and `SPECIALIST` tables are correctly implemented as lookup tables, allowing for future expansion as requested.
*   **Variable Costs:** You've effectively modelled the standard vs. actual costs for services and drugs using the `SERVICE`/`SERVICING` and `DRUG`/`PRESCRIPTION` tables.
*   **Surrogate Key:** The `visit_id` in the `VISIT` table fulfills the requirement for adding at least one surrogate key.

**Areas for Consideration:**

*   **`vet_count` Attribute:** The requirement to store the count of a specialist vet's visits outside their home clinic is tricky. Storing this directly in the `VET` table (`vet_count`) creates redundant data that's hard to keep accurate. In relational design, it's usually better to calculate such derived values when needed (e.g., using a query or view) rather than storing them directly.
*   **`visit_id2` in `VISIT` Table:** The purpose of the `visit_id2` column and the self-referencing foreign key in the `VISIT` table isn't immediately clear from the assignment brief. Ensure this accurately reflects a specific requirement you identified.

Overall, this is a strong submission demonstrating a good understanding of schema implementation. Keep up the great work!

Assignment description:
Assignment 2A:   Workbook 4
Reading & Project Planning Practice
 for Assignment 2B
Summer FIT5057 Formative Learning & Assessment
The Concepts behind using Workbooks in Assignments 1 and 2
In many units, critical and reflective thinking and literacy skills are expected and often not explicitly taught. FIT5057 is unique because it also offers students practice drills to develop their critical thinking and literacy skills when preparing for the major assignment 1 and 2 work.
Through these practice drills via Workbooks’ tasks, you will be guided & supported to:
Learn by applying the key PM concepts and techniques you need to write a project plan and understand the practice implications and real-world case studies of theoretical PM concepts.
Learn by applying the techniques that make up research, critical thinking, reading and writing skills, which collectively are your employability skills.
Learn by hands-on experience the real implications of continuous teamplay, time management, issues reporting and collaborative resolution with peers and your tutor.
Reflecting on these practice drills will place you in a better position to understand the relevant PM info you need and identify the skill-based techniques you need to apply to complete all the 3 summative assignments.
From a teaching strategy perspective, the workbooks provide you the formative learning experience, with close support from your peers and tutor. The major assignments provide the summative evidence that you are able to understand and apply the relevant PM concepts and the appropriate PM, research, critical thinking, reading and writing techniques to respond effectively to the given assignments.
If you often skip tutorials and consults, you will expose yourself to ineffective formative learning and be prepared for stressful and painful consequences  when completing the assignments near submission deadlines.
For Assignment 2, your attitude and capacity to work with other people from diverse backgrounds and different abilities can also determine the challenges you may face in groupwork and influence the quality of not just your own, but your peers’ contributions in completing the assignment work. You may also make or break relationships, which may enhance or block your future career developments as working with, relating to people from diverse backgrounds and abilities are important soft skills you need to lead and motivate people when you aim for senior management and leadership career paths.
There are 5 success factors for doing well in assignments:
That you individually learn effectively and always ask questions among each other and your tutor when not sure;
That you regular attend tutorials and consultations, and keep up to date with lecture lessons;
That you engage in effective group collaboration and communications;
That you practice effective time management and have succession plans in case some members become unavailable during the semester.
That you are able to respect each other despite coming from different backgrounds or having different learning abilities; and work positively with everyone regardless of any people issues you encounter.
Instructions for using each workbook:
Pre-Class Readings
Before attending tutorial class and doing any workbook tasks, we expect you to firstly view the online lecture resources, to grasp understanding of the concepts and techniques you will need to know when doing tutorial and workbook exercises. Without this pre-class preparation, your learning and our teaching is at high risk of being ineffective.
Each workbook will come with attached readings. You are allowed to reuse the given readings in your assignments as references, if appropriate. While you will learn research methods in this subject, the provided workbook readings are designed to direct you to find other similar, hence relevant resources for  developing the sought answers to workbook questions. Rather than spending hours searching for references, the references are supplied to help students focus more time on learning, understanding and practising applying the relevant PM concepts and techniques needed in the assignment work. If you do not attempt the workbook work effectively or on time, your actions are likely to also affect the quality of your assignment outcomes.

User Requirements Info Provided
They have provided your team with the following baseline requirement info:
Current System Environment (IT News)
First System Solution
Currently, the Salesforce integrated covid-19 contact tracing and testing systems is being rolled out, to replace the first highly manual system that uses an IBM’s i2 platform, an Advanced Analytics and Intelligence Analysis system.  The first solution was a myriad of existing departmental and different vendor systems and manual processes that feed information into the IBM’s i2 platform. It was first thought that integration configuration would provide the contact tracing and testing data processing for the Victorian Government.
The Victorian government stopped using the IBM analytics tool as part of its first digital contact tracing system after its requirements changed and the system could not be reconfigured.
On October 12, 2020, during the inquiry hearings, IBM reported that it became clear to them that “the DHHS [Department of Health and Human Services] data analysis team required a system capable of alerting them to significant events or conditions that should be followed up by an analyst, in contrast to an analyst using the system to discover these conditions or events.” However there is no clarity in the inquiry report of where this misunderstanding might have occurred.
Second System Solution
Assume the following functional overview of the SalesForce’s system solution (The Guardian, Sep 2020;  (Victorian Premier’s Office, 30 Nov 2020):
Is a digitised system covering the whole contact tracing process – from test results to interviews, phone calls, and the management of cases and contacts.
Can consolidate and align many different platforms into one platform significantly reducing pen and paper manual data entry.”
End user teams can be based remotely because they will be able to access the data from anywhere in the state.
Sends text notifications automatically to people when they tested positive.
Fax machines and phone calls are still being used in GP practices where software does not link with department systems.
An IBM artificial intelligence product from the defence department will provide predictive modelling “that can help you pick up patterns that might not be necessarily obvious”.
Feeds Victorian data to the national benchmark data reporting system that shows Victoria performance against national benchmarks in reporting cases being contacted and interviewed within 24 hours, and close contacts being identified within 48 hours.
Provides an online dashboard for Covid cases in Victoria will soon contain information about outbreaks in health workplaces, and the 14-day rolling average will be included in data for metropolitan Melbourne and regional Victoria, given it is the metric used to decide the easing of restrictions.
Instead of adopting a proven and successfully working QR contact tracing app used by the NSW and ACT Governments, a new client installed QR coded contact tracing app is currently being developed and rolled out for businesses to check in patrons’ names and contact phone numbers.
The automated new system would run in parallel to the existing contact tracing efforts of 2,600 workers in the state to ensure a smooth transition. More staff could be added to help boost efforts at a later date.

System Platform Requirements
Consideration is being looked at for redeveloping the second system solution as a cloud platform solution, which allows the Victorian Government to commercialise and potentially export its third model of integrated covid-19 contact tracing and testing system solution as covid-19 “data centres on the fly”, allowing future revenues to offset the cost of system changes since Covid-19 pandemic started in early 2020.
The Client also mentioned that they intend to use a Robotics Process Automation (RPA) tool, notably the  UiPath app to allow both state and national covid-19 responders (such as covid-19 scientists, public health statisticians and other administrators) to develop and automate their own front-end applications, including specialised reports for more powerful data analytics mining capabilities without the need for programmers. As this is a new work productivity tool, all members of the project team and covid-19 responder-teams in other state and national covid-19 work related departments need to be trained in using this apps development tool.
The Client agrees to adopt the national cybersecurity guidelines provided by the Australian Cybersecurity Centre’s advice, especially the ones regarding Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) actors targeting Australian health sector organisations and COVID-19 essential services . Your team is expected to advise the integration of the appropriate cybersecurity recommendations into your proposed project plan.
These platform expectations mean the new program needs to take a DevOps methodology in defining its cloud platform system solution. The DevOps program will involve a number of projects that innovates this system solution as a range of independently deplorable services.

Assignment 2A - SQL - Centre Breeding (CB)
Assignment weighting 10% - Lecturer in Charge: Minh Le
As natural habitats disappear, it is increasingly difficult to ensure that all species can survive
in the wild. One solution is to use zoos, reserves and other types of organizations that keep
wild animals in captivity as conservation centres for various species. To assist in recording
the animals in captivity and their offspring, a Centre Breeding database is to be created in
which all centres will record the animals which they hold and the breeding events which take
place at the centres.
Database designers have considered the requirements and arrived at the following data
model:
Page 1 of 8


==================== Page 2 ====================

Model Details:
SPECIES
Records the details of the species, the name of the species, as well as its popular name and the
genus and family to which the species belongs. The relevant details for the Tasmanian Devil, for
instance, are as follows ...
Genus: Sarcophilus
Species: harrisii
Popular name: Tasmanian Devil
Family: DASYURIDAE
It is also important to know the species’ natural range, which is usually given as a description of the
geographic regions and natural habitats where a species is found in the wild.

/*

file, create an SQL schema file which can be used to create this database in 
Oracle.

-- Center Breeding SCHEMA
-- DROP TABLES 
DROP TABLE animal CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

DROP TABLE breeding_event CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

DROP TABLE centre CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

DROP TABLE species CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;

-- CREATE TABLES
CREATE TABLE animal (
    animal_id   NUMBER(6) NOT NULL,
    animal_sex  CHAR(1) NOT NULL,
    brevent_id  NUMBER(6),
    centre_id   CHAR(6) NOT NULL,
    spec_genus  VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL,
    spec_name   VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL
);

ALTER TABLE animal
    ADD CONSTRAINT animal_sex_check CHECK ( animal_sex IN ( 'F', 'M' ) );

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.animal_id IS
    'Unique ID for this animal ';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.animal_sex IS
    'Sex of animal (M or F)';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.brevent_id IS
    'Unique identifier for breeding event';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.centre_id IS
    'Unique identifier for Centre';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.spec_genus IS
    'Species genus';

COMMENT ON COLUMN animal.spec_name IS
    'Species Name';

ALTER TABLE animal ADD CONSTRAINT animal_pk PRIMARY KEY ( animal_id );

CREATE TABLE breeding_event (
    brevent_id    NUMBER(6) NOT NULL,
    brevent_date  DATE NOT NULL,
    mother_id     NUMBER(6) NOT NULL,
    father_id     NUMBER(6) NOT NULL
);

COMMENT ON COLUMN breeding_event.brevent_id IS
    'Unique identifier for breeding event';

COMMENT ON COLUMN breeding_event.brevent_date IS
    'Date breeding event took place';

COMMENT ON COLUMN breeding_event.mother_id IS
    'Unique ID for this animal ';

COMMENT ON COLUMN breeding_event.father_id IS
    'Unique ID for this animal ';

ALTER TABLE breeding_event ADD CONSTRAINT breeding_event_pk PRIMARY KEY ( brevent_id );

CREATE TABLE centre (
    centre_id        CHAR(6) NOT NULL,
    centre_name      VARCHAR2(40) NOT NULL,
    centre_address   VARCHAR2(100) NOT NULL,
    centre_director  VARCHAR2(30) NOT NULL,
    centre_phone_no  VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL
);

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_id IS
    'Unique identifier for Centre';

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_name IS
    'Name of Centre';

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_address IS
    'Centre address';

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_director IS
    'Name of director of centre';

COMMENT ON COLUMN centre.centre_phone_no IS
    'Centre phone contact number';

ALTER TABLE centre ADD CONSTRAINT centre_pk PRIMARY KEY ( centre_id );

ALTER TABLE centre ADD CONSTRAINT centre_name_unq UNIQUE ( centre_name );

CREATE TABLE species (
    spec_genus          VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL,
    spec_name           VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL,
    spec_popular_name   VARCHAR2(40) NOT NULL,
    spec_family         VARCHAR2(20) NOT NULL,
    spec_natural_range  VARCHAR2(100) NOT NULL
);

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_genus IS
    'Species genus';

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_name IS
    'Species Name';

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_popular_name IS
    'Species popular name';

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_family IS
    'Species family';

COMMENT ON COLUMN species.spec_natural_range IS
    'Description of natural range of species';

ALTER TABLE species ADD CONSTRAINT species_pk PRIMARY KEY ( spec_genus,
                                                            spec_name );

ALTER TABLE species ADD CONSTRAINT spec_pop_name_unq UNIQUE ( spec_popular_name );

ALTER TABLE breeding_event
    ADD CONSTRAINT animalfather_be FOREIGN KEY ( father_id )
        REFERENCES animal ( animal_id );

ALTER TABLE breeding_event
    ADD CONSTRAINT animalmother_be FOREIGN KEY ( mother_id )
        REFERENCES animal ( animal_id );

ALTER TABLE animal
    ADD CONSTRAINT be_animal FOREIGN KEY ( brevent_id )
        REFERENCES breeding_event ( brevent_id );

ALTER TABLE animal
    ADD CONSTRAINT centre_animal FOREIGN KEY ( centre_id )
        REFERENCES centre ( centre_id );

ALTER TABLE animal
    ADD CONSTRAINT species_animal FOREIGN KEY ( spec_genus,
                                                   spec_name )
        REFERENCES species ( spec_genus,
                             spec_name );

-- (i)
-- drop sequence commands not required
drop sequence animal_seq;
drop sequence breedevent_seq;

--  increment by 1 not required, it is the default, should not place upper limit
create sequence animal_seq start with 500 increment by 1;

create sequence breedevent_seq start with 500 increment by 1;

-- (ii)

UPDATE animal
SET
    centre_id = (
        SELECT
            centre_id
        FROM
            centre
        WHERE
            centre_name = 'Kruger National Park'
    )
WHERE
    centre_id = (
        SELECT
            centre_id
        FROM
            centre
        WHERE
            centre_name = 'SanWild Wildlife Sanctuary'
    );

DELETE FROM centre
WHERE
    centre_name = 'SanWild Wildlife Sanctuary';

COMMIT;

"""
Created By: FIT9136 - Teaching Team
Created Date: Sun March 07 10:00:00 2021
Updated Date: Tue March 09 12:30:00 2021
Version: 1.2.0
Description: This script reads, analyze, and visualize the pgn (chess game) files.
"""

# import required Libraires
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

'''
read_pgn function is to read pgn files, and generate
a list containing strings of each game (we assume there
are mutilple files in the dataset we need to read, so we 
use 'os' to list all file paths)
'''
def read_pgn(filename):
    clean_string = []
    # read pgn of each game
    with open(filename, encoding='windows-1252') as f:
        lines = f.readlines()
        # remove all meta data and ''
        for line in lines:
            if not line.startswith('[') and line.strip() != '':
                clean_string.append(line.strip())

    # get the index of lines start with '1.'
    i_index = []
    for ind in range(len(clean_string)):
        if clean_string[ind].startswith("1."):
            i_index.append(ind)

    # join strings in the list, and return the final result
    # e.g ['1.e4 c5 2.c3 Nf6 3.e5 Nd5 4.d4 Nc6 5.Nf3 cxd4 6.cxd4 e6 7.a3 d6 8.Bd3 Qa5+ ',
    # '9.Bd2 Qb6 10.Nc3 Nxc3 11.Bxc3 dxe5 12.dxe5 Be7 13.O-O Bd7 14.Nd2 Qc7',..]
    final_list = []
    for ind in range(len(i_index)):
        if ind != (len(i_index) - 1):
            final_list.append(" ".join(clean_string[i_index[ind]:i_index[ind + 1]]))
        else:
            final_list.append(" ".join(clean_string[i_index[ind]:]))

    # Writing the game data into game string file.
    with open("game_string.txt", "w") as game_file:
        output_str = ""
        for each in final_list:
            temp_str = ' '.join(each.split()[:-1])
            output_str += str(temp_str) + "\n"

        game_file.write(output_str)

    return final_list


"""
sub_files function is to generate the sub files of the game files
"""
def sub_files(final_list):
    # game file number. e.g. 1b, 2b,...
    file_num = 1
    # iterate each string in the string list generated from 2.2
    for game in final_list:
        # split each game string by ' ' and exclude the last two strings.
        game = game.split(' ')[:-1]
        # open files named with xb.txt and xw.txt
        with open('./output/' + str(file_num) + 'w' + '.txt', 'w') as wf, \
                open('./output/' + str(file_num) + 'b' + '.txt', 'w') as bf:
            move_num = 0
            # iterate game by each move
            for move in game:
                # if move start with digit, e.g. '1.e4', then spilt it by '.',
                # write to the white file
                if move != '' and move[0].isdigit():
                    white_move = move.split('.')[1]
                    move_num = move.split('.')[0]
                    wf.write("{0}:{1} \n".format(str(move_num), white_move))
                # else write to the black file
                else:
                    bf.write("{0}:{1} \n".format(str(move_num), move))
        file_num += 1


"""
dataframe_counts function will accepts the final game string list
and will convert into dataframes 
"""
def dataframe_counts(final_list):
    # create dict to record first move count for each player
    white_count = {}
    black_count = {}
    # iterate the list generated from Q2.2
    for game in final_list:
        # for each game, split the string by ' '
        game = game.split(' ')
        white_firstmove_name = game[0].split('.')[1]
        black_firstmove_name = game[1]
        # count the first move for white player
        if white_firstmove_name not in white_count:
            white_count[white_firstmove_name] = 1
        else:
            white_count[white_firstmove_name] += 1
        # count the first move for black player
        if black_firstmove_name not in black_count:
            black_count[black_firstmove_name] = 1
        else:
            black_count[black_firstmove_name] += 1
    # create dataframe for white player
    w_move_name = [i for i in white_count.keys()]
    w_move_count = [i for i in white_count.values()]
    w_df = pd.DataFrame({'move_name': w_move_name, 'move_count': w_move_count})
    # create dataframe for black player
    b_move_name = [i for i in black_count.keys()]
    b_move_count = [i for i in black_count.values()]
    b_df = pd.DataFrame({'move_name': b_move_name, 'move_count': b_move_count})

    # return a tuple with two dataframes
    return (w_df, b_df)


"""
Show_ten function will plot the ten most common moves
"""
def show_ten(df_w, df_b):
    # create another colomn to comment the dataframe
    df_w['comment'] = 'white'
    df_b['comment'] = 'black'
    # concat  two dataframes
    concat_df = pd.concat([df_w, df_b])

    # sort df by descending, and get the top ten first move
    concat_df = concat_df.sort_values(by=['move_count'], ascending=False).iloc[0:10].reset_index(drop=True)

    # name color and labeles
    color = {'white': 'red', 'black': 'black'}
    labels = ['white', 'black']
    # get the value for x, y, and width
    width = 0.4
    x = concat_df.iloc[:, 0]
    y = concat_df.iloc[:, 1]
    # apply color to bar
    colors = [color[i] for i in concat_df.iloc[:, 2]]
    # draw bar
    bars = plt.bar(x=x, height=y, width=width, color=colors)
    # apply labels to legend
    handles = [plt.Rectangle((0, 0), 1, 1, color=color[label]) for label in labels]
    plt.legend(handles, labels)
    # create x,ylabel, and title
    plt.xlabel('First move name')
    plt.ylabel('First move count')
    plt.title('The ten most common first moves')

    plt.show()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    pgn_data = read_pgn('./Tutor_test.pgn')
    sub_files(pgn_data)
    res = dataframe_counts(pgn_data)
    print(res)
    df_w, df_b = dataframe_counts(pgn_data)
    show_ten(df_w, df_b)





































"""
Created By: FIT9136 - Teaching Team
Created Date: Sun March 08 08:00:00 2021
Updated Date: Tue March 010 04:30:00 2021
Version: 1.2.0
Description: This script has 3 classes Wrangle, Analyse, and Visualize, which reads, create dataframes, & simple plot
respectively for the pgn (chess game) files.
"""

# Import required files
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.lines import Line2D


class Wrangle:

    #  Constructer will accept the file name and save the file content into instance variable "data"
    def __init__(self, filepath):
        with open(filepath, encoding='windows-1252') as f:
            self.data = f.readlines()

    def getCleanGames(self):
        """
        Returns: list of moveText for every game

        Description:
        For every line:
        Check if line is starting with '[' or is an empty line after strip()
        if not, we add this line to the list
        """
        games = []
        current_game = []
        # looping throw the game data
        for line in self.data:
            # looking for the "[" as all the meta-data content starts with it.
            if line.startswith('['):
                continue
            # if not then, strip the line and join into the game
            elif not line.strip():
                if current_game:
                    games.append(' '.join(current_game))
                    current_game = []
            else:
                current_game.append(line.strip())
        if current_game:
            games.append(' '.join(current_game))

        # writing all the data into the game string file
        with open("game_string.txt", "w") as game_file:
            output_str = ""
            for each in games:
                temp_str = ' '.join(each.split()[:-1])
                output_str += str(temp_str) + "\n"

            game_file.write(output_str)

        return games

    def separateMoves(self):
        """
        Split white and black moves for each game, and store to files in <game number><b/w> convention
        Description:
        Split the moves based on the pattern

        Example: 1.e4 Nf6

        After splitting, if index is even number, it is white move(1.e4), otherwise it is black move(Nf6).

        The last splitted string is the result, for example: 1-0, so we leave out the last splitted string first.

        For white move, we have to split by '.' and get the latter part of the string.
        For black move, we do not have to do further processing.

        We write the white move to the white file and black move to the black file.

        Repeat for every game.

        """
        games = self.getCleanGames()
        # looping through each of the game string and getting the white and black games
        for idx in range(1, len(games) + 1):
            game = games[idx - 1]
            # simultaneous writing the data into the output folder
            with open('output/' + str(idx) + 'w', 'w') as whiteFile, open('output/' + str(idx) + 'b', 'w') as blackFile:
                moves = game.split()
                for moveIdx in range(len(moves) - 1):
                    move = moves[moveIdx]
                    # splitting the data based on the "."
                    move = move.split('.')[-1]
                    # and checking the index values (if even then it's white move)
                    if moveIdx % 2 == 0:
                        whiteFile.write(str(move) + '\n')
                    # else, it is blac move
                    else:
                        blackFile.write(str(move) + '\n')


class Analyse:

    def __init__(self, games):
        self.games = games

    def getFirstMoves(self):
        """
        Returns: a tuple of dataframe with first move counts from white and black player respectively

        Description:
        For every moveText, we split the text using space character and extract the first 2 items.
        The first item will be the first move for white player.
        The second item will be the first move for black player.

        For white player move, we have to further split by '.' and extract the latter part of the splitted text, as mentioned above.

        Create white and black dataframes with the corresponding data.

        With aggregation functions of pandas, we count the existance of every first moves.

        Rename the columns and set index.

        """

        white = []
        black = []
        # using loop to get seperate moves in to white and black list
        for game in self.games:
            moves = game.split()[:-1]
            if len(moves) >= 1:
                whiteMove = moves[0].split('.')[-1]
            if len(moves) >= 2:
                blackMove = moves[1]

            white.append(whiteMove)
            black.append(blackMove)

        # creating the dataframe and using groupby and count function to get the count values
        # reset_index function reset the index values of the dataframe
        # set_index function helps to set the index of the dataframe.
        whiteDf = pd.DataFrame(white, columns=['moves']).reset_index().groupby(
            ['moves']).count().reset_index().set_index(['moves'])
        whiteDf.columns = ['count']
        blackDf = pd.DataFrame(black, columns=['moves']).reset_index().groupby(
            ['moves']).count().reset_index().set_index(['moves'])
        blackDf.columns = ['count']

        # returning the tuple of dataframes
        return whiteDf, blackDf


class Visualize:
    def __init__(self, whiteDF, blackDF):
        self.whiteDF = whiteDF
        self.blackDF = blackDF

    def plot_top_10_moves(self):
        """
        Description:

        Concatenate the dataframes of white and black first moves, sort by count in descending order.

        Plot the bar chart with color coding.

        Set the x label, y label and title.

        """

        (white, black) = self.whiteDF, self.blackDF

        white['player'] = 'white'
        black['player'] = 'black'

        combined = pd.concat([white, black]).sort_values('count', ascending=False)[:10].reset_index()

        colors = dict(zip(['black', 'white'], ['r', 'g']))

        fig, ax = plt.subplots()
        combined.plot.bar(x='moves', y='count', color=[colors[i] for i in combined['player']], ax=ax)

        ax.legend(handles=[Line2D([0], [0], color='g', lw=4, label='white'),
                           Line2D([0], [0], color='r', lw=4, label='black')])
        ax.set_title('Count of most common first moves')
        ax.set_ylabel('count')
        plt.show()

    vis_pgn = Visualize(whiteMovesDf, blackMovesDf)

FIT5205 - Data in Society (Assignment 4) Approx. 2500 words  
Ethical Design Report – Automated Facial Recognition in Policing

Introduction  
This report critically examines the use of automated facial recognition (AFR) technology in policing, as described in the case study by the Australian Human Rights Commission (AHRC) and related literature. The analysis is structured to: (A) summarise the case study; (B) evaluate ethical design issues using Beard and Longstaff’s framework; (C) identify human rights concerns based on the AHRC Human Rights and Technology Discussion Paper; (D) discuss governance challenges and responses; and (E) provide recommendations for improvement. The report concludes that while AFR offers potential benefits for law enforcement, its current deployment raises significant ethical and human rights issues that must be addressed through robust governance and design reforms.

A. Case Study  
Automated facial recognition (AFR) technology has been increasingly adopted by law enforcement agencies in Australia and globally. AFR systems use machine learning algorithms to match images of individuals’ faces captured in public or private spaces against databases of known identities (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 12). The technology is promoted as a tool for enhancing public safety, identifying suspects, and preventing crime. However, its use has sparked controversy due to concerns about accuracy, bias, privacy, and the potential for misuse.

The AHRC case study highlights several deployments of AFR in Australia, including trials by state police and proposals for a national facial recognition database (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 13). These initiatives often proceed without comprehensive legal frameworks or public consultation. Notably, the technology’s accuracy varies across demographic groups, with higher error rates for people of colour, women, and young people (Beard & Longstaff, 2018, p. 45). There have been documented cases internationally where misidentification by AFR has led to wrongful arrests and detentions (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 14).

The lack of transparency around how AFR systems are procured, tested, and operated further exacerbates public concern. In many instances, individuals are unaware that their biometric data is being collected and processed, raising questions about consent and accountability. The case study underscores the urgent need for ethical and human rights-based approaches to the design and governance of AFR in policing.

B. Ethical Design Issues (Beard & Longstaff Framework)  
Applying Beard and Longstaff’s (2018) Ethical Design Principles to AFR in policing reveals several areas of concern:

I. Ought Before Can  
The deployment of AFR is often driven by what is technologically possible rather than what is ethically justified. The principle of “ought before can” requires that the use of AFR be guided by clear ethical reasoning, not just technical capability. In practice, law enforcement agencies have implemented AFR without fully considering the broader social implications or obtaining social licence (Beard & Longstaff, 2018, p. 19).

II. Non-Instrumentalism  
AFR systems risk treating individuals as mere data points, reducing people to their biometric features. This instrumentalises individuals, undermining their intrinsic dignity and autonomy. The lack of meaningful consent and the potential for mass surveillance further violate this principle (Beard & Longstaff, 2018, p. 22).

III. Self-Determinism  
The use of AFR in public spaces limits individuals’ ability to control how their personal data is used. People cannot opt out of surveillance in public, and there is little transparency about how their images are processed or stored. This undermines self-determination and informed consent (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 15).

IV. Responsibility  
There is a lack of clear accountability for errors or abuses arising from AFR use. When misidentification occurs, it is often unclear whether responsibility lies with the technology provider, the police, or the government. This diffusion of responsibility is contrary to ethical design, which requires clear lines of accountability (Beard & Longstaff, 2018, p. 27).

V. Net Benefit  
While AFR may offer benefits in terms of crime prevention, these must be weighed against the risks of false positives, discrimination, and erosion of civil liberties. The net benefit is questionable, especially given the technology’s documented inaccuracies and the potential for harm to vulnerable groups (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 16).

VI. Fairness  
AFR systems have been shown to exhibit bias, particularly against people of colour and women. This undermines the principle of fairness and risks perpetuating systemic discrimination in policing (Beard & Longstaff, 2018, p. 31).

VII. Accessibility  
Although AFR is not a user-facing technology, its impact is not evenly distributed. Marginalised communities are more likely to be subject to surveillance and misidentification, raising concerns about equitable treatment (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 17).

VIII. Purpose  
The stated purpose of AFR is to enhance public safety, but its use must be proportionate and justified. Mission creep—where technology is used for purposes beyond its original intent—is a significant risk, as seen in proposals to use AFR for minor offences or public order policing (Beard & Longstaff, 2018, p. 35).

C. Human Rights Issues (AHRC Framework)  
The AHRC Human Rights and Technology Discussion Paper identifies several human rights at risk from AFR in policing:

● Right to equality and non-discrimination  
AFR’s documented biases result in higher error rates for certain demographic groups, leading to discriminatory outcomes (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 18).

● Right to privacy  
AFR enables mass surveillance and the collection of sensitive biometric data without consent. This constitutes a significant intrusion into individuals’ private lives (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 19).

● Freedom of expression and assembly  
The presence of AFR in public spaces can have a chilling effect on free expression and peaceful assembly, as individuals may avoid protests or public gatherings for fear of being identified and monitored (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 20).

● Right to an effective remedy  
When AFR leads to wrongful identification or arrest, affected individuals may have limited avenues for redress, especially if the technology’s operation is opaque (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 21).

● Right to benefit from scientific progress  
While AFR represents technological advancement, its benefits are not equitably distributed, and its risks disproportionately affect marginalised groups (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 22).

D. Governance Challenges and Responses  
The governance of AFR in policing faces several challenges, as outlined in the AHRC Discussion Paper (pp. 33–46):

1. Lack of Legal Frameworks  
There is currently no comprehensive legal framework regulating the use of AFR in Australia. Existing privacy and anti-discrimination laws are inadequate to address the unique risks posed by biometric surveillance (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 34).

2. Transparency and Accountability  
AFR systems are often procured and deployed without public consultation or transparency about their operation. This undermines public trust and makes it difficult to hold agencies accountable for misuse or errors (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 36).

3. Oversight and Redress  
There is a lack of independent oversight bodies with the power to audit AFR systems, investigate complaints, and enforce compliance with human rights standards (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 38).

4. Vendor Lock-in and Proprietary Systems  
Many AFR systems are provided by private vendors who claim intellectual property protections over their algorithms, limiting external scrutiny and accountability (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 39).

5. International Precedents  
Other jurisdictions, such as the European Union, have begun to introduce stricter regulations on biometric surveillance, including moratoriums on certain uses of AFR. Australia risks falling behind international best practice if it does not act (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 41).

Responses  
The AHRC recommends a human rights-based approach to technology governance, including:

- Enacting clear, enforceable laws regulating AFR use  
- Requiring transparency and public consultation before deployment  
- Establishing independent oversight bodies  
- Ensuring access to remedies for affected individuals  
- Mandating regular audits for bias and accuracy

E. Recommendations  
Based on the analysis above, the following recommendations are proposed:

1. Moratorium on AFR Deployment  
Suspend the use of AFR in policing until comprehensive legal and ethical frameworks are in place, as recommended by the AHRC (Farthing et al., 2019, p. 45).

2. Enact Specific Legislation  
Develop and enact legislation that specifically regulates the use of AFR, including requirements for transparency, accountability, and human rights impact assessments.

3. Independent Oversight  
Establish an independent oversight body with the power to audit AFR systems, investigate complaints, and enforce compliance with human rights standards.

4. Mandatory Bias and Accuracy Audits  
Require regular, independent audits of AFR systems for bias and accuracy, with results made public.

5. Public Consultation and Participation  
Ensure meaningful public consultation before any deployment of AFR, particularly with communities most likely to be affected.

6. Right to Redress  
Guarantee effective remedies for individuals harmed by AFR misidentification or misuse.

7. Limit Scope and Purpose  
Restrict AFR use to serious offences and prohibit its use for minor infractions or general surveillance.

Conclusion  
AFR technology in policing presents significant ethical and human rights challenges. While it offers potential benefits for public safety, its current deployment risks discrimination, privacy violations, and erosion of civil liberties. Applying Beard and Longstaff’s ethical design principles and the AHRC human rights framework reveals substantial gaps in governance and accountability. Robust legal, ethical, and oversight mechanisms are urgently needed to ensure that AFR is used in a manner that respects human rights and public trust.
